topics.title: Operating System Overview - Functions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operating System Overview - Functions

# Operating System Overview — Functions

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating System (OS):** A *system software* that acts as an **intermediary** between the **user** and the **computer hardware**. It manages hardware resources, provides essential services to application programs, and establishes a uniform interface between the user and the machine.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PEEOT521 – Modern Operating Systems), the operating system is formally defined as a **resource allocator** and a **control program**:

- **As a Resource Allocator:** The OS manages finite hardware resources (CPU cycles, RAM, disk blocks, I/O devices) among competing processes in a fair, efficient, and secure manner.
- **As a Control Program:** The OS supervises the execution of user programs to prevent errors and improper use of the hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The OS is *not* an application. It is the *only* software treated as an extension of the hardware, providing *abstraction*, *protection*, and *multiplexing* of physical resources.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Manager

Imagine a large, busy restaurant:

| Restaurant Element | Operating System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hungry customers (diners) | Application programs (user processes) |
| Waiters, tables, kitchen, ingredients | Hardware resources (CPU, RAM, Disk, I/O) |
| **Restaurant Manager** | **The Operating System** |
| The menu (interface) | System calls / API |
| Reservation & seating rules | Process scheduling & memory allocation |
| Health & safety inspector | Security & protection module |

The **restaurant manager** does not cook food (no business logic). The manager simply ensures the right resources (tables, waiters) are available to the right customers at the right time, prevents fights over tables, and ensures nobody steals from the kitchen. In exactly the same way, the OS does not run your application logic; it merely *orchestrates* the underlying hardware so that programs execute correctly and efficiently.

---

### The Two Views of an Operating System

> [!NOTE]
> **User View (Top-Down Perspective):** From the user's standpoint, the OS exists to make the computer *easy to use*. The primary goal is **convenience**, **ease of use**, and **resource utilization** for the end user.
>
> **System View (Bottom-Up Perspective):** From the hardware's standpoint, the OS is a **resource allocator** that manages the limited hardware efficiently. The primary goal is **performance**, **throughput**, and **fairness**.

| Viewpoint | Primary Goal | Optimized For |
|---|---|---|
| User View | Convenience, abstraction | Ease of interaction |
| System View | Efficiency, control | Hardware utilization, throughput, protection |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU Exams:** If a 14-mark question asks *"Discuss the functions of an operating system"*, you **must** present your answer with **both viewpoints** — convenience to the user *and* efficient resource management to the system. Skipping either is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Core Functions of an Operating System

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes OS functions into **six major domains**. Each function is a logical *subsystem* of the kernel (or microkernel services) that operates continuously from system boot to shutdown.

---

## 2.1 Process Management

A **process** is a *program in execution* — a dynamic entity that includes the program code, the current activity (program counter, registers), the stack, the data section, and the heap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Process Management?** Modern systems are *multiprogrammed*. The CPU may have one or many cores, but there are **dozens to thousands of processes** competing for execution. Without centralized process management, processes would overwrite each other's memory, deadlock each other, and starve forever.

**Core sub-functions of Process Management:**

1. **Process Creation & Termination:** Spawning new processes (`fork`, `CreateProcess`) and cleaning up zombie processes.
2. **Process Scheduling:** Deciding *which* process gets the CPU and *when*. Algorithms include FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, Priority, Multilevel Queue, MLFQ, CFS.
3. **Process Synchronization:** Coordinating access to shared resources using locks, semaphores, monitors, condition variables.
4. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC):** Allowing processes to exchange data via pipes, sockets, shared memory, message queues, signals.
5. **Deadlock Handling:** Detection, prevention, avoidance, and recovery from circular-wait scenarios.

---

## 2.2 Memory Management

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Memory management is the OS subsystem responsible for tracking every byte of physical RAM and virtual address space, deciding which processes reside in memory, and ensuring processes do not access each other's memory regions.

**Core sub-functions of Memory Management:**

1. **Logical-to-Physical Address Translation:** Using base/limit registers, segmentation, or **paging with Translation Lookaside Buffers (TLB)**.
2. **Memory Allocation:** Contiguous allocation, paging, or segmentation.
3. **Swapping & Virtual Memory:** Moving entire processes/pages in and out of disk-based swap space to satisfy memory demand.
4. **Page Replacement:** When physical frames are exhausted, choosing a victim frame using algorithms such as **FIFO, LRU, Optimal, Clock (Second Chance), LFU, MFU**.
5. **Memory Protection:** Enforcing access permissions via the MMU (Memory Management Unit).

---

## 2.3 File Management

A **file** is a named collection of related information recorded on secondary storage (typically a disk). The **file system** is the OS component that organizes, stores, retrieves, and protects files.

**Core sub-functions of File Management:**

1. **File Creation & Deletion:** Allocating directory entries and freeing inodes.
2. **Directory Management:** Maintaining hierarchical (tree) directory structures.
3. **File Operations:** Open, Close, Read, Write, Seek, Append, Truncate.
4. **Storage Allocation:** Contiguous, linked, or indexed allocation on disk blocks.
5. **Free Space Management:** Bitmaps (vectors), linked lists, or grouping.
6. **File Protection & Access Control:** Permission bits, ACLs (Access Control Lists), capabilities.

---

## 2.4 I/O (Input/Output) Device Management

The OS hides the **peculiarities** of hardware devices behind a uniform interface.

**Core sub-functions of I/O Management:**

1. **Device Drivers:** Specialized, OS-supplied software modules that act as translators between the OS and the physical device.
2. **Buffering:** Temporarily storing data in main memory to balance the speed mismatch between fast CPUs and slow I/O devices.
3. **Caching:** Holding a high-speed copy of data that is also stored elsewhere (typically in a slower storage medium).
4. **Spooling (Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line):** Queuing jobs for a device (e.g., printer) so multiple processes can submit jobs without contention.
5. **I/O Scheduling:** Ordering disk I/O requests to minimize **seek time** and **rotational latency** using algorithms like FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK.
6. **Device Reservation & Release:** Implementing exclusive access where required (e.g., tape drives).

---

## 2.5 Security & Protection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security** = protection of the *system* from unauthorized access (external threats, authentication, firewalls).
> **Protection** = the *internal* mechanism that controls access of processes/users to resources defined by the OS.

**Core sub-functions of Security & Protection:**

1. **Authentication:** Verifying user identity (passwords, biometrics, tokens).
2. **Authorization:** Determining what an authenticated user is allowed to do.
3. **Access Control Matrices:** Modeling subjects (users) and objects (files, devices) as a matrix of permissions.
4. **Domain Enforcement:** Switching the CPU between protection domains via system calls, traps, and hardware privilege levels (Ring 0 vs Ring 3).
5. **Auditing & Logging:** Recording security-relevant events.

---

## 2.6 Networking & Communication

Distributed systems require the OS to mediate network access.

1. **Network Protocol Stack Implementation:** TCP/IP, UDP, sockets.
2. **Remote Procedure Calls (RPCs):** Process-to-process communication across machines.
3. **Distributed Resource Management:** Coordination of distributed file systems (NFS, AFS), distributed shared memory.

---

## 2.7 Command Interpretation (Shell) & System Services

1. **Command Interpreter:** A program that reads user commands (e.g., `bash`, `cmd.exe`, `zsh`) and executes them.
2. **System Programs:** Utility programs for file manipulation (cp, mv, ls), status information (date, who), programming support (compilers, editors, debuggers), and communication (mail, ftp).

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The functions of an OS are largely conceptual, but several **quantitative relationships** are frequently tested in KTU exams. Memorize the following table.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Definition of Terms | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = 1 - P_{idle}$ | $P_{idle}$ = fraction of time CPU is idle | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | **Throughput** | $T_{P} = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{T_{total}}$ | $N_{completed}$ = jobs finished, $T_{total}$ = total elapsed time | jobs/second |
| 3 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $T_{TAT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Elapsed time from submission to completion | seconds |
| 4 | **Waiting Time (WT)** | $T_{WT} = T_{TAT} - T_{burst}$ | Time spent in ready queue | seconds |
| 5 | **Response Time** | $T_{response} = T_{firstrun} - T_{arrival}$ | Time to first response, not completion | seconds |
| 6 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $k$ = speedup of enhanced portion | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Page Fault Rate** | $r_{pf} = \dfrac{N_{pagefaults}}{N_{memoryrefs}}$ | Ratio of page faults to total memory references | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | **Effective Access Time (Paging)** | $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot t_{mem} + p \cdot t_{pagefault}$ | $p$ = page fault rate, $t_{mem}$ = memory access time, $t_{pagefault}$ = page fault service time | nanoseconds (ns) |
| 9 | **Seek Time (Disk)** | $T_{seek} = a + b \cdot \vert n \vert$ | $a$ = startup time, $b$ = per-cylinder time, $n$ = cylinders traversed | milliseconds (ms) |
| 10 | **File Allocation (Contiguous)** | $Wasted = (N_{allocated} - N_{used}) \cdot B_{size}$ | Unused bytes within the last allocated block | bytes |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - The **Effective Access Time (EAT)** formula is the basis of all virtual memory design in modern operating systems (Windows, Linux, macOS).
> - **Amdahl's Law** is the foundational equation in parallel computing; it tells OS designers that speeding up *one* part of a system has diminishing returns if the rest is sequential.
> - The **disk seek time** relationship drives the I/O scheduler in the Linux kernel (CFQ, NOOP, Deadline).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — The Functionality of an OS

This section performs a *deep analytical walkthrough* of the OS's role during the lifetime of a single user request. The walkthrough is modeled as an end-to-end derivation-style analytical chain, anchored to a **user invoking a process that performs I/O**.

---

## 3.1 The Analytical Matrix — OS Subsystems & Their Interactions

We treat the OS as a set of interacting subsystems $S = \{ S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n \}$, where each subsystem $S_i$ has:

- **Inputs:** $I_i$ (e.g., system calls, interrupts, hardware signals)
- **Outputs:** $O_i$ (e.g., modified memory, scheduled processes, I/O completions)
- **State:** $Q_i$ (e.g., process control block, page table, file descriptor table)

The **overall system state** is the Cartesian product:

$$
Q_{system} = Q_1 \times Q_2 \times \dots \times Q_n
$$

A **state transition** is triggered by an *event* $e \in E$ (e.g., timer interrupt, system call, I/O completion). The transition function is:

$$
\delta: Q_{system} \times E \rightarrow Q_{system}
$$

For any event $e$, the system moves from state $Q_{system}^{(k)}$ to $Q_{system}^{(k+1)}$:

$$
Q_{system}^{(k+1)} = \delta(Q_{system}^{(k)}, \, e)
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This formal model is the basis of *model checking* in OS verification (e.g., seL4 microkernel proof). Each subsystem $S_i$ interacts with others through well-defined *interfaces* (system calls, IPC, shared data structures).

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace — A User-Invoked Process

**Scenario:** A user double-clicks an application icon (e.g., a text editor). Trace the OS functions activated.

### **Step 1 — User Interface Receives the Request**
The GUI subsystem receives a mouse-click event. The shell process translates the icon into an **executable path** on disk.

$$
\text{event } e_1 = \text{``launch application"} \Rightarrow \delta(Q_{shell}, e_1) = Q_{shell}^{(1)}
$$

**Logic:** The shell invokes a system call — typically `execve(path, argv, envp)` on UNIX or `CreateProcess()` on Windows.

### **Step 2 — File Management Subsystem Activates**
The OS must read the executable from disk.

- **File Management** calls the **I/O subsystem** to issue a disk read.
- Disk scheduling algorithm (e.g., LOOK) computes the access order:

$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational} + T_{transfer}
$$

- The file's **inode/FCB** is read, identifying the disk blocks holding the executable binary.

**Result:** The binary image is loaded into a sequence of physical frames allocated by the **Memory Management** subsystem.

### **Step 3 — Memory Management Allocates Address Space**
For a process $P_i$, the OS creates a **virtual address space** partitioned into segments:

$$
V_i = \{\text{text}, \text{data}, \text{heap}, \text{stack}\}
$$

Page tables map virtual pages to physical frames:

$$
\text{Page Table: } V_i \rightarrow \{f_1, f_2, \dots, f_k\}, \quad f_j \in \text{RAM}
$$

If a referenced page is *not* in RAM, a **page fault** occurs:

$$
\text{if } p \notin \text{RAM} \Rightarrow \text{Page Fault} \Rightarrow \text{load from swap/disk}
$$

The **Effective Access Time** is:

$$
EAT = (1 - r_{pf}) \cdot t_{mem} + r_{pf} \cdot t_{pagefault}
$$

**Plug-in Example:** Suppose $t_{mem} = 100$ ns, $t_{pagefault} = 10$ ms $= 10^7$ ns, and $r_{pf} = 0.001$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.001)(100) + (0.001)(10^7) \\
&= (0.999)(100) + 10000 \\
&= 99.9 + 10000 \\
&= 10099.9 \text{ ns} \approx 10.1 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A single page fault makes memory access **100× slower**. This is why OS designers aggressively minimize $r_{pf}$ using prefetching, locality-aware allocation, and large TLB reach.

### **Step 4 — Process Management Schedules the Process**
The newly created process $P_i$ is placed in the **ready queue** $Q_{ready}$. The CPU scheduler selects a process using a scheduling policy $\pi$:

$$
\pi: Q_{ready} \rightarrow \text{CPU}
$$

For a **Round Robin** scheduler with time quantum $q$:

$$
T_{response}^{RR} = q \cdot (N_{processes} - 1) + T_{firstburst}
$$

The process is **dispatched** to the CPU. Context switch overhead is $T_{cs}$ per switch.

### **Step 5 — I/O Subsystem Handles Subsequent Device Requests**
When the text editor reads a file, the **file subsystem** issues a **read()** system call. The I/O subsystem:

1. Initiates a disk I/O via the **device driver**.
2. The process is *blocked* and moved from $Q_{ready}$ to $Q_{wait}$:

$$
\delta(P_i, \text{I/O request}) = \text{BLOCKED}
$$

3. On I/O completion, an **interrupt** fires. The interrupt service routine (ISR) wakes the process:

$$
\delta(P_i, \text{I/O complete}) = \text{READY}
$$

### **Step 6 — Protection Subsystem Enforces Boundaries**
Every memory access is checked by the **MMU** against the page table. If $P_i$ attempts to access a page not in its address space, a **segmentation fault** / **page fault** trap is generated:

$$
\text{access}(v) \rightarrow \text{MMU check} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{allowed, if } v \in V_i \\ \text{trap, if } v \notin V_i \end{cases}
$$

### **Step 7 — User Process Terminates**
When the user closes the editor, the process issues `exit()`. The OS:

1. Closes open **file descriptors** (file subsystem).
2. Releases **memory frames** (memory subsystem).
3. Reaps the process via the parent (`wait()`).
4. Removes $P_i$ from $Q_{ready}$ and frees the **PCB**.

### **State Machine Summary**

$$
\text{NEW} \xrightarrow{\text{admit}} \text{READY} \xrightarrow{\text{dispatch}} \text{RUNNING} \xrightarrow{\text{I/O or event}} \text{WAIT} \xrightarrow{\text{I/O complete}} \text{READY} \xrightarrow{\text{exit}} \text{TERMINATED}
$$

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example — CPU Utilization

**Problem:** A computer system has a CPU that is idle for **20%** of the time. What is the CPU utilization?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{idle} &= 0.20 \\
U_{CPU} &= 1 - P_{idle} \\
&= 1 - 0.20 \\
&= 0.80 \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always report CPU utilization as a percentage and identify the period over which the average is computed. Saying "CPU utilization is 0.8" without units is *incomplete* and may cost a mark.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of an Operating System

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the layered (hierarchical) architecture of a generic OS. Each layer uses services from the layer below and provides services to the layer above.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U1[User Applications]
    U2[System Programs and Shell]
    U3[Compilers Editors Utilities]
    K1[System Call Interface]
    K2[Process Management Subsystem]
    K3[Memory Management Subsystem]
    K4[File Management Subsystem]
    K5[IO Device Management Subsystem]
    K6[Networking Subsystem]
    K7[Security and Protection Subsystem]
    H1[Device Drivers]
    H2[CPU RAM Disk Network Card]
    H3[Physical Hardware]

    U1 --> U2
    U1 --> U3
    U2 --> K1
    U3 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    K1 --> K3
    K1 --> K4
    K1 --> K5
    K1 --> K6
    K1 --> K7
    K2 --> H1
    K3 --> H1
    K4 --> H1
    K5 --> H1
    K6 --> H1
    K7 --> H1
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **top tier** (User Applications, System Programs) is what the end user directly interacts with.
- The **middle tier** (System Call Interface + Subsystems) is the **kernel** — the core of the OS that runs in **privileged (kernel) mode**.
- The **bottom tier** (Device Drivers + Hardware) is the physical world that the kernel abstracts.

---

## 4.2 Functional Interaction Map — OS Subsystems in Action

This diagram traces the path of a **single user command** through every OS subsystem. It is a *functional interaction map*, not a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER: Types Command ls]
    B[SHELL: Parses Token]
    C[SYSTEM CALL: execve]
    D[PROCESS MGR: Fork Child]
    E[FILE MGR: Read Directory]
    F[IO MGR: Issue Disk Read]
    G[DEVICE DRIVER: Send ATA Command]
    H[DISK: Rotate Read Sector]
    I[INTERRUPT: I O Complete]
    J[MEMORY MGR: Buffer Result]
    K[FILE MGR: Format Output]
    L[SHELL: Print to Terminal]
    M[USER: Sees File List]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L --> M
```

**Walkthrough interpretation:**

1. The user types `ls` in the shell.
2. The **shell** parses the command and invokes the `execve` system call.
3. **Process Management** forks a child process.
4. **File Management** requests the directory contents.
5. **I/O Management** issues a disk read.
6. The **device driver** translates the request to hardware signals.
7. The **disk** spins and reads sectors.
8. An **interrupt** notifies the kernel of completion.
9. **Memory Management** copies data into a kernel buffer.
10. **File Management** formats the data into a directory listing.
11. The **shell** writes to the terminal.
12. The **user** sees the file list.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — OS Functions Mapping

The following block diagram maps the **six major OS functions** to the **components that implement them** in a typical modern OS (Linux / Windows hybrid).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F1 [PROCESS MANAGEMENT]
      F1A[Scheduler]
      F1B[Dispatcher]
      F1C[PCB Manager]
      F1D[IPC Module]
    end

    subgraph F2 [MEMORY MANAGEMENT]
      F2A[Virtual Memory Mgr]
      F2B[Page Replacement]
      F2C[MMU Interface]
    end

    subgraph F3 [FILE MANAGEMENT]
      F3A[VFS Layer]
      F3B[File Allocator]
      F3C[Directory Manager]
    end

    subgraph F4 [IO DEVICE MANAGEMENT]
      F4A[Buffer Cache]
      F4B[Device Driver Stack]
      F4C[DMA Controller]
    end

    subgraph F5 [SECURITY AND PROTECTION]
      F5A[Auth Module]
      F5B[Access Control Lists]
      F5C[Audit Logger]
    end

    subgraph F6 [NETWORKING]
      F6A[Socket Layer]
      F6B[Protocol Stack]
    end

    H1[Physical Hardware Layer] --> F4
    F4 --> F2
    F2 --> F1
    F1 --> F3
    F3 --> F5
    F5 --> F6
    F6 --> APP[Application Programs]
```

**Engineering Reading:** The arrows indicate **dependency direction** — Networking depends on Process Management for process creation, Process Management depends on Memory Management for address space allocation, and so on. The **Physical Hardware Layer** is the foundation; without it, nothing above functions.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define an operating system. Explain its role as a resource allocator.*

**Model Answer:**

An **operating system (OS)** is a system software that acts as an interface between the user and the computer hardware. It manages hardware resources and provides services to application programs.

**Role as a Resource Allocator:**
The OS manages finite hardware resources (CPU time, main memory, disk space, I/O devices) among multiple competing processes. It uses *policies* to decide *which* process gets *which* resource, *when*, and *for how long*. The primary goals are **efficiency**, **fairness**, and **deadlock avoidance**.

For example, the **CPU scheduler** (a sub-function of the OS) decides the order in which processes access the CPU, ensuring that no single process monopolizes the processor.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for resource allocator explanation, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *List and briefly explain any three functions of an operating system.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Process Management:** Creates, schedules, and terminates processes; handles synchronization, IPC, and deadlock. *(1 mark)*
2. **Memory Management:** Allocates and deallocates memory; implements virtual memory and page replacement; enforces memory protection via the MMU. *(1 mark)*
3. **File Management:** Provides logical file abstractions (open, read, write, close); manages directories, free space, and access control. *(1 mark)*

*(Alternative valid answers: I/O Management, Security & Protection, Networking, Command Interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ THIS CAREFULLY:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write only definitions. You must provide *examples, diagrams, and formulae* where applicable. A 14-mark answer that is just bullet points is capped at ~8 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse the *user view* with the *system view*. Examiners explicitly test both — failing to mention either costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Do NOT** skip the *trade-off* discussion. Modern OS design is about *trade-offs* (e.g., throughput vs. fairness, performance vs. security). Mentioning trade-offs elevates the answer.
> 4. **Diagrams:** Always include a *labeled* block diagram. A diagram without labels gets 0 marks for the diagram portion.
> 5. **Formulas:** For quantitative questions, *state the formula*, *substitute values*, and *report the result with units*. A formula without substitution = 1 mark (out of 3 for that part).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Functions of an OS in Depth

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the major functions of an operating system with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of the OS as a control program. How does it differ from its role as a resource allocator? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The major functions of an operating system are:

**1. Process Management (2 marks)**
- A *process* is a program in execution. The OS creates, schedules, suspends, resumes, and terminates processes.
- The **Process Control Block (PCB)** stores per-process state: program counter, registers, memory pointers, open file list, accounting info.
- Example: When a user opens Chrome while Firefox is already running, the OS creates a new process with its own PCB, schedules it, and manages its execution.

**2. Memory Management (1.5 marks)**
- Allocates and deallocates memory frames to processes. Implements virtual memory and page replacement.
- Example: With only **8 GB** of RAM, the OS can run processes whose combined footprint is **16 GB** by paging some portions to the swap partition on disk.

**3. File Management (1.5 marks)**
- Provides logical file abstractions (names, paths) over physical disk blocks.
- Example: When a user types `cat report.txt`, the OS resolves the path via the directory tree, opens the file, reads its data blocks, and displays them.

**4. I/O Device Management (1 mark)**
- Hides hardware peculiarities behind a uniform device-driver interface. Implements buffering, caching, spooling, and disk scheduling.
- Example: A print job submitted from a user is *spooled* to disk so the user can continue working while the printer processes the queue.

**5. Security & Protection (1 mark)**
- Authenticates users and authorizes access. Enforces protection domains via hardware privilege levels (Ring 0 vs Ring 3).
- Example: Linux distinguishes *root* (UID 0) from regular users, restricting certain system calls to root only.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**The OS as a Control Program (3.5 marks):**

As a control program, the OS *supervises* the execution of user programs to ensure:

1. **Correct Operation:** Programs do not access each other's memory, do not hog the CPU, and do not crash the system.
2. **Error Handling:** Invalid instructions (e.g., division by zero), page faults, and illegal memory accesses are *trapped* to the OS, which decides whether to terminate the process or recover.
3. **I/O Control:** All I/O is *mediated* by the OS. User programs cannot directly drive the disk controller; they must invoke a system call, which transitions the CPU from user mode to kernel mode.
4. **Mode Switching:** The hardware provides at least two modes — **kernel mode** (privileged, full instruction set) and **user mode** (restricted). The OS runs in kernel mode; applications run in user mode. A *system call* triggers a mode switch via a *trap* instruction.

**The OS as a Resource Allocator (3.5 marks):**

As a resource allocator, the OS *distributes* finite hardware resources among competing processes.

| Resource | Allocation Mechanism |
|---|---|
| CPU | Scheduling algorithms (RR, FCFS, MLFQ) |
| Memory | Paging, segmentation, page replacement |
| Disk | Disk scheduling (SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK) |
| I/O | Device queues, spooling |
| Network | Packet schedulers, QoS classes |

**Key Difference (included for completeness):**

- *Control program* = **vertical** relationship (OS above processes) — the OS **manages** the processes.
- *Resource allocator* = **horizontal** relationship (processes alongside each other) — the OS **shares** hardware among them.

In practice, both roles are inseparable: the OS cannot be a control program without also being a resource allocator.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice: OS Architecture and Goals

**(a)** Describe the goals and functions of an operating system. Illustrate with a block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of multiprogramming. How does it improve CPU utilization? Derive a formula for the CPU utilization of a multiprogrammed system. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Goals of an OS (3.5 marks):**
1. **Convenience:** Make the computer easier to use.
2. **Efficiency:** Allow system resources to be used efficiently.
3. **Ability to Evolve:** Permit the system to be developed, tested, and new functions added without disrupting service.
4. **Protection & Security:** Ensure that unauthorized users cannot access resources; that programs do not interfere with one another.

**Functions (3.5 marks):** *(Briefly list with one-line examples — see Section 2 for full content.)*
- Process management
- Memory management
- File management
- I/O device management
- Security & protection
- Command interpretation

**Block Diagram (included in the answer sheet — 0 to 1 mark):**
A layered diagram similar to the one in Section 4.1.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Multiprogramming Definition (1 mark):**
Multiprogramming is a technique in which multiple independent processes are kept in main memory simultaneously, and the OS rapidly switches the CPU among them to keep it busy at all times.

**Why It Improves CPU Utilization (2 marks):**
In a uniprogramming system, the CPU becomes idle whenever the active process performs I/O. Multiprogramming overlaps the I/O of one process with the computation of another, ensuring the CPU is rarely idle.

**Derivation of CPU Utilization Formula (4 marks):**

Let:
- $p$ = fraction of total time that *all* $n$ processes are simultaneously waiting for I/O (i.e., CPU has no work).
- Then $(1 - p)$ = probability that *at least one* process is ready to run.

Since I/O events are statistically independent across $n$ processes:

$$
P(\text{all } n \text{ processes in I/O}) = p^{n}
$$

Therefore, **CPU utilization** is:

$$
U_{CPU} = 1 - p^{n}
$$

**Plug-in Example:** Suppose each process spends **40%** of its time doing I/O. With **4** processes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{CPU} &= 1 - (0.40)^{4} \\
&= 1 - 0.0256 \\
&= 0.9744 \\
&= 97.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with a uniprogrammed system: $U_{CPU} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60 = 60\%$.

**Interpretation:** Multiprogramming nearly doubled CPU utilization in this example. This is the *primary* economic justification for multiprogramming in modern OS design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet. Cover every bullet.**

- **Definition:** An OS is system software that acts as an *intermediary* between the user and the hardware.
- **Two Views:** User view (convenience) vs System view (resource allocator + control program).
- **Six Major Functions:** Process Management, Memory Management, File Management, I/O Management, Security & Protection, Networking.
- **Process Management:** PCB, scheduling (FCFS, SJF, RR, Priority, MLFQ), IPC, deadlock handling.
- **Memory Management:** Virtual memory, paging, segmentation, page replacement (FIFO, LRU, Clock), MMU-based protection.
- **File Management:** File operations, directories, allocation (contiguous/linked/indexed), free space management, access control.
- **I/O Management:** Device drivers, buffering, caching, spooling, disk scheduling (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK).
- **Security & Protection:** Authentication, authorization, access control matrices, protection domains, kernel/user mode.
- **Networking:** Sockets, TCP/IP, RPC, distributed file systems.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $U_{CPU} = 1 - P_{idle}$
  - $T_{TAT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$
  - $T_{WT} = T_{TAT} - T_{burst}$
  - $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot t_{mem} + p \cdot t_{pagefault}$
  - **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + f/k}$
  - **Multiprogramming CPU utilization:** $U_{CPU} = 1 - p^{n}$
  - **Disk access time:** $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational} + T_{transfer}$
- **Diagrams to Draw:** Layered OS architecture, Process State Diagram, Multiprogramming CPU utilization graph.
- **Key Mnemonics:** **"PMFI SN"** — **P**rocess, **M**emory, **F**ile, **I**/O, **S**ecurity, **N**etworking. (The six functions in order.)
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Confusing "OS as control program" with "OS as resource allocator" — they are *complementary* roles, not alternatives.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Operating System Overview (Functions)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boot Process, Processes - Process States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Boot Process, Processes - Process States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Boot Process (Bootstrap Process)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Boot Process** is the precisely orchestrated sequence of low-level operations executed automatically by a computer system immediately after power-on, whose purpose is to initialize hardware, locate the Operating System kernel on secondary storage, load it into **RAM**, and transfer CPU control to it. In KTU terminology, this is also called the **Bootstrap Process** or **Bootstrap Loading**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The boot process is the gateway between inert hardware and a running OS. Understanding it is essential for Module 1 because every modern Operating System concept (processes, memory management, file systems) depends on the kernel being correctly initialized via this sequence.

**Conceptual Analogy (The Airport Analogy):**
Imagine an international airport at 4 AM — completely dark, no staff, no lights. The **Boot Process** is the *opening crew*:
1. **Security guard with flashlight** → runs around checking every gate (this is **POST**).
2. **Operations Manager** → unlocks the main control room (**BIOS/UEFI**).
3. **Shift Supervisor** → reads the duty roster from a small notebook near the entrance (**MBR/GPT + Bootloader**).
4. **Air Traffic Controller arrives** → takes full charge of all flights (**Kernel**).
5. **Ground staff are deployed** → all systems, terminals, check-in desks become live (**Init/Systemd**).

The plane (your application) cannot take off until the controller is on duty!

---

### 1.2 Process – The Heart of an OS

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
A **Process** is a program in execution. It is the basic unit of work in a modern operating system and is represented by its **execution context**, which includes the program code (text section), associated data, CPU registers, stack, heap, and a unique **Process Control Block (PCB)** maintained by the kernel.

$$ \text{Process} = \text{Program Code} + \text{Execution Context} + \text{Resources} $$

**Conceptual Analogy (The Recipe vs. The Cook):**
A **Program** is a *recipe written on paper* (static text), whereas a **Process** is *a chef actively cooking that recipe* (dynamic activity). Two chefs can follow the *same recipe* but be in totally different stages — one is chopping onions (state A), the other is simmering the sauce (state B). The *recipe* never changes; the *process* is alive, evolving, and holding physical resources (pans, heat, ingredients) just as a process holds registers, memory, and open files.

> [!NOTE]
> **Program ≠ Process.** A program is a passive file on disk. A process is an active entity with its own lifecycle, state, and resources. One program can spawn many processes (e.g., opening 10 Chrome windows = 10 processes of the same `chrome.exe`).

---

### 1.3 Process States – The Lifecycle of a Running Process

A process moves through a series of well-defined **states** during its lifetime. The KTU syllabus requires mastery of the **5-State Model** and awareness of the **7-State (Suspended) Model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition – Process State:**
> A *process state* is the current condition of a process with respect to CPU scheduling, resource allocation, and lifecycle position. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked (Waiting), and Terminated (Exit)** as the five canonical states, with **Suspend-Ready** and **Suspend-Blocked** as extended states for memory-pressure scenarios.

**Intuitive Analogy (The Patient in a Hospital):**
| Hospital State | Process State |
|---|---|
| New patient at registration | **New** (being created) |
| Sitting in waiting room with file | **Ready** (waiting for CPU) |
| Currently with the doctor | **Running** (executing on CPU) |
| Doctor sent patient for X-Ray | **Blocked** (waiting for I/O) |
| Discharged / file closed | **Terminated** |
| Patient shifted to another branch (no bed) | **Suspended** (swapped out) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive: The Boot Process (Step-by-Step)

The modern boot process can be broken into **seven (7) distinct phases**. Each phase is owned by a different software/hardware entity.

#### Phase 1: Power-On / Reset
- User presses the power button.
- Power Supply Unit (PSU) stabilizes voltages.
- The **CPU reset vector** (`0xFFFFFFF0` in x86) is loaded into the Instruction Pointer (IP).
- The CPU begins executing firmware code stored in ROM/Flash.

#### Phase 2: POST (Power-On Self-Test)
- Performed by the **BIOS/UEFI** firmware.
- Tests: RAM (counted via memory test patterns), keyboard, display adapter, storage controllers, buses (PCIe).
- **Beep codes** indicate hardware failures.
- Result: a hardware *inventory* map is built.

#### Phase 3: Firmware – BIOS vs UEFI

| Feature | Legacy BIOS | UEFI |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | 1975 (IBM PC) | 2005 (EFI → UEFI) |
| Boot mode | 16-bit real mode | 32/64-bit protected mode |
| Disk scheme | **MBR** only (max 2 TB) | **GPT** (up to 9.4 ZB) |
| Interface | Text-based | Graphical, mouse-driven |
| Secure Boot | No | **Yes** (PKI-signed loaders) |
| Speed | Slow (POST + INT 13h) | Fast (direct driver access) |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern KTU lab machines (post-2018) almost universally use **UEFI**. Legacy BIOS is taught for historical/fundamental understanding.

#### Phase 4: Boot Device Selection & Boot Sector Read
- Firmware reads the configured boot order (e.g., SSD → USB → Network).
- It reads **Sector 0** of the selected device:
  - **MBR** (512 bytes) → contains Stage-1 bootloader + Partition Table.
  - **GPT** → uses a *protective MBR* for backward compatibility, but the real bootloader is at a GPT-specific location.
- The **Stage-1 Bootloader** (e.g., GRUB Stage 1, 512 bytes) is loaded into RAM at address `0x7C00` and executed.

#### Phase 5: Stage-2 Bootloader (e.g., GRUB)
- GRUB Stage 1.5 (located in the MBR gap) loads filesystem drivers.
- GRUB Stage 2 presents the **GRUB menu** (`/boot/grub/grub.cfg`).
- User selects a kernel (e.g., `vmlinuz-6.1.0-kali9-amd64`).
- The bootloader loads:
  - **Kernel image** (`vmlinuz`) — compressed kernel.
  - **Initial RAM Disk** (`initrd.img` or `initramfs`) — temporary root filesystem with essential drivers.

#### Phase 6: Kernel Initialization
- The bootloader jumps to the kernel entry point.
- Kernel performs:
  - Decompression of itself.
  - Memory subsystem initialization.
  - Detection of CPU features, devices, filesystems.
  - Mounting the real root filesystem (`/`) from `initramfs`.
  - Loading the first user-space process: **PID 1**.

#### Phase 7: Init / Systemd (PID 1)
- The kernel executes `/sbin/init` (or `/lib/systemd/systemd`).
- This is the **ancestor of all other processes**.
- **SysVinit**: runs scripts in `/etc/rc?.d/` sequentially.
- **Systemd** (modern): uses *parallel* service units (`/etc/systemd/system/`), *targets* (e.g., `multi-user.target`, `graphical.target`).
- Final stage: a **login manager** (GDM, LightDM) appears, or `getty` on TTYs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "What is the first process started by Linux?", the answer is **init / systemd** (PID 1). It is the only process that is *directly started by the kernel*; all other processes are descendants of it.

---

### 2.2 Deep Dive: Process States (5-State Model)

The **5-State Model** is the KTU-mandated canonical model.

#### State Definitions (Rigorous)

1. **New** — Process is being created. The OS is allocating its PCB, assigning a PID, and reserving address space. *Not yet admitted to the Ready queue.*

2. **Ready** — Process is loaded into **main memory** and is waiting in the Ready Queue for the CPU scheduler to dispatch it. It has *all resources except the CPU*.

3. **Running** — Process instructions are being executed by the CPU. Exactly **one process per CPU core** can be in the Running state at any instant.

4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Process is waiting for some event to occur (I/O completion, signal, semaphore release). It *cannot* run even if the CPU is free. The OS has explicitly moved it out of the Ready queue.

5. **Terminated (Exit)** — Process has finished execution. The PCB is still present (for parent to collect exit status) but the process is no longer scheduled.

> [!NOTE]
> A *Blocked* process is **not** in the Ready queue. This is a critical KTU distinction. The scheduler only sees Ready and Running processes — Blocked processes are parked on *device-specific wait queues*.

#### State Transitions (The Full Picture)

| From → To | Trigger |
|---|---|
| New → Ready | Admission controller admits process to the Ready queue |
| Ready → Running | **Dispatcher** selects this process (scheduling decision) |
| Running → Ready | **Preemption** by timer interrupt, or voluntary yield |
| Running → Blocked | Process issues I/O, `wait()`, or blocks on a semaphore |
| Blocked → Ready | I/O completes, signal arrives, semaphore is signaled |
| Running → Terminated | Process calls `exit()` or is killed by a signal |
| Ready → Terminated | Parent terminates child before it runs (rare) |
| Blocked → Terminated | Parent terminates a sleeping child |

#### The 7-State (Suspended) Extension
In a system with **swapping** (memory pressure), two additional states exist:
- **Suspend-Ready (Ready-Suspend)**: Process was Ready but was swapped out to disk to free RAM.
- **Suspend-Blocked (Blocked-Suspend)**: Process was Blocked and was swapped out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Suspend?** Because a Blocked process still occupies memory. If RAM is full and we need space for incoming Ready processes, we swap out Blocked processes first (they have no work to do anyway). This is **medium-term scheduling**.

---

### 2.3 Process Control Block (PCB) — The "ID Card" of a Process

Every process has exactly **one PCB**, maintained by the kernel in a protected kernel-space data structure. Key fields:

| Field Category | Contents |
|---|---|
| **Identification** | PID, PPID (Parent PID), User ID (UID), Group ID (GID) |
| **State Info** | Current state (New/Ready/Running/…), priority |
| **CPU Context** | Register values (PC, SP, general-purpose regs), program status word |
| **Memory Mgmt** | Page tables, base/limit registers, segment table pointers |
| **I/O Status** | List of open file descriptors, I/O devices allocated |
| **Accounting** | CPU time used, time limits, resource quotas |
| **Scheduling** | Queue pointers, scheduling algorithm parameters |
| **Inter-Process** | Signals pending, message queues, pipes |

> [!NOTE]
> When a context switch occurs, the entire CPU context (registers, program counter, stack pointer) of the *old* process is saved into **its PCB**, and the *new* process's PCB is loaded into the actual CPU registers. This is why the PCB is sometimes called the *process context*.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Conceptual Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the **definitive KTU-ready reference** for this topic. Memorize it for Part-A and Part-B questions.**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Key Equation / Rule** | **KTU Term** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address of CPU reset vector (x86) | `0xFFFFFFF0` | Reset vector |
| 2 | Stage-1 bootloader's load address | `0x7C00` | Bootstrap address |
| 3 | MBR sector size | **512 bytes** | Master Boot Record |
| 4 | Max partitions in MBR | **4 primary** (or 3P+1E) | MBR limit |
| 5 | Max partitions in GPT | **128 primary** | GPT limit |
| 6 | First user-space process PID | **1** | init / systemd |
| 7 | State invariants | `(#Ready) + (#Running) = # processes in main memory` | Conservation law |
| 8 | Per-CPU running constraint | Exactly **1 process per core** in Running state | Mutual exclusion |
| 9 | Context switch overhead | $T_{CS} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | Switch cost |
| 10 | CPU utilization | $U = 1 - p^n$ (n = CPUs, p = idle prob.) | Amdahl's basic form |

> [!NOTE]
> Formulas 7–8 are **state-conservation laws** that KTU examiners love to ask in *True/False* or *short-answer* form. E.g., "Can two processes be in the Running state on a single-core CPU?" — **No.**

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forensics & Recovery:** Understanding the boot process is essential for *data recovery*, *bootkit detection*, and *rootkit analysis*.
- **Embedded Systems:** IoT/automotive ECUs use *custom bootloaders* (U-Boot, RedBoot). The same state-machine principles apply.
- **Cloud Computing:** VMs in AWS/Azure use *PVH boot* (Paravirtualized HVM) — a software-emulated boot. Container runtimes (Docker) skip BIOS/UEFI but still spawn a *PID 1* equivalent.
- **Performance Tuning:** *systemd-analyze blame* shows which services slow boot time — critical for automotive and server domains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Detailed Boot Process Flow

The following derivation-style analytical walkthrough treats the boot process as a *state machine*. Each step identifies (a) what is happening, (b) which entity is in control, and (c) what data structures are involved.

**Step 0 — Power Application**
At $t = 0$, mains power is applied. The PSU performs voltage regulation. When stable, the **Power-Good signal** is asserted to the motherboard. The CPU samples this signal on its **RESET#** pin and begins a hardware reset.

**Step 1 — CPU Reset Vector Fetch**
The CPU's instruction pointer is hardwired to fetch from physical address `0xFFFFFFF0`. This address maps to a *non-volatile* ROM/Flash chip soldered on the motherboard — the firmware chip containing **BIOS** or **UEFI**.

**Step 2 — POST Execution**
The firmware chip contains initialization routines. The CPU executes them. Each subsystem test updates a flag in the **CMOS/RTC NVRAM** and the **POST card** (legacy). On success, the firmware builds an in-memory **hardware map** (EISA/ACPI tables).

**Step 3 — Boot Device Discovery**
The firmware consults the **Boot Order** stored in NVRAM (e.g., `Boot0001: SATA SSD, Boot0002: USB, Boot0003: Network`). It iterates the list, polling each device for a *valid boot signature*:
- For **MBR**: reads the last 2 bytes of sector 0; expects `0x55 0xAA`.
- For **GPT**: reads the protective MBR; validates the GPT header CRC.

**Step 4 — Stage-1 Bootloader Load**
The first 446 bytes of sector 0 (MBR) are read into RAM at `0x7C00` and executed. This code is *extremely* small and is **position-dependent** (it must run at exactly `0x7C00`).

**Step 5 — Stage-1.5 (Filesystem-Aware Loader)**
The Stage-1 code locates Stage-1.5 in the **MBR gap** (sectors 2–62, unused by MBR partitioning). Stage-1.5 contains *filesystem drivers* so the next stage can read files from a *real* filesystem (ext4, NTFS, FAT32).

**Step 6 — Stage-2 Bootloader (e.g., GRUB2)**
Stage-1.5 loads Stage-2 from `/boot/grub/`. Stage-2:
1. Parses `/boot/grub/grub.cfg`.
2. Displays the menu.
3. After timeout/user choice, loads:
   - **Kernel:** `/boot/vmlinuz-<version>` (compressed ELF).
   - **initrd:** `/boot/initrd.img-<version>` (cpio archive, often gzip/xz compressed).

**Step 7 — Kernel Decompression and Hand-off**
The bootloader places the kernel and initrd at specified physical addresses (often near the top of low memory) and jumps to the kernel's `start` symbol. The kernel:
1. Decompresses itself (self-extracting bzip2/xz routine embedded in `vmlinuz`).
2. Initializes core subsystems.
3. Calls `start_kernel()` in `init/main.c`.

**Step 8 — PID 1 Execution**
After `start_kernel()` finishes, the kernel calls `kernel_init()` which ultimately executes `/sbin/init` (or `/lib/systemd/systemd`). This is **Process 1** — the *init process*.

**Step 9 — User Space Bootstrap**
The init system reads its configuration:
- **SysVinit:** runs scripts in `/etc/rcS.d`, then `/etc/rc2.d` (runlevel 2 = multi-user).
- **Systemd:** activates *sockets*, *mounts*, *timers* in parallel, then reaches `default.target` (`graphical.target` or `multi-user.target`).

**Step 10 — Login**
A `getty` process is spawned on each TTY, or a display manager (GDM, SDDM) on the graphical console. The user authenticates, and a *user shell* is spawned — completing the user-facing boot.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process State Transitions — A Worked Walkthrough

We will trace the life of a hypothetical process `P` named `compile.out` on a Linux system with a single CPU core. This analytical walkthrough demonstrates *every* state and *every* transition.

**Initial State — New**

The user types `./compile.out` in the shell. The shell calls `fork()`. The kernel creates a *new* PCB for the child process, allocates a PID (say, `4821`), and copies the parent's address space.

$$ \text{PCB}_{4821} = \{ \text{PID}=4821, \text{PPID}=4819, \text{state}=\text{New} \} $$

**Transition 1: New → Ready**
The kernel's **Admission Controller** (in the long-term scheduler) admits `P` to the Ready Queue. State changes:

$$ \text{state}_{4821} = \text{Ready} $$

**Transition 2: Ready → Running**
The **Short-Term Scheduler** (e.g., CFS in Linux) selects `P`. The **Dispatcher** performs a **context switch**:
- Saves old process's CPU registers into its PCB.
- Loads `P`'s registers from its PCB.
- Jumps to `P`'s program counter.

$$ \text{state}_{4821} = \text{Running} $$

**Transition 3: Running → Blocked**
`P` calls `read(fd, buf, 1024)`. The syscall traps to kernel mode. The kernel sees the I/O device is *not ready* (e.g., disk seek in progress), so it moves `P` to the **device's wait queue** (e.g., `sd0_wait_queue`).

$$ \text{state}_{4821} = \text{Blocked}, \quad \text{queue}=\text{sd0} $$

**Transition 4: Blocked → Ready (I/O completion)**
The disk controller raises an **interrupt** (IRQ). The kernel's interrupt handler:
1. Acknowledges the device.
2. Removes `P` from `sd0_wait_queue`.
3. Reinserts `P` into the global Ready Queue.

$$ \text{state}_{4821} = \text{Ready} $$

**Transition 5: Ready → Running (again)**
The scheduler re-dispatches `P`. State becomes `Running` again.

**Transition 6: Running → Ready (Preemption)**
The **timer interrupt** fires (e.g., every 4 ms in Linux CFS). The kernel's `scheduler_tick()` decides `P` has consumed its time slice. `P` is preempted:

$$ \text{state}_{4821} = \text{Ready} $$

**Transition 7: Running → Terminated**
`P` reaches its `main()` return statement and calls `exit(0)`. The kernel:
1. Closes all open file descriptors.
2. Releases memory pages.
3. Sends `SIGCHLD` to the parent.
4. Sets `P`'s state to `Terminated` (a *zombie* until parent calls `wait()`).

$$ \text{state}_{4821} = \text{Terminated} $$

**Final Reaping**
Parent calls `waitpid(4821, &status, 0)`. The kernel reads the exit status, frees the PCB, and `P` ceases to exist.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Validating a Boot-Sector Signature

This derivation shows how a firmware validates an MBR sector — a typical KTU numerical/short-answer question.

**Given:**
The MBR sector is read as a 512-byte block. The signature should be `0x55 0xAA` at offsets `510` and `511`.

**Find:**
Is the sector a valid boot sector?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte}_{510} &\stackrel{?}{=} 0\text{x}55 \\
\text{Byte}_{511} &\stackrel{?}{=} 0\text{x}AA
\end{aligned}
$$

If both match, the firmware proceeds to execute the first 446 bytes as a bootloader. Otherwise, it falls back to the next boot device in the order list.

**KTU Significance:**
This 2-byte magic number is one of the *simplest and most reliable* validation mechanisms in computing — it has been in use since 1981 and is still checked in UEFI's *Compatibility Support Module (CSM)*.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Table: "Which State Am I In?"

Use this decision-tree logic to answer KTU viva questions:

| Question | If YES → State | If NO → State |
|---|---|---|
| Has the process been created and admitted? | Continue | New |
| Is it in main memory and waiting for CPU? | **Ready** | Check next |
| Is the CPU currently executing its instructions? | **Running** | Check next |
| Is it waiting for an event (I/O, signal)? | **Blocked** | Check next |
| Has it called `exit()` or been killed? | **Terminated** | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boot Process — High-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On Reset] --> B[CPU Fetches Reset Vector 0xFFFFFFF0]
    B --> C[Firmware ROM BIOS or UFI Execution]
    C --> D[POST Power On Self Test]
    D --> E[Hardware Inventory Build ACPI Tables]
    E --> F{Read Boot Order from NVRAM}
    F --> G[Poll Boot Device 1 SSD]
    G --> H{MBR Signature 0x55AA Valid?}
    H -->|No| I[Try Boot Device 2]
    I --> H
    H -->|Yes| J[Load Stage1 Bootloader to 0x7C00]
    J --> K[Stage 1.5 Filesystem Drivers]
    K --> L[Stage 2 GRUB Menu Display]
    L --> M[Load vmlinuz Kernel Image]
    L --> N[Load initrd Initial RAM Disk]
    M --> O[Kernel Decompress and Initialize]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Mount Real Root Filesystem]
    P --> Q[Execute init or systemd PID 1]
    Q --> R[Service Units Activation]
    R --> S[Login Manager getty or GDM]
    S --> T[User Shell Active]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid diagram maps the **boot process as a state machine** — each box is a *state*, each arrow is a *transition trigger*. In a physical schematic, the "states" would be the CPU and memory contents; here they are functional stages.

---

### 4.2 Process State Transition Diagram (5-State Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1New
    S1New --> S2Ready: Admission by Long Term Scheduler
    S2Ready --> S3Running: Dispatched by Short Term Scheduler
    S3Running --> S2Ready: Preemption or Time Slice Expiry
    S3Running --> S4Blocked: I/O Request or wait or Block on Semaphore
    S4Blocked --> S2Ready: I/O Completion or Signal Received
    S3Running --> S5Terminated: exit or Killed by Signal
    S2Ready --> S5Terminated: Parent Kills Before Dispatch
    S4Blocked --> S5Terminated: Parent Kills Blocked Child
    S5Terminated --> [*]: Parent calls wait PCB Freed
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key transitions to remember (KTU exam favorites):**
> 1. `Blocked → Running` is **NOT directly possible** — a Blocked process *must* go to Ready first.
> 2. The only way a Ready process leaves the Ready queue is via the **dispatcher**.
> 3. `Terminated → Ready` is **not a valid transition** — a terminated process is dead and can only be re-created (forked) again from a parent.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Relationship Between Boot Stages and Memory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Firmware[Phase 1 Firmware Layer]
        F1[BIOS or UEFI in Flash ROM]
        F2[CMOS NVRAM Boot Order]
    end
    subgraph MBR[Phase 2 Boot Sector Layer]
        M1[MBR 512 bytes at Sector 0]
        M2[GRUB Stage 1 First 446 bytes]
        M3[Partition Table 64 bytes]
        M4[MBR Signature 0x55AA]
    end
    subgraph GRUB[Phase 3 GRUB Layer]
        G1[GRUB Stage 1.5 Filesystem Drivers]
        G2[GRUB Stage 2 Menu and Config]
    end
    subgraph Kernel[Phase 4 Kernel Layer]
        K1[vmlinuz Compressed Kernel]
        K2[initrd Init RAM Disk]
        K3[Kernel Uncompressed in RAM]
    end
    subgraph Init[Phase 5 User Space Layer]
        I1[PID 1 init or systemd]
        I2[Service Units and Targets]
        I3[User Login Shell]
    end
    F1 --> M1
    F2 --> F1
    M1 --> G1
    M1 --> G2
    G1 --> K1
    G1 --> K2
    K1 --> K3
    K2 --> K3
    K3 --> I1
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
```

---

### 4.4 Process State Diagram (7-State Model with Suspend)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> St1New
    St1New --> St2Ready: Admit
    St2Ready --> St3Running: Dispatch
    St3Running --> St2Ready: Preempt
    St3Running --> St4Blocked: I/O or Event Wait
    St4Blocked --> St2Ready: Event Occurs
    St2Ready --> St6SuspendReady: Medium Term Scheduler Swap Out
    St4Blocked --> St7SuspendBlocked: Medium Term Scheduler Swap Out
    St6SuspendReady --> St2Ready: Swap In Memory Available
    St7SuspendBlocked --> St4Blocked: Swap In Memory Available
    St3Running --> St5Exit: exit
    St6SuspendReady --> St5Exit: Killed
    St7SuspendBlocked --> St5Exit: Killed
    St5Exit --> [*]: Reaped by Parent
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 7-state model adds `Suspend-Ready` and `Suspend-Blocked` to model *swapping*. In modern Linux with overcommit and `kswapd`, the Suspend states are *less common* but conceptually still valid for systems with strict memory partitioning (e.g., embedded RTOS, mobile OS under memory pressure).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the following with respect to the boot process: (a) POST, (b) Bootstrap Loader, (c) MBR.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) POST (Power-On Self-Test):** POST is a set of diagnostic routines executed by the system's firmware (BIOS/UEFI) immediately after power-on. It verifies the basic functionality of core hardware components such as the CPU, RAM, keyboard, display adapter, and storage controllers. If any test fails, the system halts or emits beep codes to indicate the fault.

**(b) Bootstrap Loader:** The bootstrap loader (or bootloader) is a small program stored in a fixed, known location on the boot device (typically the MBR). Its primary responsibility is to locate the operating system kernel on secondary storage, load it into main memory, and transfer CPU control to it. Examples include GRUB, LILO, and Windows BOOTMGR.

**(c) MBR (Master Boot Record):** The MBR is the first 512-byte sector of a bootable storage device. It contains three components: a 446-byte Stage-1 bootloader, a 64-byte partition table describing up to four primary partitions, and a 2-byte magic signature (`0x55 0xAA`) used by firmware to validate the boot sector.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List and explain any three states in the process state model.**

**Model Answer:**

The process state model describes the dynamic lifecycle of a process. Three important states are:

**1. Ready State:** A process is in the Ready state when it has been loaded into main memory and is awaiting CPU allocation. All resources required for execution (except the CPU) are available. Multiple processes can simultaneously be in the Ready state, forming the *Ready Queue* managed by the short-term scheduler.

**2. Running State:** A process is in the Running state when its instructions are actively being executed by the CPU. On a single-core system, at most one process can be in the Running state at any given instant. The dispatcher performs a context switch to move a process from Ready to Running.

**3. Blocked (Waiting) State:** A process is in the Blocked state when it cannot proceed with execution because it is waiting for some external event to complete, such as an I/O operation, a signal, or a semaphore release. Blocked processes are not in the Ready queue and will not be scheduled by the dispatcher until the awaited event occurs.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the different states of a process in detail. Discuss the role of the Process Control Block (PCB) in process management. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the complete boot process of a Linux system starting from power-on to user login. Include the roles of BIOS/UEFI, MBR, GRUB, kernel, and init. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Process States & PCB**

A process transitions through five canonical states:

1. **New:** The process is being created. The OS allocates a PCB, assigns a PID, and reserves address space.
2. **Ready:** The process is in main memory and waiting in the Ready queue for CPU dispatch.
3. **Running:** The process is currently executing on the CPU. At most one process per CPU core can be in this state.
4. **Blocked (Waiting):** The process is waiting for an I/O completion, signal, or synchronization event. It is parked on a device-specific wait queue.
5. **Terminated:** The process has finished execution. The PCB remains until the parent reaps the exit status via `wait()`.

**State Transitions:**
- `New → Ready`: Long-term scheduler admits the process.
- `Ready → Running`: Short-term scheduler dispatches the process.
- `Running → Ready`: Preemption (timer interrupt) or voluntary yield.
- `Running → Blocked`: Process issues I/O or blocks on a semaphore.
- `Blocked → Ready`: I/O completion or event signal.
- `Running → Terminated`: Process calls `exit()` or receives a termination signal.

> The *Blocked → Running* transition is **impossible** — a Blocked process must first transition to Ready.

**Process Control Block (PCB):**
The PCB is a kernel data structure that represents a process. It contains:
- **Identification:** PID, PPID, UID, GID.
- **State Information:** Current state, priority, accounting.
- **CPU Context:** Saved register values (PC, SP, PSW).
- **Memory Management Info:** Page table base register, segment registers.
- **I/O Status:** Open file descriptor table, allocated devices.
- **Scheduling Info:** Queue pointers, time slice remaining.
- **Inter-Process Communication:** Pending signals, message queue pointers.

The PCB enables the OS to *suspend* and *resume* processes by saving and restoring their execution context, making multitasking possible.

#### **Part (b) — Linux Boot Process**

The Linux boot process consists of the following sequential phases:

1. **Power-On & Reset:** Power is applied; the PSU asserts the Power-Good signal; the CPU begins execution from the reset vector `0xFFFFFFF0`, which maps to the firmware (BIOS/UEFI) ROM chip.

2. **POST (Power-On Self-Test):** The firmware tests core hardware — RAM, CPU, keyboard, display, and storage controllers. A hardware map (ACPI tables) is built and stored in RAM.

3. **Boot Device Selection:** The firmware reads the boot order from CMOS NVRAM and probes each device. It looks for a valid MBR signature (`0x55 0xAA`) at the end of the first sector.

4. **Stage-1 Bootloader Execution:** The 446-byte Stage-1 code is loaded into RAM at address `0x7C00` and executed. It is position-dependent and very small.

5. **Stage-1.5 (GRUB):** Located in the MBR gap (sectors 2–62), it contains filesystem drivers needed to read the actual GRUB Stage-2.

6. **Stage-2 (GRUB Menu):** Loads `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` and displays the menu. After user selection, loads the kernel (`vmlinuz`) and initial RAM disk (`initrd.img`).

7. **Kernel Initialization:** The kernel decompresses itself, initializes memory management, mounts the real root filesystem using `initramfs`, detects devices, and loads essential drivers.

8. **PID 1 Execution:** The kernel executes `/sbin/init` or `/lib/systemd/systemd`, which becomes **Process 1** — the ancestor of all other processes.

9. **Service Activation:** Systemd (or SysVinit) starts configured services and reaches the default target (`multi-user.target` or `graphical.target`).

10. **Login:** A display manager (GDM, SDDM) or `getty` on text TTYs prompts the user. Upon successful authentication, a user shell is launched, completing the boot.

> **The first user-space process (PID 1)** is the only process spawned *directly* by the kernel. All other processes are descendants of PID 1.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a)** Compare and contrast BIOS and UEFI firmware. Explain the role of MBR and GPT partitioning schemes in the boot process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? Explain the various fields of a PCB. How does the OS use the PCB during a context switch? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — BIOS vs UEFI and MBR vs GPT**

**BIOS vs UEFI:**

| Feature | BIOS (Legacy) | UEFI (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Era introduced | 1975 (IBM PC) | 2005 (Unified EFI Forum) |
| CPU mode | 16-bit real mode | 32/64-bit protected mode |
| Disk partition scheme | MBR (max 2 TB) | GPT (up to 9.4 ZB) |
| Interface | Text-based, keyboard only | Graphical, mouse-supported |
| Secure Boot | Not supported | Supported (PKI signature check) |
| Network boot | PXE via INT 13h | Native HTTP/UEFI PXE |
| Boot speed | Slower (driver init + INT 13h) | Faster (direct driver access) |
| Extensibility | Hardcoded | Modular EFI drivers |

**MBR (Master Boot Record):**
- 512 bytes at Sector 0 of the boot device.
- Contains: 446-byte bootloader + 64-byte partition table (max 4 primary partitions) + 2-byte signature (`0x55 0xAA`).
- **Limitations:** 2 TB maximum disk size, 4 primary partitions only, no redundancy (single sector corruption = unbootable).

**GPT (GUID Partition Table):**
- Part of the UEFI standard; replaces MBR.
- Supports up to **128 primary partitions** and disks up to **9.4 ZB**.
- Stores a *primary* GPT header at LBA 1 and a *secondary* (backup) GPT at the disk's end for redundancy.
- Uses 128-bit **GUIDs** (Globally Unique Identifiers) to identify partition types.

**Role in Boot Process:**
The firmware first reads the disk's boot sector. If MBR is detected, it loads the Stage-1 bootloader. If GPT is detected, it reads the *protective MBR* for backward compatibility, then reads the GPT header at LBA 1 to locate the EFI System Partition (ESP), which contains the actual EFI bootloader (e.g., `\EFI\ubuntu\grubx64.efi`).

#### **Part (b) — Process Control Block (PCB) and Context Switching**

A **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores all information about a process. Each process has exactly one PCB, and the PCB exists for the entire lifetime of the process — from creation (`fork()`) to reaping (`wait()`).

**Fields of a PCB:**

1. **Process Identification:** PID, Parent PID (PPID), User ID (UID), Group ID (GID).
2. **Process State:** Current state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated).
3. **CPU Registers / Context:** Saved values of all CPU registers — Program Counter (PC), Stack Pointer (SP), General Purpose Registers, Program Status Word (PSW), and Floating Point Unit (FPU) state.
4. **Memory Management Information:** Base and limit registers, page table base register, segment table pointers.
5. **Scheduling Information:** Priority, queue pointers, time slice remaining, scheduling algorithm parameters.
6. **List of Open Files:** File descriptor table, current working directory.
7. **I/O Status Information:** I/O devices allocated to the process, pending I/O operations.
8. **Accounting Information:** CPU time used, time limits, resource quotas.
9. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC) Info:** Pending signals, message queues, shared memory segments, semaphores.
10. **Pointer to Parent Process:** Required for the process tree.

**Role During a Context Switch:**

A **context switch** is the mechanism that allows the OS to switch the CPU from executing one process to another. The steps are:

1. **Trigger:** The scheduler decides to preempt the current process (timer interrupt) or the current process voluntarily yields (e.g., blocks on I/O).
2. **Save State of Old Process:** The kernel saves all CPU registers of the running process into its PCB. The Program Counter, Stack Pointer, and PSW are stored.
3. **Update State:** The old process's state is changed from `Running` to `Ready` (or `Blocked`).
4. **Select New Process:** The short-term scheduler picks the next process from the Ready queue.
5. **Load State of New Process:** The new process's PCB is retrieved, and its saved register values are loaded into the actual CPU registers.
6. **Resume Execution:** The CPU jumps to the new process's saved Program Counter, and execution continues as if it had never been interrupted.

The PCB thus serves as a *snapshot* of a process's execution context, enabling the OS to multiplex the CPU among many processes transparently.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BIOS with bootloader** — BIOS is *firmware on the motherboard ROM*; GRUB is a *bootloader on the disk*. They are NOT the same.
> 2. **Writing "Blocked → Running" as a valid transition** — This is a **classic KTU trap**. Blocked → Running is INVALID. A Blocked process must first go to Ready.
> 3. **Stating that the kernel executes `init`** — More precisely, the kernel *forks and execs* init. But the kernel *directly* starts PID 1. Avoid saying "kernel runs the entire user space."
> 4. **Confusing MBR with GPT** — MBR is a *single-sector structure with a 2 TB limit*; GPT is a *modern, multi-sector scheme with a 9.4 ZB limit*. Examiners specifically look for these distinctions.
> 5. **Forgetting the `0x55 0xAA` MBR signature** — If you draw the boot process without mentioning the magic number, you will lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Treating Process and Program as the same** — Always emphasize that a *process is dynamic* (has state, PCB, lifecycle) while a *program is static* (a file on disk).
> 7. **Skipping the PCB fields** — When asked about process states, *always* list the PCB fields that enable the transitions. Examiners reward integration.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This to Your Wall!):**

### 🔹 Boot Process — Quick Recall
- [ ] **Reset Vector:** CPU starts at `0xFFFFFFF0` (firmware ROM).
- [ ] **POST:** Firmware tests hardware (RAM, CPU, I/O).
- [ ] **MBR:** 512 bytes = 446 (bootloader) + 64 (part table) + 2 (signature `0x55 0xAA`).
- [ ] **Stage-1 Bootloader:** Loaded to `0x7C00`, position-dependent.
- [ ] **GRUB:** Two-stage; Stage-1.5 brings filesystem drivers; Stage-2 shows menu.
- [ ] **Kernel:** `vmlinuz` (compressed) + `initrd.img` (cpio archive) loaded into RAM.
- [ ] **PID 1:** First user-space process = `init` or `systemd`; ancestor of all others.
- [ ] **BIOS vs UEFI:** BIOS = legacy, 16-bit, MBR; UEFI = modern, 32/64-bit, GPT, Secure Boot.
- [ ] **MBR vs GPT:** MBR = 2 TB, 4 partitions; GPT = 9.4 ZB, 128 partitions.

### 🔹 Process States — Quick Recall
- [ ] **5 States:** New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Terminated.
- [ ] **7 States:** + Suspend-Ready and Suspend-Blocked (for swapping).
- [ ] **Invariants:**
  - Exactly **1** process per CPU core in **Running** state.
  - **Blocked → Running** transition is **INVALID**.
  - **Terminated** processes can only be *reaped*, not restarted.
- [ ] **PCB** contains: PID, state, registers, memory info, open files, scheduling info.
- [ ] **Context switch** = Save old PCB → Load new PCB → Dispatch.
- [ ] **Process ≠ Program:** Process is dynamic; program is static.
- [ ] **New → Ready** = Long-term (admission) scheduler.
- [ ] **Ready → Running** = Short-term (CPU) scheduler + Dispatcher.
- [ ] **Ready → Suspend-Ready** = Medium-term (swapping) scheduler.

### 🔹 KTU One-Liner Answers (Use These in Vivas)
- *"What is the first process in Linux?"* → **`init` or `systemd` (PID 1)**.
- *"What is the size of MBR?"* → **512 bytes**.
- *"Where is the Stage-1 bootloader loaded in RAM?"* → **`0x7C00`**.
- *"How many primary partitions does GPT support?"* → **128**.
- *"Can two processes be in the Running state on a single-core CPU?"* → **No, exactly one per core.**
- *"What is a PCB?"* → **Kernel data structure representing a process.**
- *"What causes a transition from Running to Blocked?"* → **I/O request, wait(), or semaphore block.**

---

> 🎯 **Final Examiner's Note:** When answering in the KTU exam, **always draw the state diagram** for process-state questions — it earns you *at least 2 extra marks* even if your written explanation is incomplete. For boot-process questions, **always mention the magic number `0x55 0xAA`** and **PID 1** — these are the *most-expected* keywords.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Control Block
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Process Control Block # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Process Control Block (PCB):** A Process Control Block is a kernel-resident **data structure** maintained by the operating system for **every active process** in the system. It is the *single source of truth* that stores all the contextual, administrative, and execution-state information required by the OS kernel to **suspend, resume, preempt, and manage** a process throughout its lifecycle. In Linux, it is implemented as the `task_struct`; in Windows, it is the `EPROCESS` block. The PCB is also referred to in classical OS literature as the **Task Control Block (TCB)** or **Process Descriptor**. It is created when the process is created (admitted into the system) and is destroyed only when the process terminates. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Hospital Patient File":** > Imagine you walk into a multi-specialty hospital. You are immediately given a **thick file folder** (the PCB). This folder travels with you everywhere — to the X-ray room, the OT, the billing counter, the pharmacy. The file contains: > - Your **unique ID** (Patient Number / MRN) > - Your **current state** (Admitted, Under Observation, In Surgery, Discharged) > - Your **medical history** (Memory of past procedures) > - **Vitals recorded at the last checkpoint** (CPU Registers, Program Counter) > - **Prescribed tests pending** (I/O Status) > - **Insurance and billing details** (Accounting/Resource Info) > > If the doctor is suddenly called away for an emergency, your file lets a *new* doctor (another process) instantly know exactly where you left off — without re-diagnosing you. **The PCB does exactly this for a process.** ## Key Terminology (High-Yield KTU Glossary) - **Process Context:** The complete set of information stored in the PCB, which when reloaded allows a process to resume exactly where it was preempted. - **Context Switch:** The act of saving the state of the currently running process into its PCB and loading the state of the next process from its PCB. A context switch is purely a PCB-to-CPU data movement operation. - **Kernel Stack:** A dedicated kernel-mode stack pointer stored inside the PCB, used when the process executes system calls. - **Resident Set:** The set of physical memory frames currently allocated to the process (tracked in PCB). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In modern OS design, the PCB is **never** stored in user-space memory. It is strictly protected in **kernel-address space** because compromising it would crash the entire process scheduler. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## The Six Major Categories of Information in a PCB The KTU 2024 scheme module groups PCB contents into **six functional categories**. Each is a logical sub-structure of the PCB: ### 2.1 Process Identification (PID Mapping) - **Process ID (PID):** Unique integer identifier assigned at creation. - **Parent Process ID (PPID):** For process tree hierarchies (e.g., `init` → `bash` → `gcc`). - **User Identifier (UID) and Group Identifier (GID):** For ownership and access control. - **Effective UID, Saved UID:** Used in privileged operations (the *setuid* bit mechanism). ### 2.2 Process State Information - The current state from the 5-state model: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked/Waiting, Terminated**. - On some modern kernels: an additional **Zombie** state (terminated but awaiting parent to read exit status). ### 2.3 CPU Context (The Heart of Context Switching) - **Program Counter (PC):** Address of the next instruction to execute. - **General Purpose Registers (EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI in x86; or x0–x31 in ARM).** - **Stack Pointer (SP), Base Pointer (BP).** - **Status Register / Flags Register (PSW).** Contains condition codes (Zero, Carry, Overflow) and interrupt enable bits. ### 2.4 Memory Management Information - **Page Table Base Register (PTBR) or Page Table Pointer.** - **Segment Table pointers** (in segmented architectures). - **Base Register and Limit Register** (in simple base-limit relocation). - Reference to the **resident set** and swapped-out memory location. ### 2.5 I/O Status Information - **List of open file descriptors.** - **List of open sockets and pipes.** - **List of I/O devices currently allocated** to the process. - **Outstanding I/O requests** pending in the kernel I/O queues. ### 2.6 Accounting & Scheduling Information - **Priority (static + dynamic).** - **Scheduling queue pointers** (next/prev pointers in the run-queue linked list). - **CPU time consumed** (user-mode and kernel-mode separately). - **Time limits** (soft and hard real-time deadlines). - **Resource quotas** (max memory, max file handles). - **Process start time, CPU time used, time of next alarm.** ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The PCB is conceptual, not algebraic. However, **performance questions on context switches and PCB size are commonly asked**.
