The key relationships are summarized below. $$ T_{\text{eff}} \;=\; T_{\text{exec}} \;+\; N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}} $$ $$ S_{\text{PCB,total}} \;=\; N_{\text{processes}} \times S_{\text{PCB,single}} $$ $$ T_{\text{cs}} \;=\; T_{\text{save}} \;+\; T_{\text{load}} \;+\; T_{\text{overhead}} $$ $$ M_{\text{kernel, PCB-pool}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{processes}}} S_{\text{PCB},i} $$ ### Definition of Symbols (Boundary Conditions Table) | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit / Range | |---|---|---| | $T_{\text{eff}}$ | Effective execution time including context switch overhead | milliseconds (ms) | | $T_{\text{exec}}$ | Pure CPU execution time of a process | ms | | $N_{\text{cs}}$ | Number of context switches in the interval | dimensionless integer | | $T_{\text{cs}}$ | Time cost of a single context switch | 1 µs to 10 ms | | $S_{\text{PCB,single}}$ | Size of a single PCB in kernel memory | **0.5 KB to 4 KB** typically | | $N_{\text{processes}}$ | Maximum concurrent processes in system | 32,768 to 131,072 (Linux) | | $M_{\text{kernel,PCB-pool}}$ | Total kernel memory consumed by all PCBs | KB / MB | ## Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > In **container orchestration platforms** like Kubernetes and Docker, every container is backed by one or more OS processes, each with a PCB. The **cgroup** subsystem in Linux directly manipulates the *accounting* and *memory management* fields of the `task_struct` to enforce per-container resource limits. The PCB is, therefore, the **enforcement mechanism for cloud resource isolation**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > While a PCB is not derived from first principles like a physics formula, the following three KTU-style analytical derivations are **frequently asked in university examinations** and are essential for full marks. --- ## Derivation 1: Memory Cost of Maintaining All PCBs in Kernel Space **Problem Setup (KTU-typical):** A KTU university exam question may give: *"The PCB of a Linux process occupies 2 KB. If the kernel reserves 4 MB of physical memory for the PCB pool, how many processes can exist concurrently in the system?"* **Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.** The total memory occupied by the PCB pool equals the number of processes times the size of one PCB: $$ M_{\text{total}} \;=\; N \times S_{\text{PCB}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.** We are given $M_{\text{total}} = 4\ \text{MB}$ and $S_{\text{PCB}} = 2\ \text{KB}$. First, normalize the units. Convert 4 MB into KB: $$ 4\ \text{MB} \;=\; 4 \times 1024\ \text{KB} \;=\; 4096\ \text{KB} $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange the equation to solve for N.** $$ N \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{total}}}{S_{\text{PCB}}} $$ **Step 4 — Perform the explicit division.** $$ N \;=\; \frac{4096\ \text{KB}}{2\ \text{KB}} \;=\; 2048\ \text{processes} $$ **Step 5 — State the final answer with units and interpretation.** $$ \boxed{\,N \;=\; 2048\ \text{concurrent processes}\,} $$ **Interpretation for KTU:** If a higher number of processes is desired, either the OS must reduce $S_{\text{PCB}}$ (e.g., store fields in a cache) or increase the reserved kernel memory pool. --- ## Derivation 2: Effective CPU Time Considering Context Switch Overhead **Problem Setup:** A process requires 100 ms of pure CPU time. During its execution, it is preempted **5 times**. Each context switch costs 0.1 ms. Find the effective execution time. **Step 1 — Recall the formula.** $$ T_{\text{eff}} \;=\; T_{\text{exec}} \;+\; N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** $$ T_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 100\ \text{ms} \;+\; 5 \times 0.1\ \text{ms} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the multiplication.** $$ 5 \times 0.1\ \text{ms} \;=\; 0.5\ \text{ms} $$ **Step 4 — Perform the addition.** $$ T_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 100\ \text{ms} \;+\; 0.5\ \text{ms} \;=\; 100.5\ \text{ms} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the percentage overhead (a KTU-favourite sub-question).** $$ \text{Overhead \%} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}}}{T_{\text{eff}}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Overhead \%} \;=\; \frac{0.5}{100.5} \times 100 \;\approx\; 0.497\% $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_{\text{eff}} = 100.5\ \text{ms}, \quad \text{Overhead} \approx 0.5\%\,} $$ --- ## Derivation 3: The CPU Utilization Wastage (Amdahl-Inspired Bound) **Problem Setup:** Show that as context switch overhead grows, CPU utilization wasted grows linearly. Given $T_{\text{exec}} = 80\ \text{ms}$ and a varying $T_{\text{cs}}$ from 0.1 ms to 2 ms with $N_{\text{cs}} = 4$. **Step 1 — Construct a sensitivity table.** $$ T_{\text{eff}}(T_{\text{cs}}) \;=\; 80\ \text{ms} \;+\; 4 \times T_{\text{cs}} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the table values.** | $T_{\text{cs}}$ (ms) | $4 \times T_{\text{cs}}$ (ms) | $T_{\text{eff}}$ (ms) | Wasted % | |---|---|---|---| | 0.1 | 0.4 | 80.4 | 0.497 % | | 0.5 | 2.0 | 82.0 | 2.439 % | | 1.0 | 4.0 | 84.0 | 4.762 % | | 2.0 | 8.0 | 88.0 | 9.091 % | **Step 3 — Analytical Matrix Summary.** $$ \text{Wasted \%} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}}}{T_{\text{exec}} + N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}}} \times 100 $$ **Step 4 — Limiting Behavior Analysis (Bonus KTU Credit).** As $T_{\text{cs}} \to 0$, the wasted fraction $\to 0$ (ideal case). As $T_{\text{cs}} \to \infty$, the wasted fraction $\to 100\%$ (thrashing). **Conclusion:** PCB design directly affects $T_{\text{cs}}$ — a smaller, cache-line-aligned PCB makes context switches faster and reduces kernel overhead. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Two Mermaid block diagrams are provided below. Both follow the **alpha-prefixed node ID rule** and use **clean uppercase labels** (no bold, italics, or HTML tags inside quoted labels). ## Diagram 4.1 — The Anatomy of a Process Control Block ```mermaid graph TB PCBroot["PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK"] PIDblk["PROCESS IDENTIFICATION
PID PPID UID GID"] Stateblk["PROCESS STATE
NEW READY RUNNING BLOCKED EXIT"] CPUblk["CPU CONTEXT
PC REGISTERS SP PSW FLAGS"] MemBlk["MEMORY MANAGEMENT
PAGE TABLE BASE LIMIT REGISTERS"] IOblk["I O STATUS
OPEN FILES SOCKETS DEVICES"] SchedBlk["SCHEDULING AND ACCOUNTING
PRIORITY CPU TIME QUOTAS POINTERS"] PCBroot --> PIDblk PCBroot --> Stateblk PCBroot --> CPUblk PCBroot --> MemBlk PCBroot --> IOblk PCBroot --> SchedBlk ``` ## Diagram 4.2 — PCB Lifecycle During a Context Switch ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as CPU CORES participant KSCHED as KERNEL SCHEDULER participant PCBold as PCB OF PROCESS P1 participant PCBnew as PCB OF PROCESS P2 CPU->>KSCHED: TIMER INTERRUPT OR YIELD KSCHED->>PCBold: SAVE CPU REGISTERS PC SP PSW KSCHED->>PCBnew: RESTORE CPU REGISTERS PC SP PSW KSCHED->>CPU: LOAD BASE REGISTER FROM PCBnew KSCHED->>CPU: JUMP TO PC VALUE FROM PCBnew Note over CPU,PCBnew: PROCESS P2 NOW EXECUTING
P1 PCB REMAINS IN KERNEL MEMORY ``` ## Diagram 4.3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: PCB Interaction with Kernel Subsystems ```mermaid graph LR APP1["USER PROCESS A"] APP2["USER PROCESS B"] APP3["USER PROCESS C"] KERN["KERNEL SPACE"] PCBP["PCB POOL IN KERNEL HEAP"] SCHED["SCHEDULER MODULE"] MM["MEMORY MANAGER"] FS["FILE SYSTEM MODULE"] IODRV["I O DEVICE DRIVERS"] APP1 -. SYS CALL .-> KERN APP2 -. SYS CALL .-> KERN APP3 -. SYS CALL .-> KERN KERN --> PCBP PCBP <--> SCHED PCBP <--> MM PCBP <--> FS PCBP <--> IODRV ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Architectural Insight:** The PCB pool is the **central nervous system** of the kernel. Every scheduler tick, every page fault, every `read()`/`write()` system call reads from or writes to one of these PCBs. The kernel's correctness **hinges** on the integrity of this data structure. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The question bank below is modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** — Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice). Each Part B sub-part is worth 7 marks, totaling 14. --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Process Control Block. List any four information items stored in it.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores all the information about a process required by the OS for its management and execution. Four information items (any four accepted): 1. **Process ID (PID)** — unique identifier. 2. **Process State** — Ready, Running, Blocked, etc. 3. **Program Counter (PC)** — next instruction address. 4. **CPU Registers** — values of general-purpose registers. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any two items, 1 mark for remaining two items with brief explanation.)* --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is a context switch? How is the PCB used during a context switch?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **context switch** is the mechanism by which the OS saves the state of the currently running process and loads the state of another process to be executed. During a context switch: 1. The **CPU registers, program counter, and status flags** of the outgoing process are saved into its PCB. 2. The PCB pointer is updated in the kernel's run-queue. 3. The **CPU registers and program counter** of the incoming process are restored from its PCB. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark definition, 1 mark for save step, 1 mark for restore step.)* --- ## PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU ESE pattern, the student answers **one of two choices**. Both Question A and Question B are given below. Each is split into part (a) for 7 marks and part (b) for 7 marks. --- ### ❖ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **A(a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure of a Process Control Block** and describe the **six categories of information** stored in it. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** A Process Control Block is a **kernel-space data structure**, typically allocated from a slab allocator in modern OS kernels, that holds the complete state of a process. **The Six Categories:** 1. **Process Identification:** PID, PPID, UID, GID, effective UID. 2. **Process State:** New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated (and Zombie in Unix). 3. **CPU Context:** PC, general-purpose registers, stack pointer, status flags — *these are the minimum information needed to resume a process after preemption*. 4. **Memory Management Info:** Base/limit registers or page table base pointer, segment table base pointer. 5. **I/O Status Info:** List of open file descriptors, allocated I/O devices, pending I/O requests. 6. **Scheduling & Accounting Info:** Priority, CPU time used, queue pointers, time limits, owner. *Refer to Diagram 4.1 from Section 4 for the visual representation. (Award 1 extra mark for the diagram.)* **Marks distribution:** 1 mark for the diagram, 3 marks for listing all six categories, 3 marks for brief explanation of each. --- **A(b).** A system has a **PCB size of 1.5 KB** and a **reserved kernel PCB pool of 6 MB**. **(i)** Calculate the maximum number of processes that can be supported. **(ii)** If the context switch overhead is 0.2 ms per switch and a process is preempted 4 times during 200 ms of execution, calculate the **effective execution time** and the **percentage of CPU time wasted** in context switching. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Maximum Number of Processes (3 marks):** $$ N \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{total}}}{S_{\text{PCB}}} \;=\; \frac{6 \times 1024\ \text{KB}}{1.5\ \text{KB}} \;=\; \frac{6144}{1.5} \;=\; 4096\ \text{processes} $$ **(ii) Effective Time and Wastage (4 marks):** $$ T_{\text{eff}} \;=\; T_{\text{exec}} \;+\; N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}} \;=\; 200\ \text{ms} \;+\; 4 \times 0.2\ \text{ms} \;=\; 200.8\ \text{ms} $$ $$ \text{Wasted \%} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}}}{T_{\text{eff}}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{0.8}{200.8} \times 100 \;\approx\; 0.398\% $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\,N = 4096\ \text{processes}, \quad T_{\text{eff}} = 200.8\ \text{ms}, \quad \text{Wastage} \approx 0.40\%\,} $$ --- ### ❖ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` (Alternative Choice) **B(a).** Explain the role of the **Process Control Block in supporting process scheduling and context switching**, with emphasis on **CPU context, memory pointers, and I/O state**. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** The PCB is the *enabling data structure* for both **preemptive scheduling** and **context switching**. **1. Role in Scheduling (2 marks):** The PCB contains the **scheduling queue pointers** (`next`, `prev`), the **current state**, and the **dynamic priority**. The scheduler iterates over PCBs in the ready queue (often an O(1) runqueue in Linux) without needing to scan user-space memory. **2. Role in CPU Context (2 marks):** The PC, registers, SP, and PSW stored in the PCB are the **exact byte-for-byte snapshot** of CPU state at the moment of preemption. Restoring this state makes the process resume *as if it was never interrupted*. Without the PCB, context switching would be physically impossible. **3. Role in Memory and I/O (3 marks):** The **page table base register** stored in the PCB defines the process's virtual address space. On a context switch, the MMU is reprogrammed with this value (CR3 register in x86-64). The **open file descriptor table pointer** in the PCB ensures that when the process resumes, its `read()`/`write()` operations continue against the correct files. Losing the PCB pointer would leave the process *anonymous* to the kernel. --- **B(b).** Consider an OS where the **PCB size is 3 KB** and the **maximum kernel-allocated memory for PCBs is 12 MB**. A process executes for **500 ms** and suffers **10 context switches** of **0.05 ms each**. **(i)** Determine the maximum number of concurrent processes. **(ii)** Compute the effective execution time and the context switch overhead as a percentage. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Maximum Concurrent Processes (3 marks):** $$ N \;=\; \frac{12 \times 1024\ \text{KB}}{3\ \text{KB}} \;=\; 4096\ \text{processes} $$ **(ii) Effective Time (4 marks):** $$ T_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 500\ \text{ms} \;+\; 10 \times 0.05\ \text{ms} \;=\; 500.5\ \text{ms} $$ $$ \text{Overhead \%} \;=\; \frac{10 \times 0.05}{500.5} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{0.5}{500.5} \times 100 \;\approx\; 0.0999\% $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\,N = 4096\ \text{processes}, \quad T_{\text{eff}} = 500.5\ \text{ms}, \quad \text{Overhead} \approx 0.10\%\,} $$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCB Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert MB → KB:** A staggering 40% of students write `6000/1.5` instead of `6144/1.5` in part (i) of numerical questions. **Always show the unit conversion explicitly.** > 2. **Confusing PCB with Process Image:** The PCB is the *control metadata*; the *process image* is the user code + data + stack in memory. They are **different** concepts. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. **Omitting the diagram in part (a) of 7-mark questions:** A neatly drawn PCB block diagram is worth at least **1 to 1.5 marks** in KTU valuation. Always include one. > 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Writing `N = 4096` without the word *"processes"* is marked down. **State the unit explicitly.** > 5. **Mixing up register types:** Students often write *"EBP, EIP, ESP, EAX"* without explaining *which is the program counter, which is the stack pointer, which is the base pointer*. Examiners expect the *role* of each register to be stated. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-density revision checklist for the Process Control Block topic:** - ⭐ **PCB is a kernel-space data structure** — not user-space. Compromising it crashes the kernel. - ⭐ **PCB is the "identity card + medical file" of a process** — created at admission, destroyed at termination. - ⭐ **Six mandatory categories** of PCB contents: Identification, State, CPU Context, Memory Mgmt, I/O Status, Scheduling/Accounting. - ⭐ **The Program Counter (PC) and CPU Registers** in the PCB are the *minimum required* for context switch. - ⭐ **Context switch** = save outgoing PCB state + restore incoming PCB state. No user code runs during this window. - ⭐ **Page Table Base Register (PTBR)** in PCB is reloaded into CR3 (x86-64) or TTBR0 (ARM) on every context switch. - ⭐ **Zombie process** in Unix = terminated but PCB retained until parent reads exit status. - ⭐ **PCB size typically 0.5 KB – 4 KB**; Linux `task_struct` is around 6–8 KB due to extensive features. - ⭐ **Formula to remember:** $T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{exec}} + N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}}$ — appears in nearly every KTU numerical. - ⭐ **Maximum concurrent processes** $N = M_{\text{PCB-pool}} / S_{\text{PCB}}$ — always convert units carefully. - ⭐ **Linked-list organization of PCBs** enables O(1) insertion/removal in run-queues. - ⭐ **PCB pointer is stored in the kernel stack** of the process during execution, allowing the scheduler to find the PCB in constant time.

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧵 THREADS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread (a.k.a. Lightweight Process / LWP):** A thread is a basic unit of **CPU utilization**; it comprises a *thread ID*, a *program counter (PC)*, a *register set*, and a *stack*. It shares with other threads belonging to the same process its **code section**, **data section**, and other **operating-system resources** such as open files, signals, and shared memory. A traditional (or *heavyweight*) process has a **single thread of control**, whereas a **multithreaded process** has *multiple threads of control* executing concurrently within the same process address space.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** definition framework, the term *thread of execution* is preferred. A thread is the entity that the operating system (OS) actually **schedules and dispatches** to the CPU cores. Modern OS kernels (Linux, Windows NT, macOS XNU) treat threads as the **fundamental schedulable entities**, while *processes* are merely *containers of resources* (address space, file descriptors, signal handlers, etc.) for one or more threads.

### 🔑 The Three Shared + Four Per-Thread Components

| **Shared by all threads in a process** | **Private to each thread** |
|---|---|
| Code (text) segment | Thread ID (TID) |
| Data (heap) segment | Program Counter (PC) |
| Open files & sockets | Register set (context) |
| Global variables & OS resources | Stack (local variables, return addresses) |
| Signal handlers & signal mask | Stack pointer & Thread-Local Storage (TLS) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a **single restaurant kitchen (the process)**. The kitchen has:
- One set of **pots, pans, and a shared pantry** (shared resources: code, data, files).
- One shared **walk-in refrigerator** (shared heap memory).

Now, a **traditional process** is like a kitchen with only **one chef (single thread)**. Even if the restaurant has 4 stoves (4 CPU cores), only 1 stove gets used because there is only 1 chef.

A **multithreaded process** is like a kitchen with **4 chefs (threads)** working in parallel:
- All 4 chefs **share** the same pantry, fridge, and utensils (shared memory).
- Each chef has their **own knife set, apron pocket, and cutting board** (private stack & registers).
- They can all **cook different dishes simultaneously**, dramatically increasing throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Lightweight"?** Creating a new chef (thread) is *much faster* than building a new kitchen (process). A `pthread_create()` call may take **microseconds (~10–100 µs)**, while a `fork()` system call takes **milliseconds (~1–10 ms)** — roughly **100× faster**, because threads avoid duplicating the address space.

## 1.3 Why Threads Matter — Motivation for the KTU 2024 Module

The driving forces behind thread support in modern OS design are:

1. **Responsiveness** — A multi-threaded GUI application can keep the UI responsive while a worker thread performs a long I/O operation.
2. **Resource Sharing** — Threads share the process's memory and resources by default; no explicit IPC mechanism (pipes, shared memory) is required.
3. **Economy** — Thread creation, context switch, and termination are cheaper than process equivalents because shared resources are not duplicated.
4. **Scalability / Parallelism** — On multicore systems, threads can run **truly in parallel** on different cores, exploiting hardware parallelism (Amdahl's Law applies).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The *single-threaded process model* is now considered obsolete in mainstream OS design. Be prepared to contrast it with the **multithreaded process model** in Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.4 Benefits & Drawbacks at a Glance

| **Benefit** | **Drawback** |
|---|---|
| Faster creation/termination | Requires careful synchronization (race conditions) |
| Faster context switching | No protection between threads (one thread can crash the whole process) |
| Efficient communication via shared memory | Debugging is harder (non-determinism) |
| Better CPU utilization on multicore | `fork()` semantics become ambiguous (copy all threads or just calling thread?) |
| Improved responsiveness for interactive apps | Performance gains capped by Amdahl's Law |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Models of Multithreading

The OS must define a relationship between **user-level threads (ULT)** and **kernel-level threads (KLT)**. The three canonical models — heavily tested in KTU exams — are:

### Model A — Many-to-One Model (ULT-only)

- **Mechanism:** Many user threads are mapped to **one** kernel thread.
- **Thread management** is done entirely in **user space** by a *thread library*.
- If **any** thread makes a blocking system call, the **entire process blocks**.
- **Cannot exploit multiprocessors** in parallel — only one thread can be in the kernel at a time.
- *Examples:* Early Solaris Green Threads, GNU Portable Threads.

### Model B — One-to-One Model (KLT-only)

- **Mechanism:** Each user thread maps to **one** kernel thread.
- Provides **true concurrency** on multicores.
- Drawback: Creating a user thread requires creating the corresponding kernel thread → **overhead**.
- Most modern OS implementations restrict the number of KLTs to avoid exhausting kernel resources.
- *Examples:* **Windows XP/7/10/11**, **Linux (NPTL)**, **FreeBSD**.

### Model C — Many-to-Many Model (Hybrid / M:N)

- **Mechanism:** *M* user threads multiplexed onto *N* kernel threads, where **M ≥ N**.
- Developers can create as many user threads as needed, while the kernel can schedule *N* of them in parallel on *N* processors.
- The OS may also offer a **two-level model** that allows binding a user thread permanently to a KLT (similar to one-to-one) for critical sections.
- *Examples:* **Solaris prior to Solaris 9**, **HP-UX**, **Tru64 UNIX**, **macOS (Grand Central Dispatch)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked:** *Compare the three threading models with diagrams.* Know the concurrency limits: Many-to-One → **1**, One-to-One → **N**, Many-to-Many → **≤ N** parallel threads.

## 2.2 Thread Libraries — The Programmer's Interface

A **thread library** provides the API for creating and managing threads. Two principal implementation strategies:

1. **Asynchronous (library-level) threading:** The library contains code for thread creation/destruction at user level; kernel is unaware. (e.g., early `user-level threads`).
2. **Synchronous (kernel-level) threading:** All thread operations are implemented as **system calls** in the kernel. The library's API is just a thin wrapper. (e.g., `pthread_create()` on Linux → `clone()` syscall).

### The Three Principal Thread Libraries

| **Library** | **Type** | **Used On** | **Standard** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **POSIX Pthreads** (`pthread.h`) | Spec for both ULT & KLT | Linux, macOS, Solaris, FreeBSD | POSIX.1c (IEEE 1003.1c-1995) |
| **Win32 Thread API** (`CreateThread`) | KLT only | Windows family | Microsoft proprietary |
| **Java Threads** (`java.lang.Thread`) | Either; JVM typically maps 1:1 | Any JVM (J2SE / OpenJDK) | JLS §17 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Explain the Pthread API,"* always include the four cardinal functions: `pthread_create()`, `pthread_join()`, `pthread_exit()`, and `pthread_attr_init()`. A diagram of the function call flow scores bonus marks.

## 2.3 Threading Issues (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The OS textbook by Silberschatz, Galvin & Gagne lists **seven canonical threading issues**. KTU typically asks 1–2 of these in Part A (3 marks) and combines them in Part B (14 marks).

### Issue 1 — `fork()` / `exec()` System Calls in a Multithreaded Program
- If one thread in a multi-threaded program calls `fork()`:
  - Should **all threads be duplicated** in the child? (Linux default: only the calling thread)
  - If the child immediately calls `exec()`, duplication is wasted work.
- The answer determines whether the child's `fork()` is **heavy** (copies all) or **light** (copies one).

### Issue 2 — Thread Cancellation
A thread requesting the termination of another thread. Two flavors:
- **Asynchronous cancellation:** Target thread terminated *immediately* — risky because resources aren't cleaned up.
- **Deferred cancellation:** Target thread periodically checks a cancellation flag and exits cleanly at safe points — preferred.

### Issue 3 — Signal Handling in Multithreaded Programs
- **Synchronous signals** (e.g., `SIGFPE` from divide-by-zero) are delivered to the **thread that caused them**.
- **Asynchronous signals** (e.g., `SIGINT` from `Ctrl+C`) must be delivered to *one* thread. Options:
  - Deliver to the **thread to which the signal applies**.
  - Deliver to **every thread**.
  - Deliver to **specific threads** (a signal mask assigned to each thread).
  - Assign **a dedicated thread** for all signals (common Linux pattern: block in all threads, unblock & `sigwait()` in one).

### Issue 4 — Thread Pools
- Creating a thread for every request is expensive. The **thread pool** creates *N* worker threads at process startup and reuses them for incoming tasks.
- Advantages: faster service, bound on the number of concurrent threads, task separation from thread creation.
- Common in web servers (Apache `mpm_event`, Java `ExecutorService`).

### Issue 5 — Thread-Specific Data (TSD / TLS)
- Useful when a procedure needs to maintain state across invocations **per thread** rather than per process.
- The Pthreads API provides **keys** (`pthread_key_create()`) and accessors (`pthread_getspecific()` / `pthread_setspecific()`).
- Example: `errno` in glibc is implemented as thread-local storage in multithreaded programs.

### Issue 6 — Scheduler Activations
- A mechanism used by the **many-to-many model** to allow the user-thread library to communicate with the kernel about thread blocking/unblocking.
- The kernel delivers an **upcall** (a "scheduler activation") to the thread library when a KLT becomes available.
- The upcall handler then re-schedules a ULT onto that KLT.

### Issue 7 — Implicit Threading
- Threads are created/managed by **compilers, runtime libraries, or APIs** rather than explicitly by the programmer.
- Examples: **Thread Pools**, **OpenMP** (`#pragma omp parallel`), **Grand Central Dispatch (GCD)** in macOS, **TBB (Threading Building Blocks)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Performance Cheat Sheet

Although threads are a systems topic with little classical "engineering math," the following performance formulas are **exam-likely** when KTU combines this with Amdahl's Law from the Architecture/Performance module.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Metric** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Speedup with $N$ threads | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | Amdahl's Law; $p$ = parallel fraction |
| Maximum theoretical speedup | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - p}$ | As $N \to \infty$ |
| Thread creation overhead (Linux) | $\approx 10\text{–}100 \,\mu s$ | vs. process `fork()`: $\approx 1\text{–}10\,ms$ |
| Context switch cost (thread) | $\approx 1\text{–}10 \,\mu s$ | vs. process: $\approx 10\text{–}100 \,\mu s$ |
| Concurrency limit (Many-to-One) | $1$ KLT per process | True parallelism impossible |
| Concurrency limit (One-to-One) | $\leq N$ KLTs (OS-defined) | True parallelism on $N$-core |
| Concurrency limit (Many-to-Many) | $\leq N$ KLTs in parallel | $M$ user threads ≥ $N$ kernel threads |
| Thread pool size heuristic | $N_{\text{threads}} = N_{\text{cores}} \cdot U_{\text{desired}} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{W}{C} \right)$ | $W/C$ = wait-time / compute-time ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on LaTeX in tables:** All $\vert$ (vertical bar) symbols in the above formulas have been rendered using standard math symbols (e.g., $\leq$, $1$–$100$ ranges) to avoid breaking the markdown table. When you must use $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in tables, write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ to keep the pipe from terminating the column.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Thread Usage** |
|---|---|
| **Web Servers (nginx, Apache)** | Thread pool to handle thousands of concurrent HTTP requests |
| **Databases (PostgreSQL)** | One thread per client connection; background writer threads for WAL |
| **GUI Apps (Browsers, IDEs)** | UI thread + worker threads (Chromium uses 1 process per tab *with many threads each*) |
| **Scientific Computing (OpenMP)** | Parallelization of `for` loops across SMP cores |
| **Game Engines (Unreal, Unity)** | Render thread + Game thread + Worker threads for asset loading |
| **Embedded / RTOS (VxWorks, FreeRTOS)** | POSIX pthreads or task threads for concurrent I/O |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Amdahl's Law Applied to a Multithreaded Program

> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):** A multithreaded program has a parallelizable fraction of $p = 0.85$ (i.e., **85%** of execution time can be parallelized). The remaining 15% is inherently serial. Calculate the **speedup** when the program is run with (a) 2 threads, (b) 4 threads, and (c) 8 threads. What is the **maximum theoretical speedup** as the number of threads → ∞?

### Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law.**

Amdahl's Law expresses the overall speedup $S(N)$ when a fraction $p$ of a computation can be parallelized across $N$ processing elements:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters.**

From the problem statement:
- Serial fraction: $1 - p = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$
- Parallel fraction: $p = 0.85$

**Step 3 — Compute for $N = 2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(2) &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.85) + \dfrac{0.85}{2}} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.425} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.575} \\
     &\approx 1.739
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute for $N = 4$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{4}} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.2125} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.3625} \\
     &\approx 2.759
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute for $N = 8$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{1}{0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{8}} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.10625} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.25625} \\
     &\approx 3.902
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute maximum theoretical speedup** ($N \to \infty$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}} \\
         &= \frac{1}{1 - p + 0} \\
         &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.85} \\
         &= \frac{1}{0.15} \\
         &\approx 6.667
\end{aligned}
$$

### 📊 Tabulated Result

| **Threads ($N$)** | **Serial Time ($T_s$)** | **Parallel Time ($T_p$)** | **Speedup $S(N)$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.00 (baseline) | 1.00 | $1.000\times$ |
| 2 | 0.15 | $0.15 + 0.425 = 0.575$ | $1.739\times$ |
| 4 | 0.15 | $0.15 + 0.2125 = 0.3625$ | $2.759\times$ |
| 8 | 0.15 | $0.15 + 0.10625 = 0.25625$ | $3.902\times$ |
| $\infty$ | 0.15 | $0.15$ | $6.667\times$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even with **infinite** threads, the speedup is capped at $1/(1-p)$ because the 15% serial portion is the bottleneck. This is the **Killer Insight** of Amdahl's Law that KTU expects you to articulate.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Thread Pool Sizing (Little's Law Variant)

> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):** A web server receives requests at a rate of $\lambda = 500$ requests/second. Each request takes an average service time of $w = 80$ ms (i.e., $W = 0.08$ s of CPU work). The wait time for I/O is $W_{\text{IO}} = 40$ ms. Compute the **minimum thread pool size** required to keep the server responsive (no queuing saturation).

### Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Use Little's Law for thread pool sizing.**

For a stable system, the number of threads in the pool must satisfy:

$$
N_{\text{threads}} \geq \frac{\lambda \cdot (W_{\text{CPU}} + W_{\text{IO}})}{U_{\text{desired}}}
$$

where $U_{\text{desired}}$ is the target CPU utilization (typically **0.8 to 0.9** to leave headroom).

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

Take $U_{\text{desired}} = 0.8$ (80%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{threads}} &\geq \frac{500 \cdot (0.08 + 0.04)}{0.8} \\
                   &= \frac{500 \cdot 0.12}{0.8} \\
                   &= \frac{60}{0.8} \\
                   &= 75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

A pool of at least **75 threads** is needed. If the server has only **8 cores**, the pool size should still be 75 because the I/O wait releases the CPU; threads blocked on I/O are not holding a core.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative formula (CPU-bound):** If tasks are purely CPU-bound with no I/O, the pool size should equal the number of cores $N_{\text{cores}}$ — using more threads only adds context-switch overhead.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Pthread Creation & Termination Flow

> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):** Explain the lifecycle of a POSIX thread from creation to termination, drawing a state diagram. Show the role of `pthread_create()`, `pthread_exit()`, `pthread_join()`, and `pthread_cancel()`.

### Analytical Matrix — Pthread Lifecycle

| **State** | **Trigger** | **API** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Created** | Initial state | `pthread_create()` | Returns `tid`; new thread is *runnable* |
| **Runnable** | Scheduler dispatches | (kernel) | Multiple runnable threads may compete for CPU |
| **Running** | Dispatched on a core | (kernel) | At most $N_{\text{cores}}$ threads in this state |
| **Blocked** | I/O, mutex, cond-var | `pthread_mutex_lock()` etc. | Releases CPU until event |
| **Terminated** | Function returns or explicit exit | `pthread_exit()` or thread function return | Status passed to joining thread |
| **Zombie (detached)** | Not joined, resources freed | `pthread_detach()` or `pthread_join()` | Avoids resource leak |
| **Cancelled** | External request honored | `pthread_cancel()` | Type: deferred (default) or async |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write `pthread_exit(NULL)` *from the main thread* expecting it to terminate the program. It actually **terminates only the main thread**; other peer threads keep running. To exit the whole process, use `exit()`.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Numerical Comparison of Thread vs Process

> **Problem (KTU Part A, 3 marks):** A program performs 50 iterations, each requiring 20 ms of CPU work. Compare execution time of (a) 50 separate **processes** vs (b) a **single multithreaded process** with 50 threads, assuming process creation cost is **5 ms** and thread creation cost is **0.1 ms**.

### Solution

**(a) Process model:**

$$
T_{\text{process}} = 50 \times (5 + 20) = 50 \times 25 = 1250 \,\text{ms}
$$

**(b) Thread model:**

$$
T_{\text{thread}} = 50 \times (0.1 + 20) = 50 \times 20.1 = 1005 \,\text{ms}
$$

**Savings:**

$$
\Delta T = 1250 - 1005 = 245 \,\text{ms} \quad (\approx 19.6\% \text{ faster})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, on a multi-core machine, the thread model would also enable **parallel execution**, dramatically increasing speedup beyond the simple creation-cost savings.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Thread vs Process

| **Attribute** | **Process** | **Thread (within process)** |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | Independent | Shared with peer threads |
| Creation cost (typical) | $1\text{–}10\,ms$ | $10\text{–}100\,\mu s$ |
| Context switch cost (typical) | $10\text{–}100\,\mu s$ | $1\text{–}10\,\mu s$ |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets, shm) | Shared variables (with sync) |
| Protection | Hardware-enforced (page tables) | None (same address space) |
| Failure impact | Isolated | Process-wide |
| Resource ownership | Yes (process = resource owner) | No (threads are *workers*) |
| Scheduling entity | Process (in old OS) | Thread (in modern OS) |
| Identification | PID | TID (a.k.a. LWP ID) |

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Pthread Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : pthread_create
    Created --> Runnable : Thread is ready
    Runnable --> Running : Scheduler dispatch
    Running --> Runnable : Time slice expired or yield
    Running --> Blocked : pthread_mutex_lock or I/O wait
    Blocked --> Runnable : Lock acquired or I/O complete
    Running --> Terminated : Function return or pthread_exit
    Running --> Terminated : pthread_cancel accepted
    Terminated --> Joined : pthread_join collects status
    Terminated --> Detached : pthread_detach releases resources
    Joined --> [*]
    Detached --> [*]
```

> **Reading Guide:** A newly created Pthread enters the **Runnable** state. The kernel scheduler moves it to **Running** on a CPU core. It can transition to **Blocked** on a mutex, condition variable, or I/O. The thread leaves the system via **Terminated**, after which its exit status may be collected by `pthread_join()` or its resources released via `pthread_detach()`.

> **Reading Guide:** The `#pragma omp parallel for` directive tells the compiler/runtime to distribute loop iterations across available threads. An **implicit barrier** synchronizes threads at the end of the parallel region before the program continues sequentially.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define a thread. How does it differ from a traditional process?**

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

A **thread** is a basic unit of CPU utilization, comprising a thread ID, program counter, register set, and a stack, which shares its code section, data section, and OS resources (open files, signals) with other threads of the same process.

| **Aspect** | **Process** | **Thread** |
|---|---|---|
| Address Space | Independent | Shared within process |
| Creation Cost | High (≈ 1–10 ms) | Low (≈ 10–100 µs) |
| Communication | IPC required | Shared memory + sync |
| Isolation | Yes (page tables) | No |

A **multithreaded process** improves responsiveness, resource sharing, economy, and parallelism on multicore systems. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q: List and briefly explain the three multithreading models.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Many-to-One Model:** Many user-level threads map to one kernel thread. If one thread blocks, all block. No multiprocessor parallelism. Example: GNU Portable Threads.
2. **One-to-One Model:** Each user thread maps to one kernel thread. Provides true concurrency. Higher overhead in thread creation. Example: Windows, Linux NPTL.
3. **Many-to-Many Model:** Multiplexes M user threads onto N kernel threads (M ≥ N). Combines flexibility of many ULTs with parallel execution of KLTs. Example: Solaris (older versions), HP-UX. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q — 14 Marks, Module 1 Choice 1]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **three multithreading models** (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many) used to map user threads to kernel threads. Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of each.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **POSIX Pthread API** for thread creation and termination. Write the syntax of `pthread_create()`, `pthread_join()`, and `pthread_exit()`. Also explain **thread cancellation** (asynchronous vs deferred) with examples.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**Many-to-One Model:**
- All user threads (ULTs) of a process are mapped to a **single kernel thread** (KLT).
- Thread management is performed in user space by a thread library.
- **Advantages:** Fast thread creation (no syscall); portable; works on any OS.
- **Disadvantages:** If one thread makes a blocking system call, the **entire process blocks**; threads cannot run in parallel on a multiprocessor; only one thread can access the kernel at a time.
- **Examples:** GNU Portable Threads, Solaris Green Threads (older).

**One-to-One Model:**
- Each user thread is mapped to one kernel thread.
- Provides **true concurrency** because multiple threads can run in parallel on multiprocessors.
- **Advantages:** Better parallelism; one thread blocking does not block others.
- **Disadvantages:** Creating a user thread requires a kernel call → overhead; the OS may limit the number of KLTs to prevent resource exhaustion.
- **Examples:** Windows, Linux (NPTL), FreeBSD 5+.

**Many-to-Many Model:**
- M user threads are multiplexed onto N kernel threads, where **M ≥ N**.
- The developer can create as many user threads as needed; the kernel decides how many to schedule in parallel.
- **Advantages:** Combines the best of both — flexibility of many ULTs, true parallelism of KLTs.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex implementation; more difficult to tune.
- **Variations:** Two-level model binds some ULTs permanently to a KLT.
- **Examples:** Solaris (older), HP-UX, Tru64 UNIX.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Many-to-One | One-to-One | Many-to-Many |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallelism | No | Yes | Yes (≤ N) |
| Blocking issue | Whole process | None | None |
| Thread create cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Complexity | Low | Low | High |
| Example | GNU Pth | Win32 / Linux | Solaris / HP-UX |

(7 marks: 2 for diagrams, 3 for explanations, 2 for comparison)

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**POSIX Pthread API — The Standard Interface:**

The IEEE POSIX 1003.1c standard defines the threads API for C programs. The most commonly used functions are:

**1. `pthread_create()` — Creating a new thread.**

The function spawns a new thread in the calling process. The new thread begins execution by invoking `start_routine` with `arg` as its sole argument.

**Signature (C language prototype):**

```c
int pthread_create(
    pthread_t *thread,
    const pthread_attr_t *attr,
    void *(*start_routine)(void *),
    void *arg
);
```

- `thread` — output: stores the ID of the new thread.
- `attr` — input: thread attributes (NULL for default).
- `start_routine` — the function the thread will execute.
- `arg` — single argument passed to `start_routine`.

**2. `pthread_join()` — Waiting for thread termination.**

Blocks the calling thread until the target thread terminates. Retrieves the exit value via the second argument.

**Signature:**

```c
int pthread_join(
    pthread_t thread,
    void **retval
);
```

**3. `pthread_exit()` — Terminating the calling thread.**

Used to explicitly terminate a thread and pass an exit status to any joining thread. Calling it from `main()` terminates *only* `main`, not peer threads.

**Signature:**

```c
void pthread_exit(void *retval);
```

**Thread Cancellation:**

A thread can request the termination of another thread. Two flavors exist:

- **Asynchronous Cancellation:** The target thread is terminated *immediately* at an arbitrary point in its execution. **Risky** because resources (memory, mutexes, file handles) may not be released — can cause memory leaks and deadlock.
- **Deferred Cancellation (default):** The target thread periodically polls a cancellation flag. It terminates only at predefined **cancellation points** (e.g., `pthread_cond_wait()`, I/O calls). This is the **safe and recommended** approach.

The type is set via `pthread_setcanceltype()`. The cancellation points are typically blocking system calls.

**Example of deferred cancellation:**

```c
pthread_setcancelstate(PTHREAD_CANCEL_ENABLE, NULL);
pthread_setcanceltype(PTHREAD_CANCEL_DEFERRED, NULL);
while (running) {
    /* do work; thread checks cancellation flag at cancel points */
}
```

(7 marks: 2 for Pthread_create/join/exit signatures, 2 for usage explanation, 2 for cancellation types, 1 for example)

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q — 14 Marks, Module 1 Choice 2]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **various threading issues** in multithreaded programs: fork-exec semantics, signal handling, thread pools, thread-specific data, and scheduler activations. Why are these issues important?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **implicit threading** with examples. Compare **Thread Pools, OpenMP, and Grand Central Dispatch (GCD)**. Show how `#pragma omp parallel` parallelizes a loop.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**Threading Issues in Multithreaded Programs:**

**1. `fork()` / `exec()` Semantics:**
- A `fork()` in a multithreaded program can either:
  - **Duplicate all threads** (heavy, but safe for child work), OR
  - **Duplicate only the calling thread** (lightweight; common in Linux).
- If the child then calls `exec()`, duplication of all threads is wasted because the address space is replaced.
- POSIX leaves the choice to the implementation; programmers must use `pthread_atfork()` handlers to lock mutexes across the fork if required.

**2. Signal Handling:**
- **Synchronous signals** (e.g., `SIGFPE` from `1/0`) are caused by and delivered to the offending thread.
- **Asynchronous signals** (e.g., `SIGINT` from Ctrl+C) are external; the OS must decide which thread receives them.
- Common solution: **All threads block all signals**; one dedicated thread calls `sigwait()` to process them.
- Each thread has its own **signal mask** (set of currently blocked signals).

**3. Thread Pools:**
- Create a fixed number of worker threads at process startup; submit tasks via a queue.
- **Advantages:**
  - Avoids the cost of creating/destroying a thread per request.
  - Bounds the number of concurrent threads (prevents resource exhaustion).
  - Separates task submission from thread management.
- **Use case:** Web servers, database servers, any high-concurrency environment.

**4. Thread-Specific Data (TSD / TLS):**
- A mechanism for storing **per-thread state** that appears global to a function but is actually local to each thread.
- Example: the C library variable `errno` is implemented as TLS in multithreaded code, so each thread has its own `errno`.
- Pthreads API: `pthread_key_create()`, `pthread_setspecific()`, `pthread_getspecific()`.

**5. Scheduler Activations:**
- A communication mechanism between the user-thread library and the kernel.
- The kernel **upcalls** the thread library when a kernel thread becomes available (unblocked) or is about to block.
- The library then re-schedules a user thread onto that kernel thread.
- Solves the problem of the kernel being unaware of the many-to-many mapping and thus mis-scheduling resources.

**Why these issues matter:**
- They affect **correctness** (signals, fork semantics), **performance** (thread pools), and **API design** (TLS, scheduler activations). A modern OS must address all of them to be usable in production.

(7 marks: 1 per issue, 2 for synthesis)

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**Implicit Threading:**

**Definition:** A programming style in which the **compiler, runtime, or API** (not the programmer) creates and manages threads. The programmer writes sequential-looking code and the system handles the parallelism.

**Why it matters:**
- Manual thread management is error-prone (race conditions, deadlocks, leaks).
- Implicit threading makes parallelism accessible to non-experts.
- The runtime can adapt to the available hardware (e.g., number of cores).

**Three Major Implicit Threading Technologies:**

**1. Thread Pools:**
- A fixed set of threads is created at startup.
- Tasks are submitted to a queue; idle threads pick up and execute them.
- Common in Java's `ExecutorService`, Python's `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor`, and C++'s `std::async`.

**2. OpenMP (Open Multi-Processing):**
- A compiler directive-based API for shared-memory parallelism, primarily in C/C++/Fortran.
- The programmer inserts `#pragma omp` directives into otherwise sequential code.
- Example: parallelizing a `for` loop:

```c
#include <omp.h>
int main() {
    int i, N = 1000;
    double a[N], sum = 0.0;
    /* ... initialize a[i] ... */
    #pragma omp parallel for reduction(+:sum)
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        sum += a[i];
    }
    printf("Sum = %f\n", sum);
    return 0;
}
```

- The `parallel for` directive splits the loop iterations among the threads in the **team** (created by the `parallel` clause).
- The `reduction(+:sum)` clause ensures each thread has its own local copy of `sum` and the results are summed at the end safely.
- An **implicit barrier** at the end of the `parallel for` synchronizes the team.

**3. Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) — Apple/macOS/iOS:**
- A runtime that manages a **thread pool** based on system load.
- Programmers enqueue **blocks of work** (closures) to **dispatch queues**:
  - **Serial queues** execute one block at a time (for ordered tasks).
  - **Concurrent queues** execute multiple blocks in parallel.
- Example in Objective-C/Swift:

```c
dispatch_queue_t queue = dispatch_get_global_queue(QOS_CLASS_DEFAULT, 0);
for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    dispatch_async(queue, ^{
        /* perform background work for index i */
    });
}
```

- GCD decides how many threads to use based on system load and number of cores.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Thread Pools | OpenMP | GCD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Language | C/C++/Java/Python | C/C++/Fortran | Swift / Obj-C (Apple) |
| Style | API-based | Directive-based | Closure/queue-based |
| Thread creation | Manual | Automatic | Automatic |
| Best for | Server / I/O | Scientific loops | Apple platform apps |
| Load balancing | Manual | Compiler-driven | OS-driven |

(7 marks: 2 for definition + motivation, 3 for OpenMP example, 2 for comparison)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Thread" with "Lightweight Process"** — They are NOT identical. Threads share the address space; LWPs are kernel-scheduled entities that may host one or more user threads. Many KTU answers mix these up.
> 2. **Drawing the wrong threading model diagram** — Students often draw a one-to-one model when asked about many-to-many. Remember: M:N has multiple ULTs per KLT.
> 3. **Forgetting `pthread_join()`** — Without joining (or detaching), terminated threads leak kernel resources. This is a classic correctness issue.
> 4. **Using `pthread_exit()` in `main()` to terminate the program** — This only kills the main thread; peer threads continue. Use `exit()` instead.
> 5. **Amdahl's Law units** — Speedup is **dimensionless** (a ratio). Do not write "seconds" or "ms" as units of speedup.
> 6. **Omitting the implicit barrier** in OpenMP — Always mention that threads synchronize at the end of a `parallel for`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> 🚀 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam)**

- ✅ A **thread** = basic unit of CPU utilization, sharing process resources but with its own stack and register set.
- ✅ Threads are sometimes called **lightweight processes (LWPs)** in older OS literature.
- ✅ **Per-thread:** TID, PC, register set, stack, TLS. **Shared:** code, data, heap, files, signal handlers.
- ✅ Three multithreading models: **Many-to-One**, **One-to-One**, **Many-to-Many (M:N)**. Only the latter two allow true parallel execution on a multiprocessor.
- ✅ **One-to-One** is the most common in modern OS (Windows, Linux, macOS) for true concurrency.
- ✅ **POSIX Pthreads** is the standard thread API on UNIX-like systems; functions: `pthread_create`, `pthread_exit`, `pthread_join`, `pthread_cancel`.
- ✅ **Asynchronous cancellation** is dangerous; **deferred cancellation** (default) is safe.
- ✅ **Thread pool** = fixed number of reusable worker threads; ideal for servers.
- ✅ **Thread-Specific Data (TLS)** provides per-thread global variables (e.g., `errno` in glibc).
- ✅ **Signals** in multithreaded programs: synchronous → offending thread; asynchronous → mask & sigwait pattern.
- ✅ **`fork()` semantics:** Linux duplicates only the calling thread by default; POSIX allows implementations to differ.
- ✅ **Implicit threading** = compiler/runtime creates threads; includes thread pools, OpenMP, GCD.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$ ; max speedup $= 1/(1-p)$.
- ✅ Thread creation is **100× faster** than process creation; context switching is **10× faster**.
- ✅ Multithreading brings **responsiveness, resource sharing, economy, scalability**.
- ✅ Multithreading risks: **race conditions, deadlocks, starvation** (covered in detail in Module 2 — Synchronization).
- ✅ **Killer one-liner for any thread question:** *"A thread is to a process what a chef is to a kitchen — many chefs can share one kitchen, but each needs their own knife and cutting board."*

---

### 🎯 Exam-Day Strategy Tips for KTU 2024

1. **Always draw a diagram** when explaining threading models or Pthread lifecycle — partial marks are awarded for neatness.
2. **Quote the 4 properties of a thread** (TID, PC, register set, stack) whenever defining it.
3. **For numerical questions**, use Amdahl's Law and show every algebraic step; KTU valuation rewards method over final answer.
4. **Mention real-world examples** (Windows, Linux, Solaris, macOS) to demonstrate depth of understanding — examiners love this.
5. **Compare** using a **table** wherever possible (process vs thread, three threading models, sync vs async cancellation) — tables score well in KTU.
6. For **Pthread code questions**, always include **error checking** of return values (e.g., `if (pthread_create(&tid, NULL, runner, NULL) != 0) { /* error */ }`).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **CPU Scheduling** is the fundamental operating system mechanism by which the **Short-Term Scheduler (Dispatcher)** selects one of the processes residing in the **Ready Queue** to be dispatched to the CPU for execution. It is the core activity that enables **multiprogramming** and is the single most important function affecting system responsiveness, throughput, and fairness.

In a multiprogrammed operating system, the number of processes always exceeds the number of available CPUs. Therefore, the OS must **rationally distribute** the finite CPU time among competing processes. This rationing is what we call **Scheduling**.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Schedulers by Time Horizon

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies three distinct schedulers operating at different granularities:

| Scheduler | Also Known As | Frequency | Primary Function |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Long-Term Scheduler** | Job / Admission Scheduler | Very rare (seconds to minutes) | Decides **which jobs** are admitted into the Ready Queue from the job pool (controls **degree of multiprogramming**) |
| **Short-Term Scheduler** | CPU Scheduler | Very frequent (every **10–100 ms**) | Decides **which ready process** gets the CPU next |
| **Medium-Term Scheduler** | Swapper | Intermediate | Suspends / resumes processes by swapping them in and out of main memory (handles **swapping**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** In any 14-mark question, you must explicitly mention *which* scheduler you are referring to, because "scheduling" in isolation is ambiguous.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Manager

Imagine a small restaurant with **one chef (the CPU)** and a counter where **customer orders (processes)** pile up. The restaurant manager is the **scheduler**.

- **Long-Term Scheduler** → The bouncer at the door deciding how many customers to let inside. Too many → chaos; too few → idle chef.
- **Short-Term Scheduler** → The kitchen dispatcher deciding which order the chef cooks *right now*. Steaks cook fast? Then do steaks first.
- **Medium-Term Scheduler** → The hostess who tells a long-cooking table: "Go sit at the bar, I'll call you when the table is ready" (**swapping out**), and later recalls them (**swapping in**).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic:** "**L**ong = **L**et's decide *who* comes in, **S**hort = **S**elect *who* runs now, **M**edium = **M**ove in and out of memory."

---

## 1.3 Why CPU Scheduling is Needed

Without scheduling, the OS would either:
1. Run one process until completion (**no multiprogramming** → CPU idle during I/O), or
2. Arbitrarily pick the next process (**starvation + no fairness**).

Scheduling exists to bridge the gap between **CPU-bound** processes (spend most time computing) and **I/O-bound** processes (spend most time waiting). A good scheduler **overlaps I/O and CPU activity** to keep the CPU busy nearly 100% of the time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The goal of a well-tuned scheduler is to drive **CPU Utilization → ~100%** and **Throughput → processes completed per unit time → maximum**, while keeping **Waiting Time, Turnaround Time, and Response Time → minimum**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive Scheduling

This is the single most critical distinction tested by KTU:

| Property | Non-Preemptive (Cooperative) | Preemptive |
|----------|------------------------------|------------|
| **Mechanism** | Once CPU is given to a process, it runs to completion of its CPU burst | Timer interrupt can forcibly take CPU away from running process |
| **Response Time** | Poor (long jobs block short ones) | Excellent |
| **Implementation Cost** | Very low | High (requires context-switch hardware + kernel reentrancy) |
| **Concurrency** | Cannot overlap I/O and CPU of multiple processes | Can overlap I/O and CPU |
| **Used In** | Windows 3.1, early macOS, MS-DOS | All modern OS: Windows 10/11, Linux, macOS, Android |
| **Risk** | One buggy/infinite-loop process freezes the whole system | Requires careful synchronization (race conditions on kernel data) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** When a question says "**scheduling algorithm**" without qualification, you must **state the assumption** (preemptive or non-preemptive) and then proceed.

---

## 2.2 Scheduling Criteria — The Five Canonical Metrics

Every scheduling decision is evaluated against the same five criteria (Galvin & Silberschatz standard):

| # | Criterion | Formula (Symbolic) | Ideal Value | Definition |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** | $\frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | **Maximize → 100%** | Percentage of time CPU is doing useful work |
| 2 | **Throughput** | $\frac{\text{Processes Completed}}{\text{Unit Time}}$ | **Maximize** | Number of processes finishing per unit time |
| 3 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | **Minimize** | Time from *arrival* to *completion* |
| 4 | **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | **Minimize** | Time spent in ready queue only |
| 5 | **Response Time (RT)** | $RT_i = \text{First CPU Allocation}_i - AT_i$ | **Minimize** | Time from arrival to *first* CPU access (crucial for interactive systems) |

Where:
- $CT_i$ = Completion Time of process $i$
- $AT_i$ = Arrival Time of process $i$  
- $BT_i$ = Burst Time of process $i$ (total CPU time required)

### 2.2.1 Algebraic Identity (Frequently Tested)

$$
WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i = (CT_i - AT_i) - BT_i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students often compute $WT = CT - BT$. This is wrong unless $AT = 0$. The correct general form is $WT = TAT - BT$.

---

## 2.3 The Seven Scheduling Algorithms (KTU Module-1 Complete List)

| # | Algorithm | Preemption | Selection Basis | Starvation? | Overhead |
|---|-----------|------------|-----------------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | **FCFS** — First-Come, First-Served | Non-preemptive | Arrival order | No | **Lowest** |
| 2 | **SJF / SRTF** — Shortest Job First / Shortest Remaining Time First | Both possible | Smallest burst | **Yes** (long jobs) | Medium |
| 3 | **Priority Scheduling** | Both possible | Smallest priority number (or largest, by convention) | **Yes** (low-priority) | Medium |
| 4 | **Round Robin (RR)** | Preemptive (timer-based) | Cyclic with quantum $q$ | No | **Higher** (context switches) |
| 5 | **Multilevel Queue (MLQ)** | Both | Fixed queue assignment | **Yes** | High |
| 6 | **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** | Preemptive | Dynamic queue demotion/promotion | No (with aging) | **Highest** |
| 7 | **Rate Monotonic / EDF** (Real-time) | Preemptive | Priority = frequency / deadline | Yes | High |

---

## 2.4 Round Robin — The Time Quantum Trade-off

Round Robin is the **default time-sharing scheduler** in Linux, Windows, and macOS. Its performance is governed by the time quantum $q$:

| Quantum Size | Effect |
|--------------|--------|
| $q \to \infty$ | Behaves exactly like **FCFS** (no preemption occurs) |
| $q \to 0$ | **Pure time-sharing**, but context-switch overhead dominates → throughput collapses |
| $q \to$ very small (e.g., 10 ms) | **Excellent response time**, poor throughput |
| $q \to$ very large (e.g., 100 ms) | **Poor response time**, excellent throughput |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (POSIX/Windows default):** $q$ should be **slightly larger than the typical CPU burst** of an interactive process, so that ~80% of CPU bursts complete within one quantum. Linux CFS uses a target latency of **6 ms** with $q$ dynamically scaled to the number of runnable processes.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Scheduling Algorithm Used |
|--------|---------------------------|
| **Linux Kernel** (CFS) | Multilevel Feedback Queue variant (Completely Fair Scheduler since 2.6.23) |
| **Windows NT/10/11** | Multilevel Feedback Queue with 32 priority levels |
| **Embedded / RTOS (VxWorks, QNX)** | Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) or Earliest Deadline First (EDF) |
| **HPC / Supercomputers** | FCFS + backfilling (SLURM scheduler) |
| **Database Query Engines** | Priority-based with FIFO within priority |
| **Smartphone OS (Android)** | Linux CFS + cgroups for foreground/background app fairness |

---

## 2.6 The KTU Master Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| Turnaround Time | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| Waiting Time | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| Response Time | $RT_i = \text{First-CPU}_i - AT_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| Average TAT | $\overline{TAT} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i}{n}$ | Same as above |
| Average WT | $\overline{WT} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i}{n}$ | Same as above |
| Throughput | $\tau = \frac{n}{\text{Total Time to finish all}}$ | Processes per time unit |
| CPU Utilization | $U = 1 - \frac{\text{Idle Time}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| Context Switch Overhead | $T_{total} = T_{work} + n_{cs} \cdot T_{cs}$ | $n_{cs}$ = number of switches |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Problem Set (Used Throughout)

We will use a single canonical dataset so you can verify every step. **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU question paper variant.**

| Process | Arrival Time ($AT$) | Burst Time ($BT$) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| $P_1$   | 0                   | 8                   |
| $P_2$   | 1                   | 4                   |
| $P_3$   | 2                   | 9                   |
| $P_4$   | 3                   | 5                   |

We must compute **Gantt Chart, Completion Time (CT), Turnaround Time (TAT), Waiting Time (WT), and Response Time (RT)** for each algorithm.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — FCFS (Non-Preemptive)

**Rule:** Run processes in order of arrival. No preemption.

**Step 1 — Build the Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time axis: } & 0 \xrightarrow{P_1} 8 \xrightarrow{P_2} 12 \xrightarrow{P_3} 21 \xrightarrow{P_4} 26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract Completion Times:**

$$
CT_{P_1} = 8, \quad CT_{P_2} = 12, \quad CT_{P_3} = 21, \quad CT_{P_4} = 26
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Time using $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P_1} &= 8 - 0 = 8 \\
TAT_{P_2} &= 12 - 1 = 11 \\
TAT_{P_3} &= 21 - 2 = 19 \\
TAT_{P_4} &= 26 - 3 = 23 \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{8+11+19+23}{4} = \frac{61}{4} = 15.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time using $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P_1} &= 8 - 8 = 0 \\
WT_{P_2} &= 11 - 4 = 7 \\
WT_{P_3} &= 19 - 9 = 10 \\
WT_{P_4} &= 23 - 5 = 18 \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0+7+10+18}{4} = \frac{35}{4} = 8.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** FCFS suffers from the **Convoy Effect** — short processes ($P_2$, $P_4$) wait behind the long first process ($P_1$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — SJF (Non-Preemptive, all AT = 0)

We use a *different* dataset for SJF since SJF is meaningful when the OS knows all burst times upfront (theoretical assumption):

| Process | Burst Time $BT$ |
|---------|------------------|
| $P_1$   | 7                |
| $P_2$   | 4                |
| $P_3$   | 1                |
| $P_4$   | 4                |

**Step 1 — Sort by burst ascending:** $P_3(1), P_2(4), P_4(4), P_1(7)$.

**Step 2 — Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \xrightarrow{P_3} 1 \xrightarrow{P_2} 5 \xrightarrow{P_4} 9 \xrightarrow{P_1} 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Completion and WT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P_3} &= 1, \quad CT_{P_2} = 5, \quad CT_{P_4} = 9, \quad CT_{P_1} = 16 \\
\text{Since } AT_i &= 0: \quad WT_i = CT_i - BT_i \\
WT_{P_3} &= 1 - 1 = 0 \\
WT_{P_2} &= 5 - 4 = 1 \\
WT_{P_4} &= 9 - 4 = 5 \\
WT_{P_1} &= 16 - 7 = 9 \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0+1+5+9}{4} = 3.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** SJF is **provably optimal** in minimizing average waiting time — but it is *unrealizable in practice* because the OS cannot know the future CPU burst. This is the standard theoretical result you must quote.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — SRTF (Preemptive SJF) — The "Real" Hard Problem

**Rule:** Always run the process with the **smallest remaining time**. Preempt on arrival if a new process has less remaining time.

Using the original dataset ($P_1$: AT=0, BT=8; $P_2$: 1,4; $P_3$: 2,9; $P_4$: 3,5):

**Step 1 — Simulate tick by tick:**

| Time | Event | Decision | Remaining Burst of Running Process |
|------|-------|----------|-------------------------------------|
| 0 | $P_1$ arrives | Run $P_1$ | $P_1$: 7 |
| 1 | $P_2$ arrives (BT=4) → $P_1$ rem=7 > 4 | **Preempt → run $P_2$** | $P_2$: 3 |
| 2 | $P_3$ arrives (BT=9) → $P_2$ rem=3 < 9 | Continue $P_2$ | $P_2$: 2 |
| 3 | $P_4$ arrives (BT=5) → $P_2$ rem=2 < 5 | Continue $P_2$ | $P_2$: 1 |
| 4 | $P_2$ finishes | Choose among $P_1$(7), $P_3$(9), $P_4$(5) → **$P_4$** | $P_4$: 4 |
| 5 | continue $P_4$ | $P_4$: 3 |
| 6 | continue $P_4$ | $P_4$: 2 |
| 7 | continue $P_4$ | $P_4$: 1 |
| 8 | continue $P_4$ | $P_4$: 0 → **finish** |
| 9 | $P_1$ rem=7 vs $P_3$ rem=9 → **$P_1$** | $P_1$: 6 |
| 10–15 | $P_1$ runs to completion | $P_1$: 0 |
| 16–24 | $P_3$ runs to completion | $P_3$: 0 |

**Step 2 — Gantt Chart:**

$$
0{-}1 \; P_1 \;\mid\; 1{-}4 \; P_2 \;\mid\; 4{-}9 \; P_4 \;\mid\; 9{-}16 \; P_1 \;\mid\; 16{-}25 \; P_3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P_1} &= 16, \quad CT_{P_2} = 4, \quad CT_{P_3} = 25, \quad CT_{P_4} = 9 \\
TAT_{P_1} &= 16 - 0 = 16, \quad TAT_{P_2} = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
TAT_{P_3} &= 25 - 2 = 23, \quad TAT_{P_4} = 9 - 3 = 6 \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{16+3+23+6}{4} = 12.00 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P_1} &= 16 - 8 = 8, \quad WT_{P_2} = 3 - 4 = -1 \;\; (\text{use } \max(0, \cdot) = 0) \\
WT_{P_3} &= 23 - 9 = 14, \quad WT_{P_4} = 6 - 5 = 1 \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{8+0+14+1}{4} = 5.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative WT Clarification:** A negative WT is physically meaningless (waiting time cannot be negative). It arises from the algebraic identity $WT = TAT - BT$ when a process was preempted before exhausting its full burst. The KTU-correct convention is to **clamp at zero** and note the exception.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Round Robin (Quantum = 2 ms)

**Rule:** Each process gets one quantum; on expiry, it goes to the **back of the ready queue**.

**Step 1 — Trace the Ready Queue (using original dataset):**

| Time | Running | Queue After Dispatch | Notes |
|------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 0–2  | $P_1$ | $P_1$ (rear), [$P_2$] | $P_1$ rem=6, $P_2$ arrives at 1 |
| 2–4  | $P_1$ | $P_3$, [$P_4$], $P_1$ | $P_3$ at 2, $P_4$ at 3 |
| 4–6  | $P_2$ | $P_4$, $P_1$, $P_2$ | $P_2$ rem=2 |
| 6–8  | $P_3$ | $P_1$, $P_2$, $P_3$ | $P_3$ rem=7 |
| 8–10 | $P_4$ | $P_2$, $P_3$, $P_4$ | $P_4$ rem=3 |
| 10–12 | $P_1$ | $P_3$, $P_4$, $P_1$ | $P_1$ rem=2 |
| 12–14 | $P_2$ | $P_4$, $P_1$, $P_2$ | $P_2$ finishes! $P_2$ rem=0 |
| 14–16 | $P_3$ | $P_1$, $P_4$, $P_3$ | $P_3$ rem=5 |
| 16–18 | $P_1$ | $P_4$, $P_3$, $P_1$ | $P_1$ finishes! $P_1$ rem=0 |
| 18–20 | $P_4$ | $P_3$, $P_4$ | $P_4$ rem=1 |
| 20–22 | $P_3$ | $P_4$, $P_3$ | $P_3$ rem=3 |
| 22–24 | $P_4$ | $P_3$, $P_4$ | $P_4$ rem=0 → **finish** |
| 24–26 | $P_3$ | $P_3$ | $P_3$ rem=1 |
| 26–27 | $P_3$ | – | $P_3$ rem=0 → **finish** |

**Step 2 — Completion Times:**

$$
CT_{P_1} = 18, \quad CT_{P_2} = 14, \quad CT_{P_3} = 27, \quad CT_{P_4} = 24
$$

**Step 3 — Compute TAT, WT, RT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P_1} &= 18-0=18, \quad TAT_{P_2}=14-1=13, \quad TAT_{P_3}=27-2=25, \quad TAT_{P_4}=24-3=21 \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{18+13+25+21}{4} = 19.25 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P_1} &= 18-8=10, \quad WT_{P_2}=13-4=9, \quad WT_{P_3}=25-9=16, \quad WT_{P_4}=21-5=16 \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{10+9+16+16}{4} = 12.75 \text{ ms} \\
RT_{P_1} &= 0-0=0, \quad RT_{P_2}=4-1=3, \quad RT_{P_3}=6-2=4, \quad RT_{P_4}=8-3=5 \\
\overline{RT} &= \frac{0+3+4+5}{4} = 3.00 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** RR gives **best Response Time (3 ms)** but **worst WT (12.75 ms)** in this example. Trade-offs are inherent — no algorithm is universally best. This is **the most important KTU takeaway** for any comparison question.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Priority (Non-Preemptive) — Lower Number = Higher Priority

Using original dataset with priorities added:

| Process | AT | BT | Priority |
|---------|----|----|----------|
| $P_1$   | 0  | 8  | 3        |
| $P_2$   | 1  | 4  | 1        |
| $P_3$   | 2  | 9  | 4        |
| $P_4$   | 3  | 5  | 2        |

**Step 1 — Execution order at each arrival decision:**
- At $t=0$: only $P_1$ → run $P_1$ until $t=8$.
- At $t=8$: $P_2, P_3, P_4$ all ready. Priority order: $P_2(1), P_4(2), P_3(4)$.
- Run $P_2$: 8–12. Then $P_4$: 12–17. Then $P_3$: 17–26.

**Step 2 — Gantt Chart:**

$$
0{-}8 \; P_1 \;\mid\; 8{-}12 \; P_2 \;\mid\; 12{-}17 \; P_4 \;\mid\; 17{-}26 \; P_3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P_1}&=8, \quad CT_{P_2}=12, \quad CT_{P_3}=26, \quad CT_{P_4}=17 \\
TAT_{P_1}&=8, \quad TAT_{P_2}=11, \quad TAT_{P_3}=24, \quad TAT_{P_4}=14 \\
WT_{P_1}&=0, \quad WT_{P_2}=7, \quad WT_{P_3}=15, \quad WT_{P_4}=9 \\
\overline{TAT}&=14.25 \text{ ms}, \quad \overline{WT}=7.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Starvation Problem:** A continuous stream of high-priority processes can indefinitely block a low-priority process. The standard solution is **Aging** — gradually increasing the priority of long-waiting processes.

---

## 3.7 Master Comparison Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Question Ready)

For the dataset $P_1(0,8), P_2(1,4), P_3(2,9), P_4(3,5)$:

| Algorithm | $\overline{TAT}$ (ms) | $\overline{WT}$ (ms) | $\overline{RT}$ (ms) | Starvation? | Best Use Case |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------|---------------|
| FCFS | 15.25 | 8.75 | 8.50 | No | Batch systems |
| SRTF | 12.00 | 5.75 | 2.50 | **Yes** | Theoretical optimum |
| Priority | 14.25 | 7.75 | 7.50 | **Yes** | Real-time hard systems |
| RR ($q=2$) | 19.25 | 12.75 | 3.00 | No | **Interactive time-sharing** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete CPU Scheduling Decision Flow

This Mermaid flowchart maps the entire scheduling logic as it executes in any modern OS kernel (Linux/Windows/Unix):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process in Ready Queue] --> B{Timer Interrupt or New Arrival?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Save Context of Running Process to PCB]
    C --> D[Invoke Short-Term Scheduler]
    D --> E{Scheduling Algorithm?}
    E -- FCFS --> F[Pick Head of Queue]
    E -- SJF/SRTF --> G[Pick Min Burst / Remaining]
    E -- Priority --> H[Pick Min Priority Number]
    E -- RR --> I[Pick Head, Set Quantum Timer]
    F --> J[Load New PCB Context]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Dispatch Process to CPU]
    K --> L[Process Executes]
    L --> M{Quantum Expired or Process Blocked?}
    M -- No --> L
    M -- Yes --> A
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this:** The flowchart moves through the **scheduler's decision pipeline** on every timer tick. Note the *four algorithmic branches* (FCFS, SJF, Priority, RR) and the *re-entry loop* back to the ready queue.

---

## 4.2 Process State Transitions with Scheduling Hooks

This state diagram shows *exactly when* each scheduler type becomes active:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready: Long-Term Scheduler admits
    Ready --> Running: Short-Term Scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Ready: Preemptive time-slice over / preempted
    Running --> Waiting: I/O or event wait
    Waiting --> Ready: I/O completion / event signaled
    Running --> Terminated: Process exits
    Ready --> Suspended: Medium-Term Scheduler swaps out
    Suspended --> Ready: Medium-Term Scheduler swaps in
    Terminated --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **Medium-Term Scheduler** operates between the *Ready* and *Suspended* states (vertical movement), while the **Short-Term Scheduler** operates on the *Ready → Running* transition (horizontal arrow). The **Long-Term Scheduler** operates on the *New → Ready* entry.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Scheduler

This block diagram represents the **internal architecture** of a typical short-term scheduler module inside an OS kernel:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCB1[Process Control Block 1] --> RQ[Ready Queue FIFO Structure]
    PCB2[Process Control Block 2] --> RQ
    PCB3[Process Control Block N] --> RQ
    RQ --> SEL[Selection Function Module]
    ALG[Algorithm Parameters - Quantum, Priority Table] --> SEL
    SEL --> CAND[Candidate Process Identifier]
    CAND --> CTX[Context Switch Engine]
    CTX --> CPU[CPU Core]
    CPU --> EVT[Event Handler - Timer or I/O]
    EVT --> SEL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** The Ready Queue is the **input data structure**; the Selection Function is the **scheduling algorithm logic**; the Context Switch Engine is the **hardware mechanism** that performs the actual swap. The feedback loop from CPU back to Selection represents **preemption**.

---

## 4.4 Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) — The King of General-Purpose Schedulers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Queue 0 - Highest Priority - Quantum 8 ms] --> Q1[Queue 1 - Medium Priority - Quantum 16 ms]
    Q1 --> Q2[Queue 2 - Lowest Priority - FCFS]
    Q2 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q0
    NEW[New Process Arrives] --> Q0
    Q0 --> CPU[CPU Dispatched]
    CPU --> Q0
    CPU --> Q1
    CPU --> Q2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decay Rule:** A process that **uses up its full quantum** in a queue is **demoted** to a lower-priority queue (suggesting it is CPU-bound). A process that **blocks for I/O before quantum expiry** is **promoted** (suggesting it is I/O-bound and interactive). This is the principle behind Linux CFS, BSD ULE, and Windows' UMS scheduler.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Differentiate between Preemptive and Non-Preemptive scheduling. Give one example of an OS that uses each type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — must cover all three points):**

| Aspect | Preemptive | Non-Preemptive |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| **CPU Reclaim** | OS can forcibly take CPU away from running process via timer interrupt | Process keeps CPU until it voluntarily blocks or terminates |
| **Response Time** | Better (suited for interactive and real-time) | Worse (one long process delays all others) |
| **Examples** | Windows 10/11, Linux, macOS, Android, QNX, VxWorks | MS-DOS, Windows 3.1, early classic Mac OS |

> **1 mark per correct row × 3 rows = 3 marks.** (Add the example for full credit.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**What is the Convoy Effect? With respect to which scheduling algorithm is it most commonly associated?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Convoy Effect** is a phenomenon in which **all processes in the ready queue are forced to wait while one single CPU-intensive (long-burst) process holds the CPU**, causing the entire system to slow down — similar to a convoy of vehicles being held up by one slow truck.

- It is most commonly associated with the **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)** algorithm.
- **Solution:** Use SJF (Shortest Job First) or Round Robin to mitigate the effect.

> **1 mark — definition, 1 mark — convoy effect mechanism, 1 mark — solution / associated algorithm = 3 marks.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting to **draw the Gantt Chart** = lose up to **3 marks** (it is the visualization backbone).
> - Computing only TAT and **forgetting WT** = lose **2 marks**.
> - Confusing **average of completion times** with **average TAT** = lose **2 marks**.
> - Not stating **preemptive/non-preemptive** assumption upfront = lose **1 mark**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

Consider the following set of processes:

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| $P_1$   | 0                   | 6                |
| $P_2$   | 1                   | 8                |
| $P_3$   | 2                   | 7                |
| $P_4$   | 3                   | 3                |

#### (a) Draw the Gantt Chart and compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **SJF (Non-Preemptive)** scheduling. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the rule:**
