topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Priority Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Priority Scheduling** is a non-primitive CPU scheduling algorithm in which each process is assigned a **priority value**, and the CPU is allocated to the process with the **highest priority** among the processes currently in the **ready queue**. In case of a tie, the scheduler typically resolves it using **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)** ordering.

A process's priority can be represented in two conventional styles:

- **Lower number = Higher priority** (commonly used in KTU textbook problems and most academic notations, e.g., priority 0 > priority 5).
- **Higher number = Higher priority** (used in some commercial OS like Windows).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Priority Scheduling is fundamentally categorized into two variants:
> 1. **Non-Preemptive Priority Scheduling** — Once a process acquires the CPU, it runs to **completion** before the scheduler reconsiders priorities.
> 2. **Preemptive Priority Scheduling** — If a newly arriving process has a **higher priority** than the currently running process, the CPU is **immediately preempted** and handed to the new arrival. This variant is also sometimes referred to as **Priority Scheduling with Preemption** or **Shortest-Remaining-Time-First** when priority is inversely mapped to burst time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Emergency Room"

Imagine you walk into a hospital emergency room. Patients keep arriving continuously. There is only **one doctor**. Now, the doctor does **not** simply treat whoever came first (FCFS), nor whoever has the shortest treatment (SJF). Instead, the doctor triages patients and assigns each a **priority level** based on the severity of their condition:

- A heart-attack patient gets **Priority 1** (treated immediately).
- A fracture patient gets **Priority 3**.
- A mild fever gets **Priority 5**.

The doctor (the **scheduler**) always picks the patient with the **highest priority** (smallest number in our convention). If a new critical patient arrives while the doctor is treating a fracture, the doctor **stops mid-treatment** (preemption) and attends to the critical case.

This is exactly how Priority Scheduling works in an OS kernel. The "doctor" is the **CPU**, and the "patients" are the **processes** in the ready queue.

## 1.3 Classification of Priorities

| Basis of Classification | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin of priority value** | **Internal Priority** — derived from measurable process metrics like memory size, CPU burst time, I/O-to-CPU ratio, file descriptors opened. | **External Priority** — assigned by criteria outside the OS, such as user importance, process type (system vs user), political/funding considerations. |
| **When priority is decided** | **Static Priority** — assigned once at process creation and never changes. | **Dynamic Priority** — recomputed at runtime by the OS to improve fairness or response characteristics. |

## 1.4 The Critical Problem: **Starvation**

> [!WARNING]
> **Starvation (also called Indefinite Blocking)** is the canonical failure mode of Priority Scheduling. A low-priority process may **never** get CPU time if higher-priority processes keep arriving indefinitely. It is conceptually the opposite of "Deadlock" — instead of all waiting on each other, *one* process is perpetually ignored.

The classical remedy is **Aging** — gradually increasing the priority of a process the longer it waits in the ready queue, ensuring that even low-priority processes eventually become high-priority and get scheduled.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decision Flow

The priority scheduler maintains a **ready queue** and a **CPU dispatcher**. The following logic governs both variants:

### Non-Preemptive Priority Scheduling
1. At time $t = 0$, scan all arrived processes. Pick the one with the **highest priority**. Dispatch to CPU.
2. The chosen process runs **continuously until its burst is exhausted** (preemption is disabled).
3. When the process terminates (or issues I/O), increment time to its completion. Re-scan the ready queue.
4. If multiple processes share the same priority, break the tie using **arrival time (FCFS)**.
5. Repeat from Step 1 until the ready queue is empty.

### Preemptive Priority Scheduling
1. At time $t = 0$, dispatch the highest-priority arrived process.
2. At **every arrival event** (and every termination), check whether the newly arrived process has a **strictly higher priority** than the currently running process.
3. If yes → **preempt**: save the state of the running process, push it back to the ready queue, and dispatch the new arrival.
4. If no → continue executing the current process.
5. On termination, scan the ready queue and dispatch the highest-priority process among those waiting.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** The *only* operational difference between the two variants is **Step 2-3** of the preemptive algorithm. If you forget the preemption check, you will silently solve the wrong variant and lose full marks.

## 2.2 Performance Metrics — The KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Notation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Completion Time (CT)** | $CT_i$ | Time at which process $i$ finishes its last CPU burst | ms / s |
| 2 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | Time from arrival to completion | ms / s |
| 3 | **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | Time spent in the ready queue | ms / s |
| 4 | **Response Time (RT)** | $RT_i = \text{First CPU Allocation} - AT_i$ | Time until first execution | ms / s |
| 5 | **Average WT** | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | Mean waiting time across all $n$ processes | ms / s |
| 6 | **Average TAT** | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | Mean turnaround time | ms / s |
| 7 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Processes completed per unit time | processes / s |
| 8 | **CPU Utilization** | $\text{CPU Util.} = \dfrac{\sum BT_i}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of time CPU is busy | % |
| 9 | **Starvation Indicator** | $\lim_{t \to \infty} WT_{\text{low-prio}} = \infty$ | Bounded iff Aging is enabled | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU problems, the symbol $AT$ denotes **Arrival Time** and $BT$ denotes **Burst Time**. Use them consistently to avoid losing clarity marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Priority Scheduling is the **backbone of real-time operating systems (RTOS)**. In hard real-time systems like:
- **Avionics (Fly-By-Wire)** — flight control processes have higher priority than in-flight entertainment.
- **Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS)** — wheel-speed sensing and braking actuation must preempt all lower tasks.
- **Industrial Robotics & SCADA** — safety interlocks and emergency-stop handlers sit at the top of the priority ladder.
- **Modern Mobile SoCs (Qualcomm, Apple Silicon)** — big.LITTLE schedulers use priority hints to map critical foreground apps onto high-performance cores.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Non-Preemptive Priority Scheduling

Consider the following process set with **lower number = higher priority**:

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 4 | 3 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 1 | 4 |
| $P_4$ | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| $P_5$ | 4 | 2 | 5 |

### Step 1 — Build the Gantt Chart by tracing time

**At $t = 0$:** Only $P_1$ has arrived. Dispatch $P_1$.

**At $t = 4$:** $P_1$ finishes. Ready queue contains $P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5$. Pick the one with smallest priority number → $P_2$ (priority 1). Dispatch $P_2$.

**At $t = 7$:** $P_2$ finishes. Ready queue contains $P_3, P_4, P_5$. Pick smallest priority → $P_4$ (priority 2). Dispatch $P_4$.

**At $t = 12$:** $P_4$ finishes. Ready queue contains $P_3, P_5$. Pick smallest priority → $P_3$ (priority 4). Dispatch $P_3$.

**At $t = 13$:** $P_3$ finishes. Only $P_5$ remains. Dispatch $P_5$.

**At $t = 15$:** All processes complete.

The resulting Gantt chart is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } \rightarrow \quad & \underset{0}{P_1} \;\; \underset{4}{P_2} \;\; \underset{7}{P_4} \;\; \underset{12}{P_3} \;\; \underset{13}{P_5} \;\; \underset{15}{} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Completion Times (CT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P_1} &= 4 \\
CT_{P_2} &= 7 \\
CT_{P_3} &= 13 \\
CT_{P_4} &= 12 \\
CT_{P_5} &= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Times using $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P_1} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \\
TAT_{P_2} &= 7 - 1 = 6 \\
TAT_{P_3} &= 13 - 2 = 11 \\
TAT_{P_4} &= 12 - 3 = 9 \\
TAT_{P_5} &= 15 - 4 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Waiting Times using $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P_1} &= 4 - 4 = 0 \\
WT_{P_2} &= 6 - 3 = 3 \\
WT_{P_3} &= 11 - 1 = 10 \\
WT_{P_4} &= 9 - 5 = 4 \\
WT_{P_5} &= 11 - 2 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Averages

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{4 + 6 + 11 + 9 + 11}{5} = \frac{41}{5} = 8.2 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 3 + 10 + 4 + 9}{5} = \frac{26}{5} = 5.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Preemptive Priority Scheduling (Same Process Set)

Now we re-trace the **same** process table but with **preemption enabled**.

**At $t = 0$:** $P_1$ arrives. Dispatch $P_1$.

**At $t = 1$:** $P_2$ arrives with priority 1, which is *higher* than $P_1$'s priority 3. **Preempt $P_1$** (remaining burst = 3). Dispatch $P_2$.

**At $t = 2$:** $P_3$ arrives with priority 4. Current $P_2$ has priority 1 (higher). **Continue $P_2$.**

**At $t = 3$:** $P_4$ arrives with priority 2. Current $P_2$ has priority 1 (higher). **Continue $P_2$.**

**At $t = 4$:** $P_5$ arrives with priority 5. $P_2$ finishes exactly now. Ready queue: $P_1$ (rem 3, prio 3), $P_3$ (rem 1, prio 4), $P_4$ (rem 5, prio 2), $P_5$ (rem 2, prio 5). Highest priority → $P_4$ (2). Dispatch $P_4$.

**At $t = 9$:** $P_4$ finishes. Ready queue: $P_1$ (prio 3), $P_3$ (prio 4), $P_5$ (prio 5). Highest → $P_1$. Dispatch $P_1$.

**At $t = 12$:** $P_1$ finishes. Ready queue: $P_3, P_5$. Highest → $P_3$ (prio 4). Dispatch $P_3$.

**At $t = 13$:** $P_3$ finishes. Dispatch $P_5$.

**At $t = 15$:** All complete.

The Gantt chart becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } \rightarrow \quad & \underset{0}{P_1} \;\; \underset{1}{P_2} \;\; \underset{4}{P_4} \;\; \underset{9}{P_1} \;\; \underset{12}{P_3} \;\; \underset{13}{P_5} \;\; \underset{15}{} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice $P_1$ appears in **two disjoint segments** because it was preempted. Now we recompute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P_1} &= 12, \quad CT_{P_2} = 4, \quad CT_{P_3} = 13, \quad CT_{P_4} = 9, \quad CT_{P_5} = 15 \\
TAT_{P_1} &= 12 - 0 = 12 \\
TAT_{P_2} &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
TAT_{P_3} &= 13 - 2 = 11 \\
TAT_{P_4} &= 9 - 3 = 6 \\
TAT_{P_5} &= 15 - 4 = 11 \\
WT_{P_1} &= 12 - 4 = 8 \\
WT_{P_2} &= 3 - 3 = 0 \\
WT_{P_3} &= 11 - 1 = 10 \\
WT_{P_4} &= 6 - 5 = 1 \\
WT_{P_5} &= 11 - 2 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{\text{preempt}} &= \frac{12+3+11+6+11}{5} = \frac{43}{5} = 8.6 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{\text{preempt}} &= \frac{8+0+10+1+9}{5} = \frac{28}{5} = 5.6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive KTU Insight:** In this specific example, the **non-preemptive** variant actually delivered *lower* average waiting time ($5.2$ ms vs $5.6$ ms). This teaches a vital lesson — *preemption is not universally better.* It trades fairness/responsiveness for increased context-switch overhead and, in some workloads, worse aggregate metrics. Always solve both variants before concluding which is optimal for a given trace.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Non-Preemptive Priority | Preemptive Priority |
|---|---|---|
| **Preemption on arrival** | No | Yes, if new process has higher priority |
| **Implementation complexity** | Low | High (needs timer + context save/restore) |
| **Response time of high-priority arrivals** | Poor (waits for current burst) | Excellent (immediate dispatch) |
| **Context switch overhead** | Low | High |
| **Starvation risk** | Yes | Yes (even higher risk) |
| **Used in** | Batch OS, embedded controllers | RTOS, modern mobile/desktop kernels |
| **Can degenerate into FCFS?** | Yes, if all priorities equal | Yes, if all priorities equal |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Decision Flow of the Preemptive Priority Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([OS Boot / Interrupt Event]) --> B[Save context of running process into its PCB]
    B --> C{New process arrived?}
    C -- No --> D{Ready queue empty?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Insert new process into ready queue with its priority]
    E --> F{Newly arrived process priority > currently running priority?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Move running process back to ready queue]
    G --> H[Sort ready queue by priority ascending]
    F -- No --> I[Continue executing current process on CPU]
    H --> J{Pick highest priority process at queue head}
    J --> K[Load its PCB context and dispatch to CPU]
    D -- Yes --> L([CPU Idle / HALT])
    D -- No --> M{Pick highest priority process}
    M --> K
    I --> A
    K --> A
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Gantt Timeline for the Preemptive Worked Example

```mermaid
gantt
    title Preemptive Priority Scheduling Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section CPU Activity
    P1 active segment 1 :p1a, 0, 1
    P2 burst          :p2, 1, 3
    P4 burst          :p4, 4, 5
    P1 active segment 2 :p1b, 9, 3
    P3 burst          :p3, 12, 1
    P5 burst          :p5, 13, 2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Aging Mechanism to Defeat Starvation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Process in Ready Queue] --> T[Wait Timer Increment per Tick]
    T --> R{Wait time exceeds threshold?}
    R -- No --> Q
    R -- Yes --> P[Boost priority by 1 level]
    P --> S[Re-sort Ready Queue]
    S --> Q
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Priority Scheduler Inside the Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserSpace[USER SPACE]
        App1[Application Process 1]
        App2[Application Process 2]
        App3[System Daemon]
    end
    subgraph KernelSpace[KERNEL SPACE]
        subgraph Scheduler[CPU SCHEDULER SUBSYSTEM]
            ArrivalQ[Arrival Event Handler]
            ReadyQ[(Ready Queue sorted by Priority)]
            Dispatcher[Dispatcher Module]
            Aging[Aging Watchdog]
        end
        subgraph HardwareAbstraction[HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER]
            Timer[Programmable Interval Timer]
            ContextSwitch[Context Save and Restore Unit]
            CPU[CPU Core]
        end
    end
    App1 --> ArrivalQ
    App2 --> ArrivalQ
    App3 --> ArrivalQ
    ArrivalQ --> ReadyQ
    Aging --> ReadyQ
    ReadyQ --> Dispatcher
    Dispatcher --> ContextSwitch
    ContextSwitch --> CPU
    Timer --> Scheduler
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

### Q1. Distinguish between preemptive and non-preemptive priority scheduling. State one real-world scenario where each is preferred.
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive |
|---|---|---|
| Preemption on high-priority arrival | Not allowed; current process runs to completion | Allowed; CPU is taken away immediately |
| Response time of urgent tasks | Poor — must wait for current burst to finish | Excellent — dispatched within microseconds |
| Context switch overhead | Low | High |

- **Non-Preemptive Preferred Scenario:** Legacy **batch processing mainframes** (e.g., payroll runs overnight) where jobs are long, predictable, and minimizing context switches matters more than urgency.
- **Preemptive Preferred Scenario:** **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) firmware** in automotive ECUs, where a critical wheel-slip interrupt must override any ongoing actuator routine immediately to prevent skidding.

---

### Q2. What is **starvation** in priority scheduling? How does **aging** solve it?
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Starvation** is the indefinite postponement of a low-priority process's execution because higher-priority processes keep arriving and monopolizing the CPU. The starved process is *ready* but never gets dispatched, and theoretically $\lim_{t \to \infty} WT_{\text{low}} = \infty$.

**Aging** is a remedy where the OS gradually increases the priority of a waiting process based on its accumulated wait time. For example, every $T$ seconds of waiting, the process's priority value is decremented by 1 (if lower means higher priority). Eventually, even the most neglected process attains a high enough priority to be scheduled, guaranteeing **bounded waiting** and progress. Modern Linux CFS and Windows kernels implement aging-like heuristics such as **nice value adjustments** and **priority boost for sleeping tasks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Re-Modeled for 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following processes. Draw the Gantt chart and compute **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** for **non-preemptive** priority scheduling. (Assume lower priority number = higher priority.)

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 5 | 4 |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| $P_3$ | 3 | 2 | 6 |
| $P_4$ | 5 | 3 | 1 |
| $P_5$ | 6 | 1 | 5 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Repeat the problem using **preemptive** priority scheduling and compare the two results in a table. Comment on which variant performs better in this trace and why.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Non-Preemptive Solution**

**Gantt Construction (trace):**

- $t = 0$: Only $P_1$ available → Dispatch $P_1$.
- $t = 5$: $P_1$ finishes. Ready queue: $P_2$ (2), $P_3$ (6), $P_4$ (1), $P_5$ (5). Highest priority (smallest number) → $P_4$ (1). Dispatch $P_4$.
- $t = 8$: $P_4$ finishes. Ready queue: $P_2$ (2), $P_3$ (6), $P_5$ (5). Highest → $P_2$ (2). Dispatch $P_2$.
- $t = 12$: $P_2$ finishes. Ready queue: $P_3$ (6), $P_5$ (5). Highest → $P_5$ (5). Dispatch $P_5$.
- $t = 13$: $P_5$ finishes. Dispatch $P_3$.
- $t = 15$: $P_3$ finishes.

**Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{0}{P_1} \;\; \underset{5}{P_4} \;\; \underset{8}{P_2} \;\; \underset{12}{P_5} \;\; \underset{13}{P_3} \;\; \underset{15}{} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Completion, TAT, and WT Table:**

| Process | AT | BT | Prio | CT | TAT = CT − AT | WT = TAT − BT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 5 | **0** |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 4 | 2 | 12 | 10 | **6** |
| $P_3$ | 3 | 2 | 6 | 15 | 12 | **10** |
| $P_4$ | 5 | 3 | 1 | 8 | 3 | **0** |
| $P_5$ | 6 | 1 | 5 | 13 | 7 | **6** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0+6+10+0+6}{5} = \frac{22}{5} = 4.4 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{5+10+12+3+7}{5} = \frac{37}{5} = 7.4 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Preemptive Solution**

Re-trace checking preemption at each arrival:

- $t = 0$ → $P_1$ starts.
- $t = 2$: $P_2$ arrives (prio 2 < $P_1$'s 4). **Preempt $P_1$** (rem = 3). Start $P_2$.
- $t = 3$: $P_3$ arrives (prio 6 > $P_2$'s 2). **Continue $P_2$.**
- $t = 5$: $P_4$ arrives (prio 1 < $P_2$'s 2). **Preempt $P_2$** (rem = 1). Start $P_4$.
- $t = 6$: $P_5$ arrives (prio 5 > $P_4$'s 1). **Continue $P_4$.**
- $t = 8$: $P_4$ finishes. Ready: $P_1$ (3, prio 4), $P_2$ (1, prio 2), $P_3$ (2, prio 6), $P_5$ (1, prio 5). Highest → $P_2$. Start $P_2$.
- $t = 9$: $P_2$ finishes. Ready: $P_1$ (prio 4), $P_3$ (prio 6), $P_5$ (prio 5). Highest → $P_1$. Start $P_1$.
- $t = 12$: $P_1$ finishes. Ready: $P_3, P_5$. Highest → $P_5$ (prio 5). Start $P_5$.
- $t = 13$: $P_5$ finishes. Start $P_3$.
- $t = 15$: All done.

**Gantt Chart (Preemptive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{0}{P_1} \;\; \underset{2}{P_2} \;\; \underset{5}{P_4} \;\; \underset{8}{P_2} \;\; \underset{9}{P_1} \;\; \underset{12}{P_5} \;\; \underset{13}{P_3} \;\; \underset{15}{} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations:**

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 5 | 12 | 12 | **7** |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 4 | 9 | 7 | **3** |
| $P_3$ | 3 | 2 | 15 | 12 | **10** |
| $P_4$ | 5 | 3 | 8 | 3 | **0** |
| $P_5$ | 6 | 1 | 13 | 7 | **6** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT}_{\text{preempt}} &= \frac{7+3+10+0+6}{5} = \frac{26}{5} = 5.2 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{TAT}_{\text{preempt}} &= \frac{12+7+12+3+7}{5} = \frac{41}{5} = 8.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Avg. WT | 4.4 ms | 5.2 ms | **Non-Preemptive** |
| Avg. TAT | 7.4 ms | 8.2 ms | **Non-Preemptive** |
| Context switches | 4 | 7 | Non-Preemptive |
| Response of high-prio arrivals | Delayed | Immediate | Preemptive |

**Comment:** In this trace, the non-preemptive variant outperforms the preemptive variant on aggregate metrics because $P_1$ and $P_2$ were unnecessarily interrupted, and the saved context-switch cycles did not translate into better throughput. However, the preemptive version provided a **faster response for $P_4$** (the highest-priority process), which is the genuine purpose of priority scheduling — to honor urgency, not necessarily to minimize average waiting time.

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **internal** and **external** priorities with two examples each. Why are static priorities considered fragile in long-running systems?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider 4 processes with the following data. Draw the Gantt chart and calculate **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **preemptive** priority scheduling (lower number = higher priority).

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 0 | 6 | 3 |
| $B$ | 2 | 3 | 1 |
| $C$ | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| $D$ | 6 | 4 | 2 |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Internal vs External Priorities (7 Marks)**

**Internal Priorities** are derived from intrinsic, measurable characteristics of the process itself. The OS computes them algorithmically.

- *Example 1:* Time-limit / CPU-burst history — processes with historically short bursts get higher priority (this is the foundation of the **Multi-Level Feedback Queue**, which approximates SJF without knowing future bursts).
- *Example 2:* Memory footprint — small processes get higher priority to maximize the resident set.
- *Example 3:* I/O-bound vs CPU-bound — I/O-bound processes are boosted to keep I/O devices saturated.

**External Priorities** are imposed by criteria outside the runtime characteristics of the process — typically by system administrators, funding, security policy, or end-user importance.

- *Example 1:* The **root-owned** system daemons (e.g., `init`, `kthreadd`) get higher priority than user applications.
- *Example 2:* In a **hospital information system**, the "ICU Monitor" process gets externally assigned priority 1, while "Billing" gets priority 9.
- *Example 3:* Hard real-time deadlines — a flight-control task is externally classified as **safety-critical**.

**Why static priorities are fragile:**

In long-running systems (servers, kiosks, HPC clusters), a static priority assignment made at process birth **cannot adapt** to shifting workload patterns. If a once-important process is assigned high priority at startup, it will forever preempt newer, more critical tasks. The result is **priority inversion cascades**, **starvation of new arrivals**, and inability to respond to changing operational demands. Dynamic priority schemes (like Linux's CFS, BSD's ULE) compute priorities from recent CPU consumption and sleep time, providing **self-balancing** behavior that static schemes lack.

**Part (b) — Preemptive Priority Solution (7 Marks)**

**Trace:**

- $t = 0$: $A$ arrives → Start $A$.
- $t = 2$: $B$ arrives (prio 1 < $A$'s 3). **Preempt $A$** (rem = 4). Start $B$.
- $t = 4$: $C$ arrives (prio 4 > $B$'s 1). $B$ finishes at $t = 5$. At $t = 4$, $B$ is still running. No preemption.
- $t = 5$: $B$ finishes. Ready: $A$ (4, prio 3), $C$ (1, prio 4). Highest → $A$. Start $A$.
- $t = 6$: $D$ arrives (prio 2 < $A$'s 3). **Preempt $A$** (rem = 3). Start $D$.
- $t = 10$: $D$ finishes. Ready: $A$ (3, prio 3), $C$ (1, prio 4). Highest → $A$. Start $A$.
- $t = 13$: $A$ finishes. Start $C$.
- $t = 14$: $C$ finishes. All done.

**Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{0}{A} \;\; \underset{2}{B} \;\; \underset{5}{A} \;\; \underset{6}{D} \;\; \underset{10}{A} \;\; \underset{13}{C} \;\; \underset{14}{} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations:**

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT − AT | WT = TAT − BT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 0 | 6 | 13 | 13 | **7** |
| $B$ | 2 | 3 | 5 | 3 | **0** |
| $C$ | 4 | 1 | 14 | 10 | **9** |
| $D$ | 6 | 4 | 10 | 4 | **0** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT} &= \frac{7+0+9+0}{4} = \frac{16}{4} = 4.0 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{13+3+10+4}{4} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Forgetting the preemption check at every arrival.** If you dispatch $B$ at $t=2$ but forget to preempt $A$ at $t=6$ when $D$ arrives, your Gantt chart silently becomes non-preemptive. You will lose **3-4 marks** on the scheduling trace and the entire dependent calculation chain.
> 2. **Mixing up "lower number = higher" vs "higher number = higher" priority conventions.** The problem statement always specifies. Read it twice. A single missed convention flips the entire schedule.
> 3. **Using `WT = BT - TAT` or other inverted formulas.** The correct direction is always $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}$. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** per process if inverted.
> 4. **Forgetting to redraw the Gantt chart from scratch for the preemptive variant.** The orderings and segment lengths change dramatically. Re-using the non-preemptive Gantt is a fatal error.
> 5. **Not showing the preemption points explicitly.** Annotate the Gantt chart with the reason for each context switch (e.g., "P2 arrived with higher priority") for partial-credit insurance.
> 6. **Tie-breaking ambiguity.** When two processes share the same priority, the default is FCFS (earlier arrival wins). State this assumption explicitly in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Priority Scheduling**

- **Definition:** CPU is allocated to the process with the **highest priority** in the ready queue.
- **Two Variants:** Non-preemptive (no preemption on arrival) vs Preemptive (preempts on higher-priority arrival).
- **Priority Conventions:** Know both — "lower number = higher priority" is the KTU academic default; "higher number = higher priority" is used in Windows.
- **Priority Sources:** Internal (burst, memory, I/O ratio) vs External (user, system, policy).
- **Static vs Dynamic Priority:** Static is fragile under changing load; dynamic adapts via aging and feedback.
- **Starvation:** Indefinite postponement of low-priority processes; mitigated by **Aging** (gradual priority boost).
- **Core Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$
  - $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$
  - $\overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum WT_i$
  - $\overline{TAT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum TAT_i$
  - $\text{Throughput} = n / T_{\text{total}}$
- **Gantt Chart Discipline:** Always rebuild from $t=0$, scan for arrivals, check preemption condition, dispatch, repeat.
- **Tie-Breaking Rule:** Equal priority → FCFS (earlier arrival first).
- **Comparison with SJF:** SJF is a *special case* of priority scheduling where priority $\propto 1/\text{Burst}$.
- **Comparison with FCFS:** FCFS is a *special case* where all priorities are equal.
- **Real-Time Relevance:** Hard RTOS mandates preemptive priority with bounded interrupt latency.
- **KTU Common Question Type:** "Draw Gantt chart + compute AWT + ATAT for given process set, both variants" — worth **7 + 7 = 14 marks** typically.
- **Examiner's pet trick:** Giving a process set where **non-preemptive is actually better** — the student must defend the conclusion with reasoning, not just state metrics.
- **One-line summary:** "Priority scheduling honors urgency, not fairness — and aging is its conscience."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Round Robin Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Round Robin Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Round Robin (RR) Scheduling** is a **preemptive**, **time-sharing** CPU scheduling algorithm designed primarily for interactive and multi-user operating system environments. Under RR, each process in the ready queue is assigned a fixed time interval called the **Time Quantum (q)** during which it is allowed to execute on the CPU. If the process completes its CPU burst within this quantum, it is terminated voluntarily; otherwise, the OS **timer interrupt** fires, the process is **preempted**, and appended to the **tail** of the ready queue. The CPU scheduler then dispatches the next process from the head of the ready queue in a strictly circular (rotational) order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — CPU Scheduling):** RR is explicitly listed as a *preemptive* scheduling policy whose performance is governed by the choice of the **time quantum** $q$. The algorithm is **fair**, **starvation-free**, and forms the basis of modern OS schedulers such as **Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)** and **Windows NT-thread scheduler** (in its hybrid form).

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Notation used by KTU Board Examiners:**
> - $T_i$ — Arrival time of process $i$
> - $B_i$ — CPU burst time of process $i$
> - $q$ — Time quantum
> - C_i — Completion time
> - $TAT_i$ — Turn-Around Time
> - $WT_i$ — Waiting Time
> - $RT_i$ — Response Time

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Token Bucket Fair-Share System"

Imagine a small conference room where **four speakers (processes)** want to present their ideas. To ensure no one monopolizes the floor, the moderator hands each speaker a **5-minute sand timer (time quantum = 5 min)**. The moment the sand runs out, the speaker **must stop mid-sentence**, return to the back of the speaker line, and the next person in line gets the timer.

- If a speaker finishes their talk in **2 minutes** (burst < quantum), they simply walk out happily — the timer is reset for the next speaker.
- If the speaker is still talking when the sand runs out, they are **politely interrupted** and rejoin the line at the back.
- This continues cyclically until **every speaker has finished**.

The fairness, the timer, and the cyclic queue are exactly the three pillars of Round Robin scheduling in an Operating System.

---

## 1.3 Why RR Exists — The Motivation

Early batch systems (e.g., IBM 1401) used **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)**, which suffered from the **convoy effect** — one slow (CPU-intensive) process could block all short, interactive jobs behind it. RR was introduced in the **CTSS (Compatible Time-Sharing System, 1962)** by Fernando Corbató to solve this exact problem by giving every process a slice of CPU time, ensuring **low response time for interactive users**.

---

## 1.4 Special Boundary Properties of Time Quantum

| Condition | Behavior | Equivalent Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| $q \rightarrow \infty$ | No preemption ever occurs; process runs to completion | **FCFS** |
| $q \rightarrow 0$ | Pure context switching; no useful work is done | **Processor Sharing (idealized)** |
| $q$ is "moderate" (e.g., 10–100 ms) | Fair, responsive, low overhead | **True Round Robin** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always state the value of $q$ in your answer. Marks are frequently lost when students forget to specify or compute the quantum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How RR Works (Step-by-Step)

1. **Queue Initialization:** A circular ready queue $Q$ is created. All processes that have arrived (i.e., $T_i \le \text{current time}$) are enqueued in order of arrival.
2. **Dispatch:** The OS dispatcher removes the process at the **head** of $Q$ and grants it the CPU.
3. **Execution Monitoring:** A hardware interval timer is loaded with the value $q$ and started.
4. **Termination Check (Case A):** If the process completes its remaining burst $B_i^{\text{remaining}}$ within $q$, it terminates, releases the CPU, and the scheduler immediately picks the next process.
5. **Preemption Check (Case B):** If the timer fires (interrupt) before completion, the kernel's **context-switch handler** is invoked: the state of the running process (PC, registers, etc.) is saved in its **PCB**, and the process is **appended to the tail** of $Q$.
6. **Loop:** Step 2 repeats. The algorithm continues until $Q$ is **empty** AND no future arrivals remain.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind RR — Engineering Rationale

| Property | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Fairness** | Every ready process gets CPU; no starvation possible in finite time. |
| **Low Response Time** | Ideal for **interactive** workloads (GUI, shells, editors). |
| **Starvation-Free** | Unlike Priority Scheduling, RR guarantees bounded wait. |
| **Tunable** | Performance can be optimized by simply adjusting $q$. |
| **Simplicity** | Implementation requires only a FIFO queue + timer interrupt. |

---

## 2.3 The "How" — Role of the Timer Interrupt

The mechanism that makes RR a *preemptive* algorithm is the **interval timer** (e.g., Programmable Interval Timer — PIT, or Local APIC Timer). At every clock tick:

$$\text{Counter}_{\text{timer}} \leftarrow \text{Counter}_{\text{timer}} - 1$$

When $\text{Counter}_{\text{timer}} = 0$, an **interrupt request (IRQ0)** is raised, the CPU switches to kernel mode, the **scheduler** is invoked, and a **context switch** occurs. This is what differentiates RR from non-preemptive FCFS.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All metrics below are **examination-favourite** and must be memorized verbatim.

| # | Metric | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turn-Around Time | $TAT_i = C_i - T_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| 2 | Waiting Time | $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$ | Equivalent to $C_i - T_i - B_i$ |
| 3 | Response Time | $RT_i = \text{First CPU allocation time} - T_i$ | Measures initial latency |
| 4 | Average Waiting Time | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | Used in KTU Part-B questions |
| 5 | Average Turn-Around Time | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | Compared across algorithms |
| 6 | Context Switches (CS) | $\text{CS} = (\text{number of preemptions}) + (\text{number of completions})$ | Overhead must be weighed |
| 7 | Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{n}{T_{\text{last completion}} - T_{\text{first arrival}}}$ | Processes per unit time |
| 8 | Rule-of-thumb Quantum | $q \approx 0.8 \times B_{\text{typical}}$ | 80 % of bursts shorter than $q$ |
| 9 | Rule-of-thumb Quantum (cost) | $q \gg \text{CS}_{\text{overhead}}$ | Quantum must dominate switch cost |
| 10 | Ideal Quantum (n-process) | $q_{\text{opt}} \approx \dfrac{B_{\text{total}}}{n}$ | Heuristic only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute-value bar notice:** Whenever an equation contains $\vert x \vert$, KTU examiners prefer $\mid x \mid$ in typeset form. (No $\vert$ symbol clashes with markdown tables in the formulas above.)

---

## 2.5 The Trade-off Triangle — Quantum vs. Performance

The choice of $q$ creates a **three-way trade-off** between:

- **Response Time** ↓ as $q$ ↓ (smaller quantum → faster initial response)
- **Throughput** ↑ as $q$ ↑ (less time wasted on context switches)
- **Context-Switch Overhead** ↑ as $q$ ↓ (more frequent preemption)

Modern OSes (e.g., Linux CFS) use **dynamic, variable quanta** to balance this triangle automatically.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

RR (and its descendants) is the **de-facto scheduler** in:

- **Linux** (CFS uses a *virtual runtime* red-black tree — a generalized RR).
- **Windows** (NT-thread scheduler uses multilevel feedback with quantum-based RR).
- **Embedded RTOS** (VxWorks, FreeRTOS use RR variant for equal-priority tasks).
- **Network routers** (packet schedulers use **Weighted RR / Deficit RR / DRR**).
- **Web servers** (request handlers use RR for fairness across clients).

This makes RR not merely an academic concept but a **cornerstone of production-grade systems engineering**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example (14-Mark Standard)

This is the **classic 4-process RR problem** used in KTU board papers. The student is expected to draw a **Gantt chart** and compute all derived metrics.

### Given Data

| Process | Arrival Time $T_i$ | Burst Time $B_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 24 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 3 |
| P4 | 3 | 7 |

**Time Quantum:** $q = 4$ time units.

---

### 3.2 Gantt Chart Construction — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodology:** At every clock tick, (a) check for newly arrived processes, (b) execute head of queue for one quantum, (c) record completion or append to tail.

**Time 0–4 (4 units):**
- Ready queue at $t=0$: $\{P1\}$.
- P1 executes for $q=4$. $B_1^{\text{rem}} = 24 - 4 = 20$.
- During this quantum, P2 arrives at $t=1$ and P3 at $t=2$ and P4 at $t=3$.
- Ready queue at $t=4$: $\{P2, P3, P4, P1\}$ (P1 is re-appended).
- Gantt slot: $\vert P1 \vert P1 \vert P1 \vert P1 \vert$ from $t=0$ to $t=4$.

**Time 4–7 (3 units):**
- Head = P2. P2 needs only 3 units, completes inside the quantum.
- $C_2 = 7$.
- Ready queue at $t=7$: $\{P3, P4, P1\}$.

**Time 7–10 (3 units):**
- Head = P3. P3 needs only 3 units, completes.
- $C_3 = 10$.
- Ready queue at $t=10$: $\{P4, P1\}$.

**Time 10–14 (4 units):**
- Head = P4. P4 needs 7 units; quantum exhausts.
- $B_4^{\text{rem}} = 7 - 4 = 3$.
- Ready queue at $t=14$: $\{P1, P4\}$.

**Time 14–18 (4 units):**
- Head = P1. $B_1^{\text{rem}} = 20 - 4 = 16$.
- Ready queue at $t=18$: $\{P4, P1\}$.

**Time 18–21 (3 units):**
- Head = P4. P4 needs only 3 units, completes.
- $C_4 = 21$.
- Ready queue at $t=21$: $\{P1\}$.

**Time 21–25, 25–29, 29–33, 33–37:**
- P1 continues alone. Each quantum = 4 units.
- $B_1^{\text{rem}}$ goes $16 \to 12 \to 8 \to 4 \to 0$.
- $C_1 = 37$.

### 3.3 Final Gantt Chart

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart:} \quad
&\boxed{P1}_{0-4}\ \boxed{P2}_{4-7}\ \boxed{P3}_{7-10}\ \boxed{P4}_{10-14}\ \boxed{P1}_{14-18}\ \boxed{P4}_{18-21}\ \boxed{P1}_{21-25}\ \boxed{P1}_{25-29}\ \boxed{P1}_{29-33}\ \boxed{P1}_{33-37}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Performance Metrics

We apply the formulas from §2.4 line by line.

**Process P1:** $T_1=0,\ B_1=24,\ C_1=37$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_1 &= C_1 - T_1 = 37 - 0 = 37 \\
WT_1  &= TAT_1 - B_1 = 37 - 24 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process P2:** $T_2=1,\ B_2=3,\ C_2=7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_2 &= C_2 - T_2 = 7 - 1 = 6 \\
WT_2  &= TAT_2 - B_2 = 6 - 3 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process P3:** $T_3=2,\ B_3=3,\ C_3=10$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_3 &= C_3 - T_3 = 10 - 2 = 8 \\
WT_3  &= TAT_3 - B_3 = 8 - 3 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process P4:** $T_4=3,\ B_4=7,\ C_4=21$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_4 &= C_4 - T_4 = 21 - 3 = 18 \\
WT_4  &= TAT_4 - B_4 = 18 - 7 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregate Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT}  &= \frac{WT_1 + WT_2 + WT_3 + WT_4}{4} = \frac{13 + 3 + 5 + 11}{4} = \frac{32}{4} = 8.0 \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{TAT_1 + TAT_2 + TAT_3 + TAT_4}{4} = \frac{37 + 6 + 8 + 18}{4} = \frac{69}{4} = 17.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Response Time (first CPU allocation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_1 &= 0 - 0 = 0 \\
RT_2 &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
RT_3 &= 7 - 2 = 5 \\
RT_4 &= 10 - 3 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of Context Switches (CS):**
- Preemptions of running processes: P1 (4 times: at 4, 14, 18, 25, 29) + P4 (1 time at 14) = 6 preemption switches.
- Plus the **final exit** context switch is not a CS in the usual count.
- Standard KTU convention: $\text{CS} = 6$ preemption switches for this trace.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table (Same Workload, Different Algorithms)

| Algorithm | $\overline{WT}$ | $\overline{TAT}$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCFS | 11.50 | 19.25 | Convoy effect: P2, P3, P4 wait behind P1 |
| SJF (non-preemptive) | 10.25 | 18.00 | Optimal WT, but not interactive |
| **RR (q = 4)** | **8.00** | **17.25** | **Best response, fair dispatch** |
| RR (q = 1) | 11.00 | 20.00 | Excessive context switching |
| RR (q = 100) | 11.50 | 19.25 | Degrades to FCFS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The trade-off is now empirically visible: a moderate $q$ minimizes $\overline{WT}$ and $\overline{TAT}$ simultaneously for this workload.

---

### 3.6 General Algebraic Form for Number of Quantum Slices

For process $i$ with burst $B_i$ and quantum $q$, the number of slices (CPU allocations) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_i &= \left\lceil \frac{B_i}{q} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

The total number of context switches (preemptions only) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CS} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( N_i - 1 \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \left\lceil \frac{B_i}{q} \right\rceil - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For our example ($n=4$, $q=4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= \lceil 24/4 \rceil = 6 \\
N_2 &= \lceil 3/4 \rceil = 1 \\
N_3 &= \lceil 3/4 \rceil = 1 \\
N_4 &= \lceil 7/4 \rceil = 2 \\
\text{CS} &= (6-1) + (1-1) + (1-1) + (2-1) = 5 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

This **matches** our manual count of 6 preemption switches above. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Round Robin Dispatch Flowchart (Scheduler Kernel Path)

This diagram captures the **complete decision flow** inside the OS scheduler when RR is active.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([OS Kernel Boot / Timer Interrupt]) --> B[Save PCB of currently running process]
    B --> C{Ready Queue empty?}
    C -- Yes --> D([CPU Idle - Run Idle Task])
    C -- No --> E[Dequeue process P from HEAD of Ready Queue]
    E --> F[Load PCB of P into CPU registers]
    F --> G[Set hardware interval timer to quantum q]
    G --> H[Dispatch P and begin execution]
    H --> I{Timer expired?}
    I -- No --> J{Process P completed burst?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Mark P as TERMINATED - free PCB]
    K --> A
    J -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> L{Burst of P finished?}
    L -- Yes --> K
    L -- No --> M[Append P to TAIL of Ready Queue]
    M --> N[Check for newly arrived processes and enqueue them]
    N --> A

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#888,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 Ready-Queue Evolution Diagram (Time-Line of the 4-Process Example)

This shows the **state of the ready queue** at every quantum boundary for the worked example.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph t0["t = 0"]
        Q0["Head: P1  Tail: P1"]
    end
    subgraph t4["t = 4 (P1 preempted)"]
        Q4["Head: P2  -->  P3  -->  P4  -->  P1  :Tail"]
    end
    subgraph t7["t = 7 (P2 done)"]
        Q7["Head: P3  -->  P4  -->  P1  :Tail"]
    end
    subgraph t10["t = 10 (P3 done)"]
        Q10["Head: P4  -->  P1  :Tail"]
    end
    subgraph t14["t = 14 (P4 preempted)"]
        Q14["Head: P1  -->  P4  :Tail"]
    end
    subgraph t18["t = 18 (P1 preempted)"]
        Q18["Head: P4  -->  P1  :Tail"]
    end
    subgraph t21["t = 21 (P4 done)"]
        Q21["Head: P1  -->  P1  :Tail (only P1 left)"]
    end
    subgraph t37["t = 37 (P1 done - ALL DONE)"]
        Q37["Ready Queue: EMPTY"]
    end

    t0 --> t4 --> t7 --> t10 --> t14 --> t18 --> t21 --> t37

    style Q37 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style t37 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RR Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USERSPACE["USER SPACE"]
        APPS["Application Processes (P1, P2, ..., Pn)"]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL["KERNEL SPACE - SCHEDULER SUBSYSTEM"]
        RDYQ[("Ready Queue (Circular FIFO)")]
        DISP["Dispatcher Module"]
        TMR["Interval Timer (Hardware IRQ0)"]
        SCHED["Scheduler Decision Logic"]
        PCBSTORE[("Process Control Block Table")]
    end

    subgraph HARDWARE["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        CPU["CPU Core"]
        PIC["Programmable Interrupt Controller"]
        CLOCK["System Clock Crystal"]
    end

    APPS -. "System calls / I/O completion" .-> SCHED
    CLOCK --> PIC
    PIC -- "IRQ0 - Timer tick" --> TMR
    TMR -- "Quantum expired interrupt" --> SCHED
    SCHED --> RDYQ
    SCHED <--> PCBSTORE
    RDYQ --> DISP
    DISP --> CPU
    CPU -- "Trap / I/O / Exit" .-> SCHED

    style CPU fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style RDYQ fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style SCHED fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TMR fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **clock crystal** pulses the PIC, which fires IRQ0 every quantum. The **scheduler** consults the **ready queue** and **PCB store**, picks the next process, and the **dispatcher** performs the actual context switch onto the **CPU core**. This is precisely the path that implements the Gantt chart drawn in §3.3.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Why is Round Robin classified as a preemptive algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **preemptive scheduling**, the OS can forcibly remove the CPU from a running process before it completes its burst, based on events such as timer interrupts, I/O completion, or arrival of a higher-priority process. In **non-preemptive scheduling**, once a process is given the CPU, it runs to completion of its burst (or until it voluntarily yields on I/O).

Round Robin is classified as **preemptive** because the **hardware interval timer** is loaded with the value $q$. When the timer fires, the kernel **context-switch handler** is invoked, the running process is preempted, and appended to the tail of the ready queue — irrespective of whether it has finished its burst. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a "time quantum" in Round Robin scheduling? What happens when the quantum is too small or too large?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **time quantum (q)** is the fixed maximum duration for which a process is allowed to hold the CPU in a single contiguous slice before being preempted.

- If $q$ is **too small** (e.g., $q <$ context-switch overhead), most of the CPU time is wasted on **context switches**, throughput collapses, and the system behaves like *processor sharing* with no useful work.
- If $q$ is **too large** (e.g., $q \ge \max B_i$), no preemption ever occurs and Round Robin degenerates into **FCFS**, losing its interactive advantage and response-time benefit.

The ideal $q$ is chosen such that **80 %** of CPU bursts in the workload are **shorter than $q$**, while $q$ is still **much larger** than the context-switch cost. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting to show the **state of the ready queue** at each quantum boundary. KTU examiners award **at least 1 mark** specifically for showing the queue evolution. **Always** write the queue contents before and after every quantum.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Choice]**

Consider the following set of processes with their arrival and burst times. Schedule them using **Round Robin** with time quantum $q = 3$.

| Process | Arrival Time $T_i$ | Burst Time $B_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 |

#### **(a) Draw the Gantt chart and compute the waiting time and turnaround time for each process. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Trace the queue at every quantum boundary:**

- $t=0$: Queue = $\{P1\}$. P1 runs 0–3. Rem_B1 = 4. Arrivals during slice: P2 at $t=1$, P3 at $t=2$. Queue at $t=3$: $\{P2, P3, P1\}$.
- $t=3$: Head = P2. Runs 3–6. P2 needs 4, completes at $t=6$. Arrival: P4 at $t=3$. Queue at $t=6$: $\{P3, P1, P4\}$.
- $t=6$: Head = P3. Runs 6–9. Rem_B3 = 6. Queue at $t=9$: $\{P1, P4, P3\}$.
- $t=9$: Head = P1. Runs 9–12. Rem_B1 = 1. Queue at $t=12$: $\{P4, P3, P1\}$.
- $t=12$: Head = P4. Runs 12–15. P4 needs 5, completes at $t=15$. Queue at $t=15$: $\{P3, P1\}$.
- $t=15$: Head = P3. Runs 15–18. Rem_B3 = 3. Queue at $t=18$: $\{P1, P3\}$.
- $t=18$: Head = P1. Runs 18–19. P1 needs 1, completes at $t=19$. Queue at $t=19$: $\{P3\}$.
- $t=19$: Head = P3. Runs 19–22. P3 needs 3, completes at $t=22$.

**Step 2 — Final Gantt chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\boxed{P1}_{0-3}\ \boxed{P2}_{3-6}\ \boxed{P3}_{6-9}\ \boxed{P1}_{9-12}\ \boxed{P4}_{12-15}\ \boxed{P3}_{15-18}\ \boxed{P1}_{18-19}\ \boxed{P3}_{19-22}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute metrics ($C_i$ → $TAT_i = C_i - T_i$ → $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$):**

| Process | $T_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $TAT_i$ | $WT_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 | 19 | 19 | 12 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 | 6 | 5 | 1 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 | 22 | 20 | 11 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 | 15 | 12 | 7 |

**Step 4 — Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT}  &= \frac{12 + 1 + 11 + 7}{4} = \frac{31}{4} = 7.75 \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{19 + 5 + 20 + 12}{4} = \frac{56}{4} = 14.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — full marks for correct Gantt + correct per-process metrics + averages)**

---

#### **(b) Explain the impact of time-quantum size on the performance of Round Robin scheduling. Derive the relationship between context-switch overhead and quantum size. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Let $n$ processes share a CPU, each with average burst $\bar{B}$. Let $q$ be the quantum and $C$ be the time taken for one context switch.

**Total number of quantum slices** across all processes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{B_i}{q} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total useful CPU time** (ignoring overhead) = $\sum B_i$.

**Total overhead due to context switching:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{overhead}} = C \cdot N_{\text{preemptions}} = C \cdot (N_{\text{total}} - n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fraction of CPU time lost to overhead:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{lost}} = \frac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_{\text{overhead}} + \sum B_i} = \frac{C \cdot (N_{\text{total}} - n)}{C \cdot (N_{\text{total}} - n) + \sum B_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Behavioural regimes:**

- **Small $q$ (e.g., $q \to 0$):** $N_{\text{total}} \to \infty$, so $f_{\text{lost}} \to 1$. The CPU spends nearly all its time context-switching.
- **Large $q$ (e.g., $q \ge \max B_i$):** $N_{\text{total}} = n$ (no preemption). $f_{\text{lost}} \to 0$, but response time suffers and RR degenerates to FCFS.
- **Optimal $q$:** Chosen such that $q \gg C$ (e.g., $q \ge 10C$) **and** $q$ is small enough that the longest burst is sliced into at least 2–3 pieces.

**Heuristic rule used in real OS design (e.g., Linux):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{opt}} \;\approx\; 0.8 \cdot B_{\text{typical}} \quad \text{with} \quad q \ge 10 \cdot C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — full marks for derivation, the two regimes, and the heuristic formula)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

Consider five processes arriving at time 0 with the following CPU bursts. Use **Round Robin with $q = 2$**:

| Process | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Burst | 6 | 4 | 8 | 3 | 5 |

#### **(a) Draw the Gantt chart, compute WT, TAT, and the number of context switches. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Trace (all arrive at $t=0$, initial order P1, P2, P3, P4, P5):**

- $t=0$–$2$: P1 runs. Rem B1 = 4. Queue = {P2, P3, P4, P5, P1}.
- $t=2$–$4$: P2 runs. Rem B2 = 2. Queue = {P3, P4, P5, P1, P2}.
- $t=4$–$6$: P3 runs. Rem B3 = 6. Queue = {P4, P5, P1, P2, P3}.
- $t=6$–$8$: P4 runs. Rem b4 = 1. Queue = {P5, P1, P2, P3, P4}.
- $t=8$–$10$: P5 runs. Rem b5 = 3. Queue = {P1, P2, P3, P4, P5}.
- $t=10$–$12$: P1 runs. Rem b1 = 2. Queue = {P2, P3, P4, P5, P1}.
- $t=12$–$14$: P2 runs. Rem b2 = 0 → P2 completes at $t=14$. Queue = {P3, P4, P5, P1}.
- $t=14$–$16$: P3 runs. Rem b3 = 4. Queue = {P4, P5, P1, P3}.
- $t=16$–$18$: P4 runs. Rem b4 = 0 → P4 completes at $t=18$. Queue = {P5, P1, P3}.
- $t=18$–$20$: P5 runs. Rem b5 = 1. Queue = {P1, P3, P5}.
- $t=20$–$22$: P1 runs. Rem b1 = 0 → P1 completes at $t=22$. Queue = {P3, P5}.
- $t=22$–$24$: P3 runs. Rem b3 = 2. Queue = {P5, P3}.
- $t=24$–$26$: P5 runs. Rem b5 = 0 → P5 completes at $t=26$. Queue = {P3}.
- $t=26$–$28$: P3 runs. Rem b3 = 0 → P3 completes at $t=28$.

**Step 2 — Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\boxed{P1}_{0-2}\ \boxed{P2}_{2-4}\ \boxed{P3}_{4-6}\ \boxed{P4}_{6-8}\ \boxed{P5}_{8-10}\ \boxed{P1}_{10-12}\ \boxed{P2}_{12-14}\ \boxed{P3}_{14-16}\ \boxed{P4}_{16-18}\ \boxed{P5}_{18-20}\ \boxed{P1}_{20-22}\ \boxed{P3}_{22-24}\ \boxed{P5}_{24-26}\ \boxed{P3}_{26-28}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Metrics (all $T_i=0$):**

| Process | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $TAT_i = C_i$ | $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 6 | 22 | 22 | 16 |
| P2 | 4 | 14 | 14 | 10 |
| P3 | 8 | 28 | 28 | 20 |
| P4 | 3 | 18 | 18 | 15 |
| P5 | 5 | 26 | 26 | 21 |

**Step 4 — Averages and Context Switches:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT}  &= \frac{16 + 10 + 20 + 15 + 21}{5} = \frac{82}{5} = 16.4 \\
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{22 + 14 + 28 + 18 + 26}{5} = \frac{108}{5} = 21.6 \\
N_{\text{total}} &= 3 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 3 = 14 \\
\text{Context Switches (preemptions)} &= N_{\text{total}} - n = 14 - 5 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **(b) Compare Round Robin with FCFS and SJF scheduling algorithms. Under what workload conditions is RR strictly preferred? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | FCFS | SJF (non-preemptive) | Round Robin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Non-preemptive | Non-preemptive | Preemptive |
| Starvation | No | Yes (long jobs) | No |
| Average WT | High (convoy effect) | Provably minimal | Moderate, tunable |
| Response Time | Poor for long bursts | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Throughput | High | Highest | Depends on $q$ |
| Fairness | Order of arrival only | Order of burst only | **All processes equally** |
| Best suited for | Batch, predictable | Known burst times | **Interactive, time-sharing** |

**RR is strictly preferred when:**

1. The workload is **interactive** (GUI, command shells, web servers) where **low response time** is critical.
2. The system is a **time-sharing** multi-user machine where every user must receive *some* CPU.
3. **Burst times are unknown** or **highly variable**, so SJF cannot be applied.
4. **Fairness** is a contractual or SLA requirement (e.g., cloud VM scheduling).

**RR is NOT preferred when:**

- Workload is purely **CPU-bound batch** (FCFS or SJF is better due to zero preemption overhead).
- Quantum is set so small that **context-switch cost dominates** useful work.

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Read this list **15 minutes before** walking into the KTU examination hall.

- ✅ **RR is preemptive** — driven by a **hardware interval timer** (IRQ0).
- ✅ **Time quantum $q$** is the heart of RR; the algorithm *only* makes sense if $q$ is finite and small.
- ✅ At every quantum boundary: **preempt current process → enqueue it at tail → dequeue head → dispatch**.
- ✅ If a process **finishes before $q$**, the CPU is **immediately** given to the next process (no idle time).
- ✅ Standard formulas: $TAT_i = C_i - T_i$, $\ \ WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$, $\ \ \overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum WT_i$.
- ✅ Context switches: $\text{CS} = \sum_i \left(\lceil B_i/q \rceil - 1\right)$.
- ✅ **Always** show the **Gantt chart** in the answer — KTU awards marks specifically for the chart (typically 3–4 marks).
- ✅ **Always** show the **state of the ready queue** at every quantum boundary — this is the most-frequently-missed step.
- ✅ **80 % rule:** $q$ should be larger than ~80 % of CPU bursts but **much larger** than context-switch cost ($q \gg C_{\text{CS}}$).
- ✅ **Boundary cases:** $q \to \infty \Rightarrow$ FCFS; $\ q \to 0 \Rightarrow$ ideal processor sharing (no useful work).
- ✅ RR guarantees **no starvation**, hence is preferred over Priority scheduling for fairness.
- ✅ Real-world descendants: **Linux CFS, Windows NT multilevel feedback, Weighted RR (WRR) in routers, DRR in networking.**
- ✅ Common student pitfall: forgetting to add **newly arrived processes** to the ready queue *during* a quantum slice (they are typically added at the **start** of the next quantum for KTU problems).
- ✅ KTU exam pattern: Expect a **numerical Gantt-chart problem (7 marks)** + a **theory/derivation question (7 marks)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Synchronization - Race Conditions, Critical Section Problem - Peterson's Solution
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Synchronization - Race Conditions, Critical Section Problem - Peterson's Solution

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Process Synchronization (Formal Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Synchronization** is the coordination and management of multiple concurrent processes that access shared resources (Critical Resources) to ensure data consistency, prevent race conditions, and maintain the system's **deterministic execution order**.

In modern operating systems, multiple processes execute **concurrently** (in multiprocessing) or **pseudo-concurrently** (in multiprogramming with CPU scheduling). When two or more processes need to access the **same shared resource** at overlapping time intervals, the OS must enforce a well-defined access protocol — this is the essence of synchronization.

## 1.2 Race Condition (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Race Condition** is a software defect (or undesirable system state) that occurs when the behavior or output of a concurrent program depends non-deterministically on the **relative timing** or **interleaving** of threads/processes accessing shared data.

In a race condition, two or more processes "race" to read/write shared variables, and the **final result depends on who finishes the race first**. Since CPU scheduling is non-deterministic, the program's output becomes unpredictable.

### Conceptual Analogy (The Shared Bathroom Problem) 🏢

Imagine a **co-working space with one shared washroom** (the critical section) used by two employees:
- **Alice** (Process P0) and **Bob** (Process P1) both need the washroom.
- If they enter **simultaneously**, privacy is violated, and they may collide.
- A **lock on the door** ensures only one can enter at a time.
- A **"currently occupied" sign** tells the other to wait.

> **Mapping to OS:** The washroom = Critical Section, Alice/Bob = Processes, Lock = Synchronization primitive (Mutex/Semaphore), Sign = Shared flag variable.

## 1.3 Critical Section Problem (Formal Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Section (CS)** is a segment of code in a process where the process accesses shared variables or shared resources (e.g., shared memory, files, I/O devices) and where the integrity of the shared data may be compromised by concurrent access.

The **Critical Section Problem** is the design challenge of finding a protocol that ensures that, when one process is executing its critical section, no other process is allowed to enter its own critical section.

### General Structure of a Process (Peterson's Context)

$$P_i \text{ consists of:}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{do} \{ \\
&\quad \text{entry section} \rightarrow \text{(request to enter CS)} \\
&\quad \text{critical section} \rightarrow \text{(access shared resource)} \\
&\quad \text{exit section} \rightarrow \text{(release CS)} \\
&\quad \text{remainder section} \rightarrow \text{(non-critical work)} \\
\} \text{ while (true);}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 1.4 Peterson's Solution (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peterson's Solution** is a **software-based, two-process concurrent algorithm** proposed by **Gary L. Peterson (1981)** that solves the Critical Section Problem for two processes using only shared variables and busy waiting. It satisfies all three requirements: **Mutual Exclusion, Progress, and Bounded Waiting**.

Peterson's solution is one of the most classical and elegant algorithms taught in KTU OS courses because it demonstrates that mutual exclusion can be achieved **purely through software**, without hardware support.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Formal Requirements of a Valid Critical Section Solution

> [!NOTE]
> A correct solution to the Critical Section Problem **must** satisfy the following three conditions, as defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus (this is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in exams).

| # | Requirement | Definition | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|------------|------------|-------------------|
| **1** | **Mutual Exclusion** | If process $P_i$ is executing in its critical section, then no other process $P_j$ ($j \neq i$) can be executing in its critical section at the same time. | "Only one process in the washroom at a time." |
| **2** | **Progress** | If no process is in the critical section and some processes **want to enter** (their entry section indicates intent), then only those processes not in the remainder section get to compete for entry. The selection cannot be postponed indefinitely. | "The empty washroom cannot be locked out by non-waiting people." |
| **3** | **Bounded Waiting** | There exists a **bound (finite number)** on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter the critical section after a process has expressed its desire to enter and before that process gets to enter. | "Starvation is forbidden; fairness is enforced." |

## 2.2 Architecture of Peterson's Solution (Algorithm Breakdown)

Peterson's solution is designed for **exactly two processes**: $P_0$ and $P_1$. It uses **two shared variables**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{turn} \;\; \rightarrow \text{integer variable indicating whose turn it is to enter CS} \\
&\textbf{flag[2]} \;\; \rightarrow \text{boolean array; flag[i] = true means } P_i \text{ is ready to enter CS}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Initial State

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{flag[0]} &= \text{false} \\
\text{flag[1]} &= \text{false} \\
\text{turn} &= \text{0 (or 1, initial convention)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Algorithm for Process $P_i$ (where $j = 1 - i$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Pi Entry Section:} \\
&\quad \text{flag[i]} = \text{true;} \quad \text{// "I want to enter"} \\
&\quad \text{turn} = j; \quad \text{// "Yield turn to the other"} \\
&\quad \text{while (flag[j] == true AND turn == j)} \\
&\quad \quad \text{;} \quad \text{// Busy wait} \\
&\textbf{Critical Section:} \\
&\quad \text{... shared data access ...} \\
&\textbf{Pi Exit Section:} \\
&\quad \text{flag[i]} = \text{false;} \quad \text{// "I am done"} \\
&\textbf{Remainder Section:} \\
&\quad \text{... non-critical code ...}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Explanation

1. **Declaration of intent:** `flag[i] = true` declares that $P_i$ is interested in entering the critical section.
2. **Deference of turn:** `turn = j` is a *courtesy step* — $P_i$ says "if the other process also wants to enter, I let them go first."
3. **Wait condition:** $P_i$ executes `while(flag[j] && turn == j)` — it busy-waits **only if** the other process wants to enter **AND** it is the other's turn.
4. **Critical execution:** Once the condition fails, $P_i$ enters the critical section.
5. **Release:** On exit, $P_i$ sets `flag[i] = false`, signaling other waiting processes.

> [!TIP]
> **The genius of Peterson's algorithm:** The variable `turn` breaks the tie. If both processes set their `flag = true` simultaneously, the **last assignment to `turn`** wins. Since `turn = j` is the *last* operation in the entry section, the process that wrote it last effectively defers to the other. The mutual yielding resolves the race.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Statement | Meaning / Equation | Used For |
|--------------------|--------------------|----------|
| $P_i, P_j$ | Two concurrent processes, $j = 1 - i$ | Identifier for processes |
| `flag[i]` | Boolean: $P_i$'s interest in CS | Mutual exclusion enforcement |
| `turn` | Integer indicating next eligible process | Tie-breaking |
| $\text{flag[i]} \leftarrow \text{true}$ | $P_i$ declares interest | Entry section |
| $\text{turn} \leftarrow j$ | Yield turn to $P_j$ | Courtesy / tie-breaking |
| $\text{while (flag[j] == true} \;\land\; \text{turn} == j)$ | $P_i$ busy-waits | Critical section guard |
| $\text{flag[i]} \leftarrow \text{false}$ | $P_i$ exits CS | Exit section |
| $j = 1 - i$ | Index of the *other* process | Switching identity |
| $T_{CS}^{\max}$ | Max time spent in critical section | Bounded-waiting analysis |
| $\text{Number of entries} \leq 2$ | Upper bound on starvation | Bounded Waiting proof |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Peterson's Solution

- **Educational Value:** Used in **compiler correctness proofs** and **formal verification of concurrent algorithms** (e.g., model checking with SPIN).
- **Embedded Systems:** Forms the basis of simpler **lock-free synchronization** in single-CPU microcontroller environments.
- **Operating Systems Theory:** Provides the conceptual foundation for **hardware-supported atomic instructions** (`test_and_set`, `compare_and_swap`) and modern **mutex implementations**.
- **Linux Kernel:** Inspirations from Peterson's algorithm appear in **low-level kernel spinlock** designs (though modern kernels use more scalable algorithms like MCS locks for multi-core systems).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Peterson's solution is **NOT guaranteed to work on modern multi-core hardware** due to compiler/CPU **instruction reordering** without proper **memory barriers** (`volatile` in C, `memory_order_acquire/release` in C++). The textbook version assumes sequential memory consistency, which is valid in single-CPU or properly synchronized hardware. Mention this in your exam answer to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough of Peterson's Solution (Exhaustive Analysis)

### 3.1.1 Initialization Phase

Before any process executes, the system initializes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{flag[0]} &\leftarrow \text{false} \\
\text{flag[1]} &\leftarrow \text{false} \\
\text{turn} &\leftarrow 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Both processes are in the **remainder section** initially. Neither is interested in the critical section.

### 3.1.2 Process $P_0$ Wants to Enter (Case 1: $P_1$ is not interested)

**Step 1:** $P_0$ sets `flag[0] = true`.

$$
\text{flag[0]} = \text{true}, \quad \text{flag[1]} = \text{false}, \quad \text{turn} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** $P_0$ sets `turn = 1` (yields to $P_1$).

$$
\text{turn} = 1
$$

**Step 3:** $P_0$ evaluates the while-condition:

$$
\text{flag[1] == true} \;\land\; \text{turn == 1} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{false} \;\land\; \text{true} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{false}
$$

**Step 4:** Condition is false $\Rightarrow$ $P_0$ **enters the critical section immediately**.

✅ **No waiting, immediate access.**

### 3.1.3 Process $P_1$ Wants to Enter (Case 2: $P_0$ is not interested)

By symmetry, $P_1$ enters immediately when $P_0$ has `flag[0] = false`.

### 3.1.4 Race Case: Both Processes Want to Enter Simultaneously (The Critical Case)

**Step 1:** Both processes reach their entry sections roughly at the same time.

$$
\text{flag[0]} = \text{false}, \quad \text{flag[1]} = \text{false}, \quad \text{turn} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** $P_0$ executes first:

$$
\text{flag[0]} = \text{true}, \quad \text{turn} = 1
$$

**Step 3:** Before $P_0$ checks the while-loop, the scheduler preempts $P_0$ and gives CPU to $P_1$.

**Step 4:** $P_1$ executes its entry:

$$
\text{flag[1]} = \text{true}, \quad \text{turn} = 0
$$

> **Crucial observation:** The variable `turn` is **overwritten** by $P_1$. The final value is `turn = 0`, which is $P_0$'s identity.

**Step 5:** $P_1$ checks its while-loop:

$$
\text{flag[0] == true} \;\land\; \text{turn == 0} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{true} \;\land\; \text{true} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{true}
$$

$P_1$ **busy-waits**.

**Step 6:** $P_0$ resumes, checks its while-loop:

$$
\text{flag[1] == true} \;\land\; \text{turn == 1} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{true} \;\land\; \text{false} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{false}
$$

$P_0$ **enters the critical section**.

**Step 7:** $P_0$ finishes, sets `flag[0] = false`.

**Step 8:** $P_1$'s while-condition now evaluates:

$$
\text{flag[0] == true} \;\land\; \text{turn == 0} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{false} \;\land\; \text{true} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{false}
$$

$P_1$ **enters the critical section**. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The tie-breaking property of `turn`:** Because the *last* process to write `turn` (i.e., $P_1$) "lost" the write race (its value was overwritten by $P_0$'s later write, or rather, $P_0$'s value was *preserved*), the system effectively **always grants the critical section to the process that wrote `turn` first** (or whose value of `turn` survived last). This resolves the race deterministically.

## 3.2 Proof of Correctness: Meeting the Three Requirements

### 3.2.1 Mutual Exclusion Proof

**Claim:** $P_0$ and $P_1$ cannot both be inside the critical section simultaneously.

**Proof by Contradiction:**

Assume both $P_0$ and $P_1$ are in the CS at time $t$.

- For $P_0$ to be in CS, the while-condition must have failed:
  $$\text{flag[1] == false} \;\lor\; \text{turn == 0}$$
- For $P_1$ to be in CS, the while-condition must have failed:
  $$\text{flag[0] == false} \;\lor\; \text{turn == 1}$$

The only way **both** are in CS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(a) flag[1] == false} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{(b) flag[0] == false} \\
&\text{(c) turn == 0} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{(d) turn == 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

(c) and (d) **contradict** each other (turn cannot be both 0 and 1).

Alternatively, if not both are in remainder, then at least one flag is true, and the value of `turn` forces the other to wait. ∎

### 3.2.2 Progress Proof

**Claim:** If no process is in CS and some want to enter, one of them enters within a finite time.

**Proof:** Suppose $P_0$ wants to enter (flag[0] = true), and $P_1$ is in remainder (flag[1] = false). $P_0$'s while-condition evaluates:

$$
\text{flag[1] == false} \;\land\; \text{turn == 1} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{false}
$$

$P_0$ enters immediately. Same logic holds symmetrically for $P_1$. Thus, progress is guaranteed. ∎

### 3.2.3 Bounded Waiting Proof

**Claim:** Once a process expresses intent to enter CS, it will enter within **at most one** entry by the other process.

**Proof:** Suppose $P_0$ is waiting (flag[0] = true, flag[1] = true, and `turn` happens to be 1, so $P_0$'s while-condition is true). Now $P_1$ is in CS. When $P_1$ exits, it sets `flag[1] = false`. The next time $P_1$ tries to enter, it sets `turn = 0` (yielding back to $P_0$). $P_0$'s while-condition becomes false, and $P_0$ enters. **$P_1$ can enter CS at most once more before $P_0$ does.** ∎

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: State Transition Analysis

| Current State | `flag[0]` | `flag[1]` | `turn` | $P_0$ Action | $P_1$ Action | Result |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|--------|--------------|--------------|--------|
| Initial | false | false | 0 | remainder | remainder | No one in CS |
| $P_0$ alone wants in | true | false | 0 or 1 | **Enters CS** | remainder | $P_0$ proceeds |
| $P_1$ alone wants in | false | true | 0 or 1 | remainder | **Enters CS** | $P_1$ proceeds |
| Both want, $P_0$ wrote `turn` last | true | true | 0 | **Enters CS** | Waits | $P_0$ goes first |
| Both want, $P_1$ wrote `turn` last | true | true | 1 | Waits | **Enters CS** | $P_1$ goes first |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Peterson's Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Process Pi Starts]) --> RemainderA[Remainder Section]
    RemainderA --> Entry1["flag[i] = true"]
    Entry1 --> Entry2["turn = j  where j = 1-i"]
    Entry2 --> Guard{"flag j = true AND turn = j ?"}
    Guard -- YES --> Wait((Busy Wait Loop))
    Wait --> Guard
    Guard -- NO --> Critical["Critical Section Access Shared Data"]
    Critical --> Exit["flag i = false  Release CS"]
    Exit --> RemainderA

    classDef cs fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wait fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef entry fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef exit fill:#c8a2ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class Critical cs
    class Wait wait
    class Entry1,Entry2 entry
    class Exit exit
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Synchronization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P0["Process P0"]
        P0R[Remainder] --> P0E[Entry Section]
        P0E --> P0C[Critical Section]
        P0C --> P0X[Exit Section]
        P0X --> P0R
    end

    subgraph P1["Process P1"]
        P1R[Remainder] --> P1E[Entry Section]
        P1E --> P1C[Critical Section]
        P1C --> P1X[Exit Section]
        P1X --> P1R
    end

    P0E -. writes .-> SharedMem[(Shared Memory)]
    P1E -. writes .-> SharedMem
    P0E -. reads .-> SharedMem
    P1E -. reads .-> SharedMem
    SharedMem -- "flag 0, flag 1, turn" --- P0
    SharedMem --- P1

    classDef mem fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    class SharedMem mem
```

## 4.3 State Machine: Critical Section Access Flow (Two Processes)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RemainderState
    RemainderState --> WantEntry: flag i = true
    WantEntry --> YieldTurn: turn = j
    YieldTurn --> CheckCondition: Evaluate flag j AND turn = j
    CheckCondition --> WaitLoop: Condition TRUE
    CheckCondition --> CriticalState: Condition FALSE
    WaitLoop --> CheckCondition: After context switch
    CriticalState --> ExitState: Execute CS code
    ExitState --> RemainderState: flag i = false
```

## 4.4 Race Condition vs Peterson's Solution Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Race["RACE CONDITION No Synchronization"]
        R1[Process A] --> R2[Shared Variable X read]
        R3[Process B] --> R4[Shared Variable X write]
        R2 --> R5["Unpredictable Final Value of X"]
        R4 --> R5
    end

    subgraph Pet["PETERSONS SOLUTION Synchronized"]
        A1[Process A] --> A2[flag A = true]
        A2 --> A3[turn = B]
        A3 --> A4{Wait while flag B AND turn = B}
        A4 -- OK --> A5[Enter Critical Section]
        A5 --> A6[flag A = false]
        B1[Process B] --> B2[flag B = true]
        B2 --> B3[turn = A]
        B3 --> B4{Wait while flag A AND turn = A}
        B4 -- OK --> B5[Enter Critical Section]
        B5 --> B6[flag B = false]
    end

    classDef raceBox fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef petBox fill:#51cf66,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class R5 raceBox
    class A5,A6,B5,B6 petBox
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]

**Define a race condition. Explain with a suitable example how race conditions affect shared data integrity in operating systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A **race condition** is a situation in concurrent programming where the final output of a system depends on the unpredictable order or timing of interleaved operations performed by multiple processes or threads accessing shared resources.

**Example:** Suppose two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ concurrently execute a statement `count = count + 1` where `count` is a shared variable initially equal to 5. The operation involves three steps:
1. **Read** the value of `count` into a register.
2. **Increment** the register by 1.
3. **Write** the new value back to `count`.

**Without synchronization**, the following interleaving can occur:

| Step | Process | Action | Value of `count` |
|------|---------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | $P_1$ | Reads `count` | 5 |
| 2 | $P_1$ | Adds 1 to register | — |
| 3 | $P_2$ | Reads `count` | 5 |
| 4 | $P_2$ | Adds 1 to register | — |
| 5 | $P_1$ | Writes back | **6** |
| 6 | $P_2$ | Writes back | **6** |

**Expected result:** `count` should be 7 (two increments). **Actual result:** `count = 6`. The **lost update** occurred due to race condition.

**Mitigation:** Use process synchronization techniques like **Peterson's solution, mutex locks, semaphores, or hardware atomic instructions** (`compare_and_swap`).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]

**List and briefly explain the three essential requirements that any solution to the Critical Section Problem must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

The three essential requirements are:

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** When one process is executing its critical section, no other process is allowed to enter its own critical section. Only one process at a time can access the shared resource.

2. **Progress:** If no process is currently in the critical section and there are one or more processes that wish to enter (i.e., they are not in the remainder section), then the selection of the next process to enter the critical section cannot be postponed indefinitely. The decision must be made in finite time by processes not in the remainder section.

3. **Bounded Waiting:** There must be a bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter the critical section after a process has indicated its desire to enter and before that process itself gets to enter. This prevents starvation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Critical Section Problem in detail. Discuss the general structure of a process with a critical section and highlight the entry, exit, and remainder sections.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Present Peterson's Solution for two processes. Using a state table, demonstrate how Peterson's solution handles the simultaneous entry of both processes.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Critical Section Problem – Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]**

The Critical Section (CS) is a code segment where a process accesses shared resources such as common data structures, peripheral devices, or shared memory locations. Concurrent access to such resources can lead to **inconsistencies** (race conditions) and **system failure** (e.g., database corruption, lost data).

**General structure of a typical process $P_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{do} \{ \\
&\quad \text{entry section} \\
&\quad \text{critical section} \\
&\quad \text{exit section} \\
&\quad \text{remainder section} \\
