\} \text{ while (true);}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Entry section:** Implements the **request** to enter the critical section (e.g., acquire lock, busy-wait check, set flags).
- **Critical section:** Accesses the shared resource. **Only one process at a time may be here.**
- **Exit section:** Implements the **release** of the critical section (e.g., release lock, reset flags).
- **Remainder section:** All non-critical code. Multiple processes may run here in parallel.

**Requirements of a valid CS solution:** (State all three as covered in Part A.)

**(b) Peterson's Solution [7 Marks]**

**Shared variables** between $P_0$ and $P_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{int turn;} \\
&\text{boolean flag[2];} \\
&\text{Initially: flag[0] = flag[1] = false; turn = 0;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Algorithm for $P_i$ ($j = 1 - i$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{do} \{ \\
&\quad \text{flag[i] = true;} \\
&\quad \text{turn = j;} \\
&\quad \text{while (flag[j] == true \&\& turn == j)} \\
&\quad \quad \text{;} \\
&\quad \text{/* critical section */} \\
&\quad \text{flag[i] = false;} \\
&\quad \text{/* remainder section */} \\
&\} \text{ while (true);}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State Table – Demonstrating Mutual Exclusion (Both $P_0$ and $P_1$ try to enter simultaneously):**

| Step | $P_0$ Action | $P_1$ Action | `flag[0]` | `flag[1]` | `turn` | Outcome |
|------|--------------|--------------|-----------|-----------|--------|---------|
| 0 | Remainder | Remainder | false | false | 0 | — |
| 1 | `flag[0] = true` | — | true | false | 0 | $P_0$ declares intent |
| 2 | `turn = 1` | — | true | false | 1 | $P_0$ yields |
| 3 | — | `flag[1] = true` | true | true | 1 | $P_1$ declares intent |
| 4 | — | `turn = 0` | true | true | 0 | $P_1$ yields back |
| 5 | Check: `flag[1] && turn==1` $\Rightarrow$ `true && false` $\Rightarrow$ false | — | true | true | 0 | $P_0$ **enters CS** |
| 6 | In CS | Waits | true | true | 0 | Mutual exclusion holds |
| 7 | `flag[0] = false` | — | false | true | 0 | $P_0$ exits |
| 8 | — | Check: `flag[0] && turn==0` $\Rightarrow$ `false && true` $\Rightarrow$ false | false | true | 0 | $P_1$ **enters CS** |
| 9 | Remainder | In CS | false | true | 0 | $P_1$ proceeds |
| 10 | — | `flag[1] = false` | false | false | 0 | $P_1$ exits |

✅ **Mutual Exclusion demonstrated.** No state ever has both processes inside the CS.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the three requirements that a solution to the Critical Section Problem must satisfy. Why is each one necessary?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Provide the complete Peterson's algorithm for two processes. Prove that it satisfies mutual exclusion and bounded waiting.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Three Requirements and Their Necessity [7 Marks]**

1. **Mutual Exclusion is necessary** to prevent data corruption. Without it, two processes could simultaneously modify a shared variable, leading to **lost updates**, **inconsistent state**, and **unpredictable program behavior** (as shown in the race condition example above).

2. **Progress is necessary** to ensure **liveness** of the system. Without progress, a deadlock-like situation could occur: even when the critical section is free, a process that wants to enter might be perpetually blocked because the decision logic depends on processes that are in the remainder section (and thus not cooperating).

3. **Bounded Waiting is necessary** to ensure **fairness** and **starvation-freedom**. Without it, a process could be repeatedly bypassed in favor of the other process, leading to indefinite postponement (starvation). Bounded waiting limits the maximum number of times other processes can enter the CS before the waiting process gets its turn.

**(b) Peterson's Algorithm with Proofs [7 Marks]**

**Algorithm (restated for clarity):**

For $P_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{do} \{ \\
&\quad \text{flag[0] = true;} \\
&\quad \text{turn = 1;} \\
&\quad \text{while (flag[1] == true \&\& turn == 1)} \\
&\quad \quad \text{;} \\
&\quad \text{/* critical section */} \\
&\quad \text{flag[0] = false;} \\
&\quad \text{/* remainder section */} \\
&\} \text{ while (true);}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $P_1$: replace `0` with `1` and `1` with `0` in the above.

**Proof of Mutual Exclusion:**

Assume for contradiction that **both $P_0$ and $P_1$** are in their critical sections simultaneously. For $P_0$ to be in CS, its while-condition must be false:

$$
\text{flag[1] == false} \;\; \lor \;\; \text{turn == 0}
$$

For $P_1$ to be in CS:

$$
\text{flag[0] == false} \;\; \lor \;\; \text{turn == 1}
$$

If both are in CS, then at least one `flag` must be true (since each process sets its own `flag = true` upon entry, and `flag` is reset only on exit). Suppose `flag[0] == true` and `flag[1] == true`:

- $P_0$ is in CS $\Rightarrow$ `turn == 0`.
- $P_1$ is in CS $\Rightarrow$ `turn == 1`.

Contradiction! `turn` cannot be both 0 and 1. ∎

**Proof of Bounded Waiting:**

Suppose $P_0$ wants to enter (sets `flag[0] = true`). $P_1$ is in CS or just entering. After $P_1$ exits, it sets `flag[1] = false`. If $P_1$ re-enters, it must set `turn = 0` (yielding to $P_0$). Hence $P_0$'s while-condition becomes false and $P_0$ enters.

Thus $P_1$ can enter the critical section **at most one more time** after $P_0$ has expressed its intent, before $P_0$ enters. This is a finite bound $\Rightarrow$ **bounded waiting** is satisfied. ∎

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do not skip the role of the `turn` variable.** Many students write the algorithm but fail to explain *why* `turn = j` is the second statement (not the first). The order matters: writing `turn` after `flag[i] = true` ensures the *last* process to express interest effectively loses the tie-break, granting CS to the *earlier* arriver.
> - **Forgetting `j = 1 - i`:** Always state that $P_j$ is the **other** process. Some students confuse this and use the same index.
> - **Not mentioning the `volatile` / memory barrier caveat** for modern hardware: Although not always required by KTU, mentioning it shows deeper understanding and can earn **bonus marks**.
> - **Do not claim Peterson's solution works for $n > 2$ processes.** It is strictly a two-process algorithm. The generalization (Dekker's algorithm, Lamport's bakery algorithm) requires more complex state.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **Process Synchronization** = coordination of processes accessing shared resources to maintain data consistency and deterministic behavior.
- ✅ **Race Condition** = outcome depends on non-deterministic interleaving of concurrent operations on shared data.
- ✅ **Critical Section (CS)** = code segment accessing shared resources; only **one** process at a time.
- ✅ **Three Requirements** of any CS solution:
  - **Mutual Exclusion** (Safety)
  - **Progress** (Liveness)
  - **Bounded Waiting** (Fairness)
- ✅ **Peterson's Solution** is a **software-only**, **two-process** algorithm using **two shared variables**: `turn` and `flag[2]`.
- ✅ **Algorithm for $P_i$:**
  1. `flag[i] = true;` (declare intent)
  2. `turn = j;` (yield to other)
  3. `while (flag[j] && turn == j);` (busy wait)
  4. **Critical Section**
  5. `flag[i] = false;` (release)
  6. Remainder section.
- ✅ **$j = 1 - i$** is the identity of the *other* process.
- ✅ **Tie-breaking rule:** The process whose write of `turn` was **last** *waits*; the other process enters first.
- ✅ **Mutual Exclusion proof** uses contradiction: `turn` cannot be both 0 and 1 at the same time.
- ✅ **Bounded Waiting bound:** A waiting process is bypassed **at most once** by the other process.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Works only for **2 processes**; assumes **atomic loads/stores** and **sequential memory consistency**; not safe on modern multi-core hardware without **memory barriers** / `volatile` qualifier.
- ✅ **Real-world extensions:** **Dekker's Algorithm** (5 shared variables), **Lamport's Bakery Algorithm** ($n$-process, ticket-based).
- ✅ **Modern alternative:** **Hardware atomic instructions** like `compare_and_swap` (CAS) and `test_and_set` provide faster, scalable synchronization.
- ✅ **KCU exam tip:** Always draw a **state transition table** when proving Peterson's solution. Examiners reward structured answers with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synchronization Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Synchronization Hardware** refers to the set of **special-purpose machine-level (hardware) instructions** and **memory-fence primitives** provided by the processor architecture that perform **atomic (indivisible) read-modify-write operations** on shared memory locations. These instructions are guaranteed by the CPU hardware to execute without interruption, thereby eliminating the **race condition window** that pure software-based synchronization algorithms (such as Peterson's or Dekker's) cannot fully close on modern preemptive, multi-core, pipelined, and out-of-order-execution processors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Synchronization Hardware is a foundational concept that bridges the gap between *software-only synchronization algorithms* and the actual *machine-level guarantees* required to enforce critical sections. This topic is a **direct prerequisite** for studying Semaphores, Monitors, and the Linux `futex` mechanism in later modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Fundamental Hardware Synchronization Concepts:**
> 1. **Atomic Test-and-Set** — `TAS`
> 2. **Atomic Compare-and-Swap** — `CAS` (also called *Compare-and-Exchange*, `CMPXCHG`)
> 3. **Fetch-and-Add** — `FAA` (used for *atomic counters / ticket locks*)

---

## 1.2 The "Why" — Problem Statement Addressed

Software solutions to the Critical Section Problem (Peterson's, Dekker's, Bakery algorithm) work correctly on **single-core CPUs with non-preemptive kernels** because the *atomicity* of memory loads and stores is implicitly guaranteed by the single execution pipeline. However, on modern systems we have:

- **Multi-core CPUs** with parallel pipelines
- **Out-of-order instruction execution**
- **Compiler instruction reordering**
- **CPU store buffers and cache coherency delays**

These factors make a non-atomic `load` → `check` → `store` sequence **vulnerable to interleaving** between cores. The solution is to push atomicity **down into the hardware itself**, which is what Synchronization Hardware does.

---

## 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogy — *The Bathroom Key with an Electric Lock*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Smart Bathroom Key"**
>
> Imagine a corporate office has a single washroom that can be used by only one employee at a time. Instead of an *honor-system* sign-in sheet (software solution — easy to cheat), the door is fitted with an **electric deadbolt controlled by a single physical key chained to the inside of the door**:
>
> - The key can be **grabbed and inserted into the holder in a single, indivisible motion** (the `TestAndSet` atomic operation).
> - You can ONLY remove the key (and enter) if it is currently in the holder. If someone else already has it, your hand is automatically blocked.
> - There is no possibility of "two people grabbing the key at the same instant" — the physical mechanism enforces it.
>
> **This mechanical indivisibility is exactly what hardware atomic instructions provide in silicon.**

---

## 1.4 Categorical Summary

| Category | Component | Primary Use in KTU Syllabus |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Ordering** | Memory Barriers / Fences (`MFENCE`, `LFENCE`, `SFENCE`, `DMB`, `DSB`) | Enforce load/store ordering across CPUs |
| **Lock-Based Atomic** | `TestAndSet`, `Swap` | Build **spinlocks** with mutual exclusion |
| **Lock-Free Atomic** | `CompareAndSwap`, `CompareAndExchange` | Build **lock-free data structures** (stack, queue) |
| **Counter Atomic** | `FetchAndAdd` | Build **ticket locks** and **sequence counters** |
| **High-Level Wrapper** | `atomic_t`, `std::atomic<T>` | Language/OS level abstraction over hardware |

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology — Must-Know for KTU

> [!NOTE]
> - **Atomic Operation:** An operation that either completes entirely or does not happen at all from the perspective of any other processor.
> - **Race Condition:** Outcome depends on the non-deterministic interleaving of concurrent operations.
> - **Memory Barrier (Fence):** A CPU instruction that forces all pending memory operations to complete before proceeding.
> - **Acquire Semantics:** A memory-ordering rule ensuring all subsequent loads/stores occur *after* the fence.
> - **Release Semantics:** A memory-ordering rule ensuring all previous loads/stores are visible *before* the fence.
> - **Spinlock:** A lock that causes the process to *busy-wait* in a tight loop until acquisition succeeds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Critical-Section Requirements (Recap from Module 2.1)

For any synchronization solution (hardware or software) to be **correct**, the following three properties *must* be satisfied. These properties form the **evaluation checklist** KTU examiners use to award marks in 14-mark questions:

1. **Mutual Exclusion (ME)** — At any instant, at most **one** process may execute inside the critical section.
2. **Progress (P)** — If no process is in the critical section and some processes *want* to enter, only those processes not in their remainder section should participate in the decision of who enters next. The decision cannot be postponed indefinitely.
3. **Bounded Waiting (BW)** — There exists a **finite bound** on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter the critical section after a process has declared its intention to enter and before that process is actually granted entry. This prevents **starvation**.

---

## 2.2 The Hardware Atomic Instructions — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 `TestAndSet` Instruction

`TestAndSet` is a single **atomic (indivisible) hardware instruction** that performs two operations in one CPU cycle:

- **Tests** the current value of a memory word.
- **Sets** (modifies) the value to a new one.

It returns the **original (old) value** of the memory location to the caller. Critically, no other processor or device can read or write the memory location between the *test* and the *set* phases.

**Pseudo-Code Definition:**

```
boolean TestAndSet(boolean *target) {
    boolean rv = *target;     // read
    *target = true;           // unconditionally set
    return rv;                // return old value
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hardware guarantees that the read followed by the write happens as a **single atomic bus transaction**. This is the heart of `TestAndSet`-based mutual exclusion.

---

### 2.2.2 `CompareAndSwap` (CAS) Instruction

`CompareAndSwap` is a more *powerful* and *flexible* atomic instruction. It compares the contents of a memory location with an expected value; **only if** they match does it swap the memory location with a new value. The whole operation is atomic.

**Pseudo-Code Definition:**

```
int CompareAndSwap(int *value, int expected, int new_value) {
    int temp = *value;
    if (temp == expected) {
        *value = new_value;
    }
    return temp;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> CAS is the **cornerstone of modern lock-free programming**. It is the primitive underlying the Java `AtomicInteger`, the C++ `std::atomic<T>::compare_exchange`, and the Linux kernel `cmpxchg` family of instructions.

---

### 2.2.3 `FetchAndAdd` Instruction

`FetchAndAdd` atomically increments a memory location by a specified value and returns the **old value** of that location. It is the basis for **ticket locks** and **fair FIFO spinlocks**.

**Pseudo-Code Definition:**

```
int FetchAndAdd(int *target, int increment) {
    int temp = *target;
    *target = temp + increment;
    return temp;
}
```

---

### 2.2.4 `Swap` (Exchange) Instruction

`Swap` atomically exchanges the value at a memory location with a value supplied by the caller, returning the original value.

---

## 2.3 Memory Barriers (Memory Fences)

Modern CPUs and compilers reorder memory accesses for performance. Memory barriers are instructions that **forbid such reordering** across the barrier boundary.

| Fence Type | x86 Mnemonic | ARM Mnemonic | Enforced Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Fence** | `LFENCE` | `DMB LD` | All loads before fence complete before any load after |
| **Store Fence** | `SFENCE` | `DMB ST` | All stores before fence are visible before any store after |
| **Full Fence** | `MFENCE` | `DMB SY` | Both loads and stores cannot cross the barrier |
| **Compiler Barrier** | (none) | (none) | Prevents compiler reordering (`asm volatile("" ::: "memory")`) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula & property sheet** for solving any KTU problem on Synchronization Hardware.

| # | Item | Formal Statement / Formula | Notation / Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mutual Exclusion Invariant (TAS) | $\text{lock} = \text{true} \Rightarrow \text{only one process in CS}$ | $\text{lock} \in \{\text{true}, \text{false}\}$ |
| 2 | CAS Success Condition | $\text{CAS}(V, E, N) \text{ succeeds iff } V = E$ | $V, E, N \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 3 | CAS Atomic Update | $V \leftarrow N \text{ when } V = E$ | — |
| 4 | Ticket Lock Ordering | $\text{now\_serving} \le \text{ticket}$ | ensures **FIFO fairness** |
| 5 | Bounded Waiting Bound (TAS) | Bound $= n - 1$ where $n$ = number of processes | Bounded by **finite constant** |
| 6 | Progress (no process in CS) | Some waiting process enters in **finite steps** | — |
| 7 | Memory Order Strength | Sequential Consistency $>$ Acq/Rel $>$ Relaxed | $SC \supset AR \supset RL$ |
| 8 | Test Value of Lock | $\text{TestAndSet}(\text{lock})$ returns $\text{true}$ if held | — |
| 9 | Atomicity Guarantee | $\text{time}(read) + \text{time}(write) \le 1 \text{ bus transaction}$ | — |
| 10 | Boolean Lock | $\text{lock} \in \{0, 1\}$ initially $= 0$ | $\vert \text{lock} \vert = 1 \text{ bit}$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Synchronization Hardware is used in industry:**
> - **Linux Kernel Spinlocks:** Built on `cmpxchg` (CAS) and `xchg` (Swap) instructions (`arch_spin_lock` in `arch/x86/include/asm/spinlock.h`).
> - **Java Concurrency:** `AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet()` compiles to a `lock xadd` (x86 Fetch-and-Add) instruction.
> - **Database Engines:** Lock-free queues in RocksDB, LevelDB use CAS for high-throughput indexing.
> - **Operating System Schedulers:** Per-CPU run-queue manipulation in Linux's CFS uses atomic operations.
> - **Real-Time Systems:** Interrupt handlers use atomic flag manipulation to communicate with task context.

---

## 2.6 Comparative Analysis — Hardware vs. Software Solutions

| Property | Software (Peterson's) | Hardware (TAS / CAS) |
|---|---|---|
| Preemptive Kernel | ❌ Fails | ✅ Works |
| Multi-core CPU | ❌ Fails | ✅ Works |
| Compiler Reordering | ❌ Vulnerable | ✅ Immune (atomic) |
| CPU Reordering | ❌ Vulnerable | ⚠️ Needs memory barrier |
| Implementation Complexity | High (state machine) | Low (single instruction) |
| Performance | Higher overhead (memory fences needed anyway) | Lower overhead, scales well |
| Available on | All architectures | All modern CPUs |
| Supports Bounded Waiting | Yes (explicit turn variable) | Yes (with `waiting[]` array) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Mutual Exclusion Using `TestAndSet`

This is the **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**. We will show every line of reasoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** We declare a single shared global variable `lock` and a shared boolean array `waiting[0..n-1]`, both initialized to `false`.

### 3.1.1 Algorithm Statement

```
Shared variables:   boolean lock = false;
                    boolean waiting[n] = {false, false, ..., false};

Process Pi (for i = 0, 1, ..., n-1):
    do {
        waiting[i] = true;            // declare intent
        key = true;
        while (waiting[i] && key) {
            key = TestAndSet(&lock);  // busy-wait acquiring lock
        }
        waiting[i] = false;           // got into critical section

        /* ---------- CRITICAL SECTION ---------- */

        j = (i + 1) % n;              // bounded-wait: find next waiter
        while ((j != i) && !waiting[j]) {
            j = (j + 1) % n;
        }
        if (j == i) {
            lock = false;             // no one waiting → release lock
        } else {
            waiting[j] = false;       // hand the lock to process Pj
        }

        /* ---------- REMAINDER SECTION ---------- */
    } while (true);
```

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Proof of Mutual Exclusion

We need to prove that **no two processes can be in the critical section at the same time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof by Contradiction:**

**Step 1 — Setup of Contradiction:**
Assume, for contradiction, that two distinct processes $P_i$ and $P_j$ are both inside the critical section at the same instant $t$. Without loss of generality, suppose $P_i$ entered first.

**Step 2 — Condition for Entry:**
A process $P_i$ exits its `while` loop and enters the critical section **only if** the `TestAndSet(&lock)` call returns `false`. This means at the moment of the call, `lock == false`. Since `TestAndSet` is atomic, the very next instruction inside the call sets `lock = true` before returning.

**Step 3 — State of `lock` After $P_i$'s Entry:**
Immediately after $P_i$ enters the critical section, we have:

$$\text{lock} = \text{true}$$

**Step 4 — Atomicity Argument:**
Because `TestAndSet` is a single atomic hardware instruction, **no other process $P_j$ can simultaneously read `lock == false` and proceed to enter**. The bus transaction for `TestAndSet` is exclusive.

**Step 5 — What $P_j$ Must Observe:**
When $P_j$ eventually executes `TestAndSet(&lock)`, it will read the **current** value of `lock`, which has been set to `true` by $P_i$. Therefore, `TestAndSet` returns `true` to $P_j$, and $P_j$ remains stuck in its `while (waiting[j] && key)` loop.

**Step 6 — Reaching the Contradiction:**
For $P_j$ to be in the critical section, the `while` loop must have terminated, which requires `key == false`. But we just established that $P_j$ always sees `key == true` as long as $P_i$ is inside. This is a **direct contradiction**.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\text{Mutual Exclusion is satisfied.} \quad \blacksquare}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Proof of Progress

**Step 1 — Initial State:**
Suppose no process is in the critical section. We need to show that some process not in the remainder section will eventually be admitted.

**Step 2 — Lock State:**
If no process is in the critical section, then `lock` is either `true` (transient, set by a process about to release) or `false` (no one is acquiring).

**Step 3 — Waiting Array:**
At least one process $P_j$ must have `waiting[j] == true` (otherwise no one wants to enter, violating the precondition). For that $P_j$, when the lock is released, the next `TestAndSet` call will return `false` and $P_j$ will exit the spin loop.

**Step 4 — No Deadlock:**
The release code (the `if (j == i) ... else ...`) **always** either releases `lock` (if no one else is waiting) or hands off `waiting[j] = false` to a successor. In neither case is there a permanent dead state.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\text{Progress is satisfied.} \quad \blacksquare}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Proof of Bounded Waiting

**Step 1 — Define the "Worst-Case" Process:**
Let $P_i$ be a process that has declared `waiting[i] = true` and is currently spinning.

**Step 2 — Trace the Release Algorithm:**
When the current holder $P_k$ exits, it scans the array `waiting[]` cyclically starting at $k+1$ and continues until it finds the first index $j$ with `waiting[j] == true`. It then executes `waiting[j] = false`.

**Step 3 — The Cyclic Scan Bound:**
The number of indices scanned is at most $n - 1$ (since the scan terminates when it either finds a waiter or returns to $i$). Therefore, $P_i$ can be bypassed by at most $n - 1$ other processes before being admitted.

**Step 4 — Bounded Waiting Bound:**
$$\text{Maximum bypass count} = n - 1$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\text{Bounded Waiting is satisfied with bound } n-1. \quad \blacksquare}$$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Mutual Exclusion Using `CompareAndSwap` (CAS)

### 3.2.1 Algorithm

```
Shared:  int lock = 0;          // 0 = free, 1 = held
         boolean waiting[n] = {false};

Process Pi:
    do {
        waiting[i] = true;
        while (waiting[i]) {
            int expected = 0;
            if (CompareAndSwap(&lock, 0, 1) == 0) {
                // acquired the lock
                break;
            }
        }
        waiting[i] = false;

        /* CRITICAL SECTION */

        // Find next waiting process (bounded waiting)
        j = (i + 1) % n;
        while ((j != i) && !waiting[j]) {
            j = (j + 1) % n;
        }
        if (j == i) {
            lock = 0;          // no one waiting → release
        } else {
            waiting[j] = false; // hand off
        }

        /* REMAINDER SECTION */
    } while (true);
```

### 3.2.2 CAS Execution Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Execution Trace (n = 2, processes P0, P1):**

**Initial State:**
$$\text{lock} = 0, \quad \text{waiting}[0] = \text{false}, \quad \text{waiting}[1] = \text{false}$$

**Step 1:** P0 wants to enter → `waiting[0] = true`. P0 evaluates `CAS(&lock, 0, 1)`:

- Read current value of `lock` → $V = 0$.
- Compare with `expected = 0` → match!
- Write new value → `lock = 1`.
- Return old value → returns `0`.
- Condition `CAS(...) == 0` is **true** → break out of `while`, P0 enters CS.

**Step 2:** P1 wants to enter → `waiting[1] = true`. P1 evaluates `CAS(&lock, 0, 1)`:

- Read current value of `lock` → $V = 1$.
- Compare with `expected = 0` → **mismatch**!
- Do **not** write. Return current value → returns `1`.
- Condition `CAS(...) == 0` is **false** → stay in `while`, P1 spins.

**Step 3:** P0 exits CS. Scan finds `waiting[1] = true`. Executes `waiting[1] = false`. P1 sees its `waiting[1]` is now `false`, exits the `while (waiting[1])` loop and enters the CS.

**Verification of Mutual Exclusion:** At no instant were both P0 and P1 in the CS, because `lock` is the **gate** and only one CAS at a time can transition it from $0$ to $1$.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Ticket Lock Using `FetchAndAdd`

> [!NOTE]
> **Ticket Lock** guarantees **strict FIFO fairness** — processes are served in the exact order they requested the lock. It uses two atomic variables.

### 3.3.1 Algorithm

```
Shared:  int next_ticket = 0;       // next ticket to give
         int now_serving = 0;       // whose turn it is

Process Pi:
    my_ticket = FetchAndAdd(&next_ticket, 1);
    while (now_serving != my_ticket) {
        // busy-wait
    }

    /* CRITICAL SECTION */

    now_serving = now_serving + 1;  // (atomic write)

    /* REMAINDER SECTION */
```

### 3.3.2 Execution Trace

**Step 1:** P0 calls `FetchAndAdd(&next_ticket, 1)`:

- Old value of `next_ticket` = 0 → returned to P0 as `my_ticket = 0`.
- New value of `next_ticket` = 1 (atomic write).

**Step 2:** P1 calls `FetchAndAdd(&next_ticket, 1)`:

- Old value of `next_ticket` = 1 → returned to P1 as `my_ticket = 1`.
- New value of `next_ticket` = 2.

**Step 3:** P0 checks `now_serving (= 0) == my_ticket (= 0)` → true → enters CS.

**Step 4:** P1 checks `now_serving (= 0) == my_ticket (= 1)` → false → spins.

**Step 5:** P0 exits CS, sets `now_serving = 1`.

**Step 6:** P1 checks `now_serving (= 1) == my_ticket (= 1)` → true → enters CS.

$$\boxed{\text{Strict FIFO ordering is preserved.}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Atomic Instruction — Internal Bus Transaction Flow

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **indivisible bus transaction** that the CPU hardware enforces when executing a `TestAndSet` (or `CAS`) instruction across two CPU cores.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[CPU0 Core] --> B0[Issue TestAndSet Instruction]
    A1[CPU1 Core] --> B1[Issue TestAndSet Instruction]
    B0 --> C0[Arbitrate for System Bus]
    B1 --> C1[Wait for Bus Grant]
    C0 --> D0[Acquire Bus Lock]
    D0 --> E0[Read Lock Variable from Memory]
    E0 --> F0[Compute New Value]
    F0 --> G0[Write Lock Variable to Memory]
    G0 --> H0[Release Bus Lock]
    H0 --> I0[Return Old Value to CPU0]
    C1 --> J1[Bus Becomes Available]
    J1 --> K1[CPU1 Re-issues TestAndSet]
    K1 --> L1[Read Updated Lock Value]
    L1 --> M1[Return New Value to CPU1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** CPU0 and CPU1 both try to execute `TestAndSet` simultaneously. The system bus acts as a *single shared arbiter* — only one CPU can perform the read-modify-write sequence at a time. CPU1 is forced to retry. This **bus-level locking** is the physical mechanism that makes the instruction atomic.

---

## 4.2 Mutual Exclusion State Machine for `TestAndSet`-Based Lock

This diagram shows the **logical state transitions** of a process attempting to enter the critical section using a `TestAndSet` lock.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Remainder Section] --> A1[Set waiting i equals true]
    A1 --> A2[Spin: TestAndSet returns true]
    A2 --> A2
    A3[TestAndSet returns false] --> A4[Enter Critical Section]
    A4 --> A5[Set waiting i equals false]
    A5 --> A6[Scan for Next Waiting Process j]
    A6 --> A7{Is there a waiting j?}
    A7 -->|No| A8[Set lock equals false]
    A7 -->|Yes| A9[Set waiting j equals false]
    A8 --> R0
    A9 --> R0
```

---

## 4.3 Memory Hierarchy and Atomic Operation Cost

This block diagram maps **where atomic operations execute** within a modern CPU memory hierarchy and their relative performance cost.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1Cache["L1 Cache (per core, ~1 ns)"]
        L1A[Per-core Local State]
    end
    subgraph L2Cache["L2 Cache (per core, ~3-10 ns)"]
        L2A[Per-core Local State]
    end
    subgraph L3Cache["L3 Cache (shared, ~10-30 ns)"]
        L3A[Shared Coherent State]
    end
    subgraph MainMemory["Main Memory (DRAM, ~50-100 ns)"]
        MMA[Shared Global Variable]
    end
    L1Cache --> L2Cache --> L3Cache --> MainMemory
    A1[Atomic CAS on local cached line] -.->|Cheap, ~1-5 ns| L1Cache
    A2[Atomic CAS on shared line with cache miss] -.->|Expensive, ~100-300 ns| L3Cache
    A3[Atomic CAS crossing cores with memory fence] -.->|Very expensive, ~500+ ns| MainMemory
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight (Cache Line Bouncing):** When two cores repeatedly CAS on the same variable, the cache line is constantly invalidated and refetched between their L1 caches — this is called *cache line bouncing* and is a major performance pitfall. Hence, modern software minimizes the duration that a cache line is held in *Modified* state.

---

## 4.4 CAS Retry Loop — The Lock-Free Pattern

This flowchart shows the canonical **CAS retry loop** used in lock-free algorithms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start CAS Operation] --> B1[Load Current Value of V]
    B1 --> B2{Does V equal Expected?}
    B2 -->|Yes| B3[Atomically Write New Value to V]
    B3 --> B4[Return Success]
    B2 -->|No| B5[Update Expected to Current V]
    B5 --> B6[Retry: Jump Back to Load]
    B6 --> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This pattern, called **CAS-retry** or **compare-retry loop**, is the backbone of the Java `AtomicInteger.compareAndSet()` and Linux kernel's `cmpxchg`-based algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the `CompareAndSwap` atomic instruction. Explain its significance in achieving mutual exclusion.**

**Model Answer:**

The `CompareAndSwap` (CAS) instruction is a hardware-supported atomic operation that performs the following sequence **indivisibly** in a single bus transaction:

1. It reads the current value `V` of a memory location.
2. It compares `V` with an `expected` value `E` provided by the caller.
3. If and only if `V == E`, it writes a `new_value` `N` to that location.
4. It returns the original value of `V` to the caller.

**Formal signature:** `int CAS(int *V, int E, int N)`.

**Significance in Mutual Exclusion:**
- CAS is **atomic at the hardware level**, hence it cannot be interrupted or interleaved between two CPU cores. This directly satisfies the **Mutual Exclusion** property of the critical section problem.
- Unlike `TestAndSet`, CAS is **lock-free** — it does not require a process to spin indefinitely; a process that fails the comparison simply retries.
- It is the foundation of all modern **lock-free concurrent data structures** (lock-free stacks, queues, hash tables) used in operating systems and high-performance servers.
- CAS enables the construction of spinlocks with **bounded waiting** by combining it with a `waiting[]` array.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always mention *all three* parameters (V, E, N) and the *atomicity* guarantee. 3 marks are typically awarded as: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for signature, 1 mark for significance.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a memory barrier? Why is it required in modern multi-core processors?**

**Model Answer:**

A **memory barrier** (also called a *memory fence*) is a hardware instruction that forces the CPU and/or the compiler to complete all pending memory operations (loads and/or stores) before proceeding to subsequent operations, thereby preventing **reordering** of memory accesses across the barrier.

**Why it is required:**
1. **Out-of-Order Execution:** Modern CPUs reorder instructions for performance. Without fences, the apparent order of memory operations may differ from the program order.
2. **Multi-Core Cache Coherency:** Each core has its own L1/L2 cache. A store on one core may not be visible to another core without a fence. Fences ensure **visibility**.
3. **Compiler Optimizations:** Compilers may also reorder instructions. Fences (and `volatile`, `atomic` qualifiers) inhibit such reordering.
4. **Release-Acquire Semantics:** Fences enable correct implementation of synchronization primitives (locks, semaphores) on relaxed memory models like ARM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always cite an example: e.g., on x86, `MFENCE` is needed after a `lock xchg` to ensure that the data protected by the lock becomes visible to other cores before the lock is released.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the `TestAndSet` instruction and show how mutual exclusion can be achieved using `TestAndSet` with bounded waiting. Prove the correctness of the solution with respect to all three requirements of the critical section problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast `TestAndSet` and `CompareAndSwap` instructions. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other in an OS kernel design? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of `TestAndSet` (1.5 marks):**
`TestAndSet` is an atomic hardware instruction that reads the value of a boolean memory location and sets it to `true`, returning the original value, all in a single indivisible operation. The atomicity is guaranteed by locking the system bus or using a cache coherency protocol (e.g., MESI).

**Step 2 — Algorithm (1.5 marks):** Present the algorithm given in Section 3.1.1 above, with the `lock` and `waiting[]` shared variables.

**Step 3 — Proof of Mutual Exclusion (1.5 marks):** Use the contradiction proof from Section 3.1.2. Key point: only the process that finds `lock == false` via `TestAndSet` enters the CS, and it immediately sets `lock = true` atomically.

**Step 4 — Proof of Progress (1 mark):** Argument from Section 3.1.3 — if no one is in the CS and someone is waiting, the holder will release the lock and the next waiting process will acquire it in finite steps.

**Step 5 — Proof of Bounded Waiting (1.5 marks):** Use the cyclic scan argument from Section 3.1.4. The bound is $n - 1$ — a process can be bypassed at most $n - 1$ times.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | `TestAndSet` | `CompareAndSwap` |
|---|---|---|
| Atomicity | Atomic (indivisible) | Atomic (indivisible) |
| Operation | Unconditional set to `true` | Conditional set only if value matches |
| Returns | Old value (always) | Old value (always) |
| Lock-Free Capable | No (always locks) | **Yes** (lock-free data structures) |
| Use in Mutex | Simple spinlock | Spinlock or lock-free |
| ABA Problem | N/A | **Vulnerable** (needs version counter) |
| Hardware Cost | 1 instruction | 1 instruction (more complex microcode) |
| Typical Use | Simple spinlocks | Lock-free stacks/queues, atomic counters |

**When to prefer `TestAndSet` (1.5 marks):**
- For **simple spinlocks** in OS kernel bottom halves and interrupt handlers where the lock is held for very short durations.
- When simplicity and minimal code size are prioritized (e.g., bootloader code).
- When a **boolean lock** (held or not-held) is sufficient — no need for value-based semantics.

**When to prefer `CompareAndSwap` (2 marks):**
- When implementing **lock-free concurrent data structures** (stacks, queues, hash tables).
- When implementing **atomic counters and sequence numbers** where the value carries semantic meaning, not just lock state.
- In **read-mostly workloads** with **occasional updates**, because CAS allows readers to proceed without spinning.
- In modern OS kernels (Linux, Windows NT) for **RCU** (Read-Copy-Update) and **seqlocks**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students write `TestAndSet` and `Swap` as two different instructions. While they are technically distinct (Swap exchanges two values), both are valid atomic primitives. Do **not** confuse them. Also, do **not** write a "lock-free" algorithm using `TestAndSet` — it is fundamentally **not** lock-free, as one process will always succeed and others must spin.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With neat diagrams and code, explain the `FetchAndAdd` atomic instruction. Show how it can be used to implement a **ticket lock** that guarantees strict FIFO ordering. Prove that the ticket lock satisfies mutual exclusion and bounded waiting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are memory barriers? Explain the different types of memory barriers (load fence, store fence, full fence) with suitable examples from the x86 and ARM architectures. Discuss why memory barriers are necessary in modern OS synchronization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of `FetchAndAdd` (1 mark):**
`FetchAndAdd(&var, val)` atomically increments the value at `var` by `val` and returns the **old** value of `var`.

**Step 2 — Ticket Lock Algorithm (2 marks):** Present the algorithm from Section 3.3.1.

**Step 3 — Why FIFO (1 mark):**
Each process obtains a unique, monotonically increasing ticket via `FetchAndAdd`. The process is served when `now_serving` equals its ticket. Since tickets are issued in order, service is also in order → **strict FIFO**.

**Step 4 — Proof of Mutual Exclusion (1.5 marks):**
At any instant, exactly one ticket number equals `now_serving`. Only the process holding that ticket can enter the CS. Others spin until their number is reached. Therefore, no two processes can be in the CS simultaneously.

**Step 5 — Proof of Bounded Waiting (1.5 marks):**
Each process waits for at most $(n - 1)$ other processes ahead of it in the queue (where $n$ is the number of contending processes). Once `now_serving` reaches its ticket, it enters. The bound is finite → **Bounded Waiting satisfied**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A memory barrier is a class of CPU instructions that constrain the reordering of memory operations by the CPU and/or the compiler, ensuring memory ordering guarantees across cores.

**Types of Memory Barriers (3.5 marks):**

| Type | x86 Mnemonic | ARM Mnemonic | Function | Example Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Fence** | `LFENCE` | `DMB LD` | All prior loads complete before any subsequent load | Reading shared flag before reading data |
| **Store Fence** | `SFENCE` | `DMB ST` | All prior stores are globally visible before any subsequent store | Publishing data before releasing lock |
| **Full Fence** | `MFENCE` | `DMB SY` | Both load and store ordering enforced | Around `lock`/`unlock` operations |

**Why Necessary in Modern OS Synchronization (2 marks):**
1. **Multi-core CPU Reordering:** Without barriers, a `lock` release on core A may be observed by core B **before** the data protected by that lock. Fences prevent this.
2. **Store Buffering:** CPUs use store buffers to defer writes. A fence flushes the buffer.
3. **Compiler Reordering:** The compiler can move loads and stores across statements. A `volatile` qualifier or explicit fence inhibits this.
4. **OS Kernel Primitives:** Spinlocks, semaphores, and RCU all rely on barriers for correctness. Linux's `smp_mb()` macro emits a barrier.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often confuse memory barriers with **mutexes** or **atomic operations**. A memory barrier does **not** lock anything — it only constrains *order*. Also, do not claim that x86 is "strongly ordered" and therefore needs no fences — this is **wrong**. x86 has strong ordering for loads, but stores can still be reordered, so `SFENCE`/`MFENCE` are still required in certain kernel code paths.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist for Synchronization Hardware**

- **Synchronization Hardware** provides *atomic* read-modify-write instructions that cannot be interrupted, even on multi-core preemptive systems.
- The **three main hardware atomic instructions** are: `TestAndSet`, `CompareAndSwap` (CAS), and `FetchAndAdd`.
- `TestAndSet` is the simplest — it *unconditionally* sets a lock to `true` and returns the old value.
- `CompareAndSwap` is *conditional* — it writes only if the current value matches the expected value. It is the basis of **lock-free programming**.
- `FetchAndAdd` atomically increments a counter and returns the old value. It is the basis of **ticket locks** with strict FIFO fairness.
- **Bounded Waiting bound for `TestAndSet` solution:** $n - 1$, where $n$ is the number of processes.
- **Ticket Lock FIFO guarantee:** Each process gets a unique monotonically increasing ticket; service is in the order of ticket issuance.
- **Memory barriers** are *not locks* — they only constrain the *order* of memory operations. They come in three flavors: load, store, and full fence.
- x86 has *strong ordering* (few fences needed in user space), but ARM is *weakly ordered* and requires explicit `DMB`/`DSB` instructions.
- **Software solutions (Peterson's, Dekker's) fail on multi-core systems** — they assume a non-preemptive single-core environment, which is obsolete in modern hardware.
- The **CAS-retry loop** is the universal pattern: `do { old = V; } while (CAS(&V, old, new) != old);`
- **ABA Problem:** A known pitfall of CAS where a value cycles `A → B → A` between the read and the CAS, causing a spurious success. Solved using a 64-bit versioned pointer (e.g., `double-width CAS`).
- **Cache Line Bouncing:** Repeated CAS on a single shared variable causes severe performance degradation due to cache invalidation traffic. Mitigated by minimizing the *critical section* duration and using **per-CPU variables**.
- **Spinlock = busy-wait lock** built on `TestAndSet` or `CAS`. Appropriate for *short* critical sections; inappropriate for *long* ones (use blocking mutexes instead).
- KTU examiners reward explicit *atomicity reasoning* — always emphasize that the operation is "indivisible" and "guaranteed by the bus/cache coherency protocol."
- Remember the three correctness criteria in order: **Mutual Exclusion → Progress → Bounded Waiting** — examiners expect proofs of all three.
- **Linux kernel** uses `xchg` (Swap) for the simplest spinlocks and `cmpxchg` (CAS) for everything else.
- The term "**lock-free**" does **not** mean "wait-free" — a lock-free algorithm guarantees system-wide progress, but an individual thread may starve. **Wait-free** algorithms guarantee per-thread progress.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mutex Locks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mutex Locks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mutex Lock (Mutual Exclusion Lock):** A *mutex* is a synchronization primitive that enforces a **single-owner access policy** to a critical section. At any instant, exactly one thread/process can hold the lock; all other contending threads are forced to wait until the owner explicitly releases it. Formally, it is a software abstraction built on top of atomic hardware instructions (e.g., `test_and_set`, `compare_and_swap`) to guarantee the three critical-section requirements: **mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a mutex is described as a **locking primitive** with a strict *ownership* attribute — only the thread that *acquires* (locks) the mutex is permitted to *release* (unlock) it. This distinguishes a true mutex from a plain binary semaphore, which has no ownership constraint.

The mathematical model of a mutex state is:

$$S_{mutex} \in \{ 0,\ 1 \}$$

where $S_{mutex} = 0$ denotes the **unlocked (available)** state and $S_{mutex} = 1$ denotes the **locked (held)** state. The state transitions are governed by the *acquire()* and *release()* atomic operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **single-stall public restroom in a railway station**:

- The stall has **one key hanging on a hook outside**.
- If a person wants to use it, they must **pick up the key** (acquire lock) and go inside.
- While they are inside, **no one else can enter** — the missing key signals "occupied".
- When they leave, they **place the key back on the hook** (release lock), allowing the next person to take it.
- Crucially, **the same person who took the key must return it** (the ownership rule). A third party cannot "release" the key for someone else.

This is exactly how a mutex behaves in the kernel: one critical section, one key, one owner at a time.

## 1.3 Why Mutexes Are Needed

In a multitasking OS, multiple threads may simultaneously attempt to read/write a shared resource (e.g., a linked list, a hardware register, a kernel data structure). Without mutual exclusion, **race conditions** occur and the resource is left in an inconsistent state. Mutexes serialize access to such shared resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Semaphore (KTU 2024 Highlight):**
> A *counting semaphore* tracks a resource count and any thread can `signal()` it. A *mutex* is always binary and has the additional **ownership rule**: only the lock-holder can unlock. This makes mutexes safer for protecting critical sections and prevents accidental "release by stranger" bugs.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value / Meaning |
|---|---|
| Lock state | Binary: **locked (1) / unlocked (0)** |
| Owner thread | Unique TID (Thread ID) of holder |
| Wait-queue | FIFO list of blocked threads |
| Atomicity guarantee | **Single-instruction, indivisible** |
| Recursive count (PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE) | Integer counter, **default = 1** |
| Priority inheritance | Boolean flag, **default = 0 (off)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Operations

A mutex supports exactly two atomic operations:

1. **`acquire()` (also called `lock()`, `wait()`, `P()`)** — A thread invokes this to enter the critical section. If the lock is free, the thread takes ownership and proceeds. If the lock is held, the thread is **suspended (blocked)** until released.
2. **`release()` (also called `unlock()`, `signal()`, `V()`)** — Only the current owner may invoke this. It frees the lock and wakes one waiting thread (if any).

The correctness invariants enforced are:

- **Mutual Exclusion:** $\forall t_1 \neq t_2 \in T,\ \text{owner}(t) \text{ is unique at any instant}$.
- **Progress:** If no thread is in the critical section and some thread wishes to enter, only those threads not in their remainder section can compete.
- **Bounded Waiting:** A thread's wait time is bounded by $N$ entries, where $N$ is the number of contending threads.

## 2.2 Hardware Foundation: Atomic Instructions

A mutex cannot be implemented using ordinary load/store instructions — they are not atomic and can be interrupted mid-way. OS kernels rely on hardware-level atomic primitives:

### 2.2.1 `test_and_set` Instruction

$$TAS(x): \quad (old \leftarrow x;\ x \leftarrow 1;\ \text{return } old)$$

This is a single, **indivisible** machine instruction. Its atomicity is guaranteed by the CPU bus-locking mechanism (e.g., `LOCK` prefix on x86, `LDREX/STREX` on ARM).

### 2.2.2 `compare_and_swap` Instruction

$$CAS(x,\ expected,\ new): \quad \text{atomic}\{ \text{if } x = expected,\ \text{then } x \leftarrow new;\ \text{return } prev_x \}$$

A more powerful primitive than `test_and_set` because it allows *lock-free* algorithms.

### 2.2.3 `atomic_swap / exchange`

$$SWAP(x,\ value): \quad (old \leftarrow x;\ x \leftarrow value;\ \text{return } old)$$

Used in many RISC architectures (e.g., MIPS `ll/sc`, ARM `swp`).

## 2.3 The Why and How: Implementation Walk-Through

**Why do we need hardware support?** Because software-only mutual-exclusion algorithms (e.g., Peterson's, Dekker's) fail on modern out-of-order CPUs with multiple memory caches. Hardware atomics give a *total order* over lock operations.

**How does a mutex work internally?**

- A mutex is essentially a `struct` containing:
  - A **state flag** (locked/unlocked).
  - A **wait-queue head** (linked list of blocked threads).
  - An **owner field** (thread ID).
- When `acquire()` is called:
  1. The thread atomically tests the flag.
  2. If free, it sets the flag and records itself as owner.
  3. If busy, it is added to the wait-queue and the scheduler suspends it.
- When `release()` is called by the owner:
  1. The flag is cleared.
  2. The first thread in the wait-queue is moved to the *ready queue*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Equation / Pseudocode | Atomicity | Bounded Wait? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `test_and_set` | $TAS \to \{0,1\}$ | Hardware | No (naive) |
| `CAS` | $CAS(x,e,n) \to bool$ | Hardware | Yes (with loop) |
| Mutex state | $S \in \{0,1\}$ | N/A | N/A |
| Wait-queue length | $W(t) \le N - 1$ | N/A | Bounded |
| Spinlock wait | $T_{wait} = T_{CS} \times (N-1)$ | N/A | Bounded but wastes CPU |
| Blocking mutex wait | $T_{wait} = O(\text{context\_switch})$ | N/A | Efficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Mutexes are the backbone of every real-time OS (RTOS) — VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS — and every general-purpose kernel (Linux `futex`, Windows `ERESOURCE`, Pthreads). They protect file-system metadata, socket buffers, device-driver state, and database transactions. **Without mutexes, no concurrent system can be correct.**

## 2.5 Spinlock vs Blocking Mutex

| Property | Spinlock | Blocking Mutex |
|---|---|---|
| Waiting mechanism | **Busy-wait** (CPU spin) | **Suspend + sleep** in wait-queue |
| CPU consumption | High (100% core) | Low (only scheduler tick) |
| Context switches | None | At least 2 (sleep + wake) |
| Suitable for | **Very short** critical sections (< 10 µs) | **Long** critical sections |
| Kernel usage | Linux `spin_lock_irqsave()` | Pthreads `pthread_mutex_lock()` |

The choice is governed by the trade-off: spinning is cheaper for brief waits (avoids the overhead of two context switches), while blocking is mandatory for long waits to avoid wasting CPU cycles.

## 2.6 The Priority Inversion Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **Classic KTU pitfall:** Students often ignore priority inversion. It is a guaranteed 7–8 mark question in Part B.

Scenario: Threads at three priority levels H (high), M (medium), L (low). L holds mutex M. H is blocked on M. M preempts L (since M > L). Result: **H is effectively waiting on M, which is the lowest priority** — a *priority inversion* of unbounded duration.

**Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol.** When a high-priority thread blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority thread, the kernel **temporarily boosts the holder's priority** to that of the highest waiter. This prevents medium-priority threads from preempting the critical section. (Used in VxWorks, Linux RT-mutex, POSIX `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT`.)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Naive `test_and_set` Mutex (No Bounded Waiting)

We start from the hardware atomic instruction and build the simplest mutex:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardware: } & TAS(x) \text{ atomically returns old } x \text{ and sets } x \leftarrow 1. \\[4pt]
\text{Mutex variable: } & lock \in \{0, 1\},\ \text{initially } lock = 0. \\[4pt]
\text{Goal: } & \text{Ensure only one thread enters } CS \text{ at a time.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Initialise the lock to 0 (free).

$$lock \leftarrow 0$$

**Step 2:** Each thread's `acquire()` loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{do} \ \{\ & old \leftarrow TAS(lock);\  \\
& \ \} \ \text{while}(old == 1);
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Reasoning per iteration:
- If `old = 0`, the thread was the one that set `lock` to 1 ⇒ it has acquired the lock and exits the loop.
- If `old = 1`, another thread holds the lock ⇒ keep spinning.

**Step 4:** `release()` is a single atomic store:

$$lock \leftarrow 0$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate mutual-exclusion property. Suppose two threads $T_1, T_2$ call $TAS$ simultaneously. Because $TAS$ is atomic, exactly one call returns 0 and sets `lock = 1`; the other returns 1. Hence, only one thread proceeds. ✓

**Step 6:** Evaluate bounded-waiting property. This implementation **fails** bounded waiting — a thread that arrives later may repeatedly win the race, starving an earlier waiter.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bounded-Waiting Mutex using `test_and_set`

To enforce bounded waiting, we add a **waiting flag array** `waiting[i]` for each thread $i$ and a global `next` ticket variable:

**Step 1:** Data structures.

$$
waiting[0 \ldots N-1] \leftarrow false, \quad next \leftarrow 0
$$

**Step 2:** Thread $i$'s `acquire()`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{do}\ \{ \ & waiting[i] \leftarrow true; \\
& key \leftarrow 1; \\
& \text{while}(key == 1 \ \text{and}\ waiting[i])\ \ key \leftarrow TAS(lock); \\
& waiting[i] \leftarrow false; \ \\
& \}\ \text{while}(key \neq 0);
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Reasoning. Thread $i$ announces it is waiting (`waiting[i] = true`), then spins on `TAS(lock)`. Once it acquires the lock (`key = 0`), it resets its flag and proceeds.

**Step 4:** Thread $i$'s `release()`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
j \leftarrow (i + 1) \bmod N; \\
\text{while}(j \neq i \ \text{and}\ \neg waiting[j])\ \ j \leftarrow (j + 1) \bmod N; \\
\text{if } j = i:\ \ lock \leftarrow 0; \\
\text{else}:\ \ waiting[j] \leftarrow false;
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluation. The releasing thread scans the circular array for the *next* waiting thread. It either unlocks the lock (if nobody is waiting) or directly hands the lock to thread $j$ by clearing `waiting[j]`. This guarantees that a thread waits at most $N - 1$ turns. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Correctness Invariant Proof

We define the invariant for the bounded-waiting mutex:

$$
I:\ \big(\text{exactly one thread in } CS\big) \ \lor\ \big(\text{lock} = 0 \ \land\ \text{all waiting}[i] = false\big)
$$

**Step 1:** Initially, $lock = 0$ and all $waiting[i] = false$. Invariant $I$ holds. ✓

**Step 2:** Assume $I$ holds. When thread $i$ enters the `do…while` loop, the only way out is via $key = 0$ from $TAS$. Because $TAS$ is atomic, no other thread can simultaneously observe $key = 0$. Hence, only thread $i$ can transition from outside the CS to inside. $I$ is preserved. ✓

**Step 3:** When thread $i$ exits, `release()` either sets `lock = 0` (preserving the right disjunct) or clears some `waiting[j]` (still no thread in CS). $I$ holds. ✓

**Step 4:** By induction, $I$ holds at all times, proving **mutual exclusion**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Acquire/Release Decision Table

| Thread $T_i$ Action | Pre-state ($lock, waiting[i]$) | Atomic Op | Post-state | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| First call, lock free | $(0, false)$ | $TAS = 0$ | $(1, false)$ | $T_i$ enters CS |
| First call, lock held | $(1, false)$ | $TAS = 1$ | $(1, true)$ | $T_i$ spins |
| Spin succeeds | $(0, true)$ | $TAS = 0$ | $(1, false)$ | $T_i$ enters CS |
| Owner releases | $(1, false)$ | $lock \leftarrow 0$ | $(0, false)$ | CS free |
| Owner releases, waiter exists | $(1, false)$ | $waiting[j] \leftarrow false$ | $(1, false)$ | $T_j$ wakes |
| Recursive lock (same thread) | $(1, true)$ | Increment counter | $(1, true,\ c{+}1)$ | Allowed in `PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE` |

## 3.5 Cost Analysis

For a single acquire/release cycle, the cost is:

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{TAS} + T_{CS} + T_{release}
$$

where:
- $T_{TAS}$ = single atomic instruction (≈ 1–50 ns on modern CPUs)
- $T_{CS}$ = time spent in critical section
- $T_{release}$ = atomic store + wait-queue scan = $O(N)$ in naive bounded form, $O(1)$ with `futex`-style kernels (Linux)

For $N$ contending threads and average CS time $T_{CS}$:

$$
T_{avg\_wait} = \frac{(N-1) \cdot T_{CS}}{N} \quad \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} \quad T_{CS}
$$

This is the well-known *linearisation* property of fair mutexes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mutex State Transition Diagram

The lifecycle of a mutex object from the kernel's perspective is shown below. Each node represents a stable state, and each edge is triggered by an atomic operation.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unlocked
    Unlocked --> Locked : acquire() by thread T1
    Locked --> Locked : acquire() by T2 (blocked, queued)
    Locked --> Unlocked : release() by T1
    Unlocked --> Locked : acquire() by T2 (woken from queue)
    Unlocked --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- `Unlocked` — the mutex is free; the next `acquire()` returns immediately.
- `Locked` — exactly one thread is the owner; other threads sit in the wait-queue.
- Edges labelled `acquire()` / `release()` are atomic and indivisible.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mutex Object

The internal anatomy of a `mutex_t` structure and how it interacts with the thread scheduler is shown in the following block diagram. This is the architecture a kernel (Linux `futex`, Windows `ERESOURCE`, VxWorks `semM`) actually uses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef state fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef queue fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef hw fill:#E6FFED,stroke:#0F7A2E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef flow fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    APICaller[Calling Thread Tcall]:::flow
    AcquireOp[acquire function]:::flow
    ReleaseOp[release function]:::flow
    StateFlag[State Flag 0 or 1]:::state
    OwnerField[Owner TID field]:::state
    WaitQueue[Wait Queue FIFO]:::queue
    Scheduler[Kernel Scheduler]:::flow
    Hardware[Hardware Atomic Instruction test_and_set or CAS]:::hw

    APICaller --> AcquireOp
    APICaller --> ReleaseOp

    AcquireOp --> StateFlag
    AcquireOp --> Hardware
    Hardware --> StateFlag

    AcquireOp --> OwnerField
    ReleaseOp --> OwnerField
    ReleaseOp --> StateFlag

    AcquireOp -- if locked --> WaitQueue
    WaitQueue --> Scheduler
    Scheduler -. wakes .-> WaitQueue
    ReleaseOp -- picks first --> WaitQueue
```

**Interpretation of the blocks:**

- `acquire function` — the user-visible API; it consults the *State Flag* via the *Hardware Atomic Instruction* (because plain reads are not safe).
- `State Flag` — the boolean core of the mutex.
- `Owner TID field` — enforces the *ownership* rule (only the owner may call `release()`).
- `Wait Queue` — doubly-linked list of blocked threads, managed by the kernel scheduler.
- `Hardware Atomic Instruction` — the indivisible CPU primitive that makes everything safe.

## 4.3 Flow Sequence: Two Threads Contending

The chronological sequence of operations when two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ race for the same mutex is depicted below. This is a *functional interaction* diagram — a substitute for a physical drawing and the format most readable on a phone or printout.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant T1 as Thread T1
    participant MX as Mutex M
    participant SCH as Scheduler
    participant T2 as Thread T2

    T1->>MX: acquire(M)
    MX-->>T1: success (owner=T1)
    Note over T1: enters critical section
    T2->>MX: acquire(M)
    MX-->>T2: blocked, added to wait-queue
    SCH-->>T2: suspend T2
    Note over T1: finishes critical section
    T1->>MX: release(M)
    MX->>SCH: wake T2 from queue
    SCH-->>T2: resume
    T2->>MX: acquire(M) (re-try)
    MX-->>T2: success (owner=T2)
    Note over T2: enters critical section
    T2->>MX: release(M)
    MX-->>T2: success (state=free)
```

**Reading the sequence:**

1. $T_1$ grabs the mutex first and enters its critical section.
2. $T_2$ tries to grab it; the mutex blocks $T_2$ and parks it in the wait-queue.
3. The scheduler **suspends** $T_2$ — it does not consume CPU.
4. $T_1$ finishes, calls `release()`. The kernel **wakes** $T_2$.
5. $T_2$ re-runs `acquire()`, succeeds, and enters its critical section.
6. $T_2$ releases. Mutex is free.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Priority Inheritance Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef high fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef med fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef low fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mtx fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    Th[Thread H priority 90]:::high
    Tm[Thread M priority 60]:::med
    Tl[Thread L priority 30]:::low
    Mx[Mutex M held by L]:::mtx
    Boost[Priority Boost L from 30 to 90]:::high

    Tl -->|holds| Mx
    Th -->|waits on| Mx
    Tm -.->|preempts normally| Tl
    Mx --> Boost
    Boost -->|prevents| Tm
```

This shows the *Priority Inheritance Protocol*: the moment $H$ blocks on the mutex held by $L$, the kernel **boosts** $L$'s effective priority to match $H$, preventing $M$ from sneaking in and causing unbounded inversion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a mutex lock. How is it different from a binary semaphore?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *mutex lock* (mutual exclusion lock) is a synchronization primitive that allows only one thread to access a critical section at a time. It has two operations: `acquire()` and `release()`.

The **key difference from a binary semaphore** is the *ownership* rule. In a mutex, only the thread that performed `acquire()` is allowed to perform `release()`. In a binary semaphore, any thread can `signal()` (release) the lock, even if it never acquired it. Mutexes also typically maintain a *wait-queue* with priority inheritance, whereas simple binary semaphores do not.

This makes mutexes **safer** for protecting critical sections because they prevent accidental "release-by-stranger" defects that commonly cause race conditions in semaphore-based code. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is priority inversion? Mention any one solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Priority inversion* is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority thread is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority thread, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of the two threads. The classic scenario involves three threads H, M, L (high, medium, low priority): L holds a mutex that H needs, and M preempts L, blocking H for an unbounded duration.

**Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol.** When a high-priority thread $H$ blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority thread $L$, the OS temporarily **boosts** $L$'s priority to that of $H$ until $L$ releases the mutex. This prevents medium-priority threads from preempting $L$ and bound the inversion time. Used in VxWorks, POSIX `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT`, and Linux RT-mutex. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the implementation of a mutex lock using the `test_and_set` atomic instruction. Show clearly how mutual exclusion is guaranteed and why the naive version does not satisfy bounded waiting.

**(b) [7 marks]** Modify the naive `test_and_set` implementation to satisfy bounded waiting. Provide the pseudocode for `acquire()` and `release()` and explain its operation.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition of `test_and_set`** — A single, indivisible hardware instruction that reads a memory location, stores 1 into it, and returns the old value:

$$
TAS(x):\ (old \leftarrow x;\ x \leftarrow 1;\ \text{return } old)
$$

**Naive Mutex Pseudocode:**

```text
int lock = 0;

acquire() {
    while (test_and_set(&lock) == 1) ;   // spin until lock was 0
}

release() {
    lock = 0;
}
```

**Mutual exclusion proof:** Since `test_and_set` is atomic, if two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ call it simultaneously, only one call can complete first. That call returns 0 (the lock was free) and sets `lock = 1`. The other call returns 1. Hence only one thread can pass the `while` loop and enter the critical section. **(3 marks)**

**Failure of bounded waiting:** The naive version has no concept of "turn" or "ticket". A thread that arrives later may repeatedly win the race if it is faster, causing an earlier thread to spin forever. Hence bounded waiting is **not satisfied**. **(4 marks for proof + analysis)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

We add an array `waiting[0...N-1]` and a global `next` ticket:

```text
boolean waiting[N] = {false};
int lock = 0;
int next = 0;

acquire(int i) {
    waiting[i] = true;
    int key = 1;
    while ((key == 1) && (waiting[i]))
        key = test_and_set(&lock);
    waiting[i] = false;
}

release(int i) {
    int j = (i + 1) % N;
    while ((j != i) && (!waiting[j]))
        j = (j + 1) % N;
    if (j == i)
        lock = 0;
    else
        waiting[j] = false;
}
```

**Working explanation:**

1. Thread $i$ announces it is waiting by setting `waiting[i] = true`.
2. It then attempts `test_and_set(&lock)`. If the lock was free, $i$ becomes the owner and clears its flag.
3. In `release()`, the exiting thread scans circularly for the next waiting thread $j$. If found, it directly hands the lock to $j$ by clearing `waiting[j]`. If no one is waiting, it sets `lock = 0`.
4. **Bounded waiting:** A thread waits at most $N-1$ turns because the circular scan visits each thread at most once before reaching the next waiter. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** What is priority inversion? Explain with a suitable example involving three threads. Why is it a problem in real-time systems?

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** and the **Priority Ceiling Protocol** as solutions to priority inversion. Compare them.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:** Priority inversion occurs when a higher-priority task is forced to wait for the execution of a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" the priorities. **(2 marks)**

**Three-thread example:**

Consider three threads with priorities:
- $T_H$ (priority 90, high)
- $T_M$ (priority 60, medium)
- $T_L$ (priority 30, low)

All three share a mutex $M$.

**Sequence of events:**

1. $T_L$ starts executing, acquires $M$, and enters its critical section.
2. $T_H$ becomes runnable, preempts $T_L$, but immediately **blocks on $M$** (held by $T_L$).
3. $T_M$ becomes runnable, preempts $T_L$ (since $T_M$'s priority 60 > $T_L$'s effective priority 30).
4. $T_M$ runs to completion, then $T_L$ resumes, finishes its CS, releases $M$.
5. Finally $T_H$ can run.

The **problem:** $T_H$, the highest priority task, was kept waiting for an unbounded duration controlled by $T_M$ and $T_L$. In a hard real-time system, this violates the deadline guarantee. The famous real-world instance is the *Mars Pathfinder* mission (1997), where a watchdog reset occurred due to priority inversion. **(5 marks for example + problem statement)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

When thread $H$ blocks on a mutex held by thread $L$, the kernel **temporarily raises** $L$'s priority to that of $H$ (or the highest waiter). $L$ runs at this boosted priority until it releases the mutex, after which its priority reverts to the original. This prevents $M$ from preempting $L$ and bounds the inversion to at most the duration of one CS. **(3 marks)**

**Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):**

Each mutex is assigned a *priority ceiling* equal to the highest priority of any thread that may lock it. A thread may acquire a mutex only if its priority is **strictly higher** than the ceilings of all mutexes currently held by other threads. This prevents deadlocks and bounds blocking to at most one CS. **(2 marks)**

**Comparison table:**

| Aspect | Priority Inheritance | Priority Ceiling |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Dynamic — only on actual blocking | Static — set at mutex creation |
| Boost mechanism | Temporary, on lock acquisition | Implicit, by ceiling comparison |
| Deadlock prevention | No (chains possible) | Yes |
| Implementation | Simpler | More complex (requires static analysis) |
| Blocking bound | One CS per waiter (transitively) | One CS, in total |
| Used in | VxWorks, POSIX, Linux RT-mutex | Ada Ravenscar, RT-Java, real-time POSIX |

**(2 marks for the comparison)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Mutex questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing mutex with binary semaphore.** Many answers mention only the binary nature and miss the *ownership* rule. Examiner deducts **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting bounded waiting.** The naive `test_and_set` satisfies *mutual exclusion* but **not** bounded waiting. Students who claim it does are marked down. Always state both properties explicitly.
> 3. **No mention of atomicity.** A correct solution must explicitly say *why* `test_and_set`/`CAS` works — it is a **single, indivisible hardware instruction**. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Priority inversion answer is too short.** Examiner expects the *three-thread scenario* (H, M, L), a description of the unbounded wait, and a real-world example (Mars Pathfinder). A one-line answer is worth at most 1 mark.
> 5. **Pseudocode syntax errors in Part B.** Use clean `acquire()` / `release()` blocks. Mixing it with C-language syntax errors in the exam is penalised.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **mutex** is a binary lock with **ownership** — only the lock-holder can unlock.
- It is implemented atop **hardware atomic instructions**: `test_and_set`, `compare_and_swap`, or `atomic_swap`.
- Mutual exclusion = atomicity of the lock-test-and-set operation.
- **Bounded waiting** requires additional state (`waiting[]` array or ticket queue); the naive spin-mutex is *unfair*.
- **Spinlock** = busy-wait, suitable for *short* critical sections in kernel interrupt handlers.
- **Blocking mutex** = suspend + scheduler wake, suitable for *long* critical sections in user-space threads.
- **Priority inversion** is the killer problem in RTOS; it is solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol** (PIP) or **Priority Ceiling Protocol** (PCP).
- Real-world manifestation: **Mars Pathfinder 1997** reset bug.
- Standard Pthreads constants:
  - `PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER`
  - `PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE`
  - `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` (POSIX option for PIP).
- Linux kernel uses `futex` (Fast Userspace muTEX) — a hybrid spin-then-block design.
- Windows kernel uses `ERESOURCE` and `KMUTEX` structures.
- A mutex is *not* a semaphore; do not substitute one for the other in exam answers.
- *Atomicity*, *ownership*, *bounded waiting* — these three words alone cover 60% of the marks in a typical KTU 14-mark question on mutexes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semaphores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Semaphore** is a fundamental synchronization primitive used in modern operating systems to control concurrent access to shared resources by multiple processes or threads. Proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** (1965), a semaphore is essentially a protected integer variable that can only be accessed through two atomic (indivisible) standard operations traditionally called **P** (Proberen / wait / down) and **V** (Verhogen / signal / up).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEOT521 – Module 2):** A semaphore is a non-negative integer variable on which exactly two standard atomic operations — **wait()** (equivalent to P) and **signal()** (equivalent to V) — are defined. It is the cornerstone of process synchronization taught in Module 2 of Modern Operating Systems.

### 1.2 Two Principal Variants of Semaphores

**1. Binary Semaphore (Mutex)**
A binary semaphore can take only the integer values **0** or **1**. It is functionally analogous to a mutex lock and is used for **mutual exclusion**, ensuring that only one process enters the critical section at a time.

**2. Counting Semaphore**
A counting semaphore can take any non-negative integer value (0, 1, 2, 3, ...). It is used to control access to a finite pool of **identical shared resources** (e.g., 5 database connections, 3 printers).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The One-Lane Bridge

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a narrow one-lane bridge that can hold at most **3 cars** at a time. A traffic controller stands at the entrance holding a counter starting at **3**.
> - Every car entering the bridge performs a **wait()** — the counter decrements (3 → 2 → 1 → 0).
> - Every car leaving performs a **signal()** — the counter increments (0 → 1 → 2 → 3).
> - When the counter reads **0**, the controller stops more cars from entering (the `wait()` operation blocks the process).
> - This is exactly how a **counting semaphore** with initial value 3 governs a resource pool of size 3.

This analogy makes it instantly intuitive why a semaphore solves both **mutual exclusion** (binary) and **resource counting** (counting) problems in concurrent systems.

### 1.4 Atomicity Guarantee
The critical property is that the **test-and-modify** of the semaphore value, plus the **context switch decision**, must be **uninterruptible**. In a single-processor system, this is achieved by disabling interrupts around the P/V code. In a multiprocessor system, it requires hardware support such as **atomic test-and-set** or **compare-and-swap (CAS)** instructions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Atomic Operations

The classical definitions of the **P (wait)** and **V (signal)** operations on a semaphore `S` are:

**wait(S) / P(S):**
1. Test the value of `S`.
2. If `S > 0`, decrement `S` by 1 and let the process continue.
3. If `S = 0`, block (sleep) the calling process and enqueue it on `S`'s waiting queue.

**signal(S) / V(S):**
1. Increment `S` by 1.
2. If there is at least one process blocked on `S`'s waiting queue, wake up (unblock) one of them according to the queue's policy (typically FIFO).

### 2.2 Why Semaphores Work — The Logic Behind the Mechanism

- **Mutual exclusion (binary semaphore):** Because `S` can only be 0 or 1, and only one process can successfully decrement it from 1 to 0, all other processes are forced to wait. When the holder executes `signal(S)`, the semaphore returns to 1, and exactly one waiter is unblocked.
- **Resource counting (counting semaphore):** The initial value represents the number of available resources. The semaphore never goes negative; instead, processes are queued. This prevents overflow access and starvation-free wake-ups (under FIFO discipline).
- **Condition synchronization:** A process can `wait()` on a semaphore initialized to 0, effectively blocking itself until another process `signal()`s the event.

### 2.3 Properties of a Well-Implemented Semaphore

- **Atomicity:** P and V execute indivisibly.
- **No busy waiting (blocking variant):** Blocked processes are suspended and removed from the ready queue, freeing the CPU.
- **No assumptions:** Semaphores do not assume the number of processes, their relative speeds, or the hardware.
- **Portability:** Higher-level constructs like monitors, mutexes, and condition variables are usually built on top of semaphores.

### 2.4 The Busy-Wait (Spinlock) vs. Blocking Semaphore

| Aspect | Busy-Wait Semaphore | Blocking Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Behaviour on contention | Loops consuming CPU | Process is suspended, CPU released |
| Implementation cost | Simple, no queue needed | Requires a PCB queue and OS scheduler |
| Suitability | Multiprocessors with short critical sections | Uniprocessor or long critical sections |
| Risk | Wastes CPU cycles, priority inversion | Slight overhead of context switch |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept / Equation | Expression / Definition | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| State invariant (binary) | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | Verify mutex correctness |
| State invariant (counting) | $S \ge 0$ | Resource pool of size $N$ initialized to $N$ |
| Initial value of mutex | $mutex = 1$ | Before any process enters CS |
| Initial value of resource sem | $resource = k$ | $k$ identical instances of a resource |
| Initial value of sync sem | $sync = 0$ | For event/condition ordering |
| Number of P–V pairs needed | $\ge 1$ per shared resource | Mutual exclusion of $n$ resources |
| Mutual exclusion condition | $\sum (\text{processes in CS}) \le 1$ | Prove correctness |
| Progress condition | No process outside CS blocks another | Prove liveness |
| Bounded waiting condition | A bounded number of processes can enter after a requesting process | Prove fairness (FIFO queue) |
| Deadlock equation | $S_1 = S_2 = \cdots = S_k = 0$ and circular wait | Diagnose deadlock in solution |
