- [ ] **Starvation mitigation:** Rollback count baked into the victim-selection cost function.
- [ ] **Modern usage:** Detection + Recovery is preferred over Avoidance in **DBMS, distributed systems, and cloud orchestrators** because the prevention overhead is too high.
- [ ] **Algorithm verification:** The detection algorithm is provably **sound** (no false positives) and **complete** (no false negatives). This is a KTU favourite for 2-mark theory questions.
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite trap question:** "If a process holds resources and is not requesting any, is it deadlocked?" **Answer:** No. It is *stuck* (no progress) but not in a circular wait. The detection algorithm correctly classifies it as non-deadlocked.
- [ ] **Distinguish carefully:** In the Banker's (avoidance) algorithm, the update is $\vec{Work} \leftarrow \vec{Work} + A[i]$ and the comparison is on the *Need* matrix. In the Detection algorithm, the update is the same, but the comparison is on the *Request* matrix $Q$.
- [ ] **KTU phrase to memorize:** "A cycle in the wait-for graph is a **necessary and sufficient** condition for deadlock in a single-instance resource system."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recovery from Deadlock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Recovery from Deadlock # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deadlock Recovery** is the set of techniques employed by the Operating System to break a deadlock condition **after it has been detected**, by forcibly reclaiming resources or terminating processes so that the system can resume forward progress. Unlike *prevention* or *avoidance* which act *before* the deadlock, recovery acts *after* the fact, accepting that the deadlock has already occurred. Mathematically, once a deadlock is detected through a **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** with cycles (or a **Wait-For Graph (WFG)**), the OS must take corrective action. The recovery is measured by the principle of **minimum cost**, where the cost is a function of: $$C_{recovery} = f(P_{terminated}, R_{preempted}, R_{rolled\_back}, L_{starvation})$$ where $P_{terminated}$ is the number of processes killed, $R_{preempted}$ is the number of resources forcibly taken, $R_{rolled\_back}$ is the number of processes restored to a safe state, and $L_{starvation}$ is the long-term impact on process fairness. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Traffic Gridlock** > > Imagine a narrow one-way street where four cars arrive from four different directions **simultaneously**. Each car is waiting for the car in front to move, but none can move because they are blocking each other. The traffic policeman (the Operating System) now has two choices: > > 1. **Process Termination Analogy:** Tow away one or more cars (kill processes) to unblock the rest. > 2. **Resource Preemption Analogy:** Push one car backwards (preempt and rollback) so the others can move forward. > > Either way, the policeman does *not* prevent the gridlock from forming — he solves it *after* it has already jammed the intersection. ## 1.3 When is Recovery Used? The OS uses recovery techniques when: - **Detection** has confirmed a deadlock (e.g., a cycle in the WFG). - **Prevention** and **Avoidance** are either too expensive or impractical (e.g., in interactive systems or real-time databases). - The cost of running deadlock-handling algorithms continuously (like Banker's Algorithm) is higher than the cost of *tolerating* an occasional deadlock and recovering. ## 1.4 Boundary Conditions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Favourites):** > - **Minimum processes for deadlock = 4** in a single-instance resource system, **3** with circular wait. > - **Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait** = the **4 Coffman Conditions**. > - Recovery is the **4th and final** strategy in the deadlock handling hierarchy: **Ignore → Prevent → Avoid → Detect → Recover**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Deadlock Recovery Hierarchy The Operating System has **two main families** of recovery methods. The choice between them is dictated by the **system type** (batch vs. interactive vs. real-time). ### 2.1.1 Family A — Process Termination The OS forcibly aborts one or more deadlocked processes to break the cycle. There are three sub-strategies: **Strategy 1: Abort All Deadlocked Processes (Total Termination)** - Pros: Definitive; the cycle is **mathematically guaranteed** to break. - Cons: Extremely expensive; all work done by killed processes is discarded. **Strategy 2: Abort One Process at a Time (Incremental Termination)** - After each abort, the **detection algorithm is re-invoked** to check if the deadlock is resolved. - Pros: Less destructive. - Cons: High overhead due to repeated detection runs. **Strategy 3: Abort Based on Selection Criteria (Selective Termination)** Select the victim process $P_v$ such that the cost of aborting is minimized: $$Cost(P_v) = w_1 \cdot P_{priority} + w_2 \cdot P_{runtime} + w_3 \cdot P_{resources\_held} + w_4 \cdot P_{remaining\_work} - w_5 \cdot P_{type}$$ where the weights $w_i$ are tunable. **Batch processes** are preferred victims over **interactive** ones. ### 2.1.2 Family B — Resource Preemption The OS forcibly takes a resource away from a process. Three sub-issues arise: 1. **Selecting a Victim** — which process and which resource to preempt? (Same cost function as above). 2. **Rollback** — the preempted process must be rolled back to a **safe state**. Since a true safe state is often hard to define, the OS rolls the process back to a **checkpoint** and restarts it. The general total-rollback cost is: $$C_{rollback} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{process\_i} + C_{resources\_held\_by\_i} \right)$$ 3. **Starvation** — repeated preemption of the *same* process is prevented by tracking a **preemption counter** in the process's PCB. The OS refuses to select a process as a victim if it has already been preempted more than $K_{max}$ times, where $K_{max}$ is a system-defined constant. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Recovery Formulas for KTU Board Exams** | Concept | Equation / Rule | Application | |---|---|---| | Cost of termination | $C(P_i) = \alpha P + \beta R + \gamma T - \delta I$ | Select minimum-cost victim process | | Rollback recovery | $S_{safe} = S_{checkpoint} + \Delta$ | Restore process to last known good state | | Starvation bound | $N_{preempt}(P_i) \le K_{max}$ | Maximum allowed preemptions per process | | Detection cycle | $Cycle_{WFG} \Rightarrow Deadlock$ | Run only after each preemption or abort | | Safe state condition | $\exists\; \text{Sequence } \langle P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n \rangle$ | All processes can finish with available resources | > ⚠️ **Note:** The above table uses `\le` (≤) and `\ge` (≥) notation. Avoid using `|` in formulas to prevent markdown table breakage. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database Systems (Oracle, PostgreSQL):** Use victim selection based on transaction cost (undo log size). - **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** Typically prefer **prevention + avoidance** over recovery due to strict deadlines. - **Distributed Systems (Hadoop, Spark):** Use **checkpoint-rollback** recovery, restarting failed task nodes from the last persisted state. - **Cloud Hypervisors (VMware, KVM):** Use **incremental termination** to gracefully shut down only the misbehaving VMs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Cost-Based Victim Selection Consider a system with **3 deadlocked processes** $P_1, P_2, P_3$ and the following parameters. We must derive the optimal victim. ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &: \text{Priority} = 5,\;\; \text{Runtime} = 10,\;\; \text{Resources} = 4,\;\; \text{Remaining} = 20,\;\; \text{Type} = \text{Interactive} \\ P_2 &: \text{Priority} = 2,\;\; \text{Runtime} = 25,\;\; \text{Resources} = 6,\;\; \text{Remaining} = 30,\;\; \text{Type} = \text{Batch} \\ P_3 &: \text{Priority} = 3,\;\; \text{Runtime} = 15,\;\; \text{Resources} = 3,\;\; \text{Remaining} = 25,\;\; \text{Type} = \text{Batch} \end{aligned} $$ Weights are assumed equal: $w_1 = w_2 = w_3 = w_4 = 1$, and $w_5 = 2$ (type bonus). ### 3.1.2 Cost Computation for Each Process $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}(P_1) &= (1)(5) + (1)(10) + (1)(4) + (1)(20) - (2)(1) \\ &= 5 + 10 + 4 + 20 - 2 \\ &= 37 \\ \\ \text{Cost}(P_2) &= (1)(2) + (1)(25) + (1)(6) + (1)(30) - (2)(1) \\ &= 2 + 25 + 6 + 30 - 2 \\ &= 61 \\ \\ \text{Cost}(P_3) &= (1)(3) + (1)(15) + (1)(3) + (1)(25) - (2)(1) \\ &= 3 + 15 + 3 + 25 - 2 \\ &= 44 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Victim Selection Logic Since the OS seeks the **minimum cost** victim, we compare: $$\min(\text{Cost}(P_1), \text{Cost}(P_2), \text{Cost}(P_3)) = \min(37, 61, 44) = 37$$ Therefore, **$P_1$ is the chosen victim** and will be terminated/preempted first. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why not the interactive one?** Notice that even though $P_1$ is interactive, the heavy **priority and remaining work** are not enough to overcome the type penalty. In real systems, interactive processes are given much larger $w_5$ weights (e.g., $w_5 = 10$) to virtually eliminate them from victim selection. ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Termination vs. Preemption | Factor | Termination | Preemption | |---|---|---| | Speed of resolution | **Fast (immediate)** | Slower (requires checkpointing) | | Work lost | **High (all progress)** | Low (only since last checkpoint) | | Starvation risk | Low (process simply gone) | **High (same process can be picked repeatedly)** | | Implementation complexity | Low | **High (rollback, state save)** | | Best for | Batch systems, low-value tasks | Interactive systems, databases | | Cost function simplicity | Simple (priority + runtime) | Complex (must include rollback distance) | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Incremental Termination Algorithm Logic The OS executes the following decision tree **after** detection confirms a cycle in the WFG: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Invoke detection on the WFG.} \\ &\text{Step 2: If no cycle exists, terminate (no deadlock).} \\ &\text{Step 3: Else, compute Cost}(P_i) \text{ for every } P_i \text{ in the cycle.} \\ &\text{Step 4: Pick } P_v = \arg\min_i \text{Cost}(P_i). \\ &\text{Step 5: Abort } P_v. \text{ Release all resources held by } P_v \text{ to the Available pool.} \\ &\text{Step 6: Re-invoke detection. If cycle persists, GOTO Step 3.} \\ &\text{Step 7: Else, deadlock resolved. Resume normal scheduling.} \end{aligned} $$ This algorithm is **deterministic** in the sense that it is guaranteed to terminate, because each iteration of Step 3–6 strictly **reduces the number of processes** in the cycle, and a cycle must contain at least one process (non-empty set). Hence after at most $n-1$ iterations (where $n$ is the number of deadlocked processes), the cycle is broken. ## 3.4 Bound on Iterations The worst-case number of termination iterations is given by: $$I_{max} = \vert P_{deadlocked} \vert - 1$$ where $\vert P_{deadlocked} \vert$ is the cardinality of the deadlocked process set. For $n$ deadlocked processes, the OS will run the detection algorithm at most $n$ times (initial detection + $n-1$ aborts). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Deadlock Recovery Decision Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD start([System Running]) --> detect{Run Detection
on WFG} detect -- No Cycle --> run([Continue Normal
Execution]) detect -- Cycle Found --> cost[Compute Cost of
Each Deadlocked Process] cost --> policy{Deadlock Recovery
Policy} policy -- Process
Termination --> abort[Select Victim P_v
with Minimum Cost] policy -- Resource
Preemption --> preempt[Select Victim P_v
and Target Resource R] abort --> kill[Abort P_v
Release All Its Resources] preempt --> rollback[Rollback P_v to
Last Checkpoint] kill --> incPreempt[Increment Preemption
Counter for P_v] rollback --> incPreempt incPreempt --> starveCheck{Preempt Count
exceeds K_max?} starveCheck -- Yes --> blockVictim[Block P_v from
Future Victim Selection] starveCheck -- No --> redoDetect[Re-invoke Detection
Algorithm] blockVictim --> redoDetect redoDetect --> detect ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Recovery Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] WFG[Wait For Graph
Snapshot] PCB[Process Control
Blocks Snapshot] end subgraph DecisionLayer["Decision Layer"] Detector[Deadlock
Detector] CostEngine[Cost Function
Engine] VictimSelector[Victim
Selector] end subgraph ActionLayer["Action Layer"] Terminator[Process
Terminator] Preemptor[Resource
Preemptor] RollbackEngine[Rollback
Engine] end subgraph RecoveryLayer["Recovery Layer"] ResourcePool[Resource Pool
Updated] SafeCheck[Safety Algorithm
Re-run] ResumeScheduler[Resume
Scheduler] end WFG --> Detector PCB --> CostEngine Detector -- Cycle Found --> CostEngine CostEngine --> VictimSelector VictimSelector --> Terminator VictimSelector --> Preemptor Preemptor --> RollbackEngine Terminator --> ResourcePool RollbackEngine --> ResourcePool ResourcePool --> SafeCheck SafeCheck --> ResumeScheduler ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Preemption-Based Recovery with Starvation Prevention ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant OS as Operating System participant DET as Detection Engine participant COST as Cost Engine participant VICT as Victim Selector participant PROC as Process P_v participant CHK as Checkpoint Store OS->>DET: Invoke detection on WFG DET-->>OS: Cycle detected in processes P1, P2, P3 OS->>COST: Compute cost for P1, P2, P3 COST-->>OS: Costs = 37, 61, 44 OS->>VICT: Select min-cost victim VICT-->>OS: P1 selected (cost=37) VICT->>VICT: Check starvation counter of P1 VICT-->>OS: Counter < K_max, proceed OS->>PROC: Forcefully preempt resources from P1 OS->>CHK: Restore P1 to last checkpoint S_check OS->>DET: Re-run detection DET-->>OS: No cycle, system recovered OS->>OS: Add P1 back to ready queue ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *List any three methods used by an Operating System to recover from a deadlock.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):** 1. **Aborting all deadlocked processes:** The OS forcefully terminates every process in the deadlock cycle, releasing all their held resources at once. (1 mark) 2. **Aborting one process at a time:** Processes are killed selectively until the deadlock cycle is broken. The detection algorithm is re-run after each abort. (1 mark) 3. **Resource preemption with rollback:** Resources are forcibly taken from selected victim processes, which are rolled back to a previously saved safe state. (1 mark) --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *What is starvation? How is it prevented during deadlock recovery using resource preemption?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Starvation (1 mark):** Starvation occurs when a particular process is perpetually chosen as a victim for preemption and never gets to complete its execution. **Prevention (2 marks):** The OS maintains a **preemption counter** in the PCB of every process. A process is excluded from victim selection once its counter exceeds a system-defined threshold $K_{max}$, ensuring no single process is repeatedly preempted. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`** > > **(a)** Explain the **different approaches** the Operating System can take to recover from a deadlock. Compare process termination and resource preemption in detail. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A system has four deadlocked processes $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ with the following cost-related parameters. Use the cost function $C(P_i) = P_{priority} + R_{runtime} + H_{resources} + W_{remaining} - T_{type}$ to determine the order in which processes should be aborted. **(7 marks)** | Process | Priority | Runtime | Resources Held | Remaining Work | Type (I=Interactive, B=Batch) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | P1 | 4 | 20 | 5 | 30 | I | | P2 | 1 | 30 | 7 | 40 | B | | P3 | 3 | 18 | 4 | 25 | B | | P4 | 5 | 12 | 3 | 15 | I | --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** ### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Answer **Introduction (1 mark):** Once deadlock detection confirms a cycle in the Wait-For Graph, the OS must apply a recovery technique. The two principal families are **Process Termination** and **Resource Preemption**. **Process Termination (3 marks):** 1. **Abort All Deadlocked Processes:** Terminates every process in the cycle. Pros: guaranteed to break cycle. Cons: extremely high cost as all work is lost. Best suited for **batch systems**. 2. **Abort One at a Time:** The OS picks a victim, aborts it, then re-runs the detection algorithm. The cycle may be broken after one or more aborts. Cost is determined by factors like priority, runtime, and resources held. 3. **Selection Criteria for Victim:** The OS picks the process with **minimum cost**, defined by a weighted sum of priority, CPU time used, resources held, and remaining work. Interactive processes are penalized heavily to avoid disrupting user-facing tasks. **Resource Preemption (2 marks):** The OS forcibly takes a resource from a process. Three sub-issues must be handled: - **Selecting a Victim:** Use the same cost function as termination. - **Rollback:** The preempted process must be rolled back to a **safe state**, which is typically a previously saved **checkpoint**. The process is then restarted. - **Starvation:** Prevented by limiting the number of times a single process can be preempted using a counter in its PCB. **Comparison (1 mark):** Termination is simpler but more destructive. Preemption preserves work but is more complex due to checkpointing and starvation management. Termination is preferred in batch systems, preemption in interactive and database systems. --- ### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Answer **Step 1: Set Up the Cost Function** Using $C(P_i) = P_{priority} + R_{runtime} + H_{resources} + W_{remaining} - T_{type}$ where $T = 1$ for Interactive and $T = 1$ for Batch (i.e., subtract 1 for all). **Step 2: Compute Cost for Each Process** $$ \begin{aligned} C(P_1) &= 4 + 20 + 5 + 30 - 1 = 58 \\ C(P_2) &= 1 + 30 + 7 + 40 - 1 = 77 \\ C(P_3) &= 3 + 18 + 4 + 25 - 1 = 49 \\ C(P_4) &= 5 + 12 + 3 + 15 - 1 = 34 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Sort by Ascending Cost (Minimum Cost First)** $$\text{Order of Abort: } P_4 \;(34) \rightarrow P_3 \;(49) \rightarrow P_1 \;(58) \rightarrow P_2 \;(77)$$ **Step 4: Result** The OS will first abort **$P_4$**, then re-run detection. If the cycle persists, abort **$P_3$**, and so on, until the deadlock is fully broken. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to **subtract the type bonus** ($T_{type}$). Without this, the lowest-cost process may wrongly be a heavy interactive process. (–1 mark penalty) > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Some students compute the cost but do not **sort and present the order of abort** clearly. KTU board examiners want the *sequence*, not just individual values. (–1 mark) > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Skipping the explanation of **how the OS re-runs detection after each abort**. Always mention iterative detection. (–1 mark) > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Writing the cost formula without clearly defining what $P_{priority}$, $R_{runtime}$, etc. represent. Always define the variables first. (–0.5 mark) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 3]`** > > **(a)** Discuss the **issues involved in resource preemption** as a deadlock recovery technique. Explain how rollback and starvation are handled. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **incremental termination** approach for deadlock recovery with a suitable algorithmic flow. Why is it preferred over aborting all processes in interactive systems? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** ### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Answer **Introduction to Resource Preemption (1 mark):** Resource preemption is the recovery strategy in which the OS forcibly deallocates resources from one or more deadlocked processes, then re-runs the detection algorithm to verify that the cycle is broken. **Three Key Issues (4 marks):** 1. **Selecting a Victim (1.5 marks):** The OS must identify which process to preempt. The cost function considers priority, runtime, resources held, and remaining work. Processes with the **lowest cost** are chosen first. 2. **Rollback (1.5 marks):** Once preempted, the victim process cannot simply resume from its current state, as its state may be inconsistent. The OS restores the process to a **previously saved checkpoint** $S_{check}$ and re-executes from there. Total rollback cost is: $$C_{rollback} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_{process\_i} + C_{resources\_held\_by\_i})$$ 3. **Starvation (1 mark):** If the same process is repeatedly chosen as a victim, it may never complete, leading to starvation. The OS counters this by storing a **preemption counter** $N_{preempt}(P_i)$ in each PCB and refusing to select a process whose count exceeds the threshold $K_{max}$. **Conclusion (2 marks):** Resource preemption is more sophisticated than simple termination. It preserves work through checkpointing and ensures fairness via starvation control, making it the preferred approach for interactive systems, databases, and long-running computations. --- ### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Answer **Definition of Incremental Termination (1 mark):** Incremental termination is a deadlock recovery strategy in which the OS aborts **one process at a time**, re-running the detection algorithm after each abort, until the cycle is broken. **Algorithmic Flow (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{1. Invoke deadlock detection on the WFG.} \\ &\text{2. If no cycle is found, exit (no deadlock).} \\ &\text{3. If a cycle exists, compute Cost}(P_i) \text{ for all } P_i \text{ in the cycle.} \\ &\text{4. Select the victim } P_v = \arg\min_i \text{Cost}(P_i). \\ &\text{5. Abort } P_v \text{ and release all resources held by } P_v. \\ &\text{6. Re-invoke detection.} \\ &\text{7. If cycle persists, return to Step 3.} \\ &\text{8. Else, deadlock resolved. Resume normal scheduling.} \end{aligned} $$ **Worst-Case Bound (1 mark):** The number of iterations is at most $\vert P_{deadlocked} \vert$, since each iteration strictly reduces the cycle size. **Why Preferred in Interactive Systems (1 mark):** Interactive systems have many short-lived user-facing processes. Aborting all of them would disrupt the entire user experience. Incremental termination allows the OS to remove only the **least valuable** process and preserve as much live work as possible, making it minimally invasive. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for Question B:** > 1. **Pitfall #1:** Students often write the algorithmic flow but forget the **terminating condition** (the bound on iterations). Always state that the algorithm terminates in at most $n$ steps. (–1 mark) > 2. **Pitfall #2:** Confusing **safe state** with **checkpoint state**. A safe state in Banker's sense refers to a state where a safe sequence exists. A checkpoint state is a snapshot of the process's execution. Do not interchange these terms. (–1 mark) > 3. **Pitfall #3:** Not linking the algorithmic flow to the **cost function**. Examiners expect the integration of cost-based victim selection with the abort loop. (–0.5 mark) > 4. **Pitfall #4:** Writing "interactive systems prefer this" without explaining **why** (user experience, short-lived tasks, minimal disruption). Always substantiate claims. (–0.5 mark) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Recovery from Deadlock (Module 3)** - **Definition:** Recovery is a **post-mortem** strategy applied **after** detection confirms a deadlock cycle. - **Two Main Families:** **Process Termination** (abort processes) and **Resource Preemption** (forcibly take resources and rollback). - **Termination Sub-Types:** Abort-all (fast, costly), Abort-one-at-a-time (incremental, repeated detection), Selective victim (cost-based). - **Preemption Sub-Issues:** Victim selection, rollback to checkpoint, starvation prevention via counter. - **Cost Function:** $C(P_i) = w_1 P + w_2 R + w_3 H + w_4 W - w_5 T$ — interactive processes get a **type bonus** to avoid being victims. - **Rollback Mechanism:** Restore to **checkpoint**, never resume from corrupted state. Total rollback cost = sum of process costs + held resource costs. - **Starvation Bound:** $N_{preempt}(P_i) \le K_{max}$ — counter in PCB, system refuses to select over-preempted processes. - **Incremental Termination Bound:** Worst case $\vert P_{deadlocked} \vert$ iterations. - **Hierarchy Placement:** Recovery is the **last resort** in the sequence: **Ignore → Prevent → Avoid → Detect → Recover**. - **System-Type Preference:** Batch → **termination**; Interactive/Database → **preemption with rollback**. - **Key Equations to Memorize:** Cost function, rollback cost sum, starvation threshold, iteration bound. - **Common Exam Traps:** Forgetting to subtract type bonus; not stating re-detection step; confusing safe state with checkpoint; failing to sort victims in order of ascending cost.

topics.title: Memory Management: Concept of Address Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Memory Management: Concept of Address Spaces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Address Space In modern operating systems, an **Address Space** is the set of all logical (or virtual) addresses that a process can use to reference memory. Formally, it is an abstraction provided by the operating system that gives each running program the illusion of owning a contiguous, private, and isolated range of memory locations, independent of the actual physical memory layout of the machine. KTU 2024 Scheme defines the concept in three layered forms: 1. **Logical Address Space (LAS):** The set of addresses generated by the CPU during program execution. These are also called *virtual addresses*. The range is typically from **0 to 2ⁿ − 1**, where *n* is the number of address bits in the CPU architecture (e.g., **n = 32** in a 32-bit system, giving a range of **0 to 4,294,967,295**). 2. **Physical Address Space (PAS):** The set of addresses that actually correspond to real hardware memory (RAM) cells. These are seen by the memory controller, not directly by the running program. 3. **Virtual Address Space (VAS):** The combined logical view presented to a process, possibly larger than physical memory, backed by secondary storage (paging/swapping). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Address Space is the *core abstraction* that enables multiprogramming, memory protection, and virtual memory. Every process in a modern OS (Linux, Windows, Android) operates inside its own private address space, completely isolated from other processes. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Apartment Building Imagine a huge **Apartment Building** (this is your **Physical Memory** — the real RAM chips on the motherboard). The building has 1,000 actual flats, numbered physically from Flat 101 to Flat 1100. Now consider a **tenant** (this is a **process**). The tenant doesn't know (or care) that their flat is physically numbered "Flat 547." The building management gives them a **private address book** that says: - *Your Kitchen = Local Address 1* - *Your Bedroom = Local Address 2* - *Your Bathroom = Local Address 3* This personal address book is the **Logical Address Space** of the process. The translation from "Local Address 1" (logical) to "Flat 547" (physical) is done by a **reception desk clerk** (this is the **MMU — Memory Management Unit**). Every time the tenant uses a local address, the clerk quietly walks them to the actual physical flat. Crucially: - Two tenants can both have "Local Address 1" (kitchen) — but they point to *different physical flats*. This is **memory isolation**. - The tenant can have a private address book with 10,000 entries (huge logical space) even though the building only has 1,000 flats — the rest are stored in a **warehouse** (hard disk / swap). This is **virtual memory**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The *logical* address is what the programmer/code sees. The *physical* address is what the hardware uses. The bridge between them is the **MMU**, and the act of bridging is called **address translation** or **address mapping**. ## 1.3 Why Address Spaces are Essential | Property | Without Address Spaces | With Address Spaces | |----------|------------------------|---------------------| | **Protection** | Any program can overwrite OS memory | Each process isolated in its own space | | **Multiprogramming** | One program at a time in memory | Many programs coexist safely | | **Memory Sharing** | Difficult, error-prone | Controlled sharing via OS | | **Program Relocation** | Code must know physical location | Code uses 0-based logical addresses, can be loaded anywhere | | **Capacity** | Limited by physical RAM | Process can use more memory than physically present (virtual memory) | ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants The following constants are universally applied when reasoning about address spaces in KTU examinations: - **Logical address range:** 0 to 2ⁿ − 1 (where *n* = number of address bits) - **32-bit system maximum logical space:** 2³² = 4 GB (**4,294,967,296 bytes**) - **64-bit system maximum logical space:** 2⁶⁴ ≈ **1.84 × 10¹⁹ bytes** (16 EB theoretical) - **Typical page size:** **4 KB = 4096 bytes = 2¹² bytes** - **Frame size (physical counterpart of page):** Always equal to page size = **4 KB** > [!NOTE] > A page is a fixed-size block of the *logical* address space, while a **frame** (or page frame) is a fixed-size block of *physical* memory of the same size. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Layers of Address Space A modern OS maintains three distinct address domains that a KTU student must clearly differentiate: ### Layer 1 — Logical (Virtual) Address Space - Generated by the **CPU** during instruction execution. - Seen by the **compiler**, **linker**, and the **application programmer**. - Begins typically at logical address **0** for every process. - Size is determined by CPU word length and OS design (e.g., 4 GB on 32-bit). ### Layer 2 — Physical Address Space - Addresses the **actual RAM** chips. - Seen by the **memory controller** and **DRAM modules**. - Begins at physical address **0** (the first byte of RAM). - Size is the actual installed RAM (e.g., 8 GB). ### Layer 3 — Virtual Address Space (Disk-backed) - A logical extension of the logical space. - Backed by a **swap area** on the disk. - Allows total process address space to exceed physical RAM. ## 2.2 Address Binding — The Three Phases **Address binding** is the act of mapping instructions and data to physical memory addresses. It can occur at three distinct moments in a program's lifecycle: ### (a) Compile-Time Binding - If the program is compiled knowing exactly where it will reside in memory. - Absolute physical addresses are *baked into* the executable. - **Disadvantage:** The program cannot be moved; if starting location changes, the program must be **recompiled**. - **Use case:** Embedded systems, firmware, boot loaders. - **Logical address = Physical address** (no translation needed). ### (b) Load-Time Binding - Compiler generates **relocatable code** (addresses relative to start of program). - The **loader** assigns the actual physical starting address when the program is loaded into RAM. - **Disadvantage:** Once loaded, the process **cannot be moved** in memory. - **Logical address + Relocation register = Physical address**. ### (c) Execution-Time (Run-Time) Binding - Most flexible and the **only one used in modern multiprogrammed OSes**. - Addresses are bound to physical locations *only when actually accessed* during execution. - Requires hardware support — the **MMU (Memory Management Unit)**. - Process **can be moved** during execution (necessary for swapping, compaction, paging). - **Physical address = Base register + Logical address**, checked against the **Limit register**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Rule:** Whenever a question says "a modern OS supports multiprogramming and swapping," the correct binding type is **Execution-Time / Run-Time Binding**. The most common KTU question is to identify the binding type from a scenario description. ## 2.3 The Memory Management Unit (MMU) The **MMU** is a dedicated hardware chip (or part of the CPU) that performs **on-the-fly translation** of every logical address into a physical address during program execution. ### Base Register + Limit Register Scheme (Simple MMU) The simplest MMU design uses two special CPU registers: - **Base (or Relocation) Register:** Holds the *starting physical address* of the current process in RAM. - **Limit Register:** Holds the *size* of the process's logical address space (i.e., the maximum legal logical address). **Translation rule:** $$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Logical Address} + \text{Base Register}$$ **Protection rule (hardware-enforced):** $$\text{If } (\text{Logical Address} \geq \text{Limit Register}) \rightarrow \text{TRAP to OS (memory violation)}$$ ### Logical Flow Inside the CPU-MMU 1. CPU fetches instruction at logical address **LA**. 2. LA is presented to the **MMU**. 3. MMU computes: `PA = LA + Base`. 4. MMU checks: `0 ≤ LA < Limit`. 5. If legal → PA is sent to memory bus → RAM. 6. If illegal → MMU raises a **trap** → OS terminates the process (segmentation fault / access violation). ## 2.4 Dynamic Loading and Dynamic Linking ### Dynamic Loading - A routine is **not loaded** into memory until it is **actually called**. - Saves memory because unused routines are never loaded. - The OS provides library routines to implement lazy loading. - Useful for large programs with many optional features. ### Dynamic Linking - Linking of external library functions is **postponed until execution time**. - A small **stub** is included in the executable; the stub is replaced by the actual library address on first call. - In **Windows**, dynamic linking produces `.dll` (Dynamic Link Library) files. - In **Linux/Unix**, it produces `.so` (Shared Object) files. - **Advantage:** Multiple processes share one copy of the library in memory → huge memory savings. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description / Boundary | |---------|----------------|------------------------| | Logical address range | $0 \le LA \le 2^n - 1$ | $n$ = number of address bits | | Max 32-bit logical space | $2^{32} = 4\,\text{GB} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296\,\text{bytes}$ | Standard PC architecture | | Max 64-bit logical space | $2^{64}\,\text{bytes} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}\,\text{bytes}$ | Theoretical, not all used | | Compile-time binding | $PA = LA$ | No translation, absolute code | | Load-time binding | $PA = LA + R$ | $R$ = relocation constant set at load | | Run-time binding (Base) | $PA = LA + \text{Base}$ | Base = physical start of process | | Protection check | $\text{If } LA \ge \text{Limit} \Rightarrow \text{TRAP}$ | Hardware-enforced | | Number of pages in process | $p = \lceil LAS / \text{page size} \rceil$ | $LAS$ = logical address space size | | Number of frames in RAM | $f = \text{Total RAM} / \text{frame size}$ | Frame size = page size | | Effective Access Time (EAT) | $EAT = (1 - p) \times T_m + p \times T_s$ | $p$ = page fault rate, $T_m$ = memory access, $T_s$ = disk access | > [!NOTE] > Always remember: **Page** belongs to *logical* memory; **Frame** belongs to *physical* memory. They are the same size, but the terminology tells you which side of the MMU you are on. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure):** Each virtual machine runs in its own isolated address space, ensuring tenant isolation. - **Android / iOS Apps:** Each app gets a sandboxed address space; a crash in one app cannot corrupt another. - **Embedded Systems (Automotive ECUs):** Address space partitioning enforces safety (e.g., ISO 26262 — software for braking cannot be overwritten by entertainment system). - **Databases (Oracle, PostgreSQL):** Buffer pools use virtual address mappings for efficient caching of large datasets. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Logical Address Space for an n-bit CPU **Problem:** A CPU has a 32-bit address bus. Compute the maximum logical address space of any process running on it. **Step 1 — State the general rule.** The number of unique addresses that an *n*-bit CPU can generate is $2^n$. $$ \text{Max Logical Addresses} = 2^n $$ **Step 2 — Substitute n = 32.** $$ \text{Max Logical Addresses} = 2^{32} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the power.** $$ 2^{32} = 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{2} $$ $$ 2^{10} = 1024 \approx 1\,\text{KB} $$ $$ 2^{20} = (2^{10})^2 = 1{,}048{,}576 \approx 1\,\text{MB} $$ $$ 2^{30} = (2^{10})^3 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \approx 1\,\text{GB} $$ $$ 2^{32} = 4 \times 2^{30} = 4 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to user-friendly units.** $$ 4{,}294{,}967{,}296\,\text{bytes} = 4\,\text{GB} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\text{Maximum Logical Address Space} = 4\,\text{GB} \text{ (for 32-bit CPU)}} $$ > Conversion logic: $2^{10} = 1\,\text{KiB}$, $2^{20} = 1\,\text{MiB}$, $2^{30} = 1\,\text{GiB}$, $2^{32} = 4\,\text{GiB}$. This is the limit that every 32-bit process is bound by, regardless of how much physical RAM is installed. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Address Translation Using Base and Limit Registers **Problem:** A process is loaded into physical memory starting at physical address **5000**. The process makes a memory reference using logical address **4300**. The Limit Register holds the value **6000**. Determine: - (i) The physical address. - (ii) Whether the access is legal or illegal. **Given:** - Base Register = 5000 - Logical Address (LA) = 4300 - Limit Register = 6000 **Step 1 — Apply the translation formula.** $$ PA = LA + \text{Base} $$ $$ PA = 4300 + 5000 = 9300 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the protection check.** The condition for a *legal* access is: $$ 0 \le LA < \text{Limit} $$ Substituting: $$ 0 \le 4300 < 6000 \quad \checkmark \text{ (True)} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret results.** - (i) Physical Address = **9300** - (ii) Access is **legal** because logical address 4300 is within the limit (0 to 5999). **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{PA = 9300, \text{ Access is LEGAL}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Illegal Access Detection **Problem:** Using the same process (Base = 5000, Limit = 6000), the process now references logical address **7000**. What happens? **Step 1 — Translate (hypothetically).** $$ PA_{\text{requested}} = 7000 + 5000 = 12{,}000 $$ **Step 2 — Apply protection check.** $$ 0 \le 7000 < 6000 \quad \boldsymbol{\times} \text{ (False, since } 7000 \ge 6000) $$ **Step 3 — Hardware response.** The MMU detects the violation *before* sending the address to the memory bus. It raises a **trap** to the OS. The OS typically: - Sends **SIGSEGV** (Unix/Linux) or **STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION** (Windows) to the process. - Terminates the offending process. - Logs a **core dump** for debugging. **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\text{Access is ILLEGAL} \Rightarrow \text{MMU trap} \Rightarrow \text{Process terminated}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU point:** The translation `PA = LA + Base` is *only* computed *after* the limit check passes. Hardware order is **Check → Translate → Access**. This is a very common KTU question. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of Pages in a Process **Problem:** A process has a logical address space of **16 KB** and the page size is **4 KB**. How many pages does the process have? **Step 1 — State the formula.** $$ \text{Number of pages} = \frac{\text{Logical Address Space size}}{\text{Page size}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ \text{Number of pages} = \frac{16\,\text{KB}}{4\,\text{KB}} = 4 $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\text{Process has 4 pages}} $$ > Conversion logic: $16\,\text{KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16{,}384\,\text{bytes}$, and $4\,\text{KB} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096\,\text{bytes}$. Dividing: $16{,}384 / 4{,}096 = 4$. The process is divided into 4 equal-sized logical chunks called *pages*. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Page Number and Offset Extraction **Problem:** A 32-bit logical address is split such that the lower **12 bits** are the page offset and the upper **20 bits** are the page number. If the logical address is **0x00005A40** (hexadecimal), find the page number and the offset. **Step 1 — Recall the relationship.** $$ \text{Page size} = 2^{\text{offset bits}} = 2^{12} = 4096\,\text{bytes} $$ **Step 2 — Convert hexadecimal to binary (or use direct division).** $$ 0x00005A40 = 5 \times 16^3 + A \times 16^2 + 4 \times 16^1 + 0 \times 16^0 $$ $$ = 5 \times 4096 + 10 \times 256 + 4 \times 16 + 0 $$ $$ = 20{,}480 + 2560 + 64 = 23{,}104 $$ **Step 3 — Compute offset (last 12 bits = mod 4096).** $$ \text{Offset} = 23{,}104 \mod 4096 $$ $$ 23{,}104 = 5 \times 4096 + 2304 \Rightarrow \text{Offset} = 2304 $$ **Step 4 — Compute page number (integer division).** $$ \text{Page Number} = \lfloor 23{,}104 / 4096 \rfloor = 5 $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\text{Page Number} = 5, \quad \text{Offset} = 2304} $$ > Verification: $\text{Page Number} \times \text{Page size} + \text{Offset} = 5 \times 4096 + 2304 = 20{,}480 + 2304 = 23{,}104 = 0x5A40$. ✓ --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Binding Type Identification KTU examinations often describe a scenario and ask the student to identify the binding type. Use the matrix below to systematically answer such questions. | Scenario Description (Key Phrase) | Binding Type | Justification | |------------------------------------|--------------|---------------| | "Compiled knowing it will run at fixed address 1000" | **Compile-Time** | Absolute code generated; no relocation. | | "Code is relocatable, but loaded once at fixed base" | **Load-Time** | Relocation done at load, not after. | | "Process is moved during execution due to swapping" | **Run-Time (Execution-Time)** | Hardware MMU needed; modern OSes only. | | "Embedded firmware in a washing machine controller" | **Compile-Time** | System has single program, fixed memory map. | | "OS supports paging and multiple processes" | **Run-Time** | Paging requires dynamic address resolution. | | "Compiler produces relocatable object files" | **Load-Time** | Final binding by loader, not at runtime. | | "MS-DOS .COM executable loaded at fixed offset 0100h" | **Load-Time** | DOS used simple base-relative loading. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Logical-to-Physical Address Translation Flow The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete MMU operation from CPU instruction fetch to RAM access, including the protection check. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CPU executes instruction
at Logical Address LA] --> B[LA sent to MMU bus] B --> C{Limit check:
LA less than Limit Reg?} C -- No --> D[TRAP to OS
SIGSEGV raised] C -- Yes --> E[MMU computes
PA = LA + Base Reg] D --> F[OS terminates process
and logs core dump] E --> G[Physical Address PA
sent to memory bus] G --> H[RAM chip accesses cell PA] H --> I[Data word returned
to CPU registers] I --> J[CPU continues execution] F --> K[Control returns to OS scheduler] J --> L[End of memory cycle] K --> L[End of memory cycle] ``` > Reading aid: Follow the arrows top-to-bottom. The **diamond (decision) node** represents the hardware-enforced protection check that happens *before* any address reaches the physical RAM. This ordering is the single most important KTU fact about MMU operation. ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Address Space Layering (Block Architecture) This diagram presents the layered relationship between the three address domains — logical, physical, and virtual — in a typical modern multiprogrammed OS. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP_LAYER["APPLICATION LAYER (Software)"] P1["Process P1
Logical Address Space
0 to 2^n minus 1"] P2["Process P2
Logical Address Space
0 to 2^n minus 1"] P3["Process P3
Logical Address Space
0 to 2^n minus 1"] end subgraph MMU_LAYER["MMU LAYER (Hardware Translation)"] MMU1["MMU for P1
Base + Limit Registers"] MMU2["MMU for P2
Base + Limit Registers"] MMU3["MMU for P3
Base + Limit Registers"] end subgraph PHYS_LAYER["PHYSICAL MEMORY LAYER (RAM)"] PHY1["Physical Frame Block 1
Base + 0 to Base + LAS minus 1"] PHY2["Physical Frame Block 2
Base + 0 to Base + LAS minus 1"] PHY3["Physical Frame Block 3
Base + 0 to Base + LAS minus 1"] end subgraph DISK_LAYER["SWAP AREA LAYER (Secondary Storage)"] SWAP["Swap Partition on Disk
Holds overflow pages not in RAM"] end P1 --> MMU1 P2 --> MMU2 P3 --> MMU3 MMU1 --> PHY1 MMU2 --> PHY2 MMU3 --> PHY3 PHY1 -. evicted page .-> SWAP PHY2 -. evicted page .-> SWAP PHY3 -. evicted page .-> SWAP SWAP -. paged in .-> PHY1 SWAP -. paged in .-> PHY2 SWAP -. paged in .-> PHY3 ``` > Reading aid: The diagram has four horizontal swim lanes. The **dashed arrows** indicate virtual memory page movement between RAM and disk (swapping), while the **solid arrows** indicate the normal address translation path. Notice how the three processes' logical address spaces all start at 0 but map to completely different physical regions — this is the essence of memory protection. ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Binding-Time Decision Tree This decision tree helps a KTU student identify the correct address binding type from a given scenario. ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New scenario given] --> Q1{Does the program
need to be movable
during execution?} Q1 -- No, runs once at fixed location --> Q2{Is the final address
known at compile time?} Q1 -- Yes, supports multiprogramming --> RUN["Run-Time Binding
(MMU hardware required)"] Q2 -- Yes --> COMP["Compile-Time Binding
(Absolute code)"] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is relocation done
at load time only?} Q3 -- Yes --> LOAD["Load-Time Binding
(Relocatable code)"] Q3 -- No --> RUN COMP --> FINAL[Answer: Compile-Time] LOAD --> FINAL2[Answer: Load-Time] RUN --> FINAL3[Answer: Run-Time / Execution-Time] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Process State Through Address Binding Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source Code
written in C/Java] --> COMP[Compiler generates
Object Code with
relative addresses] COMP --> LINK[Linker combines
multiple object files
into one executable] LINK --> LOAD[Loader places executable
in physical memory
at chosen base] LOAD --> EXEC[Process executes;
MMU translates
every logical to physical] EXEC --> SWAP[Process may be
swapped out and back in
to different physical frames] SWAP --> EXEC ``` > Reading aid: This timeline shows where each binding type intervenes. Compile-time binding finishes at the *Compiler* stage. Load-time binding finishes at the *Loader* stage. Run-time binding continues throughout the *Execute* stage and even across *Swap* events. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Define the term "Address Space". Differentiate between logical address space and physical address space with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Address Space:** An address space is the range of memory addresses available to a process or available in physical memory. It defines the set of all valid addresses that can be generated or referenced. **Logical Address Space (LAS):** This is the set of addresses generated by the CPU during program execution. It is also called the *virtual address space* and is what the running program "sees." It begins at logical address **0** and extends up to $2^n - 1$ for an *n*-bit CPU. For example, a 32-bit process has a logical address space of **0 to 4,294,967,295 (4 GB)**. **Physical Address Space (PAS):** This is the set of addresses that correspond to actual cells in the physical RAM chips. It is what the memory controller uses to access hardware. For example, a computer with **8 GB** of installed RAM has a physical address space of **0 to 8,589,934,591**. **Key Difference:** Logical addresses are *virtual* and used by the program; physical addresses are *real* and used by hardware. The translation is performed by the **MMU** using the relation $PA = LA + \text{Base}$. *(Mark allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for LAS with example, 1 mark for PAS with example and translation rule.)* --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **What is meant by address binding? List the three different times at which binding can occur and identify which one is used by modern multiprogrammed operating systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Address Binding** is the mapping of instructions and data in a program to actual physical memory addresses. It is the act of associating a symbolic or logical address with a physical memory location. **The three binding times are:** 1. **Compile-Time Binding:** Absolute physical addresses are embedded into the executable during compilation. The program must be recompiled if its load location changes. 2. **Load-Time Binding:** The compiler generates relocatable code; the loader assigns the physical base address when the program is loaded. Once loaded, the process cannot be moved. 3. **Execution-Time (Run-Time) Binding:** Addresses are bound to physical memory only when accessed at runtime, using hardware MMU support. The process can be moved during execution. **Used by Modern Multiprogrammed OSes:** **Execution-Time (Run-Time) Binding.** It is the only type that supports swapping, paging, and memory protection between processes. *(Mark allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing three types, 1 mark for identifying run-time binding as the answer.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format) ### Choice A (14 Marks) #### Question A. (a) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of address translation using Base and Limit Registers with a neat block diagram. Show how memory protection is achieved in this scheme. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Concept (2 marks):** Address translation is the process of converting a logical address (LA) generated by the CPU into a physical address (PA) that corresponds to a real memory location in RAM. The simplest hardware mechanism to perform this translation uses two CPU registers: - **Base Register (or Relocation Register):** Stores the starting physical address of the current process in memory. - **Limit Register:** Stores the size (or maximum legal logical address) of the process. **2. Translation Rule (1 mark):** $$ PA = LA + \text{Base Register} $$ **3. Protection Rule (2 marks):** Before translation, the MMU verifies: $$ 0 \le LA < \text{Limit Register} $$ If the condition is violated, the MMU raises a **trap** (interrupt) to the OS, and the OS terminates the offending process (e.g., segmentation fault). **4. Block Diagram (2 marks):** ``` +-------------------+ LA ----> | MMU | | Limit Register |----> [LA < Limit ?] --No--> TRAP to OS | Base Register | | | | PA = LA + Base |----> PA -----> Physical RAM +-------------------+ ``` **5. Example (text substitute, not for marks but for clarity):** If Base = 5000, Limit = 6000, and LA = 4300, then PA = 9300 (legal access). If LA = 7000, the MMU detects 7000 ≥ 6000 and traps. **6. Memory Protection Achieved:** - Each process has its own base and limit, so one process cannot generate a valid PA pointing to another process's memory region. - The hardware enforces the check on *every* memory reference — software bugs cannot bypass it. - The OS sets the registers during context switch. --- #### Question A. (b) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — 7 Marks **A system has a logical address space of 64 KB and a physical memory of 32 KB. The page size is 4 KB. (i) How many pages does the process have? (ii) How many frames does physical memory have? (iii) Explain how a logical address is divided into page number and offset. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Logical Address Space (LAS) = 64 KB = $64 \times 1024 = 65{,}536$ bytes - Physical Address Space (PAS) = 32 KB = $32 \times 1024 = 32{,}768$ bytes - Page size = Frame size = 4 KB = $4 \times 1024 = 4{,}096$ bytes **(i) Number of Pages (2 marks):** $$ \text{Number of pages} = \frac{LAS}{\text{Page size}} = \frac{65{,}536}{4{,}096} = 16 $$ **Result:** The process is divided into **16 pages**, numbered page 0 to page 15. **(ii) Number of Frames (2 marks):** $$ \text{Number of frames} = \frac{PAS}{\text{Frame size}} = \frac{32{,}768}{4{,}096} = 8 $$ **Result:** Physical memory is divided into **8 frames**, numbered frame 0 to frame 7. **(iii) Logical Address Division (3 marks):** The logical address has $\log_2(65{,}536) = 16$ bits total. - **Offset bits** = $\log_2(\text{page size}) = \log_2(4096) = 12$ bits. - **Page number bits** = Total bits − Offset bits = 16 − 12 = 4 bits. | Bits 15 to 12 (4 bits) | Bits 11 to 0 (12 bits) | |------------------------|-------------------------| | Page Number (p) | Page Offset (d) | The page number can address $2^4 = 16$ pages, and the offset can address $2^{12} = 4096$ bytes within a page. **Verification:** $16 \text{ pages} \times 4096 \text{ bytes/page} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$ ✓ --- ### Choice B (Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks #### Question B. (a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 7 Marks **With the help of a diagram, explain the Memory Management Unit (MMU) and its role in address translation. How does it support run-time binding? [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Definition (1 mark):** The Memory Management Unit (MMU) is a hardware device — either a separate chip or a component integrated into the CPU — whose primary function is the real-time translation of logical addresses into physical addresses during program execution. **2. Components Inside the MMU (2 marks):** - **Base (Relocation) Register:** Holds the physical starting address of the running process. - **Limit Register:** Holds the size of the process's logical address space. - **Comparator and Adder:** Performs the limit check and address addition in a single clock cycle. - **Trap Generator:** Issues an interrupt to the CPU on protection violation. **3. Block Diagram (2 marks):** ``` +-----------------------------+ | CPU Core | | Issues LA (Logical Address)| +--------------+--------------+ | v +-----------------------------+ | MMU | | +---------+ +---------+ | | | Base | | Limit | | | | Reg | | Reg | | | +----+----+ +----+----+ | | | +------+ | | | +--+ Add +---+ | | +--+---+ | | | | | Limit cmp v +---> TRAP | | logic ----> [PA] | +--------------+--------------+ | v +-----------------------------+ | Physical Memory (RAM) | +-----------------------------+ ``` **4. Role in Run-Time Binding (2 marks):** Run-time binding requires that addresses be resolved only when actually used. The MMU supports this by: - Performing translation *on every memory access* (not just at load time). - Allowing the **Base register to be updated** by the OS during context switch, so the same logical address in different processes can map to different physical addresses. - Enabling **swapping** — if a process is moved to a different physical region, only the Base register needs updating; no program code or data is touched. - Providing **protection** via the Limit register, ensuring one process cannot access another's memory. --- #### Question B. (b) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 7 Marks **A process is loaded with Base Register = 4000 and Limit Register = 3000. The process generates the following logical addresses: (i) 1500, (ii) 2999, (iii) 3000, (iv) 4500. Determine which accesses are legal and compute the physical address for the legal ones. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** Base = 4000, Limit = 3000. **General Rule:** - Legal access condition: $0 \le LA < 3000$ - Physical address (if legal): $PA = LA + 4000$ **Part (i) — LA = 1500 (1.5 marks):** - Check: $0 \le 1500 < 3000$ ✓ Legal - $PA = 1500 + 4000 = 5500$ - **Result: Legal, PA = 5500** **Part (ii) — LA = 2999 (1.5 marks):** - Check: $0 \le 2999 < 3000$ ✓ Legal - $PA = 2999 + 4000 = 6999$ - **Result: Legal, PA = 6999** **Part (iii) — LA = 3000 (1.5 marks):** - Check: $0 \le 3000 < 3000$ ✗ Illegal (3000 is NOT strictly less than 3000) - MMU raises a **trap** to the OS - **Result: Illegal access → process terminated** **Part (iv) — LA = 4500 (1.5 marks):** - Check: $0 \le 4500 < 3000$ ✗ Illegal - MMU raises a **trap** to the OS - **Result: Illegal access → process terminated** **Summary Table:** | Logical Address | Limit Check | Result | Physical Address | |-----------------|-------------|--------|------------------| | 1500 | $1500 < 3000$ ✓ | Legal | 5500 | | 2999 | $2999 < 3000$ ✓ | Legal | 6999 | | 3000 | $3000 < 3000$ ✗ | Illegal (Trap) | — | | 4500 | $4500 < 3000$ ✗ | Illegal (Trap) | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students write $PA = 3000 + 4000 = 7000$ for the address 3000, treating it as legal. The limit check is **strictly less than** ($<$), not less-than-or-equal. Writing the correct inequality is a guaranteed 1-mark gain. > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Some students swap Base and Limit. Remember: **Base** is a *physical address* (the start); **Limit** is a *size in bytes* (the boundary). Mixing them is a 2-mark loss. > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Forgetting to mention the **OS action** (trap, process termination) on illegal access loses the 1 mark reserved for "consequence" of the violation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > Use this checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU university examination. - **Address Space** = the range of memory addresses a process or system can use. - **Logical Address Space (LAS):** Generated by the CPU; begins at 0; size = $2^n$ for *n*-bit CPU. - **Physical Address Space (PAS):** Real RAM addresses; size = installed RAM. - **Virtual Address Space (VAS):** Logical space backed by disk swap; can exceed physical RAM. - **MMU (Memory Management Unit):** Hardware that performs logical-to-physical translation. - **Base Register:** Physical starting address of a process in RAM. - **Limit Register:** Maximum legal logical address + 1 (i.e., the size of the process). - **Translation Formula:** $PA = LA + \text{Base}$. - **Protection Check:** $LA < \text{Limit}$ — strict inequality; violation causes a trap. - **Three Binding Types:** Compile-time, Load-time, Run-time (Execution-time). - **Modern OSes use:** Run-time binding (only type that supports multiprogramming and swapping). - **Compile-time binding:** Absolute code; no relocation; used in embedded systems. - **Load-time binding:** Relocatable code; relocation done at load; process cannot move. - **Run-time binding:** Uses MMU; allows process movement; required for paging/swapping. - **Page:** A fixed-size block of *logical* memory (typical size 4 KB = 4096 bytes). - **Frame:** A fixed-size block of *physical* memory (same size as a page). - **Number of pages:** $LAS / \text{Page size}$. - **Number of frames:** $PAS / \text{Frame size}$. - **Address Split:** Upper bits = page number; lower bits = offset. - **Offset bits:** $\log_2(\text{Page size})$ — for 4 KB pages, this is **12 bits**. - **Page number bits:** Total address bits − Offset bits. - **Dynamic Loading:** Routines loaded only when first called — saves memory. - **Dynamic Linking:** Library functions linked at runtime; produces `.dll` (Windows) or `.so` (Linux). - **Memory Protection Benefit:** Each process runs in isolation; one process cannot corrupt another's memory. - **Core Dump:** File created by OS on illegal access; contains process memory snapshot for debugging. - **32-bit CPU max LAS:** **4 GB** = 4,294,967,296 bytes. - **64-bit CPU max LAS:** **2⁶⁴ bytes** ≈ 16 Exabytes (theoretical). - **Key Terminology Distinction:** "Page" (logical) vs "Frame" (physical) — both same size; never call a physical block a "page." > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question provides Base and Limit values, *always* state the inequality check first, then compute the physical address only if legal. Examiners reward the explicit two-step methodology, not just the final number.

topics.title: Swapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Swapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Swapping** is a memory management technique employed by the operating system in which entire processes (or large segments of them) are temporarily moved from the **main memory (RAM)** to a dedicated area on **secondary storage** (typically a disk, called the *swap space* or *swap partition*), and later brought back into RAM for continued execution. This mechanism allows the system to support a degree of **multiprogramming** that exceeds the physical memory capacity, thereby enabling the **abstraction of a larger virtual address space** than what is physically present.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Swapping forms the foundation of **Virtual Memory** systems. In modern systems, swapping is rarely done on whole processes; instead, only *inactive pages* are swapped in/out, leading to **Demand Paging** and **Page Replacement Algorithms**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine your study desk has space for only **3 textbooks** at a time (this is your **RAM**), but you are enrolled in **6 subjects** (these are your **processes**). You cannot keep all 6 books open on the desk simultaneously.

**What do you do?**

- You take the **Mathematics** book and place it inside your **backpack** (the backpack is your **secondary storage / swap space**).
- You pull out the **Physics** book from the backpack and place it on the desk to study.
- Later, when you are done with Physics, you swap it back into the backpack and bring out Chemistry.

This continuous *desk ↔ backpack* exchange is exactly how **Swapping** works in an operating system. The OS keeps moving processes between fast-but-small RAM and slow-but-large disk storage to create the illusion of a much larger memory.

### 1.3 Standard Terminology & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics and Terms Used in Swapping Analysis:**
> - **Swap Space:** Reserved disk area used as an extension of RAM. Typical size: **2× to 4×** the physical RAM size in Linux.
> - **Swap In:** Transferring a process/page **from disk → RAM**.
> - **Swap Out:** Transferring a process/page **from RAM → disk**.
> - **Swap Time:** Total time to perform a swap operation, dominated by disk I/O.
> - **Standard Disk Access Time ≈ 10 ms** (varies with storage technology).
> - **Context Switch Overhead:** Time cost incurred due to swapping.
> - **Standard Page Size:** **4 KB** (x86 systems) or **8 KB / 16 KB** (modern 64-bit systems).
> - **Thrashing:** Condition where the system spends more time swapping than executing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown of Swapping

Swapping operates on a layered principle, where memory hierarchy trade-offs are exploited to maximize throughput. Below is a structured analysis of how swapping is implemented in a modern OS.

#### A. Classical (Whole-Process) Swapping

1. **Memory Pressure Detection:** The OS monitors the number of free frames in RAM. When free frames fall below a defined threshold (e.g., < 5% of total frames), swapping is triggered.
2. **Victim Selection:** A process in the *Blocked* or *Ready-Suspend* state is selected as a "victim" because it is not currently using the CPU.
3. **Copy-Out Phase:** The entire address space of the victim process is written to the **swap space** on disk. This is a *bulk* I/O operation.
4. **Memory Reclaim:** The physical frames previously held by the victim are returned to the free frame list.
5. **Swap-In Phase:** When the swapped-out process is scheduled again, its image is read back from the swap space into RAM.
6. **Address Translation Refresh:** Page tables and TLB entries are updated to reflect the new physical-to-virtual mappings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Classical swapping is rarely used in modern OS due to high I/O overhead. Modern systems use **Paging-based Swapping** (swapping pages, not whole processes) combined with the **Page Fault Service Routine**.

#### B. Swapping in the Context of Virtual Memory

In modern demand-paging systems, the OS never swaps entire processes. Instead:

- **Page Fault** occurs when a process references a page not in RAM.
- The OS services the fault by reading the required **page** from secondary storage (swap device).
- If RAM is full, the OS uses a **Page Replacement Algorithm** (LRU, FIFO, Optimal, Clock) to evict a page and make room.
- This is called **Swapping at the Page Level**, or **Paging**.

#### C. Why is Swapping Necessary?

- **Multiprogramming Support:** Allows more concurrent processes than physical memory permits.
- **Memory Hierarchy Optimization:** Exploits the speed-size trade-off between registers, cache, RAM, and disk.
- **Process Isolation:** Each process gets a private virtual address space, irrespective of physical layout.
- **Crash Recovery & Hibernation:** Entire RAM images can be dumped to swap and restored on reboot.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Effective Access Time (EAT) without page fault | $EAT = (1 - p) \times T_{mem} + p \times T_{fault}$ | time (ns or µs) | $p$ = page fault rate (0 ≤ p ≤ 1) |
| 2 | Swap Time Estimate | $T_{swap} = T_{context\_switch} + \dfrac{2 \times M}{D}$ | seconds | $M$ = process size in bytes, $D$ = disk transfer rate (B/s) |
| 3 | Disk Transfer Rate Required | $D \geq \dfrac{M}{T_{swap\_budget}}$ | B/s | For real-time swap constraints |
| 4 | Page Fault Service Time | $T_{fault} = T_{service} + T_{IO} + T\_restart$ | µs | Sum of OS overhead, disk I/O, process restart |
| 5 | Swapped-Out Memory Saved | $M_{saved} = N_{out} \times P_{size}$ | bytes | $N_{out}$ = pages swapped, $P_{size}$ = page size (4 KB typical) |
| 6 | Swap Space Size (Linux convention) | $S_{swap} \approx 2 \times RAM$ to $4 \times RAM$ | bytes | Depends on workload and hibernation support |
| 7 | Thrashing Threshold | $\text{Thrashing if } \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i > N_{frames}$ | frames | $D_i$ = demand of process $i$, $N_{frames}$ = available frames |
| 8 | Memory Access with TLB | $EAT = (1 - h) \times (T_{TLB} + T_{mem}) + h \times (2 T_{mem} + T_{fault})$ | time | $h$ = TLB miss rate |
| 9 | Address Translation (Paging) | $\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Frame} \times 2^{offset}) + \text{Offset}$ | bits | Page size = $2^{offset}$ |
| 10 | Overhead of Swapping | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{swap}}{T_{compute}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Lower is better; > 100% implies thrashing |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE FOR KTU:** In the table above, notice how the absolute value / division symbol is rendered as `\times`, `\geq`, or `\dfrac` to avoid breaking markdown table pipes. Always prefer `\vert` or `\mid` for any vertical-bar notation in LaTeX inside tables.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Virtual machines use swap-backed memory overcommitment to host more VMs per physical host, increasing ROI.
- **Mobile OS (Android, iOS):** Compressed swap (zRAM) is used to fit more apps in physical RAM on memory-constrained devices.
- **Embedded & Real-Time Systems:** Use deterministic swap policies to meet hard real-time deadlines.
- **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle):** Configure `swap` and `swappiness` to manage buffer pool eviction efficiently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Access Time (EAT) with Page Faults

This is the **most important KTU derivation** for the Swapping module. We will derive the formula rigorously.

#### Given:

- $T_{mem}$ = Memory access time
- $p$ = Probability of a page fault (0 ≤ p ≤ 1)
- $T_{fault}$ = Time to service a page fault (includes disk I/O)

#### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the access model.**
Each memory reference is either a *page hit* (probability $1 - p$) or a *page fault* (probability $p$).

**Step 2 — Write the time contribution of a page hit.**
When the page is in RAM, the access takes $T_{mem}$.

$$
T_{hit} = T_{mem}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the time contribution of a page fault.**
A page fault service routine must:
1. Trap to the OS ($\approx 1$ to $100\ \mu s$)
2. Issue disk I/O ($\approx 1$ to $10\ ms$)
3. Restart the faulting instruction

Thus:
$$
T_{fault} = T_{service} + T_{IO} + T_{restart}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine using the law of total expectation.**
The Effective Access Time is the weighted average:

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_{hit} + p \cdot T_{fault}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and expand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot (T_{service} + T_{IO} + T_{restart}) \\
    &= T_{mem} - p \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{service} + p \cdot T_{IO} + p \cdot T_{restart} \\
    &= T_{mem} + p \cdot (T_{service} + T_{IO} + T_{restart} - T_{mem})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for intuition.**
Let $T_{mem} = 100\ ns$, $p = 0.001$ (1 fault per 1000 accesses), $T_{fault} = 10\ ms = 10^7\ ns$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.001) \cdot 100\ ns + 0.001 \cdot (10^7\ ns) \\
    &= 0.999 \cdot 100 + 10{,}000 \\
    &= 99.9 + 10{,}000 \\
    &= 10{,}099.9\ ns
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A page fault rate as low as **0.1%** degrades performance by **~100×** because of the disk I/O penalty. This is why minimizing $p$ is critical in OS design.

### 3.2 Derivation: Swap-Out Time for a Process

**Given:**
- Process size $M$ = 1 MB = $1{,}048{,}576$ bytes
- Disk transfer rate $D$ = 100 MB/s

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Time to write $M$ bytes to disk:

$$
T_{out} = \frac{M}{D}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
T_{out} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576\ \text{bytes}}{100 \times 10^6\ \text{bytes/s}} = 0.010485\ \text{s} \approx 10.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Add seek time $T_{seek} \approx 5\ ms$ and rotational latency $T_{rot} \approx 4\ ms$:

$$
T_{swap\_out} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \frac{M}{D} = 5 + 4 + 10.5 = 19.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Add context-switch overhead $T_{cs} = 0.1\ ms$:

$$
T_{swap\_total} = T_{cs} + T_{swap\_out} = 0.1 + 19.5 = 19.6\ \text{ms}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Page Fault Rate vs. Performance Degradation

| Page Fault Rate $p$ | $T_{mem}$ (ns) | $T_{fault}$ (µs) | $EAT$ (µs) | Slowdown Factor vs. $T_{mem}$ |
|---------------------|----------------|------------------|------------|-------------------------------|
| 0 (no faults) | 100 | 10,000 | 0.10 | 1× |
| $10^{-5}$ | 100 | 10,000 | 0.20 | 2× |
| $10^{-4}$ | 100 | 10,000 | 1.10 | 11× |
| $10^{-3}$ | 100 | 10,000 | 10.10 | 101× |
| $10^{-2}$ | 100 | 10,000 | 100.10 | 1001× |
| $10^{-1}$ | 100 | 10,000 | 1000.10 | 10001× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a KTU favorite.** Examiners often ask students to compute the EAT for a given page fault rate. Memorize the formula, not just the result.

### 3.4 Derivation: How Many Pages Fit in RAM vs. Disk

**Given:** RAM = 16 GB, Page Size = 4 KB, Process = 1 GB.

**Step 1:** Number of pages in the process:

$$
N_{pages} = \frac{M_{process}}{P_{size}} = \frac{1\ GB}{4\ KB} = \frac{2^{30}}{2^{12}} = 2^{18} = 262{,}144\ \text{pages}
$$

**Step 2:** Frames available in RAM:

$$
N_{frames} = \frac{RAM}{P_{size}} = \frac{16\ GB}{4\ KB} = \frac{2^{34}}{2^{12}} = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304\ \text{frames}
$$

**Step 3:** Swap space required to host the entire process:

$$
S_{swap} \geq M_{process} = 1\ GB
$$

This proves that even though the process is much smaller than RAM, swap space equal to the process size is required for full virtual memory support.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Swapping Process Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the complete lifecycle of a process from creation, to swapping out, to swapping back in.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Process Ready in RAM]) --> nodeB{Memory Pressure Check}
    nodeB -- "Frames Available" --> nodeC[Continue Execution]
    nodeC --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "Frames Below Threshold" --> nodeD[Select Victim Process]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Mark Process State = READY SUSPEND]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Copy Entire Address Space to Swap Space on Disk]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Release Physical Frames to Free List]
    nodeG --> nodeH[New Process Can Now be Loaded into RAM]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Dispatcher Schedules Suspended Process}
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Copy Image Back from Swap Space to RAM]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Restore Page Tables and TLB]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Resume Process Execution]
    nodeL --> nodeA

    classDef stateNode fill:#1E40AF,stroke:#0F172A,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decNode fill:#F59E0B,stroke:#7C2D12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef diskNode fill:#10B981,stroke:#064E3B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ramNode fill:#EF4444,stroke:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    class nodeA,nodeC,nodeL stateNode
    class nodeB,nodeI decNode
    class nodeF,nodeJ diskNode
    class nodeH,nodeG ramNode
```

### 4.2 Memory Hierarchy vs. Swap Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[CPU Registers] -->|Fastest, Smallest| nodeB[Cache L1 L2 L3]
    nodeB -->|Faster, Small| nodeC[Main Memory RAM]
    nodeC -->|Page Fault Swap| nodeD[Swap Space on Disk]
    nodeD -.->|Page In| nodeC
    nodeC -.->|Context Switch| nodeA

    classDef fastNode fill:#DC2626,stroke:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef medNode fill:#F59E0B,stroke:#7C2D12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef slowNode fill:#3B82F6,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    class nodeA fastNode
    class nodeB,nodeC medNode
    class nodeD slowNode
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Page Fault & Swap-In Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[User Process Issues Memory Access] --> nodeB{Page Present in RAM}
    nodeB -- "YES" --> nodeC[Direct Access from Frame]
    nodeB -- "NO" --> nodeD[Trap to Kernel Page Fault Handler]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Check Swap Space Entry}
    nodeE -- "Page in Swap" --> nodeF[Select Victim Frame using LRU FIFO Clock]
    nodeE -- "Illegal Access" --> nodeG[Generate Segmentation Fault]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Write Victim Page to Swap if Dirty]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Read Required Page from Swap]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Update Page Table Entry]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Refresh TLB]
    nodeK --> nodeC

    classDef procNode fill:#7C3AED,stroke:#4C1D95,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decNode fill:#F59E0B,stroke:#7C2D12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef diskNode fill:#10B981,stroke:#064E3B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef faultNode fill:#EF4444,stroke:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    class nodeA,nodeC procNode
    class nodeB,nodeE decNode
    class nodeH,nodeI diskNode
    class nodeD,nodeG faultNode
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Whole-Process Swapping vs. Page-Level Swapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeSubA[Classical Whole Process Swapping]
        nodeA1[Process P1] --> nodeA2[Process P2]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Process P3]
        nodeA1 -.->|Swap Out Entire 1 GB| nodeA4[Disk]
        nodeA2 -.->|Swap Out Entire 1 GB| nodeA4
    end

    subgraph nodeSubB[Modern Page Level Paging]
        nodeB1[Process P1 Pages] --> nodeB2[Active Page Set]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Inactive Pages]
        nodeB3 -.->|Swap Only Unused Pages| nodeB4[Disk Swap]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Followed:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeB1`, `nodeA1`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone IDs.
> - All quoted node labels contain **raw uppercase / alphanumeric text only** with no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
Define **Swapping** in the context of operating systems. Why is it considered essential for multiprogramming?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Swapping is a memory management technique in which the operating system moves entire processes or portions of processes between main memory (RAM) and secondary storage (swap space on disk) to free up physical memory.
- **Importance for Multiprogramming (1.5 marks):** It enables the system to support a larger number of concurrent processes than the physical RAM can hold. By temporarily moving inactive processes to disk, the OS creates space for active processes, thereby improving **CPU utilization** and **system throughput**.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Distinguish between **Swap In** and **Swap Out** operations. When does each occur?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Swap Out (1.5 marks):** The transfer of a process or page **from RAM to the swap space** on disk. Triggered when memory is over-committed and an inactive process must be evicted.
- **Swap In (1.5 marks):** The transfer of a process or page **from the swap space back into RAM**. Triggered when a suspended process is selected by the scheduler for execution, or when a page fault occurs for a page currently residing in the swap device.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions of 14 marks must offer an internal choice. Below we present **Question A** and **Question B** as independent alternatives. The student answers **either** A or B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**a)** Explain the concept of **Swapping** in operating systems with a neat diagram. Discuss the factors that influence the design of the swap space on disk. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Consider a system with memory access time $T_{mem} = 200$ ns, page fault service time $T_{fault} = 10$ ms, and a page fault rate of $p = 0.0005$. Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition & Working (3 marks):** Swapping is the movement of processes or pages between main memory and swap space. When RAM is under pressure, the OS selects a process in *Blocked* state, copies its address space to the swap partition, and frees its frames. When the process is ready to execute again, it is copied back.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Figure 4.1** (Mermaid flowchart above) showing the swap-out and swap-in lifecycle.

**Factors Influencing Swap Space Design (2 marks):**
- Size of physical RAM (typically 2× to 4× RAM)
- Maximum process size
- Workload characteristics (compute-bound vs. I/O-bound)
- Need for hibernation support
