- Disk type (HDD vs. SSD affects speed)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given values:
- $T_{mem} = 200$ ns $= 200 \times 10^{-9}$ s
- $T_{fault} = 10$ ms $= 10 \times 10^{-3}$ s $= 10^7$ ns
- $p = 0.0005 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$

**Step 2:** Apply the EAT formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{fault} \\
    &= (1 - 0.0005) \cdot 200 + 0.0005 \cdot (10^7) \\
    &= 0.9995 \cdot 200 + 5000 \\
    &= 199.9 + 5000 \\
    &= 5199.9\ \text{ns} \approx 5.2\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Comment on the result (2 marks):

$$
\text{Slowdown Factor} = \frac{5199.9}{200} \approx 26\times
$$

Even a tiny page fault rate of 0.05% slows the system by **26 times**. This demonstrates the critical need for **good page replacement algorithms**, **TLBs**, and **process working-set tuning**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how **Demand Paging** combines the concept of swapping with paging to provide virtual memory. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A system has 8 GB of RAM and uses a page size of 4 KB. A process requires 2 GB of address space. Calculate: (i) Number of pages in the process, (ii) Number of frames in RAM, (iii) Minimum swap space needed on disk. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Demand paging is a memory management scheme where pages of a process are loaded into RAM **only when they are actually needed** (i.e., on demand), not in advance. When a referenced page is not in RAM, a *page fault* occurs, and the OS fetches the page from secondary storage.

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** Refer to **Figure 4.3** (Mermaid block diagram). The flow is:
- Process issues a memory reference →
- MMU checks Page Table →
- If page is invalid, *Page Fault* is raised →
- OS checks swap space →
- If found, page is loaded into a free/victim frame →
- Page Table and TLB are updated →
- Instruction is restarted.

**How it Combines Swapping + Paging (2 marks):** Swapping is done at the **page granularity** rather than the process granularity. The swap space is divided into page-sized slots, and the OS swaps only the missing pages in/out. This drastically reduces I/O volume compared to classical swapping.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- RAM = 8 GB $= 8 \times 2^{30}$ bytes
- Page size $P = 4$ KB $= 4 \times 2^{10} = 2^{12}$ bytes
- Process size $M = 2$ GB $= 2 \times 2^{30} = 2^{31}$ bytes

**(i) Number of pages in the process (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{pages} &= \frac{M}{P} \\
          &= \frac{2^{31}}{2^{12}} = 2^{19} \\
          &= 524{,}288\ \text{pages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of frames in RAM (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{frames} &= \frac{RAM}{P} \\
           &= \frac{8 \times 2^{30}}{2^{12}} = 8 \times 2^{18} \\
           &= 2{,}097{,}152\ \text{frames}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Minimum swap space needed (3 marks):**
For full virtual memory support, the entire process must fit in swap space when it is completely evicted:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{swap\_min} &= M \\
              &= 2\ GB = 2^{31}\ \text{bytes} \\
              &= 2{,}147{,}483{,}648\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The OS may allocate slightly more (e.g., 2.5 GB) for metadata and headroom, but the theoretical minimum equals the process size.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert milliseconds to nanoseconds in the EAT formula. Always show unit conversion steps explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-p)$ term:** Many students write $EAT = p \cdot T_{fault}$ only. This loses 2-3 marks. The full formula has both terms.
> 3. **Confusing Paging with Swapping:** Swapping is the *act* of moving data between RAM and disk. Paging is a *memory management scheme* that *uses* swapping at the page level. Examiners expect students to articulate this distinction.
> 4. **No Diagram:** A 7-mark question on Swapping **must** include a diagram. Skipping the diagram costs 2 marks minimum.
> 5. **Missing Swapping Overhead Discussion:** EAT questions often expect a concluding comment on the slowdown factor, not just a numerical answer.
> 6. **Swapping ≠ Virtual Memory:** Swapping is one *technique* that helps implement virtual memory. Conflating them is a conceptual error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Swapping** = movement of processes/pages between **RAM ↔ Disk swap space**.
- [x] **Swap In** = Disk → RAM; **Swap Out** = RAM → Disk.
- [x] **Swap space** is typically 2× to 4× the size of physical RAM.
- [x] **Standard page size** = 4 KB on x86; 8 KB / 16 KB on modern 64-bit systems.
- [x] **Effective Access Time formula:** $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{fault}$.
- [x] A page fault rate of just $10^{-3}$ can slow a system by **100×** — disk I/O is the bottleneck.
- [x] Classical swapping moves **whole processes**; modern systems swap **individual pages** (demand paging).
- [x] **Thrashing** occurs when total page demand of all processes exceeds available frames; system spends more time swapping than executing.
- [x] Linux uses **swappiness** parameter (0 to 100) to control the kernel's preference for swap out.
- [x] Windows uses the `pagefile.sys` file for swap; Linux uses dedicated **swap partitions** or **swap files**.
- [x] Key components involved: **MMU, Page Table, TLB, Swap Space, Page Fault Handler, Page Replacement Algorithm**.
- [x] Swapping is essential for **multiprogramming, process isolation, and providing a larger virtual address space** than physical memory.
- [x] Diagram-based questions on Swapping require a clear **swapping flow diagram** (use the Mermaid template in Section 4 as a model).
- [x] EAT numerical problems must always show: **formula → substitution → unit conversion → final answer → comment**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contiguous Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Contiguous Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Contiguous Memory Allocation** is a classical memory management scheme in which the operating system allocates a **single, continuous block of physical memory** (a contiguous range of physical addresses) to each process. The entire process — its code, data, stack, and heap segments — must reside in one uninterrupted span of RAM, with no gaps between the segments of the same process.

The scheme is implemented and enforced by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** using a pair of CPU registers:

- **Base Register (Relocation Register):** Holds the starting physical address of the process's allocated memory block.
- **Limit Register (Bound Register):** Holds the size (or end address) of the allocated block; every memory reference is validated against this bound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Contiguous Memory Allocation, each process is confined to a single contiguous section of physical memory. The base and limit registers define the process's addressable region and prevent it from accessing memory belonging to the OS or other processes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cinema Hall Booking" Model

Imagine a single-screen cinema hall with **120 seats arranged in one straight row**.

- When a group of **5 friends** arrives, the manager assigns them **seats 21–25** (5 consecutive seats). They cannot be split across rows 1 and 2.
- The "starting seat number" is the **Base Register**, and the "number of seats allotted" is the **Limit Register**.
- If a friend in seat 23 tries to walk over to seat 50 (outside their booking), the manager stops them — this is **memory protection**.

Now scale this up: the cinema is **physical RAM (e.g., 4 GB)**, the groups are **processes**, and the manager is the **MMU + OS**. The OS must also keep a **"seat map"** (free list / partition table) to know which sections are available when new processes arrive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Contiguous"?** Because just like the cinema seats, every byte of a process's working set must lie in a single unbroken run of physical addresses. This is the defining constraint that distinguishes this scheme from **Non-Contiguous Allocation** (paging, segmentation), which we will study later in the module.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Logical (Virtual) Address** | Address generated by the CPU; relative to the process (starts at 0) |
| **Physical Address** | Actual address in physical RAM |
| **Relocation** | Mapping a logical address to a physical address at run-time |
| **Partition** | A contiguous block of memory reserved for a process |
| **Hole** | A free block of memory available for allocation |
| **Fragmentation** | Wasted memory that cannot be effectively used |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric used by KTU questions:** Address space size is typically given in **KB**, **MB**, or **GB**, and address calculations are performed in **bytes** (1 KB = 1024 B; 1 MB = 1024 KB).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Flow — How a Memory Reference is Resolved

Every CPU-generated memory access undergoes the following sequence inside the MMU:

1. **Trap / Interrupt on context switch:** The OS loads the chosen process's `Base` and `Limit` values into the dedicated CPU registers.
2. **CPU issues a logical address `LA`** (e.g., to fetch an instruction or read a variable).
3. **MMU checks the bound:** if `LA ≥ Limit`, the MMU raises a **trap** (address error / segmentation fault) and the offending process is terminated.
4. **MMU computes the physical address:**

$$\text{Physical Address (PA)} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Logical Address (LA)}$$

5. The physical address is placed on the memory bus and the requested byte/word is fetched from RAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always remember the **order of checks** — *bound check first, then address translation*. Skipping the bound check before computing the addition is a common mistake that costs marks.

## 2.2 Two Variants of Contiguous Allocation

### (a) Fixed (Static) Partitioning
- Memory is divided into a fixed number of unequal (or equal) partitions **at system boot time**.
- Each partition can hold exactly **one process**.
- **Drawback:** Suffers from **Internal Fragmentation** (wasted space inside an allocated partition).

### (b) Variable (Dynamic) Partitioning
- Partitions are created dynamically — sized exactly to match the process request.
- **Advantage:** No internal fragmentation.
- **Drawback:** Suffers from **External Fragmentation** (small free holes scattered through memory that collectively satisfy a request but are not contiguous).

## 2.3 Placement Strategies (for Variable Partitioning)

When the OS searches the free list to place a new process, three classical strategies are used:

| Strategy | Selection Rule | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **First-Fit** | Allocate the **first** hole that is large enough | Fast; tends to leave small holes at the front |
| **Best-Fit** | Allocate the **smallest** hole that is large enough | Minimizes wasted space per allocation; leaves many tiny unusable holes |
| **Worst-Fit** | Allocate the **largest** hole available | Leaves the largest possible remainder; rarely optimal in practice |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-ready and have appeared in previous KTU ESE question papers for the Modern Operating Systems course.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PA = \text{Base} + LA$ | Physical address generation by the MMU |
| 2 | $\text{Valid iff } 0 \leq LA < \text{Limit}$ | Bound check condition for memory protection |
| 3 | $\text{Internal Fragmentation} = \text{Partition Size} - \text{Process Size}$ | Wasted bytes inside a fixed partition |
| 4 | $\text{External Fragmentation} = \sum \text{Holes} - \text{Request Size}$ (if request cannot be satisfied) | Memory that is free but unusable due to non-contiguity |
| 5 | **50% Rule (First-Fit):** Expected unusable memory $\approx 0.5 \cdot N$ blocks | For $N$ allocated blocks, half a block is lost to fragmentation on average |
| 6 | $\text{Memory Utilization} (\%) = \dfrac{\text{Used Memory}}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100$ | Standard performance metric |
| 7 | $\text{Logical Address Range} = [0, \, 2^n - 1]$ for an $n$-bit CPU | Defines the address space size |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although pure contiguous allocation is rarely used in modern general-purpose OSes (replaced by paging), the **base + limit** concept survives in:

- **Embedded & Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** Predictable memory layout, no paging overhead.
- **Kernel Address Space Protection:** The lower region of kernel memory is contiguous and protected by the MMU.
- **Single-Process Controllers & Boot Loaders:** Where only one program runs and the entire memory map is statically defined.
- **GPU Shader Cores & DSPs:** Local memory blocks are allocated contiguously for latency determinism.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logical-to-Physical Address Translation

### Given
- A process $P$ has been loaded into a memory partition that **starts at physical address** $B$ (Base Register value).
- The partition has **length** $L$ (Limit Register value, in bytes).
- The CPU generates a logical address $LA$ (offset from the start of the process).

### Step 1 — Validate the Logical Address
The MMU must first verify that $LA$ is within the process's allotted range.

$$\text{Condition for valid access: } 0 \leq LA < L$$

If the condition fails, the MMU raises a **trap** to the OS, and the process is terminated with a memory violation error.

### Step 2 — Translate to a Physical Address
Once validated, the logical address is translated by simple offset addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= B + LA \\
   &= \text{(starting physical address of partition)} + \text{(offset of the requested byte within the process)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Issue the Memory Request
The physical address $PA$ is dispatched over the address bus, and the requested byte/word is read from (or written to) physical RAM.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

### Problem
A system uses Contiguous Memory Allocation. The OS has loaded Process $P_1$ with **Base Register = 3000** and **Limit Register = 1200**.
The CPU generates a logical address **LA = 532**.

**(a)** Is the access valid?  
**(b)** Compute the corresponding physical address.

### Solution — Step by Step

#### Part (a): Validity Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check: } & 0 \leq LA < L \\
& 0 \leq 532 < 1200 \\
& \text{Both conditions hold.}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **The access is valid.** The process is permitted to reference this logical address.

#### Part (b): Physical Address Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= \text{Base} + LA \\
   &= 3000 + 532 \\
   &= 3532
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **The CPU will access physical address 3532.**

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example: Fragmentation Analysis

### Problem
A system uses **Fixed Partitioning** with partition sizes: **100 KB, 250 KB, 150 KB, 400 KB**. The OS must schedule the following processes in order:

| Process | Size |
|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 90 KB |
| $P_2$ | 210 KB |
| $P_3$ | 130 KB |
| $P_4$ | 380 KB |

Compute the **total internal fragmentation** after all processes are placed.

### Solution — Step by Step

| Process | Allocated Partition | Process Size | Internal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 100 KB | 90 KB | $100 - 90 = 10$ KB |
| $P_2$ | 250 KB | 210 KB | $250 - 210 = 40$ KB |
| $P_3$ | 150 KB | 130 KB | $150 - 130 = 20$ KB |
| $P_4$ | 400 KB | 380 KB | $400 - 380 = 20$ KB |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Internal Fragmentation} &= 10 + 40 + 20 + 20 \\
&= 90 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example: First-Fit Allocation

### Problem
Memory has free holes at the following (start address, size) pairs: **(100 K, 50 K)**, **(200 K, 150 K)**, **(350 K, 100 K)**, **(550 K, 200 K)**. Requests arrive in order: **60 K, 120 K, 70 K, 180 K**.

Show the allocation using **First-Fit** and compute any **external fragmentation**.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Request 1 — 60 K:**
- First hole from 100 K has size 50 K → insufficient.
- Next hole from 200 K has size 150 K → ✅ **allocated**.  
- Remaining hole: 150 K − 60 K = **90 K** at address 200 K.

**Request 2 — 120 K:**
- First hole from 100 K has size 50 K → insufficient.
- Hole at 200 K (now 90 K) → insufficient.
- Hole at 350 K has size 100 K → insufficient.
- Hole at 550 K has size 200 K → ✅ **allocated**.  
- Remaining hole: 200 K − 120 K = **80 K** at address 550 K.

**Request 3 — 70 K:**
- Hole at 100 K = 50 K → insufficient.
- Hole at 200 K = 90 K → ✅ **allocated**.  
- Remaining hole: 90 K − 70 K = **20 K** at address 200 K.

**Request 4 — 180 K:**
- Hole at 100 K = 50 K → insufficient.
- Hole at 200 K = 20 K → insufficient.
- Hole at 350 K = 100 K → insufficient.
- Hole at 550 K = 80 K → insufficient.
- ❌ **Request cannot be satisfied.**

### Final Memory State
| Address | Size | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 100 K | 50 K | Free |
| 200 K | 20 K | Free |
| 350 K | 100 K | Free |
| 550 K | 80 K | Free |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{External Fragmentation} &= (50 + 20 + 100 + 80) \text{ K} \\
&= 250 \text{ K of total free memory, none of which can serve a 180 K request}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Fixed Partitioning | Variable Partitioning |
|---|---|---|
| Partition size | Static | Dynamic |
| Number of partitions | Fixed at boot | Varies with workload |
| Internal Fragmentation | **Yes** (wasted inside partition) | **No** (exact fit) |
| External Fragmentation | **No** | **Yes** (scattered holes) |
| Degree of multiprogramming | Limited by partition count | Limited by total memory |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate (free-list management) |
| Typical use | Old batch systems (IBM OS/360) | Modern pre-paging OSes, RTOS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the MMU and Address Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU generates Logical Address LA"] --> REG["MMU Base and Limit Registers"]
    REG --> CHECK{"LA less than Limit"}
    CHECK -- No --> TRAP["Trap to OS Address Error Process Terminated"]
    CHECK -- Yes --> ADD["Add Base Register to LA"]
    ADD --> BUS["Place result on Address Bus"]
    BUS --> RAM["Physical RAM Read or Write Completed"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The CPU emits a logical address on the left. The MMU first checks the limit; on failure, a trap halts the process. On success, the base is added to produce a physical address, which then reaches RAM.

## 4.2 Memory Layout under Variable Partitioning (after several allocations)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OS["OPERATING SYSTEM KERNEL 0 to 100 K"]
    P1["PROCESS P1 100 K to 250 K Size 150 K"]
    H1["HOLE 250 K to 300 K Size 50 K"]
    P2["PROCESS P2 300 K to 475 K Size 175 K"]
    H2["HOLE 475 K to 575 K Size 100 K"]
    P3["PROCESS P3 575 K to 700 K Size 125 K"]
    H3["HOLE 700 K to 800 K Size 100 K"]
    OS --> P1 --> H1 --> P2 --> H2 --> P3 --> H3
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow: Allocation Strategies on the Same Free List

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    REQ["New Process Request Size S"] --> FF{"First-Fit"}
    FF -- Allocates --> FFR["First hole greater than S"]
    REQ --> BF{"Best-Fit"}
    BF -- Allocates --> BFR["Smallest hole greater than S"]
    REQ --> WF{"Worst-Fit"}
    WF -- Allocates --> WFR["Largest hole available"]
    FFR --> OUTCOME["Process Loaded in RAM"]
    BFR --> OUTCOME
    WFR --> OUTCOME
```

## 4.4 Base + Limit Register Operational Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 OS dispatcher loads Base and Limit into MMU on context switch"] --> B["Step 2 CPU fetches next instruction generating LA"]
    B --> C["Step 3 MMU performs bound check LA less than Limit"]
    C -- Pass --> D["Step 4 MMU computes PA equals Base plus LA"]
    C -- Fail --> E["Step 4a Trap to OS kernel address violation"]
    D --> F["Step 5 Physical address dispatched on address bus"]
    F --> G["Step 6 RAM returns operand to CPU pipeline"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ✏️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is Contiguous Memory Allocation? Explain the role of the base and limit registers in memory protection.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Contiguous Memory Allocation is a memory management technique in which **each process is allocated a single, continuous block of physical memory** for its entire execution. The process's code, data, stack, and heap must reside within one uninterrupted range of physical addresses.

Memory protection is enforced by the **MMU** using two special CPU registers:

- **Base Register:** Contains the starting physical address of the process's allocated partition. It is the *relocation offset* added to every logical address to obtain a physical address.
- **Limit Register:** Contains the size of the allocated partition. Before translation, the MMU verifies that the generated logical address is strictly less than the limit value.

**Logical-to-Physical translation:** $PA = \text{Base} + LA$, provided $0 \leq LA < \text{Limit}$.

If a process attempts to access an address outside its limit, the MMU raises a **trap** to the OS, preventing illegal access to other processes' memory or kernel space.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation in contiguous memory allocation.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Wasted memory *inside* an allocated partition | Wasted memory that exists as scattered free holes *outside* any allocation |
| **When it occurs** | Fixed-size partitions when process size is less than partition size | Variable-size partitions after repeated allocations and deallocations |
| **Cause** | Over-provisioning by the OS | Non-contiguity of free holes |
| **Remedy** | Use variable-size partitions | Compaction, paging, segmentation |
| **Example** | Process of 90 KB in 100 KB partition → 10 KB wasted | Total free memory 250 K scattered as 50 K + 20 K + 100 K + 80 K, cannot serve a 180 K request |

---

## ✏️ PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. An internal choice between two full questions is offered; students answer **one** complete set.

---

### 🟦 **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain **Contiguous Memory Allocation** using the **base and limit register** concept. Show how a logical address is translated into a physical address.

**(b)** A system has the following free memory holes in order: **120 K, 350 K, 90 K, 200 K, 600 K, 75 K**. Processes arrive in the order **P1 = 250 K, P2 = 400 K, P3 = 150 K, P4 = 180 K**.  
Allocate memory using (i) **First-Fit** and (ii) **Best-Fit** strategies.  
Calculate the **remaining holes** in each case and identify which strategy gives **better memory utilization**.

#### Model Answer

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:** Contiguous Memory Allocation is a memory management scheme in which the OS allocates each process a **single continuous block of physical memory**.

**Architecture:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **CPU** | Generates logical addresses from program execution |
| **MMU** | Performs bound check and address translation |
| **Base Register** | Stores starting physical address of the process partition |
| **Limit Register** | Stores size (length) of the process partition |
| **Physical RAM** | Stores the actual bytes of code and data |

**Translation Process:**

1. The OS loads the chosen process's Base and Limit values into the MMU registers.
2. The CPU generates a logical address $LA$.
3. The MMU checks the bound: if $LA \geq \text{Limit}$, a **trap** is raised.
4. If valid, the MMU computes: $PA = \text{Base} + LA$.
5. The physical address is sent to RAM.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
+----------------+      Logical Address (LA)
|      CPU       | ------------------------+
+----------------+                         |
                                          v
+----------------+   +----------------+   +----------------+
|     Base       | + |  LA            | = |   Physical     |
|   Register     |   |                |   |   Address (PA) |
+----------------+   +----------------+   +----------------+
                                                  |
+----------------+                                 |
|     Limit      | ---> Bound Check: LA < Limit?  |
|   Register     |                                 |
+----------------+                                 v
                                          +----------------+
                                          |   Physical     |
                                          |     RAM        |
                                          +----------------+
```

**(7 Marks awarded for: definition 1, register roles 2, translation steps 2, diagram 2)**

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Holes (in order):** 120 K, 350 K, 90 K, 200 K, 600 K, 75 K

#### (i) First-Fit Allocation

| Request | Holes Searched (left to right) | Allocated Hole | Remaining Hole |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1 = 250 K** | 120 K ❌, **350 K ✅** | 350 K | **100 K** |
| **P2 = 400 K** | 120 K ❌, 100 K ❌, 90 K ❌, 200 K ❌, **600 K ✅** | 600 K | **200 K** |
| **P3 = 150 K** | 120 K ❌, 100 K ❌, 90 K ❌, **200 K ✅** | 200 K | **50 K** |
| **P4 = 180 K** | 120 K ❌, 100 K ❌, 90 K ❌, 50 K ❌, 200 K ❌, 75 K ❌ | — | **Not Allocated** |

**Final Free Holes (First-Fit):** 120 K, 100 K, 90 K, 50 K, 75 K

#### (ii) Best-Fit Allocation

| Request | Holes Sorted for Best Fit | Allocated Hole | Remaining Hole |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1 = 250 K** | Sorted ≥ 250 K: 350 K, 600 K → smallest = **350 K** | 350 K | **100 K** |
| **P2 = 400 K** | Sorted ≥ 400 K: 600 K → **600 K** | 600 K | **200 K** |
| **P3 = 150 K** | Sorted ≥ 150 K: 200 K → **200 K** | 200 K | **50 K** |
| **P4 = 180 K** | Sorted ≥ 180 K: none | — | **Not Allocated** |

**Final Free Holes (Best-Fit):** 120 K, 100 K, 90 K, 50 K, 75 K

#### Comparison
- Both strategies fail to allocate P4 = 180 K.
- **First-Fit** is **faster** (stops at first match), **Best-Fit** is **slower** but minimizes the size of wasted remainder per allocation.
- In this specific case, **First-Fit performs equally** because the smallest suitable holes happen to be the first ones encountered.

**(7 Marks awarded for: First-Fit table 2, Best-Fit table 2, comparison 2, conclusion 1)**

---

### 🟩 **Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **Fixed Partitioning** and **Variable Partitioning** schemes. Explain why **variable partitioning suffers from external fragmentation** with a suitable example.

**(b)** Consider a system using **Fixed Partitioning** with partition sizes: **200 K, 400 K, 100 K, 300 K, 150 K**. Processes arrive with sizes: **P1 = 180 K, P2 = 90 K, P3 = 350 K, P4 = 140 K, P5 = 250 K**.  
Determine:  
(i) Which process is placed in which partition.  
(ii) The total **internal fragmentation**.  
(iii) The total **wasted memory** that cannot be used for any further process.

#### Model Answer

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Feature | Fixed Partitioning | Variable Partitioning |
|---|---|---|
| Partition size | Fixed at boot | Sized exactly to process request |
| Internal fragmentation | Yes (high) | No |
| External fragmentation | No | Yes |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Implementation | Simple tables | Free-list management |

**Why external fragmentation occurs in variable partitioning (example):**

Suppose memory has free holes of sizes **50 K, 70 K, 80 K, 100 K** scattered non-contiguously. A new process requiring **200 K** cannot be loaded even though the total free memory is **300 K**, because no single hole is large enough. This unusable-but-free memory is called **external fragmentation**.

The only remedies are **compaction** (expensive and time-consuming) or moving to a **non-contiguous scheme** like paging.

**(7 Marks awarded for: comparison table 3, example with figure 4)**

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Partitions:** 200 K, 400 K, 100 K, 300 K, 150 K

| Process | Size | Allocated Partition | Internal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 180 K | 200 K | $200 - 180 = 20$ K |
| P2 | 90 K | 100 K | $100 - 90 = 10$ K |
| P3 | 350 K | 400 K | $400 - 350 = 50$ K |
| P4 | 140 K | 150 K | $150 - 140 = 10$ K |
| P5 | 250 K | 300 K | $300 - 250 = 50$ K |

**(i)** Assignment shown in table above.

**(ii) Total Internal Fragmentation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 20 + 10 + 50 + 10 + 50 \\
&= 140 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Wasted Memory:**

The 140 K of internal fragmentation inside the partitions is **unavailable** for any future process, because the system uses fixed-size partitions. Hence, total wasted memory = **140 K**.

**(7 Marks awarded for: allocation table 3, internal fragmentation sum 2, total wasted memory 2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the bound check before translation** (–1 mark): Always write *"check LA < Limit, then PA = Base + LA"*. Computing the sum blindly is incomplete.
> 2. **Mixing up the unit (KB vs K):** KTU papers use **1 K = 1024 bytes** when address arithmetic is in binary, but for fragmentation problems both **K (decimal) and KB (1024-byte)** are often treated as 1000/1024 interchangeably — *read the question stem carefully* and write the unit you assumed.
> 3. **Not showing the remaining hole after each allocation** in placement problems (–2 marks): The remaining hole, not the original, becomes the new entry in the free list.
> 4. **Confusing First-Fit with Best-Fit:** First-Fit = *first* match scanning left to right; Best-Fit = *smallest sufficient* match across the entire list. Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for showing this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in part (a):** A labelled address-translation diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question on this topic.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Contiguous Memory Allocation assigns **one unbroken block** of physical memory to each process.
- **Two Registers — The Heart of the Scheme:**
  - **Base Register** = starting physical address of the process's block.
  - **Limit Register** = size of the block; used to validate every access.
- **Translation Formula:** $PA = \text{Base} + LA$ **only if** $0 \leq LA < \text{Limit}$.
- **Bound check FIRST, then translate** — order matters for marks and for OS correctness.
- **Fixed Partitioning** → suffers from **Internal Fragmentation** (compute as Partition − Process).
- **Variable Partitioning** → suffers from **External Fragmentation** (cannot serve a request even if total free memory is sufficient).
- **Three Placement Strategies:** First-Fit (fast), Best-Fit (minimizes per-allocation waste, but slow), Worst-Fit (rarely used; leaves largest remainder).
- **50% Rule:** With First-Fit, on average **one-third of memory** may be unusable due to external fragmentation; for $N$ allocated blocks, approximately $0.5N$ blocks are lost.
- **Limitations of Contiguous Allocation:** Suffers from both fragmentation types, limits multiprogramming, no sharing of code, no partial loading — paving the way for **paging and segmentation** (next topics in the module).
- **MMU must run in privileged/kernel mode** to modify Base/Limit registers — the user process must never directly load these values.
- **Compaction** is a possible (but expensive) remedy for external fragmentation, requiring process relocation and invalidation of all in-memory pointers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed and Variable Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fixed and Variable Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Contiguous Memory Allocation** is a classical memory management scheme in which each process is allocated a single, continuous block of physical memory. Under this scheme, the OS partitions the main memory into chunks, and every resident process occupies one such chunk whose physical addresses form a contiguous range from a base to a limit register. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Modern Operating Systems*, the two foundational contiguous allocation strategies are:

- **Fixed (Static) Partitions** — formally known as *Multiprogramming with a Fixed number of Tasks (MFT)*. The memory is divided at system boot time into a fixed number of fixed-size partitions.
- **Variable (Dynamic) Partitions** — formally known as *Multiprogramming with a Variable number of Tasks (MVT)*. Partitions are created dynamically at load time to exactly match the size of each incoming process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** *Fixed and Variable Partitions* is a high-weightage topic in Module 3. It directly addresses **Course Outcome CO3**: *“Analyze memory management, virtual memory, and file system techniques used in modern operating systems.”* Expect 7 to 14-mark questions in University examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Theatre Ticket Analogy**
> Imagine a cinema hall with **120 seats**.
> - **Fixed Partition** = The hall is pre-divided into 4 blocks of 30 seats each (3 boxes for the *Avengers*, 1 for the *Chhava* audience). A group of 10 people cannot use Block 1 even if 20 seats are empty — those 20 seats are **wasted** forever. This wasted space is *Internal Fragmentation*.
> - **Variable Partition** = The hall has no fixed blocks. When a group of 10 arrives, a 10-seat section is **carved out** for them. When a 40-person group arrives, a 40-seat section is created. As groups leave, the hall becomes a patchwork of free and occupied patches. If the patches are too small for new large groups, the hall looks full even when **half the seats are empty** — this is *External Fragmentation*.

The two key efficiency metrics you must memorize for KTU problems are:
- **Internal Fragmentation** = Space allocated **but never used** (lies inside a partition).
- **External Fragmentation** = Free space available **but unusable** (too small / scattered).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Total Physical Memory | $M$ | **KB / MB / GB** |
| Partition Size | $S_i$ | KB |
| Process Size | $P_i$ | KB |
| Number of Partitions | $n$ | dimensionless |
| Fragmentation Ratio | $F_r$ | percentage (%) |

Explicit physical constants: **1 KB = $2^{10}$ bytes = 1024 bytes**, **1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes**, **1 GB = $2^{30}$ bytes**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fixed (Static) Partitions — Operational Theory

In **MFT**, at system generation (boot) time, the OS designer decides:
1. How many partitions $(n)$ will exist.
2. How large each partition $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$ will be (all equal OR unequal).

**Why fixed partitions?** Simplicity. The OS keeps a simple *partition table* with base register and limit register for each partition. Dispatch logic is O(1).

**How it works — step-by-step logic:**
- On process arrival, the **Ready Queue** is scanned.
- A process $P_i$ of size $P_i$ is loaded into **any partition $j$** such that $S_j \geq P_i$.
- If no such partition exists, the process waits (or is swapped out).
- Since $S_j$ is fixed, leftover space is always wasted.

**The “Why” — Internal Fragmentation:**
If $P_i = 60$ KB and is placed in a partition $S_j = 100$ KB, the **40 KB slack** is *unusable* by anyone else because the boundary is fixed. This is **internal fragmentation** — it is *inside* the allocated block.

**Equation of Internal Fragmentation for Fixed Partitions:**

$$IF_i = S_j - P_i \quad \text{(per process)}$$

$$IF_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (S_{assigned(i)} - P_i) \quad \text{(for } k \text{ loaded processes)}$$

**Average (expected) internal fragmentation** when partition size is $S$ and process sizes are uniformly distributed in $[0, S]$:

$$E[IF] = \frac{S}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MFT was used in **IBM OS/360 (MFT)**, **CTSS**, and early mainframes. It is *deterministic* — real-time engineers loved it because worst-case memory access time is bounded. Modern RTOS kernels (e.g., **FreeRTOS heap_4.c**) still use static partitioning for embedded firmware.

### 2.2 Variable (Dynamic) Partitions — Operational Theory

In **MVT**, no fixed boundaries exist. The OS maintains a list of **free holes** (contiguous free blocks). When a process $P_i$ of size $P_i$ arrives, the OS searches the free list and allocates *exactly* $P_i$ bytes from one hole, leaving a smaller hole behind.

**The “Why” — No Internal Fragmentation:**
Because the partition is sized exactly to the process, $IF = 0$ by construction. However, a new problem emerges: **External Fragmentation**.

**External Fragmentation Equation:**

$$EF = \text{Total Free Memory} - \text{Largest Single Free Hole}$$

$$EF_{ratio} = \frac{1 - \frac{\text{Largest Free Hole}}{\text{Total Free Memory}}}{1} \times 100\%$$

**Three Allocation Strategies (KTU exam favourite):**

| Strategy | Selection Rule | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Fit** | Allocate the **first** hole large enough | Fastest; simple | Leaves small unusable holes at the front |
| **Best-Fit** | Allocate the **smallest** hole that fits | Minimizes leftover size | Produces many tiny unusable fragments; slower search |
| **Worst-Fit** | Allocate the **largest** hole | Leaves the largest leftover | Destroys big holes; bad long-term behaviour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Memory Trick:** **“First is Fast, Best is Bad, Worst is Wasteful.”** Empirical studies (Knuth 1973) show **First-Fit** is generally the fastest AND has comparable fragmentation to Best-Fit. KTU examiners often ask: *“Why is First-Fit preferred in practice?”* — Answer: Speed + statistical performance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Internal Fragmentation (per process) | $IF_i = S - P_i$ | Bytes; $S > P_i$ |
| 2 | Expected Internal Fragmentation | $E[IF] = S/2$ | Uniform distribution |
| 3 | External Fragmentation (absolute) | $EF = F_{total} - F_{largest}$ | Bytes |
| 4 | External Fragmentation (ratio) | $EF_r = \left(1 - \frac{F_{largest}}{F_{total}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 5 | Number of Partitions (MFT) | $n = \left\lfloor M / S \right\rfloor$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Wasted Memory Ratio (MFT) | $W = \frac{nS - \sum P_i}{M}$ | Percentage |
| 7 | Compaction Bound (50% rule) | Unmovable $\leq 0.5 \times M$ | Condition for $O(1)$ compaction |
| 8 | Buddy System Allocation | $2^k$ where $2^{k-1} < P_i \leq 2^k$ | Power of 2 |
| 9 | Buddy System Internal Frag. | $IF < 50\%$ of requested block | Upper bound |
| 10 | Address Translation | $PA = Base + LA$ | Logical to Physical |

**Note:** In the table above, $F_{total}$ denotes total free memory and $F_{largest}$ denotes the largest single contiguous free hole. The vertical bar notation for “size of” has been written as $\vert x \vert$ only outside table rows to preserve markdown syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MFT (Fixed):** Used in **embedded safety-critical systems** (AUTOSAR, DO-178C avionics) where memory must be deterministically partitioned to satisfy WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time) guarantees.
- **MVT (Variable):** The conceptual ancestor of today’s **heap allocators** — `malloc()` in glibc, **dlmalloc**, **jemalloc**, and **tcmalloc** all use best-fit or size-class strategies derived from variable partitioning.
- **Compaction** is the direct conceptual ancestor of **Garbage Collection** in Java/.NET runtimes.
- **Buddy System** is used in the **Linux kernel page allocator** (zones like ZONE_NORMAL use a buddy-like system for power-of-2 page frames).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expected Internal Fragmentation in Fixed Partitions

**Problem statement:** Partition size is $S$. A process requesting memory is uniformly distributed in $[a, S]$ with $0 \leq a < S$. Compute $E[IF]$.

**Step 1 — Set up the expectation.**
The internal fragmentation for a process of size $x$ placed in partition $S$ is:

$$IF(x) = S - x$$

**Step 2 — Write the expectation integral.**

$$E[IF] = \int_{a}^{S} (S - x) \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Substitute uniform PDF $f(x) = \frac{1}{S - a}$.**

$$E[IF] = \frac{1}{S - a} \int_{a}^{S} (S - x) \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral using the antiderivative $\int (S-x)\,dx = Sx - \frac{x^2}{2}$.**

$$E[IF] = \frac{1}{S-a} \left[ Sx - \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{a}^{S}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute upper limit $x=S$:**

$$Sx - \frac{x^2}{2} \bigg|_{x=S} = S^2 - \frac{S^2}{2} = \frac{S^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute lower limit $x=a$:**

$$Sx - \frac{x^2}{2} \bigg|_{x=a} = Sa - \frac{a^2}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Subtract and simplify:**

$$E[IF] = \frac{1}{S-a} \left[ \frac{S^2}{2} - Sa + \frac{a^2}{2} \right] = \frac{1}{S-a} \cdot \frac{S^2 - 2Sa + a^2}{2}$$

$$E[IF] = \frac{(S-a)^2}{2(S-a)} = \frac{S-a}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Special case $a=0$ (process can be of any size from 0 to $S$):**

$$E[IF]_{a=0} = \frac{S}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** On average, **half a partition is wasted** due to internal fragmentation in MFT — a brutal inefficiency that motivated the move to MVT.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Solved KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]:**
Memory size $M = 100$ KB. A fixed partition system has **three partitions** of sizes $S_1 = 30$ KB, $S_2 = 40$ KB, $S_3 = 30$ KB. Processes arrive in the order: $P_1 = 25$ KB, $P_2 = 10$ KB, $P_3 = 30$ KB, $P_4 = 15$ KB. Use **First-Fit**. Compute total internal fragmentation.

**Step 1 — Tabulate partitions and their current status (initially all free).**

| Partition | Size | Status | Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | 30 KB | Free | — |
| $S_2$ | 40 KB | Free | — |
| $S_3$ | 30 KB | Free | — |

**Step 2 — Place $P_1 = 25$ KB using First-Fit.** First free partition is $S_1 = 30$ KB.

$$S_1 \geq P_1 \;\Rightarrow\; 30 \geq 25 \;[\text{TRUE}]$$

Place $P_1$ in $S_1$. Fragmentation:

$$IF_1 = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3 — Place $P_2 = 10$ KB.** Next free partition is $S_2 = 40$ KB.

$$S_2 \geq P_2 \;\Rightarrow\; 40 \geq 10 \;[\text{TRUE}]$$

Place $P_2$ in $S_2$. Fragmentation:

$$IF_2 = 40 - 10 = 30 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 — Place $P_3 = 30$ KB.** Next free partition is $S_3 = 30$ KB.

$$S_3 \geq P_3 \;\Rightarrow\; 30 \geq 30 \;[\text{TRUE}]$$

Place $P_3$ in $S_3$. Fragmentation:

$$IF_3 = 30 - 30 = 0 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 5 — Place $P_4 = 15$ KB.** All partitions are full. **Cannot allocate — $P_4$ waits.**

**Step 6 — Total internal fragmentation:**

$$IF_{total} = IF_1 + IF_2 + IF_3 = 5 + 30 + 0 = 35 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 7 — Memory utilization:**

$$\text{Used} = 25 + 10 + 30 = 65 \text{ KB}$$

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{65}{100} \times 100\% = 65\%$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solved Variable Partition Numerical

**Problem:** A system has free holes of sizes: **50 KB, 200 KB, 70 KB, 130 KB, 90 KB, 300 KB** (in that order in the free list). Processes $P_1 = 115$ KB, $P_2 = 32$ KB, $P_3 = 220$ KB arrive. Compute allocation and external fragmentation for each strategy.

#### (a) First-Fit

**$P_1 = 115$ KB:**
Scan from start. $50 < 115$ (skip), $200 \geq 115$ ✓.
Allocate 115 KB in hole 200 KB. **Hole becomes $200 - 115 = 85$ KB.**

**$P_2 = 32$ KB:**
Continue scanning. $70 \geq 32$ ✓.
Allocate in hole 70 KB. **Hole becomes $70 - 32 = 38$ KB.**

**$P_3 = 220$ KB:**
Continue scanning. $130 < 220$, $90 < 220$, $300 \geq 220$ ✓.
Allocate in hole 300 KB. **Hole becomes $300 - 220 = 80$ KB.**

**Final holes:** 50, 85, 38, 130, 90, 80 KB.
**Total free** = $50+85+38+130+90+80 = 473$ KB.
**Largest free hole** = 130 KB.

$$EF = 473 - 130 = 343 \text{ KB}$$

#### (b) Best-Fit

**$P_1 = 115$ KB:** Smallest hole ≥ 115 is **130 KB**. Allocate. **Leftover: 15 KB.**
**$P_2 = 32$ KB:** Smallest hole ≥ 32 is **50 KB**. Allocate. **Leftover: 18 KB.**
**$P_3 = 220$ KB:** Smallest hole ≥ 220 is **300 KB**. Allocate. **Leftover: 80 KB.**

**Final holes:** 200, 18, 70, 15, 90, 80 KB.
**Total free** = $200+18+70+15+90+80 = 473$ KB.
**Largest free hole** = 200 KB.

$$EF = 473 - 200 = 273 \text{ KB}$$

#### (c) Worst-Fit

**$P_1 = 115$ KB:** Largest hole = 300 KB. Allocate. **Leftover: 185 KB.**
**$P_2 = 32$ KB:** Largest hole = 200 KB. Allocate. **Leftover: 168 KB.**
**$P_3 = 220$ KB:** Largest hole = 185 KB. $185 < 220$ → **Cannot allocate!**

**Final holes:** 50, 168, 70, 130, 90, 185 KB.
**Total free** = $50+168+70+130+90+185 = 693$ KB.
**Largest free hole** = 185 KB.

$$EF = 693 - 185 = 508 \text{ KB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Trap:** Always check whether the requested process can be allocated under the strategy! Worst-Fit *failed* for $P_3$ in this example, even though there was plenty of total memory. Students lose **3 marks** by not stating the failure explicitly.

---

### 3.4 The 50% Rule (Compaction Derivation)

**Statement:** When using First-Fit, approximately **half of the free memory** is rendered unusable due to external fragmentation, *regardless* of the optimization strategy used.

**Sketch of proof:**
Consider $N$ free holes with sizes $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_N$. The probability that any *one* hole is too small to satisfy a random request of size $x$ (uniform in $[0, S_{max}]$) is high. Knuth’s analysis shows that on average, **half the holes are too small** to be useful for the next request.

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} E\left[\frac{\text{Unusable Free}}{\text{Total Free}}\right] = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Compaction** (defragmentation) is the only way to recover this 50%. Cost: $O(M)$ I/O and CPU time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fixed Partition Memory Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OS["OS KERNEL 10 KB"]
    P1["PARTITION 1 n 30 KB"]
    P2["PARTITION 2 n 40 KB"]
    P3["PARTITION 3 n 30 KB"]
    P4["PARTITION 4 n 40 KB"]
    P5["PARTITION 5 n 30 KB"]
    UNUSED["UNUSED FRAGMENT 20 KB"]
    OS --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> UNUSED
    PROC1["PROCESS A 25 KB"] -. Occupies .-> P2
    PROC2["PROCESS B 18 KB"] -. Occupies .-> P3
    PROC3["PROCESS C 35 KB"] -. Occupies .-> P4
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice that each of P2, P3, P4 is fully occupied. The **grey difference between the partition boundary and the process boundary** is internal fragmentation — that space is wasted and inaccessible to other processes.

### 4.2 Variable Partition — Free List Evolution (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["INITIAL FREE LIST 100 KB Hole"]
    A1["After P1 30 KB allocated First Fit"]
    A2["After P2 20 KB allocated"]
    A3["After P1 exits 30 KB freed"]
    A4["After P3 40 KB arrives Cannot fit anywhere"]
    A5["After COMPACTION 60 KB usable hole"]
    INIT --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
```

**Detailed state representation in the diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StepA["STEP 1: 100 KB free"]
        S1A["Hole 100 KB"]
    end
    subgraph StepB["STEP 2: P1 30 KB loaded"]
        S2A["Process 30 KB"] --> S2B["Hole 70 KB"]
    end
    subgraph StepC["STEP 3: P2 20 KB loaded"]
        S3A["Process 30 KB"] --> S3B["Process 20 KB"] --> S3C["Hole 50 KB"]
    end
    subgraph StepD["STEP 4: P1 exits"]
        S4A["Hole 30 KB"] --> S4B["Process 20 KB"] --> S4C["Hole 50 KB"]
    end
    subgraph StepE["STEP 5: P3 40 KB wants memory"]
        S5A["Hole 30 KB"] --> S5B["Process 20 KB"] --> S5C["Hole 50 KB"]
        S5X["External Fragmentation 80 KB total BUT no single hole of 40 KB adjacent"]
    end
    subgraph StepF["STEP 6: After Compaction"]
        S6A["Process 20 KB"] --> S6B["Hole 80 KB"]
        S6Y["P3 40 KB now fits inside the 80 KB hole"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Strategy Comparison Flowchart (Decision Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ARRIVE["New Process P of size p arrives"]
    Q1{"Is ANY free hole large enough for p?"}
    FAIL["Place P in WAIT queue Memory Full"]
    FF["FIRST FIT Scan free list from start Pick first hole h_i with h_i greater or equal p"]
    BF["BEST FIT Scan entire list Pick SMALLEST h_i with h_i greater or equal p"]
    WF["WORST FIT Scan entire list Pick LARGEST h_i"]
    ALLOC["Allocate: h_i becomes h_i minus p Process loaded into Ready Queue"]
    ARRIVE --> Q1
    Q1 -->|NO| FAIL
    Q1 -->|YES| STRAT{"Allocation Strategy?"}
    STRAT -->|First Fit| FF
    STRAT -->|Best Fit| BF
    STRAT -->|Worst Fit| WF
    FF --> ALLOC
    BF --> ALLOC
    WF --> ALLOC
```

### 4.4 Memory Management Data Structure — Linked List of Holes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD["HEAD pointer"] --> N1["Node 1: Base 0 Size 50 KB Next"]
    N1 --> N2["Node 2: Base 50 Size 200 KB Next"]
    N2 --> N3["Node 3: Base 250 Size 70 KB Next"]
    N3 --> N4["Node 4: Base 320 Size 130 KB Next"]
    N4 --> N5["Node 5: Base 450 Size 90 KB Next"]
    N5 --> N6["Node 6: Base 540 Size 300 KB Next"]
    N6 --> NULL["NULL"]
```

**Note on implementation:** Each node contains `(base_address, size, next_pointer)`. A bitmap is an alternative representation where each bit represents one allocation unit (e.g., 1 KB). Bitmaps are space-efficient for large $M$; linked lists are faster for variable-size holes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
*Distinguish between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation. Which one occurs in fixed partition allocation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Internal Fragmentation (1 mark):** Wasted memory that lies *inside* an allocated partition. Occurs when the partition is larger than the process it holds. Example: 60 KB process in 100 KB partition wastes 40 KB.
- **External Fragmentation (1 mark):** Wasted memory that exists as free holes too small to satisfy any pending request, even though total free memory is sufficient.
- **Answer (1 mark):** **Fixed partition allocation suffers from INTERNAL fragmentation.** Variable partition allocation suffers from EXTERNAL fragmentation.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Compare First-Fit, Best-Fit, and Worst-Fit allocation strategies in variable partition systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **First-Fit:** Allocates the *first* hole that is large enough. Fastest; generally best in practice.
- **Best-Fit:** Allocates the *smallest* hole that is large enough. Minimizes immediate waste but produces tiny unusable fragments.
- **Worst-Fit:** Allocates the *largest* hole. Leaves large leftovers but tends to break up big holes; usually worst overall.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not skip drawing the memory map.** Always draw a memory layout diagram showing the partitions/holes before and after allocation. Missing diagram = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Always state the strategy assumption** (First-Fit / Best-Fit / Worst-Fit). If unstated, the examiner assumes First-Fit by default and may mark you wrong if your arithmetic follows another rule.
> 3. **Compute fragmentation TWICE** (once internal, once external) when both apply. Forgetting one = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Mention the 50% Rule** for First-Fit when discussing long-term external fragmentation — this is a favourite KTU follow-up question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose ANY ONE**

**Q.A. (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Explain the Fixed Partition memory allocation scheme in detail. A system has 200 KB memory divided into 4 fixed partitions of sizes 40 KB, 60 KB, 50 KB, and 50 KB. Processes arrive in the order: 35 KB, 55 KB, 20 KB, 45 KB. Use First-Fit and compute total internal fragmentation.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the concept (3 marks):**
In **Fixed (Static) Partition Multiprogramming (MFT)**, memory is divided into a fixed number of partitions at system boot time. Each partition can hold exactly one process. Partitions can be of equal or unequal sizes. The OS keeps a **partition table** with base and limit registers.

**Pros:** Simple, low overhead, deterministic.
**Cons:** Internal fragmentation, low multiprogramming level if partitions are too large, partition count is fixed at boot.

**Step 2 — Tabulate partition availability (1 mark):**

| Partition | Size | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40 KB | Free |
| 2 | 60 KB | Free |
| 3 | 50 KB | Free |
| 4 | 50 KB | Free |

**Step 3 — Allocate using First-Fit (2 marks):**
- $P_1 = 35$ KB → goes to **Partition 1 (40 KB)**. $IF_1 = 40 - 35 = 5$ KB.
- $P_2 = 55$ KB → goes to **Partition 2 (60 KB)**. $IF_2 = 60 - 55 = 5$ KB.
- $P_3 = 20$ KB → goes to **Partition 3 (50 KB)**. $IF_3 = 50 - 20 = 30$ KB.
- $P_4 = 45$ KB → goes to **Partition 4 (50 KB)**. $IF_4 = 50 - 45 = 5$ KB.

**Step 4 — Compute total internal fragmentation (1 mark):**

$$IF_{total} = 5 + 5 + 30 + 5 = 45 \text{ KB}$$

**Memory utilization:** $\frac{35+55+20+45}{200} = \frac{155}{200} = 77.5\%$. Wasted = 22.5%.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Explain the Buddy System memory allocation algorithm. A system has 64 KB initial free memory. Allocate requests for 14 KB, 18 KB, 10 KB, and 9 KB. Show the allocation and deallocation steps.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (3 marks):**
The **Buddy System** is a memory allocation algorithm that restricts allocations to **powers of 2** ($2^k$). When a request arrives, the smallest $2^k \geq P_i$ is allocated. The allocated block is split recursively, and its *buddy* is the other half. On deallocation, buddies are merged (coalesced).

**Advantages:** Fast allocation $O(\log M)$, easy coalescing, low external fragmentation.
**Disadvantages:** Up to 50% internal fragmentation (worst case when $P_i$ is just above $2^{k-1}$).

**Step 2 — Show the initial 64 KB free block (1 mark):**
Free list: [64].

**Step 3 — Allocation steps (2 marks):**
- **Request 14 KB:** Smallest $2^k \geq 14$ is **16 KB**. Split 64 into [32, 32], then split one 32 into [16, 16]. Allocate 16 KB to request 1. Free: [32, 16, 16].
- **Request 18 KB:** Smallest $2^k \geq 18$ is **32 KB**. Allocate one 32 KB block. Free: [16, 16].
- **Request 10 KB:** Smallest $2^k \geq 10$ is **16 KB**. Allocate one 16 KB block. Free: [16].
- **Request 9 KB:** Smallest $2^k \geq 9$ is **16 KB**. Allocate the last 16 KB block. Free: [] (empty).

**Step 4 — Deallocation + coalescing (1 mark):**
When the 16 KB block (allocated to request 4) is freed, its buddy (16 KB allocated to request 3) is also free → **coalesce to 32 KB**. Its buddy (32 KB allocated to request 2) is also free → **coalesce to 64 KB**. Full memory recovered.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Explain Variable Partition memory allocation. A system has free holes of 200 KB, 400 KB, 100 KB, 300 KB, and 150 KB in the order shown. Processes P1 = 220 KB, P2 = 80 KB, P3 = 280 KB arrive. Show allocation using (i) First-Fit, (ii) Best-Fit, and (iii) Worst-Fit. Compute external fragmentation in each case.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
In **Variable (Dynamic) Partition Multiprogramming (MVT)**, partitions are sized exactly to the process at load time. No internal fragmentation. Suffers from **external fragmentation**, recovered by **compaction** (relocating processes) or by using the **Buddy System**.

**Step 2 — First-Fit (1.5 marks):**
- $P_1 = 220$ KB → hole **400 KB** (first ≥ 220). Leftover 180 KB.
- $P_2 = 80$ KB → next free hole = 100 KB. Leftover 20 KB.
- $P_3 = 280$ KB → next free hole = 300 KB. Leftover 20 KB.

Free holes: 200, 180, 20, 20, 150. **Total free = 570 KB. Largest hole = 200 KB.**
$$EF_{FF} = 570 - 200 = 370 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3 — Best-Fit (1.5 marks):**
- $P_1 = 220$ KB → smallest hole ≥ 220 is **300 KB**. Leftover 80 KB.
- $P_2 = 80$ KB → smallest hole ≥ 80 is **100 KB**. Leftover 20 KB.
- $P_3 = 280$ KB → smallest hole ≥ 280 is **400 KB**. Leftover 120 KB.

Free holes: 200, 120, 20, 150, 80. **Total free = 570 KB. Largest hole = 200 KB.**
$$EF_{BF} = 570 - 200 = 370 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 — Worst-Fit (1.5 marks):**
- $P_1 = 220$ KB → largest hole **400 KB**. Leftover 180 KB.
- $P_2 = 80$ KB → largest hole now **300 KB**. Leftover 220 KB.
- $P_3 = 280$ KB → largest hole now **220 KB**. **220 < 280 → ALLOCATION FAILS.**

Free holes: 200, 220, 100, 150, 180. **Total free = 850 KB. Largest hole = 220 KB.**
$$EF_{WF} = 850 - 220 = 630 \text{ KB}$$
Allocation failure of $P_3$ is the **key takeaway** — Worst-Fit is poor when a large request arrives.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
First-Fit and Best-Fit both succeeded. Worst-Fit failed. Therefore, **First-Fit is preferred** for this workload.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is compaction? Explain the 50% rule. A system has the following free holes in order: 20 KB, 60 KB, 30 KB, 40 KB. Is compaction needed if a request for 70 KB arrives? Justify with the 50% rule.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define compaction (2 marks):**
**Compaction** is a technique to eliminate external fragmentation by **relocating all occupied partitions to one end of memory**, so that all free memory is coalesced into a single contiguous hole. It requires dynamic relocation support (base + limit registers) and is expensive ($O(M)$ I/O).

**Step 2 — Explain the 50% rule (2 marks):**
For **First-Fit allocation**, statistical analysis shows that on average, **half the free memory** exists as small unusable holes. Hence, out of $F$ free KB, only $F/2$ is statistically usable. The unusable portion is $F - F/2 = F/2$, i.e., **50%**.

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} E\left[\frac{\text{Unusable}}{\text{Total Free}}\right] \approx 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Apply to the problem (2 marks):**
Free holes: 20, 60, 30, 40 KB. Total free = $20+60+30+40 = 150$ KB.
Largest single hole = **60 KB** < 70 KB requested → **Direct allocation fails**.

**By 50% rule:** Expected usable memory = $150/2 = 75$ KB. Since 75 KB ≥ 70 KB, statistically, **compaction is justified** to consolidate the scattered holes.

**Step 4 — After compaction (1 mark):**
Move all allocated processes to the low-address end → single free hole of 150 KB. Allocate 70 KB → leftover 80 KB.

**Result:** Compaction recovers 100% of the 50% that was wasted.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ☐ **MFT (Fixed)** → Internal fragmentation. Partitions fixed at boot. Simple but wasteful.
- ☐ **MVT (Variable)** → External fragmentation. Partitions dynamic, no internal waste.
- ☐ **First-Fit** = Fastest in practice; preferred default. Suffers from 50% rule.
- ☐ **Best-Fit** = Smallest leftover; good memory utilization but slow search and many tiny holes.
- ☐ **Worst-Fit** = Largest leftover; worst overall. Try to remember a problem where it fails.
- ☐ **Internal Fragmentation Formula** → $IF_i = S_j - P_i$ per process.
- ☐ **External Fragmentation Formula** → $EF = F_{total} - F_{largest}$ absolute; or ratio percentage.
- ☐ **50% Rule** → Half of free memory becomes unusable in steady state under First-Fit.
- ☐ **Compaction** → $O(M)$ I/O cost, requires relocation hardware (base + limit registers).
- ☐ **Buddy System** → Power-of-2 allocation, $O(\log M)$, internal frag ≤ 50%, used in Linux kernel page allocator.
- ☐ **Address Translation** → $PA = Base + LA$. Requires **relocation register** (base) and **limit register** for protection.
- ☐ **CO3 Mapping** → This topic directly satisfies KTU 2024 CO3: *“Analyze memory management techniques used in modern operating systems.”*

**Frequently Asked KTU Sub-Questions (Must Prepare):**
1. Differentiate internal vs external fragmentation with examples. (3 marks)
2. Why does fixed partition cause internal fragmentation? Show with a numerical. (3–7 marks)
3. Explain First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit with a common numerical. (7–14 marks)
4. What is compaction? When is it triggered? Explain the 50% rule. (7 marks)
5. Explain Buddy System with a numerical allocation/deallocation trace. (7–14 marks)
6. Compare MFT and MVT. Why did industry shift from MFT to MVT? (7 marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Segmentation — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Segmentation** is a non-contiguous memory management scheme that partitions a program's logical address space into **variable-sized segments** based on the natural divisions imposed by the programmer or the compiler. Each segment represents a logical unit of the program such as the *main code*, *functions*, *global data*, *the heap*, *the stack*, or *symbol tables*. A logical (two-dimensional) address is expressed as an ordered pair:

$$ \text{Logical Address} = \langle \text{segment-number},\ \text{offset} \rangle $$

The operating system maintains a **Segment Table** for every process, where each entry stores the *base address* of the segment in physical memory, the *segment limit (length)*, protection/validity bits, and other control information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Segmentation is a memory-management technique that supports the **programmer's view of memory** as a collection of logically distinct address spaces, allowing code, data, and stack to be addressed independently and protected separately.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multi-storey library building**:
- Each **floor** is a *segment* (Floor 1 → Code, Floor 2 → Data, Floor 3 → Stack).
- Floors have **different sizes** (the Code floor may be larger than the Stack floor) — exactly like variable-sized segments.
- To locate a book, you specify a **floor number** (segment number) and a **position along the shelf** (offset).
- A directory at the entrance maps each *floor name* to the *physical address* where the floor actually starts in the building, and the floor's *capacity* (limit).

In the same way, a programmer references memory using `<segment, offset>`, and the OS uses the **Segment Table** to find where that segment physically resides in RAM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Paging:** In paging, both physical and logical memory are divided into **fixed-size** blocks. In segmentation, programs are divided into **variable-sized** logical units — but physical memory is still allocated in chunks matching the segment size.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Typical Value / Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------------|
| Segment Number | `s` | Index into the Segment Table (0, 1, 2, …) |
| Offset / Displacement | `d` | Position within the segment, $0 \le d < \text{limit}$ |
| Segment Base | `base` | Starting physical address of the segment |
| Segment Limit | `limit` | Length of the segment (in bytes) |
| Number of Segments per process | `k` | Typically **4 to 8** in early systems, up to **16K** in modern x86 |
| Page Size (in hybrid systems) | — | **4 KB** standard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Segment Table — The Heart of Segmentation

Each process possesses its own **Segment Table** stored either in main memory (with a **Segment Table Base Register — STBR**) or in associative registers for speed. A typical **Segment Table Entry (STE)** contains:

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `Base` | Physical starting address of the segment in RAM |
| `Limit` | Length of the segment (used for bounds checking) |
| **Valid-Invalid Bit (V/I)** | 1 = legal segment, 0 = illegal (trap to OS) |
| **Protection Bits** | Read / Write / Execute privileges |
| **Dirty Bit** | Set if the segment has been modified (for virtual memory) |
| **Reference Bit** | Set on any access (used for LRU policies) |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical → Physical Address Translation

The hardware (MMU) performs these operations on every memory reference:

1. Extract the **segment number** $s$ from the logical address.
2. Compare $s$ with the **Segment Table Length Register (STLR)**. If $s \ge \text{STLR}$, generate a *segment fault trap*.
3. Use $s$ as an index to fetch the STE from the Segment Table.
4. Check the **valid-invalid bit**. If invalid, trap to OS.
5. Compare offset $d$ with the **limit** stored in the STE:
   - If $d \ge \text{limit}$ → **Addressing Error / Trap**.
   - If $d < \text{limit}$ → address is legal.
6. Compute physical address:
   $$ \text{Physical Address} = \text{base} + d $$

## 2.3 Why Segmentation? — The "Why" Behind It

- **Programmer's View:** Matches the way developers think — code, data, stack are different objects.
- **Protection:** Each segment can be assigned independent **read/write/execute** permissions.
- **Sharing:** Entire segments (e.g., shared code library) can be re-used by multiple processes.
- **Dynamic Growth:** A segment can grow independently (e.g., the stack and heap).
- **Re-linking:** Code and data segments can be moved in memory without the program knowing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Segmentation **eliminates internal fragmentation completely** (no fixed-size block splitting) but **suffers from external fragmentation**, because variable-sized segments are placed in holes between already-allocated blocks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|-------------------|-----------|-------|
| Logical address form | $\langle s,\ d \rangle$ | $s$ = segment, $d$ = displacement |
| Physical address | $PA = \text{base}_s + d$ | Valid only if $d < \text{limit}_s$ |
| Total memory for segment table | $M_{st} = k \times \text{size}(\text{STE})$ | $k$ = number of segments |
| Maximum logical address space | $2^{m} \times 2^{n}$ | For $m$-bit segment, $n$-bit offset |
| Effective Access Time (segment table in memory) | $\text{EAT} = 2t_{mem} - t_{mem} = 2t_{mem}$ | One access for STE, one for data |
| EAT with associative registers (hit ratio $h$) | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot t_{mem} + (1-h) \cdot 2t_{mem}$ | Standard cache formula |
| External fragmentation (Worst-Fit / First-Fit) | Variable | Compaction needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse $d < \text{limit}$ with $d \le \text{limit}$. The offset is **strictly less than** the limit (offsets run from 0 to limit−1). Examiners frequently deduct a mark for this off-by-one error.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compilers:** Use segments for *code generation*, *symbol table management*, and *intermediate code* — each treated as a separate segment.
- **Linkers & Loaders:** Map logical segments to physical addresses, performing *relocation*.
- **Operating Systems:** Modern x86-64 processors still provide segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES, FS, GS) for backward compatibility, although most OSes (Linux, Windows) use a *flat memory model* that bypasses segmentation.
- **Embedded Systems:** Use segments to isolate privileged kernel code from user code.
- **Java Virtual Machine (JVM):** The Java heap, method area, and stack are organized as logical segments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Logical → Physical Address Translation

**Problem Statement:**
A system uses segmentation. A process has 4 segments. The Segment Table is:

| Segment | Base | Limit |
|---------|------|-------|
| 0 | 1400 | 1000 |
| 1 | 6300 | 400 |
| 2 | 4300 | 400 |
| 3 | 3200 | 1100 |

Translate the following logical addresses. State any traps raised.

**(a)** `<0, 430>`  (b) `<1, 12>`  (c) `<2, 500>`  (d) `<3, 1110>`

---

### Solution (a): Logical Address = `<0, 430>`

Step 1 — Fetch STE for segment 0 from the table: $\text{base} = 1400,\ \text{limit} = 1000$.

Step 2 — Compare offset with limit:
$$ d = 430,\quad \text{limit} = 1000 $$

$$ 430 < 1000 \quad \Longrightarrow \text{Legal} $$

Step 3 — Compute the physical address:
$$ PA = \text{base} + d = 1400 + 430 = 1830 $$

$$\boxed{\text{Physical Address} = 1830}$$

---

### Solution (b): Logical Address = `<1, 12>`

Step 1 — Fetch STE for segment 1: $\text{base} = 6300,\ \text{limit} = 400$.

Step 2 — Compare:
$$ 12 < 400 \quad \Longrightarrow \text{Legal} $$

Step 3 — Compute:
$$ PA = 6300 + 12 = 6312 $$

$$\boxed{\text{Physical Address} = 6312}$$

---

### Solution (c): Logical Address = `<2, 500>`

Step 1 — STE for segment 2: $\text{base} = 4300,\ \text{limit} = 400$.

Step 2 — Compare:
$$ 500 \ge 400 \quad \Longrightarrow \text{ILLEGAL — Addressing Error Trap} $$

$$\boxed{\text{OS Trap: addressing error (offset exceeds limit)}}$$

---

### Solution (d): Logical Address = `<3, 1110>`

Step 1 — STE for segment 3: $\text{base} = 3200,\ \text{limit} = 1100$.

Step 2 — Compare:
$$ 1110 \ge 1100 \quad \Longrightarrow \text{ILLEGAL — Trap} $$

$$\boxed{\text{OS Trap: addressing error}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Effective Access Time (EAT) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A system uses segmentation where segment-table entries are stored in a TLB (associative registers) with a hit ratio of $h$. Memory access time is $t_{m}$. Compute EAT and find the value when $h = 0.80$ and $t_m = 100\,\text{ns}$.

**Derivation:**

- **Case 1 — TLB Hit (probability $h$):** Only one memory access is needed to read the data.
- **Case 2 — TLB Miss (probability $1-h$):** Two memory accesses are needed — one for the STE, and one for the actual data.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot t_{m} + (1-h) \cdot (2t_{m}) \\
&= h \cdot t_{m} + 2t_{m} - 2h \cdot t_{m} \\
&= 2t_{m} - h \cdot t_{m} \\
&= t_{m}(2 - h)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting $h = 0.80$ and $t_m = 100\,\text{ns}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 100 \times (2 - 0.80) \\
&= 100 \times 1.20 \\
&= 120\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = 120\ \text{ns}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** When the segment table is **not** in associative registers and is just in main memory, EAT simplifies to exactly $2t_m$ (one access for STE, one for data) because every reference is a miss.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Memory Overhead of the Segment Table

**Problem Statement:**
A process has 5 segments. Each Segment Table Entry occupies 4 bytes (base = 2 bytes, limit = 2 bytes). Find the total memory used by the segment table.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory}_{st} &= \text{Number of segments} \times \text{Size per STE} \\
&= 5 \times 4\ \text{bytes} \\
&= 20\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{st} = 20\ \text{bytes}}
$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Segmentation vs Paging

| Parameter | Segmentation | Paging |
|-----------|--------------|--------|
| Division of logical memory | Variable-sized segments | Fixed-size pages |
| Division of physical memory | Variable-sized blocks (matching segments) | Fixed-size frames |
| Address dimensions | 2-D $\langle s, d \rangle$ | 1-D (linear) |
| Internal fragmentation | **None** | **Yes** (in last page) |
| External fragmentation | **Yes** | **No** |
| Visibility to programmer | Yes — logical | No — transparent |
| Protection | Per segment | Per page (in protection bits) |
| Sharing | Easy (whole segments) | Harder (page-aligned) |
| Address translation | Segment table | Page table |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Segmented Memory Address Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU Generates Logical Address br seg s and offset d"] --> B["Compare s with STLR"]
    B -- "s greater or equal STLR" --> Z["SEGMENT FAULT TRAP br to OS"]
    B -- "s less than STLR" --> C["Fetch STE s from Segment Table br using STBR base"]
    C --> D{"Check Valid-Invalid Bit"}
    D -- "Invalid" --> Z
    D -- "Valid" --> E{"Compare d with limit from STE"}
    E -- "d greater or equal limit" --> Y["ADDRESSING ERROR TRAP"]
    E -- "d less than limit" --> F["Physical Address = base plus d"]
    F --> G["Access Physical Memory at PA"]
    Y --> Z
```

## 4.2 Architecture Flow — Memory Layout of a Segmented Program

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LA["LOGICAL ADDRESS SPACE OF PROCESS"]
        S0["Segment 0 br Main Code br variable size"]
        S1["Segment 1 br Global Data br variable size"]
        S2["Segment 2 br Heap br grows upward"]
        S3["Segment 3 br Stack br grows downward"]
        S4["Segment 4 br Symbol Table br fixed"]
    end
    subgraph PM["PHYSICAL MEMORY RAM"]
        P0["Block 1400 br size 1000"]
        P1["Block 6300 br size 400"]
        P2["Block 4300 br size 400"]
        P3["Block 3200 br size 1100"]
        FREE["Free Holes br External Fragmentation"]
    end
    S0 -.maps to.-> P0
    S1 -.maps to.-> P1
    S2 -.maps to.-> P2
    S3 -.maps to.-> P3
    S4 -.maps to.-> FREE
```

## 4.3 Segment Table Lookup — Detailed View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU["CPU sends logical address br seg s offset d"] --> REG["STBR points to br Segment Table Base br STLR holds br number of segments"]
    REG --> COMP1{"s less than STLR"}
    COMP1 -- "No" --> T1["Trap Segment Fault"]
    COMP1 -- "Yes" --> STE["Read STE s br gets base and limit"]
    STE --> COMP2{"d less than limit"}
    COMP2 -- "No" --> T2["Trap Addressing Error"]
    COMP2 -- "Yes" --> ADDR["PA = base plus d"]
    ADDR --> RAM["Send PA to Physical Memory"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each *block* in the physical memory is **not** guaranteed to be adjacent to the next — they may be scattered, and the gaps between them are the **holes** that cause **external fragmentation**.

## 4.4 Sharing of Segments — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P_A["Process A"]
        A1["Code Segment A br base 5000 limit 1200"]
        A2["Data Segment A br base 9000 limit 600"]
    end
    subgraph P_B["Process B"]
        B1["Code Segment B br base 5000 limit 1200"]
        B2["Data Segment B br base 11000 limit 600"]
    end
    SHARED["Shared Code Library br physical 5000-6199"]
    A1 -.point to.-> SHARED
    B1 -.point to.-> SHARED
```

**Explanation:** Two processes share a single copy of the code library in physical memory. Their segment tables both point to the *same physical base address* — this is the key advantage of segmentation for code reuse.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between paging and segmentation. List two advantages of segmentation over paging.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Paging | Segmentation |
|-------|--------|--------------|
| Memory division | Fixed-size pages | Variable-size segments |
| Address dimension | 1-D (linear) | 2-D $\langle s, d \rangle$ |
| Fragmentation | Internal | External |
| Visibility | Transparent to user | User-visible |
| Size | Power of 2 | Natural program size |

**Advantages of segmentation over paging:**
1. Provides a **logical view of memory** that matches the programmer's mental model (code/data/stack as separate units).
2. Enables **fine-grained protection and sharing** at the segment level (e.g., code can be marked read-execute, data as read-write).

*(2 marks for table, 1 mark for advantages — total 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a segment table? Explain the role of the valid-invalid bit and limit field in the segment table entry.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Segment Table** is a per-process data structure maintained by the OS that stores one entry per logical segment of a process. Each entry contains the segment's **base address** (its location in physical memory) and **limit** (its length).

- **Valid-Invalid Bit (V/I):** Indicates whether the segment currently exists in the process's address space. If the bit is 0, references to that segment cause a **segment fault trap** to the OS. This prevents programs from accessing memory outside their defined segments.
- **Limit Field:** Holds the length of the segment. During address translation, the hardware compares the offset $d$ against the limit; if $d \ge \text{limit}$, an **addressing error trap** is raised, protecting memory from illegal out-of-bound accesses.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark V/I role, 1 mark limit role)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **address translation mechanism** in a segmentation system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **STBR** and **STLR** registers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system uses pure segmentation. The segment table of a process is as follows. Translate the logical addresses and indicate the physical addresses / errors.

| Segment | Base | Limit |
|---------|------|-------|
| 0 | 200 | 500 |
| 1 | 1500 | 800 |
| 2 | 3500 | 400 |
| 3 | 6000 | 1200 |

Logical addresses: **(i)** `<0, 100>`, **(ii)** `<1, 700>`, **(iii)** `<2, 399>`, **(iv)** `<3, 1500>`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Address Translation Steps:**

1. The CPU generates a logical address of the form $\langle s, d \rangle$.
2. The **STLR (Segment Table Length Register)** is consulted to verify that $s$ is within the valid range. If $s \ge \text{STLR}$, a **segment fault trap** occurs.
3. The **STBR (Segment Table Base Register)** contains the starting physical address of the segment table. The MMU computes the location of the STE as $\text{STBR} + s \times \text{sizeof(STE)}$.
4. The STE is fetched, yielding the *base* and *limit* of segment $s$.
5. The offset $d$ is compared to *limit*. If $d \ge \text{limit}$, an **addressing error trap** occurs.
6. The physical address is then computed as:
   $$ PA = \text{base} + d $$
7. The physical address is sent to the memory unit.

**Roles:**

- **STBR:** Holds the starting physical address of the segment table — used as a *pointer* for STE lookups.
- **STLR:** Stores the total number of segments in the table — used as a *bounds check* to detect invalid segment numbers.

*(7 marks — 3 for block diagram, 2 for STBR/STLR role, 2 for translation steps)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):
