**(i) `<0, 100>`:** STE: base = 200, limit = 500. Since $100 < 500$, legal.
$$ PA = 200 + 100 = 300 $$

**(ii) `<1, 700>`:** STE: base = 1500, limit = 800. Since $700 < 800$, legal.
$$ PA = 1500 + 700 = 2200 $$

**(iii) `<2, 399>`:** STE: base = 3500, limit = 400. Since $399 < 400$, legal.
$$ PA = 3500 + 399 = 3899 $$

**(iv) `<3, 1500>`:** STE: base = 6000, limit = 1200. Since $1500 \ge 1200$, **illegal — trap generated**.

*(7 marks — 1.5 each for 4 addresses, 1 mark for trap explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss **protection and sharing** in segmented memory systems. Explain how segmentation supports modular programming. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a segmentation system with TLB hit ratio $h = 0.75$ and memory access time $t_m = 200\,\text{ns}$. Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. Also compute the EAT when the segment table is in main memory without TLB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Protection in Segmentation:**

The MMU automatically enforces two protection checks during every memory access:
1. **Valid-Invalid Bit Check** — ensures the segment belongs to the process.
2. **Limit Check** — ensures offset $d < \text{limit}$, preventing out-of-bound access.

Additionally, **protection bits** in the STE specify whether the segment is *read-only*, *execute-only*, or *read-write*, providing finer-grained security.

**Sharing in Segmentation:**

Re-entrant code (e.g., shared libraries, editor code) can be shared by mapping multiple processes' segment-table entries to the **same physical base address**. Since the segment descriptor is a logical unit, it is much easier to share than with paging, which requires sharing at page granularity.

**Modular Programming Support:**

- Each module (function, procedure, data structure) can be compiled as a separate segment.
- Modules can be **independently compiled, linked, and loaded**.
- A module can call another by referring to its segment number — supporting *clean information hiding*.
- Segments can grow independently (e.g., stack/heap) without disturbing other modules.

*(7 marks — 2 protection, 2 sharing, 2 modular, 1 diagram/conclusion)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Case 1: Segment Table in TLB**

- On TLB **hit** (probability $h = 0.75$): 1 memory access → $t_m$.
- On TLB **miss** (probability $1-h = 0.25$): 2 memory accesses → $2t_m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot t_m + (1-h) \cdot 2t_m \\
&= 0.75 \cdot 200 + 0.25 \cdot 400 \\
&= 150 + 100 \\
&= 250\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: Segment Table in Main Memory (No TLB)**

Every memory reference needs 2 accesses — one for the STE and one for the data.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 2 t_m = 2 \times 200 = 400\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT with TLB} = 250\ \text{ns}\quad\quad \text{EAT without TLB} = 400\ \text{ns}}
$$

*(7 marks — 3 marks for derivation with TLB, 2 marks for final value, 2 marks for no-TLB case)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Off-by-one error in offset check:** Writing $d \le \text{limit}$ instead of $d < \text{limit}$ costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing STBR and STLR:** STBR = *Base*, STLR = *Length/Count*. Mixing them loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention traps:** When a logical address is illegal, you *must* explicitly say "OS trap is generated" — silently skipping this loses a mark.
> 4. **EAT derivation:** Skipping the algebraic simplification step (showing $2t_m - h \cdot t_m$) is penalized.
> 5. **Diagrams:** Drawing paging diagrams (page tables) instead of segmentation diagrams (segment tables) is an instant 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Segmentation**

- ✅ Segmentation divides a program's **logical address space** into **variable-sized** logical units called *segments*.
- ✅ Logical address = $\langle \text{segment-number},\ \text{offset} \rangle$ (a 2-D address).
- ✅ **Segment Table** holds `base`, `limit`, `valid-invalid bit`, and `protection bits` for each segment.
- ✅ **STBR** = Segment Table Base Register; **STLR** = Segment Table Length Register.
- ✅ Address translation: $PA = \text{base}_s + d$ — valid only if $d < \text{limit}_s$.
- ✅ **No internal fragmentation**, but **external fragmentation exists** (variable-sized memory chunks).
- ✅ **Protection** is two-tiered: validity check + bounds (limit) check + protection bits.
- ✅ **Sharing** is easier than in paging — multiple segment tables can point to the *same physical base*.
- ✅ **Effective Access Time (EAT)**:
  - Segment table in memory: $\text{EAT} = 2t_m$.
  - With TLB of hit ratio $h$: $\text{EAT} = t_m (2 - h)$.
- ✅ Modern x86-64 systems retain segment registers for backward compatibility, but most OSes use a *flat model* where segmentation is effectively disabled.
- ✅ Compilers, linkers, JVM, and embedded systems widely use the segment concept.
- ✅ **Key trade-off:** Segmentation is more user-friendly and supports protection/sharing well, but suffers from external fragmentation — hence the move toward **paging** and **segmentation-with-paging** hybrids.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Paging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paging** is a non-contiguous memory allocation scheme in modern operating systems that eliminates the problem of external fragmentation by dividing both the **logical address space** and the **physical address space** into fixed-sized blocks. The fixed-sized block of the logical address space is called a **Page**, while the corresponding fixed-sized block in the physical address space is called a **Frame** (or Page Frame). A data structure known as the **Page Table** maintained by the Operating System maps each page number to its corresponding frame number in main memory.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, paging is positioned as the **de-facto memory management technique** in virtually every modern general-purpose OS (Windows NT family, Linux, macOS, Android). It forms the backbone of the **virtual memory subsystem**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Page | — | Fixed-size block of **logical** memory |
| Frame | — | Fixed-size block of **physical** memory |
| Page Number | `p` | Index into the page table |
| Page Offset | `d` | Displacement within a page/frame |
| Page Table | `PT` | Per-process mapping structure |
| Page Table Base Register | `PTBR` | Holds the starting physical address of the page table |
| Page Table Length Register | `PTLR` | Indicates the size of the page table |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Filing System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a **library** where books (processes) do not sit on a single continuous shelf. Instead, each book is torn into chapter-sized packets. A librarian's master register (the **Page Table**) records exactly which shelf slot (frame) each packet currently occupies. When you request Chapter 7 of Book 3, the librarian checks the register, locates the correct shelf and slot, and retrieves the packet — even if it sits on a non-adjacent shelf. The reader never knows the physical layout; they only see the logical "book order."

This is precisely what paging accomplishes: a process perceives its memory as a **contiguous, linear, 0-to-N address space**, while in physical reality, its pages are scattered all over RAM.

## 1.4 Why Paging? (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Problems Paging Solves:**
> 1. **External Fragmentation** — eliminated entirely because any free frame can be allocated to any page.
> 2. **Logical–Physical Address Separation** — provides each process its own **private linear address space**, enabling memory protection and process isolation (a core KTU 2024 Module 3 outcome).

> [!NOTE]
> A small amount of **internal fragmentation** (≤ one page per process) is the only trade-off. The standard page size in x86-64 systems is **4 KB** (with 2 MB and 1 GB "huge pages" also available).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Paging

The CPU's **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** performs address translation on every single memory access. The pipeline is as follows:

1. **Logical Address Generation** — The CPU issues a logical (virtual) address `A` of size `m` bits.
2. **Address Splitting** — The MMU divides `A` into:
   - **Page Number** `p` (the upper bits)
   - **Page Offset** `d` (the lower bits)
3. **Page Table Lookup** — Using `p` as an index, the MMU fetches the corresponding **frame number** `f` from the page table.
4. **Physical Address Construction** — The MMU concatenates `f` with `d` to form the physical address sent to the memory bus.
5. **Memory Access** — RAM returns the data at the constructed physical address.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the 'How'

- **Why fixed sizes?** Fixed-size blocks mean the offset `d` is identical in both logical and physical addresses — only the page number needs to be translated. This makes hardware translation blazingly fast.
- **Why powers of 2?** Page sizes are always powers of 2 (e.g., 4 KB = 2¹², 16 KB = 2¹⁴). This lets the MMU extract `p` and `d` using **simple bit-shifting and masking** — no division required.
- **Why a per-process page table?** Each process has a private logical address space. The PTBR register is **swapped on every context switch**, giving every process an isolated view of memory.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they cover 90% of KTU numerical questions on Paging.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of Pages | $P = 2^{p}$ | where $p$ = page number bits |
| 2 | Number of Frames | $F = 2^{f}$ | where $f$ = frame number bits |
| 3 | Page Size | $S = 2^{d}$ | where $d$ = offset bits (bytes) |
| 4 | Logical Address Space | $LAS = P \times S$ | bytes |
| 5 | Physical Address Space | $PAS = F \times S$ | bytes |
| 6 | Address Split Rule | $m = p + d$ | total logical address bits |
| 7 | Page Table Size | $PTS = P \times E$ | bytes (E = entry size) |
| 8 | TLB Hit Ratio | $h = \dfrac{\text{TLB Hits}}{\text{Total Lookups}}$ | ratio $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 9 | TLB Miss Ratio | $\alpha = 1 - h$ | ratio $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 10 | Effective Access Time | $EAT = h \cdot (T_{m} + T_{tlb}) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{m} + T_{tlb} + T_{m})$ | nanoseconds |
| 11 | EAT (with memory access $T_{m}$) | $EAT = (2 - h) \cdot T_{m} + T_{tlb}$ | assumes single memory access $T_m$ |
| 12 | EAT (Two-Level PT) | $EAT = (2 + h) \cdot T_{m} + T_{tlb}$ | with TLB; 2 memory accesses on miss |

> **NOTE on notation:** $T_{m}$ = main memory access time, $T_{tlb}$ = TLB lookup time, $E$ = page table entry size in bytes (typically 4 bytes on 32-bit systems, 8 bytes on 64-bit systems).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Paging is the cornerstone of:
- **Virtual Memory** (covered in detail in the next sub-topic) — enables execution of processes larger than physical RAM.
- **Demand Paging** — pages are loaded only when accessed, reducing I/O.
- **Memory Protection** — the Valid/Invalid bit in each PTE prevents a process from accessing memory it doesn't own.
- **Shared Memory** — read-only code segments (e.g., `libc.so`) can share frames by mapping the same frame number into multiple page tables.
- **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** — used by `fork()` in Unix/Linux; parent and child initially share frames, and a private copy is made only on a write attempt.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logical-to-Physical Address Translation

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Logical address $A$ of $m$ bits, page size $S = 2^{d}$ bytes, total pages $P = 2^{p}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Page Number } (p) &= \left\lfloor \frac{A}{S} \right\rfloor = A \gg d \quad \text{(right shift by } d \text{ bits)} \\
\text{Page Offset } (d) &= A \mod S = A \,\&\, (S - 1) \quad \text{(bitwise AND with } S-1) \\
\text{Frame Number } (f) &= \text{PageTable}[p] \quad \text{(fetched from RAM)} \\
\text{Physical Address} &= f \times S + d = (f \ll d) \;\vert\; d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation of each step:**
- **Step 1:** The page number is found by integer-dividing the address by the page size. In hardware, this is implemented as a **bitwise right shift** by $d$ positions — extremely fast.
- **Step 2:** The offset is the remainder of the address modulo the page size. Implemented as a **bitwise AND** with the bitmask $(S-1)$.
- **Step 3:** The frame number is fetched by indexing the page table at entry $p$. This requires **one physical memory access** to read the PTE.
- **Step 4:** The physical address is reconstructed by **left-shifting** the frame number by $d$ bits and OR-ing in the offset.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Access Time (EAT) — Single-Level Page Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU University Exams for Module 3.**

Let:
- $T_{m}$ = time to access main memory (e.g., 100 ns)
- $T_{tlb}$ = time to search the TLB (e.g., 20 ns)
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio

**Case 1: TLB Hit (probability = $h$)**
- One TLB lookup + One memory access for data
- Time taken $= T_{tlb} + T_{m}$

**Case 2: TLB Miss (probability = $1 - h$)**
- One TLB lookup + One memory access for the PTE + One memory access for the data
- Time taken $= T_{tlb} + T_{m} + T_{m} = T_{tlb} + 2 T_{m}$

**Expected (Effective) Access Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= h \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{m}) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{tlb} + 2 T_{m}) \\
&= h \cdot T_{tlb} + h \cdot T_{m} + T_{tlb} + 2 T_{m} - h \cdot T_{tlb} - 2 h \cdot T_{m} \\
&= T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity Check:**
- If $h = 0$ (TLB never hits): $EAT = T_{tlb} + 2 T_{m}$ → two memory accesses required. ✓
- If $h = 1$ (TLB always hits): $EAT = T_{tlb} + T_{m}$ → just one memory access. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Page Table Size

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Logical address space = $L$ bytes, Page size = $S$ bytes, PTE size = $E$ bytes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Pages } (P) &= \frac{L}{S} \\
\text{Page Table Size } (PTS) &= P \times E = \frac{L \times E}{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Address Splitting (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A system has a logical address space of **16 KB** and a page size of **1 KB**. Translate logical address **5000** to physical address, given that page 4 is stored in frame 7.

**Step 1 — Compute address split parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logical Address Space} &= 16\,\text{KB} = 2^{14}\,\text{bytes} \quad \Rightarrow m = 14 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Page Size } (S) &= 1\,\text{KB} = 2^{10}\,\text{bytes} \quad \Rightarrow d = 10 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Page Number bits } (p) &= m - d = 14 - 10 = 4 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute page number and offset of address 5000:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Page Number} &= \left\lfloor \frac{5000}{1024} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 4.88 \right\rfloor = 4 \\
\text{Offset} &= 5000 \mod 1024 = 5000 - 4 \times 1024 = 5000 - 4096 = 904
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Look up frame number:**

Given in the problem: page 4 → frame 7, so $f = 7$.

**Step 4 — Construct physical address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= f \times S + d = 7 \times 1024 + 904 = 7168 + 904 = 8072
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Logical address **5000** maps to physical address **8072**.

---

### Example 2: Page Table Size Calculation

> **Problem:** A 32-bit system has a page size of **4 KB**. Each page table entry is **4 bytes**. Calculate the size of the page table.

**Step 1 — Compute number of pages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logical Address Space } (L) &= 2^{32}\,\text{bytes} = 4\,\text{GB} \\
\text{Page Size } (S) &= 4\,\text{KB} = 2^{12}\,\text{bytes} \\
\text{Number of Pages } (P) &= \frac{L}{S} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute page table size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Page Table Size } (PTS) &= P \times E = 2^{20} \times 4\,\text{bytes} = 4 \times 2^{20}\,\text{bytes} = 4\,\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** A single-level page table consumes **4 MB** per process. This is why modern systems use **multi-level (hierarchical) page tables** — a key KTU 2024 Module 3 concept.

---

### Example 3: Effective Access Time Calculation

> **Problem:** TLB lookup time = **20 ns**, main memory access time = **100 ns**, TLB hit ratio = **80%**. Compute the EAT.

**Step 1 — Apply the EAT formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_{m} \\
&= 20 + (2 - 0.80) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.20 \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 120 \\
&= 140\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Effective Access Time = **140 ns**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — TLB Hit Ratio Sensitivity

| Hit Ratio ($h$) | EAT (with $T_m=100$, $T_{tlb}=20$) | Observation |
|---|---|---|
| 0% | 220 ns | Worst case — every access needs 2 memory reads |
| 50% | 170 ns | Modest improvement |
| 80% | 140 ns | Typical real-world value |
| 95% | 125 ns | Near-optimal |
| 100% | 120 ns | Best case — single memory access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice how increasing $h$ from 0% to 80% reduces EAT by 36%, but going from 80% to 100% only saves 14%. This **diminishing return** is why a well-designed TLB (~98% hit rate) is "good enough" in practice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical-to-Physical Address Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Issues Logical Address A"] --> B["MMU Splits A into Page Number p and Offset d"]
    B --> C{"TLB Lookup for Page Number p"}
    C -->|"TLB Hit"| D["Retrieve Frame Number f from TLB"]
    C -->|"TLB Miss"| E["Access Page Table in Main Memory"]
    E --> F["Retrieve Frame Number f from PTE"]
    F --> G["Update TLB with p to f mapping"]
    G --> H["Proceed to Physical Address Construction"]
    D --> H
    H --> I["Concatenate f and d to form Physical Address"]
    I --> J["Send Physical Address to Memory Bus"]
    J --> K["RAM Returns Data at Physical Address"]
    K --> L["Data Delivered to CPU"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Paging System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_BLOCK["CPU CHIP"]
        CPUCORE["CPU Core"]
        MMU["Memory Management Unit"]
        TLB["Translation Lookaside Buffer"]
    end

    subgraph REG_BLOCK["Control Registers"]
        PTBR["Page Table Base Register PTBR"]
        PTLR["Page Table Length Register PTLR"]
    end

    subgraph PHYS_MEM["Physical Memory RAM"]
        PT["Page Table Region"]
        FRAMES["Frame 0 to Frame N minus 1"]
    end

    CPUCORE -->|"Logical Address"| MMU
    MMU -->|"Lookup Request"| TLB
    TLB -->|"On Miss, Read PTE"| PT
    PTBR -->|"Base Address"| MMU
    PTLR -->|"Bounds Check"| MMU
    MMU -->|"Physical Address"| FRAMES
    FRAMES -->|"Data Word"| CPUCORE
```

## 4.3 Page Table Entry (PTE) Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PTE["32 bit Page Table Entry"] --> F1["Frame Number 20 bits"]
    PTE --> F2["Valid Invalid Bit 1 bit"]
    PTE --> F3["Protection Bits 2 bits"]
    PTE --> F4["Reference Bit 1 bit"]
    PTE --> F5["Modify Dirty Bit 1 bit"]
    PTE --> F6["Caching Disabled Bit 1 bit"]
    PTE --> F7["Unused Reserved 6 bits"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The exact bit allocation of a PTE varies by architecture (x86, ARM, RISC-V). The figure above shows a typical 32-bit PTE. On 64-bit systems, the frame number field widens to 40+ bits to address large physical memory.

## 4.4 Page Number to Frame Number Mapping (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOG["Logical Address Space of Process P"]
        L0["Page 0"]
        L1["Page 1"]
        L2["Page 2"]
        L3["Page 3"]
        L4["Page 4"]
        L5["Page 5"]
        L6["Page 6"]
        L7["Page 7"]
    end

    subgraph PT["Page Table for Process P"]
        T0["PTBR plus 0: Frame 5"]
        T1["PTBR plus 4: Frame 2"]
        T2["PTBR plus 8: Frame 8"]
        T3["PTBR plus 12: Invalid"]
        T4["PTBR plus 16: Frame 1"]
        T5["PTBR plus 20: Frame 9"]
        T6["PTBR plus 24: Frame 3"]
        T7["PTBR plus 28: Frame 6"]
    end

    subgraph PHYS["Physical Memory RAM"]
        P0["Frame 0 OS Kernel"]
        P1["Frame 1 Page 4 Data"]
        P2["Frame 2 Page 1 Data"]
        P3["Frame 3 Page 6 Data"]
        P4["Frame 4 Free"]
        P5["Frame 5 Page 0 Data"]
        P6["Frame 6 Page 7 Data"]
        P7["Frame 7 Free"]
        P8["Frame 8 Page 2 Data"]
        P9["Frame 9 Page 5 Data"]
    end

    L0 -.-> T0 -.-> P5
    L1 -.-> T1 -.-> P2
    L2 -.-> T2 -.-> P8
    L3 -.-> T3
    L4 -.-> T4 -.-> P1
    L5 -.-> T5 -.-> P9
    L6 -.-> T6 -.-> P3
    L7 -.-> T7 -.-> P6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how the **logical pages are contiguous (0,1,2,3,...)** from the process's perspective, but the **physical frames are scattered** (5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3, 6) in RAM. Also note **Page 3 → Invalid**: the process has not yet been allocated any physical frame for this page (this is the foundation of **Demand Paging** covered later in Module 3).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define paging. Explain how paging eliminates external fragmentation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paging** is a memory management scheme that divides the logical address space of a process into fixed-sized blocks called **pages**, and the physical memory into blocks of the same size called **frames**. A **page table** maintained by the OS maps each page to its corresponding frame.

**Elimination of external fragmentation:**
- In paging, any free frame in physical memory can hold any page of a process.
- Since all pages and frames are of **uniform size**, there is no "awkward leftover" piece of memory that is too small to satisfy a request.
- Therefore, **external fragmentation — the existence of small unusable holes between allocated blocks — simply cannot occur.**
- The only fragmentation is a small amount of **internal fragmentation** in the last page of a process, which is at most one page in size.

*(Word limit target: 80–100 words for a 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? How does it improve paging performance?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, fast, fully-associative **hardware cache** inside the MMU that stores recently-used **page number → frame number** translations.

**Performance improvement:**
- A page table lookup normally requires **one extra physical memory access** to fetch the PTE, doubling the access time.
- The TLB, being implemented in SRAM, can be searched in **~20 ns** versus **~100 ns** for main memory.
- On a TLB **hit** (probability $h$, typically 80–99%), the frame number is obtained in a single TLB lookup, reducing memory accesses from 2 to 1.
- The Effective Access Time becomes $EAT = T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_{m}$, which approaches $T_{tlb} + T_{m}$ as $h \to 1$.

*(Word limit target: 80–100 words for a 3-mark answer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. The structure below mirrors the standard two-sub-part layout of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the basic concept of paging. How is a logical address translated into a physical address using a page table?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
Paging is a non-contiguous memory allocation technique. The logical address space of a process is divided into equal-sized **pages**, and physical memory is divided into equal-sized **frames**. A **page table** maps each page number to its corresponding frame number. The OS maintains one page table per process, and the base address is held in the **PTBR** register.

**Step 2 — Address Translation Steps (3 marks):**

A logical address $A$ of $m$ bits is split by the MMU into:
- **Page number** $p$ — the upper $p$ bits
- **Page offset** $d$ — the lower $d$ bits

The MMU then:
1. Uses $p$ as an index into the page table.
2. Fetches the frame number $f$ from $\text{PageTable}[p]$.
3. Constructs the physical address as $f \cdot 2^{d} + d$.

**Step 3 — Diagram (2 marks):**

```
+-----------------+-----------------+
|  Page Number p  |   Offset d      |  → Logical Address
+-----------------+-----------------+

            |  Page Table Lookup
            v

+-----------------+-----------------+
|  Frame Number f |   Offset d      |  → Physical Address
+-----------------+-----------------+
```

**Step 4 — Worked Example (extra for clarity):**
For address 5000 with page size 1024: $p = 4$, $d = 904$. If PageTable[4] = 7, then physical address = $7 \times 1024 + 904 = 8072$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a paging system with a page size of **4 KB**. A process generates the logical address **0x3A7F**. The page table entry for the corresponding page contains the value **0x12**. Compute the physical address. Assume each address is 16 bits.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify address structure (2 marks):**
- Logical address space = $2^{16}$ bytes = 64 KB
- Page size = $4\,\text{KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes $\Rightarrow d = 12$ offset bits
- Page number bits = $16 - 12 = 4$ bits

**Step 2 — Convert logical address to binary (2 marks):**

$$
0x3A7F = 0011\,1010\,0111\,1111_2
$$

**Step 3 — Split into page number and offset (2 marks):**
- Upper 4 bits (page number): $0011_2 = 3_{10}$
- Lower 12 bits (offset): $1010\,0111\,1111_2 = 0xA7F = 2687_{10}$

**Step 4 — Look up frame number and compute physical address (1 mark):**

Given: PTE value = $0x12 = 18_{10}$ (frame number).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= f \times S + d \\
&= 18 \times 4096 + 2687 \\
&= 73728 + 2687 \\
&= 76415_{10} = 0x12A7F
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The logical address **0x3A7F** maps to physical address **0x12A7F** (= 76415 in decimal).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** in paging. Derive the formula for **Effective Access Time (EAT)** in a paging system with a TLB.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — TLB Role (3 marks):**
The **TLB** is a small associative hardware cache (typically 16–1024 entries) inside the MMU that stores recent **page number → frame number** translations. It is searched in parallel using content-addressable memory, making lookups extremely fast (~20 ns vs. ~100 ns for main memory). It exploits **temporal locality** and **spatial locality** of memory references.

**Step 2 — Variables and Cases (2 marks):**
Let $T_m$ = memory access time, $T_{tlb}$ = TLB lookup time, $h$ = TLB hit ratio.

- **TLB Hit** (prob $h$): Time = $T_{tlb} + T_m$
- **TLB Miss** (prob $1-h$): Time = $T_{tlb} + 2T_m$ (PTE fetch + data fetch)

**Step 3 — EAT Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= h(T_{tlb} + T_m) + (1 - h)(T_{tlb} + 2T_m) \\
&= hT_{tlb} + hT_m + T_{tlb} + 2T_m - hT_{tlb} - 2hT_m \\
&= T_{tlb} + (2 - h)T_m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Formula:** $EAT = T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_m$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given:
- TLB lookup time = **20 ns**
- Main memory access time = **100 ns**
- TLB hit ratio = **80%**

Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. Also compute the EAT if the hit ratio improves to **98%**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute EAT for $h = 0.80$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_m \\
&= 20 + (2 - 0.80) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.20 \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 120 = 140\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EAT for $h = 0.98$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 20 + (2 - 0.98) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.02 \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 102 = 122\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - At 80% hit ratio, EAT = **140 ns** (40% slower than ideal $T_{tlb}+T_m$ = 120 ns).
> - At 98% hit ratio, EAT = **122 ns** (only 1.7% slower than ideal).
> - **Conclusion:** Modern systems target a TLB hit ratio of **>98%** to make paging's overhead negligible. This is achieved via large TLBs and smart replacement policies (LRU).

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** EAT = **140 ns** for $h = 0.80$; EAT = **122 ns** for $h = 0.98$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include $T_{tlb}$ in the EAT formula.** A very common slip. The full formula is $EAT = T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_m$, **not** $EAT = (2 - h) \cdot T_m$. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
>
> 2. **Confusing page number with offset.** The **upper** bits are the page number, the **lower** bits are the offset. Getting this backwards will yield a wrong physical address in numerical questions. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
>
> 3. **Ignoring internal fragmentation.** When asked "why is paging better than contiguous allocation?", many students write only about external fragmentation. **Always mention that internal fragmentation is the (small) trade-off** — up to one page per process. (Lose 1 mark.)
>
> 4. **Writing the page table size formula incorrectly.** It is $PTS = P \times E$ (number of pages × entry size), **not** $PTS = PAS \times E$ or $PTS = S \times E$. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
>
> 5. **Forgetting to convert hex to binary explicitly in address-translation problems.** KTU examiners expect to see the binary split clearly written out, not just the final number. (Lose 1 mark.)
>
> 6. **Not drawing a diagram in 7-mark sub-parts.** Any "explain" question worth ≥ 5 marks **must** have a labeled diagram. A page-table block diagram with logical address on the left and physical address on the right is the minimum acceptable. (Lose 1–2 marks.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Paging**
>
> ☐ **Paging** = non-contiguous memory allocation using fixed-size **pages** (logical) and **frames** (physical).
>
> ☐ A **Page Table** maps each page to a frame. One per process, located via the **PTBR** register.
>
> ☐ Logical address = **Page number ($p$)** ∥ **Page offset ($d$)** — split via bit-shift/mask operations.
>
> ☐ Page and frame sizes are always **equal** and always a **power of 2** (typically 4 KB).
>
> ☐ Key formula: $m = p + d$ where $m$ is the total logical address bits.
>
> ☐ Page Table Size: $PTS = P \times E = \dfrac{L \times E}{S}$.
>
> ☐ Paging **eliminates external fragmentation** entirely; **internal fragmentation** is limited to at most one page per process.
>
> ☐ **TLB** = hardware cache of recent page-to-frame mappings. Exploits locality.
>
> ☐ **EAT formula:** $EAT = T_{tlb} + (2 - h) \cdot T_m$. Must include $T_{tlb}$!
>
> ☐ **EAT bounds:** $T_{tlb} + T_m$ (best, $h=1$) $\le EAT \le T_{tlb} + 2T_m$ (worst, $h=0$).
>
> ☐ A 32-bit system with 4 KB pages has $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ pages, producing a **4 MB single-level page table** — motivating **multi-level paging**.
>
> ☐ Paging enables **virtual memory**, **memory protection** (Valid/Invalid bit), **shared memory**, and **Copy-on-Write** semantics for `fork()`.
>
> ☐ On a TLB **miss**, the PTE is fetched from the page table in RAM and **the TLB is updated** with the new translation (mandatory step).
>
> ☐ The **PTLR** register enforces bounds checking: a logical page number $\ge$ PTLR triggers a **page fault** or addressing exception.
>
> ☐ Page table entries typically contain: **Frame number, Valid/Invalid bit, Protection bits (R/W/X), Reference bit, Dirty/Modify bit, Caching-disable bit**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Virtual Memory** is a storage hierarchy and memory management technique implemented by the Operating System that decouples the **logical address space** (what a process "sees") from the **physical address space** (what the hardware RAM physically provides). It permits a process's execution to begin even when only a subset of its pages resides in main memory, while the remaining pages are kept on **secondary storage (disk)**. The OS, in cooperation with the Memory Management Unit (MMU) and the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB), brings pages into RAM on **demand**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Virtual Memory is a technique that allows the execution of a process that may not be completely in memory. The main advantage of this scheme is that programs can be larger than the size of physical memory."* — Silberschatz (Galvin), adopted as the canonical reference for KTU 2024 scheme.

### 1.2 The "Why" — Motivation for Virtual Memory

Modern programs (browsers, IDEs, databases) routinely consume **gigabytes of RAM**. However:

- Physical RAM is **finite and expensive**.
- A process usually does **not** use all its code/data at exactly the same instant.
- Without Virtual Memory, the *entire* process must fit in RAM before it can start.

Virtual Memory solves this by loading only the **active** portions and leaving the rest on the much larger (but slower) **swap space on disk**.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Research Library Desk"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: A Student's Library Desk**
> Imagine you are writing a thesis. The **Library Building** is your hard disk (huge but slow to walk to). Your **Study Desk** is your physical RAM (small but instantly accessible). **Virtual Memory** is the rule: "Keep only the books and notes you are *currently* writing from on your desk. Fetch more books from the library *only when* you need them, and return them when you don't." Your brain (CPU) only works with what's on the desk, just as the CPU only executes instructions from RAM.

### 1.4 Key Subsystem: Demand Paging

The most common implementation of Virtual Memory in modern OS (Linux, Windows, macOS) is **Demand Paging** — pages are loaded *only* when a process demands them (a "lazy" loader).

| Term | Standard Metric (KTU Assumption) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Memory Access Time (ma)** | **100 ns** (typical) |
| **Page Fault Service Time** | **25 ms** (≈ 250,000 × ma) |
| **Page Size** | **4 KB** (typical modern OS) |
| **TLB Lookup Time** | **20 ns** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Page Fault**: A *trap* to the OS raised by the hardware when a referenced page is **not** in physical memory (valid/invalid bit is cleared).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Architecture of Demand Paging

The MMU checks a **valid–invalid bit** (or "present" bit) attached to each entry of the **Page Table**:

- If the bit is **1 (valid)** → the page is in a physical frame → translation completes normally.
- If the bit is **0 (invalid)** → the page is NOT in RAM → the MMU triggers a **page-fault trap**.

The OS then services the fault in a **strictly defined sequence**:

1. Check the process's internal page table to confirm the reference is legal.
2. Find a free frame (using a free-frame list) OR evict a victim frame via a page-replacement algorithm.
3. Schedule a disk I/O to read the demanded page into the chosen frame.
4. While the I/O is in progress, **context-switch** to another ready process (the OS never idles).
5. Receive the disk interrupt; update the page table with the new frame number and set the valid bit.
6. **Restart** the faulting instruction.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why context-switch during disk I/O?** Disk I/O is millions of times slower than RAM. Idle-waiting would be criminal CPU waste.
- **Why restart the instruction, not the next one?** Because in architectures like x86, memory operands must be re-fetched. Mid-instruction restart is hardware-supported.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Effective Access Time (EAT)** | $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot \text{Page Fault Service Time}$ | $p$ = page fault rate (0 to 1); $ma$ in ns | Most-tested formula in KTU ESE |
| **Page Fault Service Time** | $T_{pf} = T_{overhead} + T_{swap\ in} + T_{swap\ out} + T_{restart}$ | All in ms | Each term is OS- and disk-dependent |
| **Full EAT with TLB hit** | $EAT = \varepsilon + (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot [ \text{ma} + (1-p)\cdot ma + p \cdot T_{pf} ]$ | $\varepsilon$ = TLB hit ratio (0.9+ typical) | If TLB is in syllabus |
| **Optimal Page Replacement (Belady's MIN)** | Replace the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future | String of page references | Theoretical lower bound on faults |
| **Thrashing Condition** | $\sum D_i > m$ (Sum of working sets > physical frames) | $D_i$ = demand of process $i$; $m$ = total frames | Causes severe CPU utilization drop |
| **Working Set Window ($\Delta$)** | $W(t, \Delta) = \{ \text{pages referenced in } [t-\Delta+1, t] \}$ | $\Delta$ in references or time | Used for working-set model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule Reminder:** Notice that the use of `\vert` or `\cdot` is preferred over `|` inside table rows to prevent the markdown parser from breaking the table layout.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Virtual Memory is the **backbone of modern computing**:
- **Android/iOS** apps routinely exceed physical RAM but run smoothly because of VM.
- **Cloud servers** (AWS EC2, Azure VMs) oversubscribe physical RAM using VM, achieving >10× density.
- **Database engines** (PostgreSQL, Oracle) use huge *buffer pools* that are essentially VM-aware memory caches.
- **Embedded systems** with no disk use variations of VM with Flash as backing store (e.g., eMMC in smartphones).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Effective Access Time (EAT)

Let $p$ be the **page fault rate** — the probability (0 to 1) that any given memory reference triggers a page fault.

**Step 1 — Identify the two mutually exclusive outcomes for any memory access:**
- **Outcome A**: The page IS in memory (probability $1 - p$). Cost = one memory access time $= ma$.
- **Outcome B**: The page is NOT in memory (probability $p$). Cost = full page-fault service time $= T_{pf}$.

**Step 2 — Express the Expected (Average) Time as a weighted sum:**

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot T_{pf}
$$

**Step 3 — Decompose the page-fault service time into its OS components:**

$$
T_{pf} = T_{trap} + T_{I/O} + T_{restart}
$$

where $T_{trap} \approx 1$–$10\ \mu s$ (OS trap handling), $T_{I/O} \approx 8$–$25$ ms (disk seek + rotational latency + transfer), and $T_{restart} \approx 1$–$10\ \mu s$ (restart the faulting instruction).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example (Classic KTU Style)

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
> Given a demand-paged system with:
> - Memory access time $ma = 100$ ns
> - Page fault service time $T_{pf} = 25$ ms $= 25{,}000{,}000$ ns
>
> Compute the Effective Access Time (EAT) for the following page fault rates:
> (a) $p = 0$ (no faults)
> (b) $p = 0.0001$ (1 fault per 10,000 references)
> (c) $p = 0.000001$ (1 fault per 1,000,000 references)
>
> Comment on the results.

**Case (a): p = 0**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0)(100\ \text{ns}) + (0)(25\ \text{ms}) \\
    &= 100\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* This is the ideal scenario — no page faults at all. The system performs exactly at memory speed.

**Case (b): p = 0.0001**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.0001)(100\ \text{ns}) + (0.0001)(25{,}000{,}000\ \text{ns}) \\
    &= (0.9999)(100) + (0.0001)(25{,}000{,}000)\ \text{ns} \\
    &= 99.99\ \text{ns} + 2500\ \text{ns} \\
    &= 2599.99\ \text{ns} \approx 2.6\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* A page fault every 10,000 references **slows the system by ~26×**. Even a tiny page fault rate is catastrophic.

**Case (c): p = 0.000001**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.000001)(100\ \text{ns}) + (0.000001)(25{,}000{,}000\ \text{ns}) \\
    &= (0.999999)(100) + (0.000001)(25{,}000{,}000)\ \text{ns} \\
    &= 99.9999\ \text{ns} + 25\ \text{ns} \\
    &= 125\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Even 1 fault per million references causes a **25% slowdown** over the no-fault ideal. This is why keeping $p$ extremely small is the holy grail of memory management.

### 3.3 The "100× Rule" for EAT Degradation

We can formalize the slowdown ratio by dividing $EAT$ by $ma$:

$$
\text{Slowdown Factor} = \frac{EAT}{ma} = (1 - p) + p \cdot \frac{T_{pf}}{ma}
$$

Substituting $T_{pf} / ma \approx 250{,}000$ (because $25\ \text{ms} / 100\ \text{ns} = 250{,}000$):

$$
\text{Slowdown Factor} \approx 1 + p \cdot (250{,}000 - 1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since the ratio $T_{pf}/ma$ is enormous (~10⁵ to 10⁶), keeping $p$ below **10⁻⁵** is the only way to stay within **a few percent** of the in-memory speed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Virtual Memory Address Translation (Paging + TLB) — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **logical-to-physical address translation pipeline** in a modern demand-paged system, with the page-fault pathway made explicit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Generates Virtual Address VA = PageNum : Offset"] --> B["TLB Lookup - Hit or Miss"]
    B -->|TLB Hit| C["Get Physical Frame Number P FN"]
    B -->|TLB Miss| D["Access Page Table in RAM"]
    D --> E{"Valid Invalid Bit Check"}
    E -->|Valid = 1| F["Update TLB with P FN"]
    F --> C
    E -->|Valid = 0 - Page Fault| G["Trap to OS Kernel"]
    G --> H["OS Invokes Page Replacement Algorithm"]
    H --> I["Select Victim Frame or Free Frame"]
    I --> J["Issue Disk Read to Swap Space"]
    J --> K["Context Switch to Another Process"]
    K --> L["Disk I O Completes - Interrupt"]
    L --> M["Update Page Table - Set Valid = 1"]
    M --> N["Update TLB with New Frame"]
    N --> C
    C --> O["Form Physical Address PA = P FN : Offset"]
    O --> P["Send PA to Physical Memory and Cache"]
    P --> Q["Return Data to CPU"]
```

### 4.2 The Page-Fault Service Time — Block-Level Timing Flow

This second diagram decomposes the *page-fault service time* ($T_{pf}$) into the precise sequence of OS operations a KTU examiner expects you to enumerate for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["1 Trap to Kernel - Service Routine Starts"] --> S2["2 Save Process State - Registers and PC"]
    S2 --> S3["3 Validate Reference - Check Legal Page Access"]
    S3 --> S4["4 Locate Free Frame or Select Victim"]
    S4 --> S5["5 Issue Disk I O - DMA Transfer to Swap"]
    S5 --> S6["6 While I O Pending - Context Switch to Other Process"]
    S6 --> S7["7 Receive Disk Interrupt - Acknowledge I O Complete"]
    S7 --> S8["8 Update Page Table Entry - Frame Number and Valid Bit = 1"]
    S8 --> S9["9 Place Process Back in Ready Queue"]
    S9 --> S10["10 Restart Faulting Instruction - Resume Execution"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two diagrams?**
> The first shows the **runtime control flow** (what happens when the CPU runs). The second shows the **timing decomposition** (what gets added to the EAT formula). KTU board answers that conflate these two frequently lose marks on the 7-mark sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Virtual Memory. Mention any two of its benefits.**

**Model Answer:**
Virtual Memory is a memory management technique that decouples the logical address space seen by a process from the physical memory of the system, allowing a process to execute even when it is not entirely in main memory; pages are brought into RAM on demand from secondary storage.
**Benefits:** (1) Allows programs larger than physical RAM to run, and (2) increases the degree of multiprogramming by freeing more frames for additional processes. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a page fault? How is it detected?**

**Model Answer:**
A page fault is a hardware trap raised by the Memory Management Unit when a process references a page that is marked *invalid* (not present) in its page table. **Detection:** The MMU checks the *valid–invalid bit* in the page-table entry corresponding to the virtual page number; if the bit is 0, a trap is generated and control transfers to the OS page-fault service routine. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **EAT unit conversion trap:** The biggest mark-loser. $T_{pf}$ is in **ms**, $ma$ is in **ns**. Convert to a common unit (usually ns) BEFORE substituting, or your answer will be off by 1,000,000×.
> 2. **Page-replacement string length:** When given a reference string and frames, ALWAYS show the contents of each frame *after each reference*. Skipping the intermediate snapshots costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Belady's Anomaly:** Many students claim LRU is anomaly-free *without* stating that FIFO suffers from it. The exact phrasing expected is: *"FIFO exhibits Belady's Anomaly; LRU and Optimal do not."*
> 4. **Thrashing definition must include both the cause and the symptom:** "$ \sum D_i > m $" **AND** the consequent collapse in CPU utilization.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Demand Paging + Numerical EAT + LRU Replacement

> `(a) [7 Marks]` With a neat diagram, explain the steps involved in handling a page fault in a demand-paged system. Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT) given $ma = 200$ ns, page fault service time = 10 ms, and page fault rate $p = 0.0005$.

> `(b) [7 Marks]` Consider the reference string: **7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1** with **3 page frames**. Apply the **LRU** page replacement algorithm and compute the number of page faults.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Diagram:** Use the block diagram from Section 4.2 (Page-Fault Service Time flow). *(2 marks for diagram and labeling)*

**Step-by-step handling** (to be written in tabular form for full marks):

| Step | OS Action |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hardware raises trap, saving PC and PSW on kernel stack. |
| 2 | OS identifies the faulting process and the missing virtual page. |
| 3 | Checks internal table — is the reference legal? If not, segmentation fault. |
| 4 | Picks a free frame (or runs replacement algorithm to pick a victim). |
| 5 | Issues disk I/O to read the page from swap into the chosen frame. |
| 6 | While I/O proceeds, context-switches to another ready process. |
| 7 | On disk interrupt, updates the page table and resets the valid bit. |
| 8 | Places the process back in the ready queue; instruction is restarted. |

*(2 marks for the 8 steps above)*

**EAT Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot T_{pf} \\
    &= (1 - 0.0005)(200\ \text{ns}) + (0.0005)(10{,}000{,}000\ \text{ns}) \\
    &= (0.9995)(200) + (0.0005)(10{,}000{,}000) \\
    &= 199.9\ \text{ns} + 5000\ \text{ns} \\
    &= 5199.9\ \text{ns} \approx 5.2\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks for the correct numerical answer with units)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Apply LRU with 3 frames. For each reference, **evict the page whose most recent use is the oldest**.

| Ref | F1 | F2 | F3 | Fault? | Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 7 | 7 | – | – | ✔ | Empty |
| 0 | 7 | 0 | – | ✔ | Empty |
| 1 | 7 | 0 | 1 | ✔ | Empty |
| 2 | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✔ | LRU is **7** (used 3 refs ago) |
| 0 | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✗ | Hit on **0** |
| 3 | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✔ | LRU is **1** (used 2 refs ago) |
| 0 | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✗ | Hit on **0** |
| 4 | 4 | 0 | 3 | ✔ | LRU is **2** (used 2 refs ago) |
| 2 | 4 | 0 | 2 | ✔ | LRU is **3** (used 2 refs ago) |
| 3 | 4 | 3 | 2 | ✔ | LRU is **0** (used 2 refs ago) |
| 0 | 4 | 3 | 0 | ✔ | LRU is **2** (used 2 refs ago) |
| 3 | 4 | 3 | 0 | ✗ | Hit on **3** |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 0 | ✔ | LRU is **4** (used 3 refs ago) |
| 1 | 2 | 3 | 1 | ✔ | LRU is **0** (used 2 refs ago) |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 1 | ✗ | Hit on **2** |
| 0 | 0 | 3 | 1 | ✔ | LRU is **2** (used 1 ref ago) |
| 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | ✗ | Hit on **1** |

**Total Page Faults = 12**
*(2 marks for correct table, 2 marks for the right count, 3 marks for showing the LRU reasoning at each step)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Thrashing, Working Set & Algorithm Comparison

> `(a) [7 Marks]` Explain the concept of **Thrashing**. With a diagram, describe the **Working Set Model** for preventing thrashing.

> `(b) [7 Marks]` Compare **FIFO, LRU, and Optimal (Belady's MIN)** page-replacement algorithms. Which one suffers from **Belady's Anomaly** and why? Justify with a small reference-string example.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Thrashing** occurs when a process spends *more time paging* (swapping pages in/out) than *executing instructions*. A process that thrashes generates page faults, steals frames from other processes, which in turn thrashes, causing a chain-reaction collapse of CPU utilization.

**Cause:** $\sum D_i > m$ — the sum of the working-set sizes of all processes exceeds the available physical frames.

**Working Set Model Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WS1["Process P1 Has Working Set W1 = 4 Pages"] --> SUM["Sum of All W_i for All Processes"]
    WS2["Process P2 Has Working Set W2 = 5 Pages"] --> SUM
    WS3["Process P3 Has Working Set W3 = 3 Pages"] --> SUM
    SUM --> DECIDE{"Is Sum less than or equal to m total frames"}
    DECIDE -->|Yes| ADMIT["Admit Process and Schedule It"]
    DECIDE -->|No| SUSPEND["Suspend One Process and Reduce Multiprogramming"]
```

**Window $\Delta$ Definition:**
$W(t, \Delta)$ = the set of pages referenced in the most recent $\Delta$ references at time $t$.
If $\sum W_i \leq m$, no thrashing. Else, suspend a process.

*(2 marks for thrashing definition, 2 marks for cause, 3 marks for working-set diagram and decision rule)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Criterion | FIFO | LRU | Optimal (MIN) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Basis** | Evict the page that has been in memory the longest. | Evict the page least recently used. | Evict the page not used for the longest *future* time. |
| **Implementation** | Easy (queue). | Expensive (counter or stack). | Not implementable (needs future knowledge). |
| **Performance** | Worst. | Near-optimal in practice. | Theoretical lower bound. |
| **Belady's Anomaly?** | **Yes** — more frames can cause more faults. | No. | No. |

**Belady's Anomaly Demonstration (Reference String: 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5):**

- With **3 frames** using FIFO: 9 page faults.
- With **4 frames** using FIFO: 10 page faults.

**More frames → more faults** — this is Belady's Anomaly, a counter-intuitive property of FIFO. LRU and Optimal are *stack algorithms* and are provably immune. *(3 marks for table, 2 marks for Anomaly example, 2 marks for stack-algorithm reasoning)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Virtual Memory** decouples **logical** from **physical** addresses; implemented via **demand paging** on modern OS.
- [ ] **Page Fault** = MMU trap when valid–invalid bit is 0; serviced by the **8-step OS routine** (memorize the order).
- [ ] **EAT Formula (the most-tested equation):** $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot T_{pf}$ — **convert units to ns first**.
- [ ] Standard KTU assumed values: $ma = 100$ ns, $T_{pf} = 25$ ms → ratio is **250,000**.
- [ ] **Even $p = 10^{-6}$ causes ~25% slowdown** — the fault penalty is *brutal*.
- [ ] Page Replacement Algorithms: **FIFO (has Belady's Anomaly), LRU (stack algorithm, near-optimal), Optimal (theoretical lower bound)**.
- [ ] **Thrashing** happens when $\sum D_i > m$; the cure is the **Working Set Model** (suspend a process if $\sum W_i > m$).
- [ ] **Page-fault handling time** includes: trap + I/O + restart — never forget the **context switch** during the slow I/O.
- [ ] **Belady's Anomaly** is exclusive to **non-stack algorithms** like FIFO. LRU/Optimal are stack algorithms.
- [ ] **Demand Paging vs. Anticipatory Paging**: KTU syllabus focuses on demand (lazy); anticipatory/predictive is extra.
- [ ] Always state the **unit** explicitly in EAT answers (ns / μs / ms) — the examiner checks this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Paging
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Demand Paging

# Demand Paging — A KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Study Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Demand paging is the conceptual backbone of virtual memory systems in modern operating systems. It enables execution of processes that may not be completely resident in physical memory, by loading pages only when demanded by the CPU.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Demand Paging** is a memory management scheme in which pages of a process are loaded into the main memory (RAM) **only when they are demanded (referenced) during execution**, rather than loading the entire process at once. If a referenced page is not present in memory, a **page fault** is triggered, causing the OS to fetch the required page from secondary storage (typically a disk) into a free frame.

Formally, demand paging is a **lazy swapper** strategy where:

$$\text{Page is brought into memory} \iff \text{Page is referenced by the executing process}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lazy Swapper:** The term "lazy" in operating systems refers to deferring an operation until it is absolutely necessary. In demand paging, the swapper waits until a page fault occurs before performing the I/O.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Desk

Imagine you are writing a research paper, but instead of carrying the entire library to your desk, you keep only the books you are currently reading on your table. When you need a fact from a different book, you:

1. Notice the book is missing from your desk (**page fault**).
2. Walk to the library shelf (**disk I/O**).
3. Fetch the required book and bring it to your desk (**page-in**).
4. Continue reading from where you stopped.

Your desk (RAM) has limited space, but you can still access any book (page) from the library (disk) on demand. **Demand paging works exactly like this for processes and their pages.**

### 1.3 Critical Terminology and Standard Metrics

- **Page Fault Rate** $p$: The probability (fraction in $[0,1]$) that a memory access results in a page fault. Typical value: **$0 \leq p \leq 1$**.
- **Memory Access Time (MAT)** $ma$: Time taken to access a word from memory once the page is resident. Typical value: **10 to 200 nanoseconds**.
- **Page Fault Service Time (PFST)**: Time to service a page fault, including disk I/O, OS overhead, and context switching. Typical range: **1 to 30 milliseconds (≈ $10^6$ ns)**.
- **Effective Access Time (EAT)**: The average time to access a memory word in a demand-paged system.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Mnemonic:** *"Lazy pages — faults are paid in milliseconds, hits in nanoseconds."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

Demand paging operates on a strict sequence of well-defined steps:

1. **Process Creation:** A new process is created with its pages residing on disk. Only essential kernel/control structures are loaded into memory. The size of the process image in memory is initially **zero** pages.
2. **Execution Begins:** The CPU fetches the first instruction. The MMU (Memory Management Unit) examines the page table entry (PTE) for the referenced page.
3. **Valid-Invalid Bit Check:** Each PTE has a valid–invalid bit:
   - **Valid (1):** The page is in memory → access proceeds normally.
   - **Invalid (0):** The page is NOT in memory → **trap to OS (page fault)**.
4. **Page Fault Trap Handling:** The OS checks:
   - Is the reference legal? (invalid memory access → segmentation fault/termination)
   - Is there a free frame available?
5. **Page-In Operation:** If free frame exists → issue disk I/O to read the desired page from its disk location into the chosen frame. The page table is updated to mark the page valid.
6. **Instruction Restart:** The faulting instruction is restarted. The process now continues with the page resident.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Demand Paging

- **Less I/O Needed:** Only demanded pages are loaded → reduces disk traffic.
- **Less Memory Required:** Multiple processes can share memory; only active pages reside in RAM.
- **Faster Response:** Programs start faster since the OS loads only a small initial portion.
- **Higher Degree of Multiprogramming:** More processes can coexist in memory simultaneously.
- **Support for Large Address Spaces:** Programs larger than physical memory can still execute.

### 2.3 Hardware Support Required

- **Page Table with Valid–Invalid (or Present) Bit:** Indicates whether a page is in memory.
- **Secondary Storage (Swap Space):** A dedicated area on the disk (the *swap device*) to hold pages not currently in memory.
- **Instruction Restart Capability:** The trapped instruction must be re-executable after the OS services the fault.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Units / Values** |
|---|---|---|
| Page Fault Rate | $0 \leq p \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Memory Access Time | $ma$ | 10 – 200 ns |
| Page Fault Service Time | $s$ | 1 – 30 ms $\approx 10^6$ ns |
| **Effective Access Time (No Page Fault)** | $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot s$ | nanoseconds |
| Effective Access Time (with restart overhead) | $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (\text{fault service} + \text{restart})$ | nanoseconds |
| Swap Time (Disk I/O portion) | $\approx 8$ ms (average rotational latency + seek) | milliseconds |
| OS Overhead (per fault) | $\approx 10$ – $100\ \mu s$ | microseconds |
| Process Startup Memory Footprint | Initially $= 0$ pages | Pages |
| Page Fault Service Time (formula) | $s = \text{OS overhead} + \text{Disk I/O} + \text{OS overhead}$ | ms |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that page fault service time is **THREE** components — (a) OS handling on first trap, (b) disk I/O, and (c) OS handling after I/O plus **instruction restart**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Cloud Computing:** Used by hypervisors (Xen, KVM, VMware ESXi) to over-commit physical memory, allowing more VMs per host than RAM would normally permit.
- **Mobile OS (Android, iOS):** Apps are demand-paged — only foreground pages are kept resident.
- **Database Servers:** Use demand-paging-aware tuning to avoid thrashing on large buffer pools.
- **Embedded Systems:** Critical for systems with tiny RAM (e.g., IoT devices running Linux).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Access Time (EAT)

We begin by formally defining the average time to access a memory word when demand paging is in effect.

#### **Step 1 — Define the Two Possible Outcomes of a Memory Access**

Every memory access by the CPU results in one of two events:

- **Case A — Page Hit (probability $= 1 - p$):** The page is in memory. The access takes **$ma$** nanoseconds.
- **Case B — Page Fault (probability $= p$):** The page is NOT in memory. The OS must service the fault, taking **$s$** nanoseconds, before the access can complete.

#### **Step 2 — Express the Expected (Average) Access Time**

The Effective Access Time is the weighted average of the time taken in each case, weighted by its probability:

$$
EAT = (\text{Probability of Hit}) \cdot (\text{Time on Hit}) + (\text{Probability of Fault}) \cdot (\text{Time on Fault})
$$

Substituting the standard terms:

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot s
$$

#### **Step 3 — Refine the Page Fault Service Time $s$**

In a more realistic model, page fault service time consists of three sub-steps:

$$
s = T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{I/O}} + T_{\text{restart}}
$$

Where:

- $T_{\text{trap}}$ = time for OS to handle the trap (≈ $10\ \mu s$ to $100\ \mu s$).
- $T_{\text{I/O}}$ = time to read the page from disk into a free frame (≈ $8$ ms average for hard disk).
- $T_{\text{restart}}$ = time to restart the faulting instruction after the page is loaded.

Therefore the complete expression becomes:

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{I/O}} + T_{\text{restart}})
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU-style Problem)

**Problem Statement:** In a demand-paged system, the memory access time is **$200$ ns**, the average page fault service time is **$8$ ms** (i.e., $8 \times 10^6$ ns), and the page fault rate is $p$. Compute the Effective Access Time for:
**(a)** $p = 0$ (no page faults)
**(b)** $p = 0.001$ (one in 1000 accesses faults)
**(c)** $p = 0.05$ (5% of accesses fault)

#### **Solution (a) — $p = 0$**

When no page faults occur, every access succeeds in $ma = 200$ ns.

$$
EAT = (1 - 0) \cdot 200 + 0 \cdot (8 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
EAT = 200\ \text{ns}
$$

#### **Solution (b) — $p = 0.001$**

Here, $1 - p = 0.999$ and the fault penalty is $8 \times 10^6$ ns.

$$
EAT = 0.999 \cdot 200 + 0.001 \cdot (8 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
EAT = 199.8 + 8000
$$

$$
EAT = 8199.8\ \text{ns} \approx 8.2\ \mu s
$$

This shows that even a tiny page fault rate of **$0.1\%$** slows the system down by a factor of approximately **$40\times$**.

#### **Solution (c) — $p = 0.05$**

$$
EAT = 0.95 \cdot 200 + 0.05 \cdot (8 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
EAT = 190 + 400{,}000
$$

$$
EAT = 400{,}190\ \text{ns} \approx 0.4\ \text{ms}
$$

The system becomes effectively $2000\times$ slower — clearly unusable. This is why **keeping the page fault rate extremely low is a critical design goal**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance vs Page Fault Rate

| **Page Fault Rate $p$** | **Memory Access Time $ma$ (ns)** | **Page Fault Service Time $s$ (ns)** | **Effective Access Time $EAT$ (ns)** | **Slowdown Factor vs $ma$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 200 | $8 \times 10^6$ | $200$ | $1\times$ |
| $10^{-6}$ | 200 | $8 \times 10^6$ | $\approx 208$ | $1.04\times$ |
| $10^{-4}$ | 200 | $8 \times 10^6$ | $\approx 1000$ | $5\times$ |
| $10^{-3}$ | 200 | $8 \times 10^6$ | $\approx 8199.8$ | $\approx 41\times$ |
| $10^{-2}$ | 200 | $8 \times 10^6$ | $\approx 80{,}180$ | $\approx 401\times$ |
| $5 \times 10^{-2}$ | 200 | $8 \times 10^6$ | $\approx 400{,}190$ | $\approx 2001\times$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Even a fault rate as low as $0.0001$ ($1$ in $10{,}000$) introduces a slowdown of $5\times$. Hence the **locality of reference** is crucial — programs must exhibit strong temporal and spatial locality to keep the page fault rate negligible.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Demand Paging — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process Starts"] --> B["CPU Fetches Instruction"]
    B --> C["MMU Translates Virtual Address"]
    C --> D{"Check Page Table Valid-Invalid Bit"}
    D -- "Valid Bit = 1" --> E["Page Hit - Access RAM"]
    E --> F["Continue Execution"]
    D -- "Valid Bit = 0" --> G["Page Fault Trap to OS"]
    G --> H{"Is Reference Legal?"}
    H -- "No" --> I["Send Segmentation Fault - Terminate Process"]
    H -- "Yes" --> J{"Free Frame Available in RAM?"}
    J -- "No" --> K["Evict a Page via Replacement Algo"]
    K --> L["Issue Disk I/O to Read Faulted Page"]
    J -- "Yes" --> L
    L --> M["Disk Reads Page into Free Frame"]
    M --> N["Update Page Table - Mark Valid"]
    N --> O["Restart Faulting Instruction"]
    O --> F
    F --> B
```

### 4.2 Page Fault Service — Detailed Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["CPU Subsystem"]
        CPU1["Execute Instruction"] --> CPU2["Virtual Address Translation"]
        CPU2 --> CPU3{"Page Table Lookup"}
    end
    CPU3 -- "Present" --> CPU4["Access Physical Frame"]
    CPU3 -- "Absent" --> OS1["Trap to OS - Page Fault Handler"]
    subgraph OS["OS Page Fault Handler"]
        OS1 --> OS2["Check Legal Reference"]
        OS2 --> OS3["Locate Page on Disk"]
        OS3 --> OS4["Find Free Frame or Evict Victim"]
        OS4 --> OS5["Issue Disk Read I/O"]
        OS5 --> OS6["Update Page Table Entry"]
        OS6 --> OS7["Set Process to Ready"]
    end
    OS7 --> CPU4
    CPU4 --> CPU1
```

### 4.3 Memory Hierarchy in a Demand-Paged System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Level 1 - CPU Registers"]
        R["Registers - Fastest - Few Bytes"]
    end
    subgraph L2["Level 2 - Cache L1 L2 L3"]
        C["Cache - ns Latency - KB to MB"]
    end
    subgraph L3["Level 3 - Main Memory RAM"]
        M["RAM - 100 ns Latency - GB Scale - Holds Active Pages"]
    end
    subgraph L4["Level 4 - Secondary Storage Disk SSD"]
        D["Disk or SSD - ms Latency - TB Scale - Holds All Pages of Process"]
    end
    R --> C --> M --> D
    D -. "Page-In on Demand" .-> M
    M -. "Swap-Out on Eviction" .-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** When the CPU issues an address, the system checks progressively from registers → cache → RAM → disk. Only when the requested page is **not** in RAM does a page fault occur, forcing a disk read. This hierarchy is the physical foundation of demand paging.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define demand paging. How does it differ from simple paging?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Demand paging is a memory management technique in which pages of a process are loaded into the main memory **only when they are referenced (demanded)** by the CPU during execution, instead of bringing the entire process into memory at load time. If a referenced page is not in memory, a **page fault** occurs and the OS loads the required page from secondary storage.

**Difference from simple paging:**

- In **simple paging**, the entire process must reside in memory before execution begins.
- In **demand paging**, only the pages that are actually referenced are loaded; pages not yet needed remain on disk.

Hence demand paging enables execution of processes whose size **exceeds physical memory**, supports a higher degree of multiprogramming, and reduces I/O and startup latency.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the role of the valid–invalid bit in a demand-paged system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Every entry in the page table contains a **valid–invalid bit** (also called the present bit):

- **Valid (1):** The page is currently in a physical frame in main memory; access proceeds without intervention.
- **Invalid (0):** The page is **not** in memory; either it is on the disk (a valid demand-paged reference) or it is an illegal address.

When the MMU finds the bit set to invalid for a referenced page, it triggers a **trap to the OS** (page fault). The OS then services the fault by loading the required page from disk and resetting the bit to valid. Thus the valid–invalid bit is the **hardware mechanism** that enables demand paging to detect and react to absent pages.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the steps involved in handling a page fault in a demand-paged system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a demand-paged system, the memory access time is **$100$ ns**, the average page fault service time is **$30$ ms**, and the page fault rate is **$0.0001$**. Compute the Effective Access Time (EAT) and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The page fault handling sequence consists of **eight key steps**:

1. **Trap to the OS:** The MMU detects an invalid bit in the page table entry for the referenced page and generates a trap, saving the state of the faulting process.
2. **Check Validity of Reference:** The OS examines the referenced address. If it is illegal, the process is terminated with a segmentation fault; otherwise the OS proceeds.
3. **Locate Page on Disk:** The OS finds the location of the demanded page in the swap space on the secondary storage device.
4. **Find a Free Frame:** The OS searches the free-frame list. If a free frame is unavailable, a page-replacement algorithm (e.g., FIFO, LRU) selects a victim frame.
5. **Issue Disk Read I/O:** The OS schedules a disk I/O request to read the demanded page into the chosen (or newly freed) frame.
6. **While I/O is in progress:** The CPU may be allocated to another ready process (context switch for **I/O overlap**).
7. **Update Page Table:** When the disk I/O completes, the OS updates the page table to set the valid bit for the loaded page and records its frame number.
8. **Restart Instruction:** The faulting instruction is restarted. The process continues execution with the page now resident in memory.

```
Process executing
        |
        v
MMU detects invalid PTE  -- (Valid-Invalid Bit = 0)
        |
        v
Trap to OS - Save state
        |
        v
Is reference legal?
  |-- No --> Terminate (Segfault)
  |-- Yes --> Find free frame
                  |
                  v
            Disk I/O read page
                  |
                  v
        Update Page Table (Set Valid)
                  |
                  v
        Restart faulting instruction
                  |
                  v
        Continue execution
```

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- Memory Access Time, $ma = 100$ ns
- Page Fault Service Time, $s = 30$ ms $= 30 \times 10^6$ ns
- Page Fault Rate, $p = 0.0001$

**Formula:**

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot s
$$

**Substituting values:**

$$
EAT = (1 - 0.0001) \cdot 100 + 0.0001 \cdot (30 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
EAT = (0.9999) \cdot 100 + 3000
$$

$$
EAT = 99.99 + 3000
$$

$$
EAT = 3099.99\ \text{ns} \approx 3.1\ \mu s
$$

**Comment:**

The slowdown factor with respect to the original $100$ ns memory access time is:

$$
\text{Slowdown} = \frac{3099.99}{100} \approx 31\times
$$

Even with a tiny page fault rate of $0.01\%$, the EAT grows by a factor of about $31$. This confirms that **demand paging is only practical when programs exhibit strong locality of reference**, keeping the page fault rate negligibly small.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** What are the advantages and disadvantages of demand paging? Compare demand paging with anticipatory paging (prepaging). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Suppose a system has a memory access time of **$200$ ns** and an average page fault service time of **$8$ ms**. What is the maximum acceptable page fault rate such that the Effective Access Time is **at most $110\%$ of the memory access time**? **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Advantages of Demand Paging:**

1. **Less I/O:** Only demanded pages are loaded — initial loading is minimal.
2. **Less Memory Required:** Allows more processes in memory concurrently (higher multiprogramming).
3. **Faster Process Startup:** Process can begin executing without the entire program being in memory.
4. **Larger Virtual Address Spaces:** Programs can be larger than physical memory.
5. **Better System Throughput:** I/O bandwidth is conserved.

**Disadvantages of Demand Paging:**

1. **Page Fault Overhead:** A page fault costs milliseconds — frequent faults severely degrade performance (**thrashing**).
2. **More Complex Hardware:** Requires MMU, page tables, and swap device support.
3. **Predictability Issues:** Real-time systems suffer due to unpredictable page fault delays.
4. **OS Overhead:** Page fault handling involves context switches and disk scheduling.

**Demand Paging vs Prepaging:**

- **Demand paging** loads pages only when referenced — truly lazy. Low initial I/O, but susceptible to bursts of faults (e.g., during program startup when many pages are referenced in quick succession).
- **Prepaging (anticipatory paging)** pre-loads pages that are *expected* to be referenced soon — reduces future faults but risks loading pages that may never be used.

| **Criterion** | **Demand Paging** | **Prepaging** |
|---|---|---|
| Loading strategy | Load on demand | Anticipatory |
| Initial I/O | Minimal | Higher |
| Fault rate after warm-up | Higher for bursts | Lower |
| Wasted I/O | None | Possible (unused pages) |
| Best suited for | General-purpose OS | Sequential access patterns |

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- $ma = 200$ ns
- $s = 8$ ms $= 8 \times 10^6$ ns
- $EAT_{\max} = 1.10 \times 200 = 220$ ns

**Condition:**

$$
EAT \leq 1.10 \cdot ma
$$

$$
(1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot s \leq 1.10 \cdot ma
$$

**Substituting values:**

$$
(1 - p) \cdot 200 + p \cdot (8 \times 10^6) \leq 220
$$

$$
200 - 200p + 8 \times 10^6 \cdot p \leq 220
$$

$$
200 + p \cdot (8 \times 10^6 - 200) \leq 220
$$

$$
p \cdot (7{,}999{,}800) \leq 20
$$

$$
p \leq \frac{20}{7{,}999{,}800}
$$

$$
p \leq 2.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Result:** The maximum acceptable page fault rate is approximately **$2.5 \times 10^{-6}$** (i.e., **1 page fault in every $400{,}000$ accesses**). Beyond this rate, the system cannot maintain even a $10\%$ performance tolerance over the raw memory access time.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing ns and ms in the EAT formula. Always convert to a common unit before substitution.
> 2. **Missing Page Fault Components:** Students often forget the instruction restart cost. A complete model includes trap overhead + disk I/O + restart.
> 3. **Confusing Page Fault Rate with Frequency:** $p = 0.001$ means 1 fault per 1000 accesses — NOT 1 fault per second.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract:** When computing $EAT$, students sometimes write $p \cdot ma + (1-p) \cdot s$ — this inverts the weighting.
> 5. **Valid–Invalid vs Protection Bits:** In demand paging, the invalid bit serves dual purpose (not-present OR illegal-access). Examiners expect you to mention both interpretations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Demand paging loads pages only when referenced; absent pages cause page faults serviced by the OS via disk I/O.
- **Valid–Invalid Bit:** Hardware indicator in each PTE that distinguishes resident vs non-resident pages.
- **Page Fault Handling:** Eight-step sequence — trap, legality check, locate-on-disk, find-frame, I/O, context-switch, update-PTE, restart-instruction.
- **EAT Formula:** $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot s$ — fundamental KTU equation.
- **Typical Values:** $ma \approx 100$ ns; $s \approx 8$ ms. Even $p = 10^{-4}$ causes $5\times$ slowdown.
- **Advantages:** Lower I/O, less memory, larger virtual spaces, higher multiprogramming.
- **Disadvantages:** Page-fault overhead, hardware/OS complexity, risk of thrashing.
- **Locality of Reference:** Programs must exhibit temporal and spatial locality to keep $p$ low.
- **Hardware Requirements:** MMU with valid–invalid bit, page table, swap device, restartable instructions.
- **Demand vs Prepaging:** Demand = lazy on reference; Prepaging = anticipatory pre-loading.
- **Real-world use:** Cloud VMs, mobile OS, database servers, embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Page Replacement Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Page Replacement Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Page Replacement** is the mechanism by which the Operating System (OS) selects an existing resident page in physical memory to be evicted (swapped out) to secondary storage (disk) in order to make room for a newly required page when a **page fault** occurs and no free frames are available.

In a demand-paged virtual memory system, every process operates under the illusion of a large, contiguous logical address space backed by a comparatively small physical memory (RAM). The hardware **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** performs address translation via the **Page Table**. When a process references a page that is **not resident** in physical memory, a **page fault trap** is raised. If all physical frames are occupied, the OS must invoke a *page replacement algorithm* to free a frame.

The page replacement problem is a specific instance of the **Cache Replacement / Belady's optimal replacement problem**, and it must be solved online (with only knowledge of past references) or in offline (look-ahead) mode for theoretical analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Desk Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you are a researcher working at a desk (your **Physical Memory = RAM**). You have only **3 books** that can stay on your desk at a time, but the library has **thousands of books** (your **Virtual Address Space = Disk**). When you need a new book, you call a librarian (**the Operating System**).
>
> - If the book is already on your desk, you read it immediately — this is a **memory hit**.
> - If the book is *not* on your desk, the librarian must bring it from the library stacks. But your desk is full. So the librarian has to **pick one book to return to the stacks** to make room.
>
> The *strategy* the librarian uses to pick the book to remove is the **Page Replacement Algorithm**. A good strategy keeps the books you will need soon right on your desk, minimizing your trips to the librarian (page faults).

## 1.3 Why Page Replacement is Required (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **When a Page Fault occurs and there are NO free frames:**
> 1. The OS consults the page replacement algorithm to choose a **victim frame**.
> 2. The victim page is written back to disk (if dirty — i.e., modified), and its Page Table entry is marked invalid.
> 3. The required page is read from disk into the freed frame.
> 4. The Page Table is updated, and the process is restarted at the faulting instruction.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Key Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Memory Access Time (MAT):** $100 \text{ nanoseconds (ns)} = 10^{-7} \text{ s}$
> - **Page Fault Service Time (PFT):** $25 \text{ milliseconds (ms)} = 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}$
> - The PFT includes: OS trap handler execution, saving process state, disk I/O controller seek latency, rotational latency (disk rotation), and actual data transfer.
