## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a File Control Block (FCB). List any four fields stored in it.**

**Model Answer:**
A **File Control Block (FCB)** is an on-disk data structure that stores all the metadata about a file. It is created by the OS when the file is created and is used by the file-system module whenever the file is accessed.

**Four important fields stored in an FCB:**
1. **File size** — total number of bytes/blocks in the file.
2. **File type** — regular file, directory, character device, block device, etc.
3. **Protection / Permission bits** — read, write, execute permissions for owner, group, others.
4. **Timestamps** — creation time, last modification time, last access time.

*(Other valid fields: file name, owner ID, group ID, link count, pointers to data blocks / inode address.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Compare contiguous allocation and linked allocation of disk space in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Contiguous Allocation | Linked Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| **External fragmentation** | Yes — wastes disk space over time | No — blocks are scattered |
| **Random access** | Direct — $O(1)$ using start + offset | Sequential only — $O(n)$ traversal required |
| **File growth handling** | Difficult — may need relocation | Easy — just append a new block |

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the three main disk allocation methods with suitable diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Contiguous Allocation:**

- Each file occupies a **set of consecutive disk blocks**.
- The directory entry stores the **starting block address** and the **length** of the file.
- Access to block $k$ of the file: $\text{disk\_address} = \text{start} + k$.

*Advantages:*
- Excellent **random access** support.
- Minimal seek time — only one seek for the whole file (sequential).

*Disadvantages:*
- Suffers from **external fragmentation**.
- Difficult to extend file — may require full relocation.

**2. Linked Allocation:**

- File is a **linked list of disk blocks** scattered across the disk.
- Each block contains a **pointer to the next block**.
- Directory entry stores the address of the **first block** only.

*Advantages:*
- No external fragmentation.
- File can grow dynamically.

*Disadvantages:*
- **No direct random access** — to read block $k$, we must traverse $k-1$ blocks.
- Pointer overhead in each block.
- Poor reliability — a single bad pointer loses all subsequent blocks.

**3. Indexed Allocation:**

- An **index block** is allocated per file, containing an array of pointers to all data blocks.
- Directory entry stores the address of the **index block** only.
- Supports both direct and indirect indexes (multi-level).

*Advantages:*
- **Direct random access** — $O(1)$ for blocks referenced in the index.
- No external fragmentation.

*Disadvantages:*
- **Index block overhead** — wastes space for very small files.
- Maximum file size is limited by index block size (unless multi-level indexing is used).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Consider a UNIX file system with the following parameters:**
- Block size $B = 4096$ bytes
- Pointer size $p = 8$ bytes (64-bit addressing)
- Inode contains: **12 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect pointer, 1 double-indirect pointer, 0 triple-indirect pointers**
- **Compute the maximum file size in MB.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the number of pointers per block.

$$
n_{\text{block}} = \frac{B}{p} = \frac{4096}{8} = 512
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the data blocks addressable via direct pointers.

$$
N_d = 12 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the data blocks addressable via the single-indirect pointer.

$$
N_{si} = 1 \times n_{\text{block}} = 1 \times 512 = 512 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the data blocks addressable via the double-indirect pointer.

$$
N_{di} = 1 \times (n_{\text{block}})^2 = 1 \times 512^2 = 1 \times 262144 = 262144 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum all addressable data blocks.

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 12 + 512 + 262144 = 262668 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the maximum file size in bytes.

$$
S_{\max} = N_{\text{total}} \times B = 262668 \times 4096
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
S_{\max} = 262668 \times 4096 = 262668 \times 4000 + 262668 \times 96
$$

$$
S_{\max} = 1050672000 + 25216128 = 1075888128 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to MB ($1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \times 1024 = 1048576$ bytes).

$$
S_{\max} \text{ (in MB)} = \frac{1075888128}{1048576} \approx 1026.16 \text{ MB} \approx 1.00 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Maximum file size $\approx \mathbf{1026.16 \text{ MB}}$ (approximately **1 GB**).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the layered architecture of a file system. Illustrate the role of each layer with an example.**

**Model Solution:**

A modern file system is organized as a **stack of layers**, each hiding the complexity of the layer below it. This follows the Silberschatz / KTU 2024 textbook model:

**Layer 1 — Application Programs**
- The topmost layer, where users and applications issue file operations like `open()`, `read()`, `write()`.
- *Example:* A text editor like `vi` opens `notes.txt` and reads its contents.

**Layer 2 — Logical File System**
- Manages **metadata**, **directory structure**, and **File Control Blocks (FCBs)**.
- Translates symbolic file names into numeric file identifiers.
- *Example:* Resolves `notes.txt` to inode `1024`.

**Layer 3 — File-Organization Module**
- Translates **logical block numbers** into **physical block addresses**.
- Manages **free space** using techniques like bitmaps or linked lists.
- *Example:* Maps logical block 5 of `notes.txt` to physical sector `78432`.

**Layer 4 — Basic File System**
- Issues generic block-level I/O commands to the device driver.
- Manages **buffer cache** to optimize repeated accesses.
- *Example:* Reads sector `78432` into the buffer cache.

**Layer 5 — I/O Control (Device Driver)**
- Interacts directly with the disk hardware.
- Issues low-level commands like `SEEK`, `READ`, `WRITE`.
- *Example:* Drives the disk arm to cylinder 1023, head 2, sector 47.

> **Advantage of layering:** Each layer can be **modified or replaced independently**. For example, the buffer cache can be optimized without changing the directory implementation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the four common techniques for free-space management. Under what condition is a bit vector preferred over a linked list?**

**Model Solution:**

**Technique 1 — Bit Vector (Bitmap):**
- A vector of $n$ bits is maintained, one per disk block.
- A bit value of **1 means the block is free**, **0 means allocated** (or vice versa).
- Finding the first free block: linear scan (or use hardware `find-first-1` instruction).
- *Example:* `0011100011111000...` — bit at position 3 indicates the fourth block is free.

**Technique 2 — Linked List:**
- Link all **free disk blocks** together, each block pointing to the next free block.
- No waste of space; minimal metadata.
- *Drawback:* Traversal for the $k$-th free block is $O(k)$ — slow for large disks.

**Technique 3 — Grouping:**
- A variation of the linked list where the **first free block in each group** stores the addresses of up to $n$ other free blocks.
- The last entry in that block points to the next group of free blocks.
- Significantly faster than simple linked list.

**Technique 4 — Counting:**
- Maintain pairs of **(starting block address, count)** for contiguous runs of free blocks.
- Especially efficient when contiguous allocation is used and free blocks tend to cluster.
- Requires less space than a full bitmap when runs are long.

**Condition for preferring Bit Vector:**
A **bit vector** is preferred when **the entire vector can be kept in main memory**. For a disk with $n$ blocks, the vector requires $n$ bits, which for a 1 TB disk with 4 KB blocks is $2^{28}$ bits (32 MB) — easily cacheable. Once in memory, bitwise operations (e.g., `find-first-set`) are extremely fast, making the bitmap vastly superior in **allocation speed**.

A linked list is preferred only when the entire bitmap cannot fit in memory or when free blocks are highly fragmented and a sequential allocation strategy is being used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** In the maximum file size derivation, students frequently forget to convert bytes → KB → MB and lose 1–2 marks. Always show the division explicitly.
> 2. **Pointer size confusion:** Block size $B$ is in bytes, but pointer size $p$ is also in bytes — make sure both are in the same unit before dividing.
> 3. **Confusing inode with directory entry:** The **directory entry** stores the inode number; the **inode** stores the actual pointers to data blocks. Confusing these two structures costs marks.
> 4. **Missing the $n_d$, $n_{si}$ factors:** Some students compute only $n_{\text{block}}$ (or $(n_{\text{block}})^2$) and forget to multiply by the number of such pointers in the inode. Read the inode structure carefully!
> 5. **Wrong definitions for external vs. internal fragmentation:** *External fragmentation* occurs when free space is split into non-contiguous holes. *Internal fragmentation* occurs when allocated space exceeds requested space (e.g., wasted space in the last block of a file).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **File-System Implementation** = the on-disk and in-memory data structures that map logical files to physical disk blocks.
- **Key on-disk structures:** Boot Control Block, Volume Control Block, Directory Structure, File Control Block (FCB / inode).
- **Key in-memory structures:** Mount Table, Directory Cache, System-wide Open-File Table, Per-Process Open-File Table.
- **Layered architecture (top → bottom):** Application → Logical FS → File-Organization Module → Basic FS → I/O Control.
- **Three allocation methods:**
  - **Contiguous** — best random access, suffers external fragmentation.
  - **Linked** — no external fragmentation, no random access.
  - **Indexed** — combines both; the basis of the UNIX inode.
- **Maximum file size formula (inode):**
$$
S_{\max} = B \times \left[ n_d + n_{si} \cdot \frac{B}{p} + n_{di} \cdot \left(\frac{B}{p}\right)^{2} + n_{ti} \cdot \left(\frac{B}{p}\right)^{3} \right]
$$
- **Free-space management techniques:** Bit Vector (fast if in memory), Linked List, Grouping, Counting.
- **Directory implementation:** Linear List $O(n)$ vs. Hash Table $O(1)$ average.
- **Standard block size:** typically **512 bytes to 4096 bytes (4 KB)**.
- **Efficiency tips:** Use a unified **buffer cache / page cache** to reduce disk I/O; use **read-ahead** and **write-behind** policies.
- **Recovery:** Journaling (ext4, NTFS) and log-structured (LFS) approaches ensure consistency after crashes.
- **Common KTU keywords to memorize:** FCB, inode, FAT, VCB, BCB, mount table, open-file table, free-space bitmap, contiguous allocation, linked allocation, indexed allocation, single-indirect, double-indirect, external fragmentation, internal fragmentation.
- **Famous KTU numerical pattern:** Always given $B$, $p$, $n_d$, $n_{si}$, $n_{di}$, $n_{ti}$ — derive $S_{\max}$ in MB/GB. Practice at least 5 such numericals before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Directory Implementation, Allocation Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Directory Implementation, Allocation Methods # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Directory Implementation — Formal Definition In an Operating System, a **directory** is a special type of file that maintains a mapping between human-readable file names and their low-level attributes (metadata) and physical locations on secondary storage. **Directory implementation** refers to the specific data structure and algorithm used inside the OS to store, search, insert, and delete these name-to-location mappings efficiently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Directory implementation is a high-weightage topic. You must know all three major schemes — **Linear List**, **Hash Table**, and **Tree-based (hierarchical)** — along with their time complexity trade-offs. ### Intuitive Analogy: The Office Filing Cabinet Imagine a massive office warehouse with thousands of boxes (files) scattered across many shelves (disk blocks). You cannot memorize where every box is. So, you maintain a **register** (the directory) at the entrance. When you want "Project_Report.pdf," the register tells you: *“Aisle 4, Shelf 2, Box 17.”* The **way the register is organized** (alphabetical, hashed by color code, or in a tree) is the *Directory Implementation Method*. A poorly designed register makes you scan the entire book every time; a smart design (like a hash table) lets you jump straight to the entry. > [!NOTE] > **Core Design Goals of a Directory Structure:** > - **Efficiency** → Locate a file quickly (minimum disk seeks). > - **Naming** → User-friendly symbolic names, not numeric addresses. > - **Uniqueness** → No two files in the same directory share a name. > - **Organization** → Support logical grouping (hierarchical folders). --- ## 1.2 File Allocation Methods — Formal Definition **File allocation** is the strategy an Operating System uses to map a file’s logical address space (byte/block offsets 0, 1, 2 …) to the actual **physical disk blocks** (sectors/blocks 0, 1, 2 …). The OS must decide, for each file, *where* on the disk the data blocks will physically reside and *how* the file system will keep track of them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The three classical allocation methods are: > 1. **Contiguous Allocation** > 2. **Linked Allocation** > 3. **Indexed Allocation** > > Each method balances the three classic trade-offs: **storage efficiency, access speed, and support for dynamic file growth.** ### Intuitive Analogy: The Train, The Treasure Map & The Index Card Think of a 100-page document you need to store: - **Contiguous Allocation** = *A reserved train of 100 connected coaches*. Super fast to walk from page 1 to page 100, but the moment one coach is added or removed, the whole train must be reshuffled. - **Linked Allocation** = *A treasure hunt*. Each page (block) contains a small pointer arrow to the *next* page. Cheap to extend (just draw a new page), but finding page 75 means flipping through all 74 arrows before it. - **Indexed Allocation** = *A dedicated index card* (the i-node block) listing every page number. You consult the card, then jump directly to the page. Fast random access, with a small fixed-size card overhead. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Storage Metrics You Must Memorize:** > - **Block / Sector Size:** typically **512 bytes** or **4 KB** on modern drives. > - **Pointer Size:** on a 32-bit system, a disk pointer is **4 bytes**; on 64-bit, **8 bytes**. > - **Seek Time:** ~**5–10 ms** (HDD); SSD seek ≈ **0.1 ms**. --- ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme | Aspect | Relevance | |---|---| | **Course Outcome (CO)** | CO4 — *Design and evaluate file system structures, allocation strategies, and directory organization for performance.* | | **Bloom’s Level** | Apply, Analyze, Evaluate | | **Mark Pattern (ESE)** | Part A: 3 marks (short) · Part B: 7+7 marks (detailed) | | **Industry Link** | NTFS, ext4, APFS, and FAT file systems are all built on variations of these ideas. | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Directory Implementation — Operational Breakdown The OS keeps a **directory entry** (a row in a directory file) for every file and subdirectory. The data structure used to organize these rows is the *implementation scheme*. ### A. Linear List (Simplest Method) A plain array of entries, sorted or unsorted. - **Search:** Scan sequentially from index 0 to *n−1* until a name match is found. - **Insertion:** Add at the end (or sort and shift). Requires checking for duplicate names → another full scan. - **Deletion:** Find the entry, mark its slot as free. Optionally, compact the array. **Pros:** Simple to implement, low memory overhead. **Cons:** Linearly slow on large directories. ### B. Hash Table Implementation A hash function $h(name)$ converts a file name into a slot index in a fixed-size table. Collisions (two names hashing to the same slot) are resolved by **chaining** (linked list per bucket) or **open addressing** (probing). - **Search:** Compute $h(name)$, jump to that slot, follow chain or probe. - **Insertion:** Compute $h$, add entry to chain. - **Deletion:** Compute $h$, unlink from chain. **Pros:** Constant-time average lookup. **Cons:** Fixed table size; collisions degrade performance; hash function must be re-computed on resizing. ### C. Tree-Based Implementation (Hierarchical) Directory is organized as a **hierarchical tree** (the standard Unix/Windows model). Subdirectories contain files and other subdirectories. To speed up lookup, the tree is stored in a **B-Tree** (or B+ tree) on disk. - **Search:** Walk tree path from root; balanced B-Tree ensures $O(\log n)$ disk accesses. - **Insertion/Deletion:** B-Tree node split/merge operations. **Pros:** Scalable, ordered, supports alphabetic sorting & range queries. **Cons:** Complex to implement; node rebalancing overhead. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world usage:** NTFS uses **B-Tree** directories; Linux ext4 uses **Hashed B-Tree (HTree)** — a hybrid of hash + B-Tree. This is a favorite KTU short-answer. --- ## 2.2 File Allocation Methods — Operational Breakdown ### A. Contiguous Allocation Each file occupies a set of **consecutive block addresses** on disk. The directory entry needs only two values: *starting block* and *length (in blocks)*. **Logical-to-Physical Mapping:** $$ \text{Physical Block} = \text{Start\_Block} + \text{Logical\_Block\_Number} $$ **Pros:** - Excellent **sequential** and **random** access (just arithmetic, no disk seeks between blocks). - Simple implementation. **Cons:** - **External fragmentation** (free holes too small for new files). - Difficult to grow a file; if neighboring blocks are occupied, the entire file must be **moved** (compaction). - Need to know file size at creation time (or pre-allocate maximum). ### B. Linked Allocation Each disk block contains two parts: a **data payload** and a **pointer to the next block**. The directory entry stores only the **starting block** number. **Logical-to-Physical Mapping:** Walk the chain from the start, counting pointers. **Pros:** - No external fragmentation — any free block can be used. - Easy to extend (just allocate a new block, link it at the end). - Easy to delete (just unlink the head). **Cons:** - **Random access is slow** (must traverse the chain). - Pointer overhead eats into block space. - A single bad pointer corrupts the rest of the file. (FAT mitigates this.) > [!IMPORTANT] > **FAT (File Allocation Table)** is a hybrid: a centralized table acts like an *array of pointers* indexed by block number. It is essentially a *linked allocation* with the pointers pulled out of blocks and stored in a separate table in memory for fast access. **This is a guaranteed KTU question!** ### C. Indexed Allocation A special **index block** is allocated for each file. The index block contains an array of pointers to all data blocks of the file. The directory entry stores the index block number. **Logical-to-Physical Mapping:** $$ \text{Data\_Block} = \text{Index\_Block}[\text{Logical\_Block\_Number}] $$ **Pros:** - Supports **direct random access** in $O(1)$ (one index lookup + one disk access). - No external fragmentation. **Cons:** - **Index block size** limits maximum file size. - Wastes space for small files (the index block is reserved even if the file is only 1 block long). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two-Level Indexed Scheme:** To overcome the size limit, Unix uses a *multi-level index* (direct pointers + single-indirect + double-indirect + triple-indirect). With a 4 KB block and 4-byte pointers, **single-indirect** addresses up to **1024 blocks** (4 MB); **double-indirect** addresses up to $1024^2$ blocks (~4 GB). This is a classic KTU long-answer sub-question. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Table | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---| | Contiguous: physical block | $B_p = B_{\text{start}} + i$ | $i$ = logical block index | | Indexed: data block | $B_p = \text{Index}[i]$ | $O(1)$ access | | Linked: traversal cost | $T(n) = n \cdot t_{\text{block}}$ | Linear in $n$ | | Indexed: max file size (1 level) | $S_{\max} = N_p \cdot S_b$ | $N_p$ = pointers per index block, $S_b$ = block size | | Indexed: 2-level max | $S_{\max} = N_p^2 \cdot S_b$ | Square of pointer count | | Hash search avg. | $O(1)$ | Worst case $O(n)$ with collisions | | Linear list search | $O(n)$ | Sequential scan | | B-Tree search | $O(\log n)$ | Balanced disk-friendly tree | | FAT pointer size | $\vert S_b \div 4 \vert$ entries per block | For 4-byte pointers | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Goal of this section:** Derive the *maximum file size* supported by a single-level and two-level indexed allocation scheme — the most repeated numerical problem in KTU exams. --- ## 3.1 Problem Setup & Given Assumptions Let: - **Block size** $S_b$ (bytes per disk block) - **Pointer size** $S_p$ (bytes per disk address) - **Number of pointers per block** $N_p = \dfrac{S_b}{S_p}$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Maximum File Size with Single-Level Index ### Step 1: Count pointers that fit in one index block. Since each pointer occupies $S_p$ bytes, the maximum number of pointers that fit in a single block is: $$ N_p = \frac{S_b}{S_p} $$ ### Step 2: Each pointer references exactly one data block of size $S_b$. If the index block has $N_p$ pointers, it can address $N_p$ data blocks. ### Step 3: Total addressable size is number of data blocks times block size. $$ S_{\max}^{(1)} = N_p \cdot S_b $$ ### Step 4: Substitute the value of $N_p$. $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\max}^{(1)} &= \left( \frac{S_b}{S_p} \right) \cdot S_b \\ &= \frac{S_b^2}{S_p} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Numerical check with $S_b = 4096$ bytes, $S_p = 4$ bytes. $$ \begin{aligned} N_p &= \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ pointers} \\ S_{\max}^{(1)} &= 1024 \times 4096 = 4\,194\,304 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Maximum File Size with Two-Level Index ### Step 1: Treat each entry in the outer index block as a pointer to an inner index block. A two-level index has **one outer block** and **$N_p$ inner blocks**. ### Step 2: Each inner block is itself an index of $N_p$ data-block pointers. So total data block pointers = (number of inner blocks) × (pointers per inner block) = $N_p \times N_p = N_p^2$. ### Step 3: Total data addressable = $N_p^2$ blocks × $S_b$ bytes each. $$ S_{\max}^{(2)} = N_p^2 \cdot S_b $$ ### Step 4: Substitute $N_p = S_b / S_p$. $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\max}^{(2)} &= \left( \frac{S_b}{S_p} \right)^2 \cdot S_b \\ &= \frac{S_b^3}{S_p^2} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Numerical check ($S_b = 4096$, $S_p = 4$). $$ \begin{aligned} N_p^2 &= 1024^2 = 1\,048\,576 \text{ data blocks} \\ S_{\max}^{(2)} &= 1\,048\,576 \times 4096 = 4\,294\,967\,296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Logical → Physical Block Mapping **Problem.** A file uses *indexed allocation*. The index block resides at disk block **5**. The index block contains (in order) the pointers: `[ 12, 7, 33, 19, 8, 26 ]`. Find the physical block that stores the file’s **logical block 4** (zero-based). ### Step 1: Identify the relevant pointer position. In a single-level index, the pointer for logical block $i$ is at slot $i$ of the index block. ### Step 2: Read the value at position 4. $$ \text{Index}[4] = 8 $$ ### Step 3: Conclusion. $$ \boxed{B_p = 8} $$ The OS issues one disk read to fetch block 5 (the index), reads the entry at offset $4 \times S_p$, then issues a second disk read to block 8 (the data). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Allocation Method | Criterion | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed | |---|---|---|---| | Sequential access speed | **Excellent** | Good (no seeks) | Excellent | | Random access speed | **Excellent** | Poor (must walk) | **Excellent** | | External fragmentation | **Yes (severe)** | None | None | | Internal fragmentation | Only last block | Pointer overhead | Index block overhead | | Supports file growth easily | Poor (may need move) | **Excellent** | Good (alloc new + update index) | | Space overhead per file | Just start+length | Pointer per block | One index block minimum | | Robustness to bad blocks | Tolerated | **Fragile** (one bad ptr loses rest) | Tolerated (recoverable via index) | | Implementation complexity | Low | Low–Medium | Medium–High | | Example real system | CD-ROM ISO 9660 | Old FAT, simple FS | UNIX inodes, NTFS | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture of File System Modules ```mermaid flowchart TD userApp["USER APPLICATION
open read write close"] --> vfsLayer["VFS / FILE SYSTEM INTERFACE
system call dispatcher"] vfsLayer --> dirMod["DIRECTORY MODULE
implements lookup insert delete"] dirMod --> allocMod["ALLOCATION MODULE
picks contiguous linked or indexed"] allocMod --> freeSpace["FREE SPACE MANAGER
bitmap or free list"] freeSpace --> diskDrv["DISK DRIVER
issues read write requests"] diskDrv --> hwDisk["PHYSICAL DISK
platters or NAND flash"] vfsLayer --> bufferCache["BUFFER CACHE
in memory block cache"] bufferCache --> diskDrv dirMod --> bufferCache allocMod --> bufferCache ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid — Contiguous Allocation Disk Map ```mermaid flowchart LR diskBlk["DISK BLOCKS 0 TO 199"] diskBlk --> fA["FILE A
blocks 2 to 9"] diskBlk --> fB["FILE B
blocks 11 to 14"] diskBlk --> fC["FILE C
blocks 18 to 25"] diskBlk --> fD["FILE D
blocks 27 to 31"] fA -.-> dirA["DIRECTORY ENTRY A
start 2 length 8"] fB -.-> dirB["DIRECTORY ENTRY B
start 11 length 4"] fC -.-> dirC["DIRECTORY ENTRY C
start 18 length 8"] fD -.-> dirD["DIRECTORY ENTRY D
start 27 length 5"] dirA --> gap1["HOLE blocks 0 1 10"] dirB --> gap2["HOLE blocks 15 16 17"] dirC --> gap3["HOLE block 26"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid — Linked Allocation Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR dirEnt["DIRECTORY ENTRY
file X start 9"] dirEnt --> blk9["BLOCK 9
data part 1 pointer to 16"] blk9 --> blk16["BLOCK 16
data part 2 pointer to 1"] blk16 --> blk1["BLOCK 1
data part 3 pointer to NIL"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid — Single-Level Indexed Allocation ```mermaid flowchart LR dirEnt["DIRECTORY ENTRY
file Y index at 4"] dirEnt --> idxBlk["INDEX BLOCK 4
table of pointers"] idxBlk --> p0["ptr 0 data at 7"] idxBlk --> p1["ptr 1 data at 12"] idxBlk --> p2["ptr 2 data at 33"] idxBlk --> p3["ptr 3 data at 19"] idxBlk --> pN["ptr N data at ..."] ``` --- ## 4.5 Mermaid — Two-Level Indexed (UNIX-style) Inode ```mermaid flowchart TD dirEntry["DIRECTORY ENTRY
inode number 217"] dirEntry --> inode["INODE 217
metadata plus pointers"] inode --> d0["direct 0"] inode --> d1["direct 1"] inode --> d2["direct 2"] inode --> d3["direct 3"] inode --> d4["direct 4"] inode --> d5["direct 5"] inode --> d6["direct 6"] inode --> d7["direct 7"] inode --> d8["direct 8"] inode --> d9["direct 9"] inode --> si["SINGLE INDIRECT
points to index block A"] si --> blkA["INDEX BLOCK A
1024 pointers to data"] inode --> di["DOUBLE INDIRECT
points to block of index blocks"] di --> lvl2["INDEX BLOCKS lvl2
each holds 1024 pointers to index blocks"] lvl2 --> inner["INNER INDEX BLOCKS
each holds 1024 data pointers"] inode --> ti["TRIPLE INDIRECT
for very large files"] si --> dataA["DATA BLOCKS A1 A2 ... A1024"] inner --> dataB["DATA BLOCKS B1 B2 ... B1M"] ti --> dataC["DATA BLOCKS C1 ... C1G"] ``` --- ## 4.6 Mermaid — Linear List vs Hash Table vs B-Tree Directory ```mermaid flowchart TD userReq["USER REQUESTS FILE quiz.txt"] userReq --> m1{"IMPLEMENTATION METHOD"} m1 --> lin["LINEAR LIST
scan slot 0 1 2 ... n minus 1"] lin --> linRes["cost O of n
disk seeks per lookup"] m1 --> hash["HASH TABLE
compute h quiz.txt equals 7"] hash --> bucket["jump to bucket 7
follow chain if collision"] bucket --> hashRes["average cost O of 1
worst case O of n"] m1 --> btree["B TREE
root node compare keys"] btree --> child["descend child node
repeat until leaf"] child --> btreeRes["cost O of log n
balanced and sorted"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Linked Allocation and Indexed Allocation of files. Mention one advantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Linked Allocation | Indexed Allocation | |---|---|---| | **Pointer storage** | Pointer to next block is stored inside each data block. | All pointers are stored together in a dedicated index block. | | **Directory entry stores** | Start block number only. | Index block number only. | | **Random access** | Poor — must walk the chain. | Excellent — direct lookup in $O(1)$. | | **Space overhead** | One pointer per data block. | One full index block (wasted for tiny files). | | **Advantage** | No external fragmentation; easy to extend. | Direct random access; no external fragmentation. | *(1 mark for difference, 1 mark each for advantages = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is a File Allocation Table (FAT)? How does it improve upon simple linked allocation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **File Allocation Table (FAT)** is a centralized array, kept in main memory, where each entry $FAT[i]$ holds the block number that follows block $i$ for a given file. It is essentially a *linked allocation* with all the scattered next-pointers pulled out and placed in a single RAM-resident table indexed by block number. **Improvement over simple linked allocation:** - The chain pointers no longer have to be fetched from disk (one by one) — they are read from memory, dramatically speeding up traversal. - Random access is faster: to reach block $k$, the OS follows $FAT[start], FAT[FAT[start]], \ldots$ in memory. - Bad-pointer faults are easier to detect (centralized invalid entries). *(1 mark definition, 2 marks improvement = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do not** confuse *directory implementation* with *file allocation*. The first is *how file metadata is stored*; the second is *how file data is stored*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this mix-up. > - In numerical problems, **always state your assumptions** ($S_b$, $S_p$, $N_p$) before substituting. Skipping this loses 1 mark. > - When asked for “pros and cons,” *give at least two of each*. A single advantage is considered incomplete. > - Always draw a **small diagram** for allocation questions; the KTU marking scheme awards 1–2 marks for a labelled sketch. --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** ### *(a)* Explain the three common schemes of **Directory Implementation** with their advantages and disadvantages. *(7 marks)* ### *(b)* A file system uses **indexed allocation** with a two-level index. The block size is **2 KB** and each disk pointer occupies **8 bytes**. Calculate the maximum file size. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) The three common directory implementation schemes are: **1. Linear List (3 marks for description + pros/cons)** - A simple, sequential list of directory entries, one per file. - **Search:** O(n) — must scan the list name by name. - **Insertion:** O(n) — must check for duplicate name (another scan) plus append/sort. - **Deletion:** O(n) — find entry, mark free (or compact). - **Advantage:** trivial to implement; low memory overhead; works for small directories. - **Disadvantage:** slow for large directories; insertion of duplicate name check is expensive. **2. Hash Table (2 marks)** - A hash function $h(name) \rightarrow slot$ maps file names to slots in a fixed table. - Collisions resolved by **chaining** (linked list per bucket) or **open addressing**. - **Search/Insertion/Deletion:** average $O(1)$; worst case $O(n)$ under heavy collision. - **Advantage:** extremely fast lookups for fixed-size directories. - **Disadvantage:** fixed size; rehashing required on growth; collision handling adds complexity. **3. Tree-Based (Hierarchical) (2 marks)** - Directories form a **hierarchy** (root → subdirectories → files), the standard Unix/Windows model. - The on-disk tree is typically a **B-Tree** to keep disk accesses low. - **Search/Insertion/Deletion:** $O(\log n)$ — balanced tree traversal. - **Advantage:** scalable to millions of entries; supports sorted listing; hierarchical organization mirrors user mental model. - **Disadvantage:** most complex to implement; B-Tree rebalancing overhead. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** - Block size $S_b = 2\,\text{KB} = 2048$ bytes - Pointer size $S_p = 8$ bytes **Step 1: Number of pointers per index block.** $$ N_p = \frac{S_b}{S_p} = \frac{2048}{8} = 256 $$ **Step 2: In a two-level index, total data block pointers = $N_p \times N_p$.** $$ \text{Total pointers} = 256 \times 256 = 65\,536 $$ **Step 3: Maximum file size = total pointers × block size.** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\max} &= 65\,536 \times 2048 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 134\,217\,728 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 128 \text{ MB} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification using the formula:** $$ S_{\max}^{(2)} = \frac{S_b^3}{S_p^2} = \frac{2048^3}{8^2} = \frac{8\,589\,934\,592}{64} = 134\,217\,728 \text{ bytes} = 128 \text{ MB} $$ **Conclusion:** The file system can address files up to **128 MB** with a two-level index. --- ### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** ### *(a)* Describe **Contiguous Allocation** and **Linked Allocation** methods. Compare them on the basis of *access time, fragmentation, and support for file growth.* *(7 marks)* ### *(b)* Consider a disk with **4096-byte** blocks. A file uses **contiguous allocation** starting at block **100** and has a length of **6 blocks**. List the physical block numbers occupied. If logical block 5 is accessed, what is the physical block number? *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Contiguous Allocation (3 marks)** - Each file occupies a set of *consecutive* disk blocks. - The directory stores: `(starting_block, length)`. - Logical block $i$ resides at physical block $\text{start} + i$. - **Pros:** excellent sequential & random access (pure arithmetic, no seeks); very simple. - **Cons:** suffers from *external fragmentation*; growing a file is hard — if next blocks are occupied, the file must be moved; final block may have *internal fragmentation* (unused tail). **Linked Allocation (3 marks)** - File blocks can be scattered anywhere on the disk. - Each block contains: `[ data | next_pointer ]`. - The directory stores only the `start_block`; the rest is found by following pointers. - **Pros:** *no external fragmentation*; easy file growth (allocate a free block, link at end); easy deletion. - **Cons:** *random access is slow* — must walk the chain from the start; pointer overhead reduces usable data space; a single bad pointer corrupts the rest of the file. **Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Criterion | Contiguous | Linked | |---|---|---| | Access time | $O(1)$ — direct arithmetic | $O(n)$ — must follow chain | | External fragmentation | Yes | No | | File growth support | Poor (move required) | Excellent (just link new block) | | Pointer/space overhead | None (only start+length) | 1 pointer per data block | --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** - Block size $S_b = 4096$ bytes - Starting block $\text{start} = 100$ - File length $L = 6$ blocks **Step 1: List the physical blocks occupied.** The file occupies blocks $\text{start}, \text{start}+1, \ldots, \text{start}+L-1$: $$ \{100,\ 101,\ 102,\ 103,\ 104,\ 105\} $$ **Step 2: Compute the physical block for logical block 5 (zero-based).** Using the contiguous mapping formula: $$ B_p = B_{\text{start}} + i = 100 + 5 = 105 $$ **Step 3: Verify by listing the index.** | Logical block $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Physical block $B_p$ | 100 | 101 | 102 | 103 | 104 | **105** | **Answer:** Logical block 5 maps to physical block **105**. The OS performs just one disk read to block 105 — no chained lookup required. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] A **directory** is a mapping from *file name → metadata + location*. The directory is itself stored as a file on disk. - [x] **Three directory schemes:** Linear List (slow, simple), Hash Table (fast, fixed size), Tree (B-Tree, scalable). - [x] Modern systems (NTFS, ext4) use **B-Tree / Hashed B-Tree** directories. - [x] **Contiguous allocation** → directory entry holds `(start, length)`; physical block = `start + i`; suffers external fragmentation; best for read-only media. - [x] **Linked allocation** → directory entry holds `start`; each block has a next-pointer; supports growth easily; bad for random access. - [x] **FAT** = linked allocation with a centralized in-memory pointer table → fast traversal. - [x] **Indexed allocation** → one index block per file; directory entry holds index block number; supports $O(1)$ random access; wastes index block for small files. - [x] **UNIX inode** = multi-level indexed (12 direct + single-indirect + double-indirect + triple-indirect). - [x] **Block size $S_b$**, **pointer size $S_p$**, **$N_p = S_b / S_p$** — memorize and apply. - [x] **Single-level max file size** $= N_p \cdot S_b$. - [x] **Two-level max file size** $= N_p^2 \cdot S_b$. - [x] With $S_b = 4\,\text{KB}$, $S_p = 4\,\text{B}$ → $N_p = 1024$ → single level = **4 MB**, double level = **4 GB**. - [x] With $S_b = 2\,\text{KB}$, $S_p = 8\,\text{B}$ → $N_p = 256$ → single level = **512 KB**, double level = **128 MB**. - [x] **Random access cost:** Contiguous = $O(1)$ · Indexed = $O(1)$ · Linked = $O(n)$. - [x] **External fragmentation:** Contiguous = YES · Linked/Indexed = NO. - [x] **Examiner mantra:** Always state assumptions, show formulas, and draw a labelled diagram for full marks.

topics.title: Storage Management: Magnetic Disks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage Management: Magnetic Disks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Magnetic Disk Storage** is a non-volatile, electromechanical secondary storage device that uses rapidly rotating rigid platters coated with magnetic material to store and retrieve digital data. The Operating System (OS) manages this hardware through a set of policies, algorithms, and abstractions collectively known as the **Storage Management Subsystem** (also called the **I/O Management Subsystem**). In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this encompasses disk geometry, formatting, scheduling algorithms, swap space management, and **RAID architectures**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> "Storage Management – Mass-storage structure; Disk structure, Disk scheduling algorithms (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK); Swap-space management; RAID structure (RAID levels 0 through 6)."

### 1.2 Physical Anatomy of a Magnetic Disk
A hard disk drive (HDD) consists of one or more **platters** mounted on a common spindle. Each platter has two surfaces (top and bottom), and each surface is accessed by a **read/write head** attached to a **disk arm**. All arms move together as a single assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **Track**: A concentric circle on a platter surface where data is stored.
> - **Sector**: The smallest addressable unit on a track (typically **512 bytes** historically, **4096 bytes** in modern Advanced Format drives).
> - **Cylinder**: A set of all tracks that are at the same distance from the center on all platter surfaces. Reading/writing a cylinder avoids arm movement.
> - **Spindle Speed**: Rotational speed measured in **RPM** (e.g., 5400 RPM, 7200 RPM, 15000 RPM for enterprise drives).
> - **Seek Time**: Time taken for the disk arm to position the head over the correct track. Typical range: **3 ms to 10 ms**.
> - **Rotational Latency**: Time waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the head. Average = $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{RPM} \times 60$ seconds.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy
Think of a magnetic disk as a **multi-storey circular library**:
- The **platters** are the floors.
- The **tracks** are the circular bookshelves on each floor.
- The **sectors** are individual books on the shelves.
- The **read/write head** is a librarian who rides a single elevator (the **disk arm**) up and down.
- To fetch a "book" (sector), the librarian must:
  1. Move the elevator to the correct floor (**seek time**).
  2. Wait for the right shelf section to spin into view (**rotational latency**).
  3. Grab the book (**transfer time**).

The OS's job is to issue fetch requests (I/O requests) in an order that minimizes the total travel of the librarian — this is exactly what **disk scheduling algorithms** accomplish.

### 1.4 The I/O Stack Context
At a high level, the OS interacts with the disk through layers:
1. **Application** issues a `read()` / `write()` system call.
2. **File System** translates the file name to logical block numbers.
3. **Scheduler / Device Driver** reorders requests for efficiency.
4. **Disk Controller** translates logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical (CHS — Cylinder-Head-Sector).
5. **Physical Disk** performs the electromechanical I/O.

> [!TIP]
> Modern disks hide the physical geometry behind **Logical Block Addressing (LBA)**, where every sector is given a single linear address starting from 0. The disk's internal firmware handles the CHS translation. KTU questions often test this concept under "modern disk architecture."

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Disk Performance — The Three Time Components
Every disk I/O operation incurs three additive delays:

#### 2.1.1 Seek Time ($T_{seek}$)
The time required to move the disk arm across tracks. It is a function of the **distance** traveled.

- $T_{seek} = a + b \cdot d$
  - $a$ = arm acceleration/settling constant (ms)
  - $b$ = per-track traversal time
  - $d$ = number of tracks crossed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU simplification is: **Average Seek Time ≈ $\frac{1}{3} \times$ Maximum Seek Time**, assuming a uniform distribution of cylinder requests.

#### 2.1.2 Rotational Latency ($T_{rot}$)
- $T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{RPM}$ seconds
- For a **7200 RPM** disk: $T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{7200} = 4.16$ ms

#### 2.1.3 Transfer Time ($T_{transfer}$)
The time to actually read/write the bytes once the head is positioned.
- $T_{transfer} = \frac{\text{Bytes to transfer}}{\text{Bytes per track}} \times \frac{60}{RPM}$

#### 2.1.4 Total Access Time
$$T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$$

### 2.2 Disk Scheduling Algorithms — Deep Theory

#### (a) FCFS — First-Come, First-Served
- **Logic**: Process requests in the exact order of arrival.
- **Pros**: Fair, simple, no starvation.
- **Cons**: Wildly inefficient — can produce massive head travel (think: "library" elevator bouncing between 1st and 50th floor repeatedly).

#### (b) SSTF — Shortest Seek Time First
- **Logic**: From the current head position, always service the *nearest* request (shortest seek distance).
- **Pros**: Significantly reduces average seek time compared to FCFS.
- **Cons**: **Starvation** is possible — far-away requests may wait indefinitely.

#### (c) SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)
- **Logic**: The head moves in one direction servicing all pending requests until it hits the **last cylinder**, then reverses.
- **Pros**: Bounded waiting time; low variance; good throughput.
- **Cons**: The middle cylinders (the most recently serviced area) have to wait while the head travels to the edge before reversing — biased behavior.

#### (d) C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)
- **Logic**: Head moves in one direction servicing requests. When it reaches the end, it **jumps back to the beginning (0)** without servicing any request, then continues.
- **Pros**: Provides a **more uniform wait time** than SCAN — every request gets a deterministic max-wait.
- **Cons**: More head movement wasted on the return jump (but no data transfer during jump).

#### (e) LOOK and C-LOOK
- **Logic**: Like SCAN/C-SCAN, but the head **reverses at the last request** instead of going all the way to the disk edge.
- This is the **practically implemented** version of SCAN/C-SCAN in real OS kernels (Linux, Windows).

#### (f) FSCAN and N-Step SCAN
- **Logic**: Maintain **two request queues**. The first is the active queue being processed. New requests arriving during the current sweep are queued in the second. When the current sweep completes, the queues are swapped.
- **Pros**: Avoids "arm-stickiness" (where new requests arriving near the current head can monopolize the arm in SSTF).

### 2.3 RAID — Redundant Array of Independent Disks

RAID combines multiple physical disks into a logical unit for **performance, reliability, or both**. It uses two key techniques:
- **Striping**: Spreading data across multiple disks (improves throughput).
- **Mirroring / Parity**: Storing redundant data (improves reliability).

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---------|---------------------|------|
| 1 | Rotational Latency | $T_{rot} = \frac{30}{RPM}$ | ms |
| 2 | Transfer Time (full track) | $T_{trans} = \frac{60}{RPM}$ | ms |
| 3 | Access Time | $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{trans}$ | ms |
| 4 | Avg Seek Time | $\approx \frac{1}{3} T_{max\,seek}$ | ms |
| 5 | Effective disk transfer rate | $R = \frac{\text{Bytes per track}}{T_{rot} \times 2}$ | B/s |
| 6 | Sector address | LBA = $\text{Cylinder} \times \text{Heads} \times \text{Sectors/track}$ + $\text{Head} \times \text{Sectors/track}$ + $\text{Sector}-1$ | — |
| 7 | RAID 0 capacity | $N \times D$ (no redundancy) | — |
| 8 | RAID 1 capacity | $(N/2) \times D$ (mirrored) | — |
| 9 | RAID 4/5 capacity | $(N-1) \times D$ (1 parity disk equivalent) | — |
| 10 | RAID 6 capacity | $(N-2) \times D$ (2 parity) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board frequently frames numerical questions around: *"Given a disk with $X$ RPM, $Y$ ms average seek, and $Z$ bytes/sector, find the access time for reading $K$ sectors."* Master formula 3 above.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Database engines** (e.g., PostgreSQL) tune disk schedulers to favor reads over writes.
- **Cloud storage** systems use RAID-6/RAID-10 for data centers to tolerate concurrent disk failures.
- **Solid State Drives (SSDs)** have replaced HDDs in most consumer laptops, but HDDs remain dominant in **bulk archival storage** due to cost-per-terabyte.
- The **Linux kernel** uses the **CFQ, Deadline, and NOOP schedulers** for I/O — analogous in spirit to disk scheduling.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

### 3.1 Worked Problem 1: Computing Total Access Time (Standard KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A disk has the following specifications:
- Spindle speed: **5400 RPM**
- Average seek time: **8 ms**
- Bytes per sector: **512 bytes**
- Sectors per track: **200**

**Find:** The total average access time to read 1 sector of data.

**Step 1 — Identify the three time components.**

For reading a single sector, the transfer time covers only that one sector (not the full track).

**Step 2 — Calculate Average Rotational Latency.**

$$T_{rot} = \frac{30}{RPM} = \frac{30}{5400}$$

$$T_{rot} = 0.00555 \text{ s} = 5.55 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Transfer Time for 1 sector.**

Full track rotation time:
$$T_{full\,track} = \frac{60}{5400} = 11.11 \text{ ms}$$

Time per sector (one sector = 1/200 of the track):
$$T_{transfer} = \frac{11.11}{200} = 0.0555 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Sum up the three components.**

$$T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$$

$$T_{access} = 8 + 5.55 + 0.0555$$

$$\boxed{T_{access} \approx 13.61 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The **transfer time is negligible** compared to seek and rotational latency. This is why disk scheduling (which only affects seek time) yields such large performance gains.

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 2: Disk Scheduling — SSTF / SCAN

**Problem:** A disk has 200 cylinders (0 to 199). The disk head is currently at cylinder **53**. The queue of pending requests, in order, is:
**98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**

Compute the total head movement for: **(a) FCFS, (b) SSTF, (c) SCAN (moving toward 199 first), (d) C-SCAN**.

#### Part (a) — FCFS

Starting at 53, service in the given order:
$$|53-98| + |98-183| + |183-37| + |37-122| + |122-14| + |14-124| + |124-65| + |65-67|$$

Step-by-step sum:
$$45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2$$

$$\boxed{\text{FCFS Total Head Movement} = 640 \text{ cylinders}}$$

#### Part (b) — SSTF

Always pick the closest unserviced request from current position.

- Start at **53**. Closest: **65** (dist 12). Move: $53 \to 65$ = 12.
- At **65**. Closest: **67** (dist 2). Move: $65 \to 67$ = 2.
- At **67**. Closest: **37** (dist 30). Move: $67 \to 37$ = 30.
- At **37**. Closest: **14** (dist 23). Move: $37 \to 14$ = 23.
- At **14**. Closest: **98** (dist 84). Move: $14 \to 98$ = 84.
- At **98**. Closest: **122** (dist 24). Move: $98 \to 122$ = 24.
- At **122**. Closest: **124** (dist 2). Move: $122 \to 124$ = 2.
- At **124**. Closest: **183** (dist 59). Move: $124 \to 183$ = 59.

Sum:
$$12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 = 236$$

$$\boxed{\text{SSTF Total Head Movement} = 236 \text{ cylinders}}$$

#### Part (c) — SCAN (toward 199 first)

Move the head in the **increasing** direction, servicing all requests $\geq 53$, then go to 199 and reverse.

Sorted requests: **14, 37, 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183**

- $53 \to 199$ (cover 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183): $199 - 53 = 146$
- $199 \to 0$ (cover 37, 14): $199 - 0 = 199$

Total:
$$146 + 199 = 345$$

$$\boxed{\text{SCAN Total Head Movement} = 345 \text{ cylinders}}$$

#### Part (d) — C-SCAN

Move head in the **increasing** direction, service all requests $\geq 53$, then **jump to 0** (no servicing), then continue forward servicing remaining.

- $53 \to 199$ (service 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183): $199 - 53 = 146$
- $199 \to 0$ (jump, no service): $199$
- $0 \to 37$ (service 14, 37): $37 - 0 = 37$

Total:
$$146 + 199 + 37 = 382$$

$$\boxed{\text{C-SCAN Total Head Movement} = 382 \text{ cylinders}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive result**: C-SCAN has a *higher* total head movement than SCAN! This is because the "return jump" travels the entire disk. C-SCAN's real advantage is **uniformity of wait time**, not raw distance.

---

### 3.3 Worked Problem 3: RAID Capacity Calculation

**Problem:** A RAID system uses **8 disks**, each of size **1 TB**. Calculate the usable capacity for:
**(a) RAID 0, (b) RAID 1, (c) RAID 5, (d) RAID 6**.

#### (a) RAID 0 — Striping, no parity
$$\text{Capacity} = N \times D = 8 \times 1 = 8 \text{ TB}$$
No fault tolerance.

#### (b) RAID 1 — Mirroring
For N disks in RAID 1, usable capacity is $N/2 \times D$:
$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{8}{2} \times 1 = 4 \text{ TB}$$

#### (c) RAID 5 — Striping + distributed parity (1 parity equivalent)
$$\text{Capacity} = (N-1) \times D = 7 \times 1 = 7 \text{ TB}$$
Can tolerate 1 disk failure.

#### (d) RAID 6 — Striping + double distributed parity
$$\text{Capacity} = (N-2) \times D = 6 \times 1 = 6 \text{ TB}$$
Can tolerate 2 simultaneous disk failures.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU trap:** Students often confuse RAID 1 (which needs pairs) with RAID 10. RAID 1+0 (RAID 10) = mirror first, then stripe — best of both worlds. RAID 0+1 = stripe first, then mirror. The two have different failure characteristics!

---

### 3.4 Worked Problem 4: Swap Space Sizing

**Problem:** A system has **4 GB** of physical RAM. Recommended swap space policy is:
- If RAM $\leq 2$ GB: swap = $2 \times$ RAM
- If $2 <$ RAM $\leq 8$ GB: swap = equal to RAM
- If RAM $> 8$ GB: swap = $0.5 \times$ RAM

**Find:** Recommended swap size.

Since 4 GB falls in the middle range:
$$\text{Swap} = 1 \times 4 \text{ GB} = 4 \text{ GB}$$

The swap partition typically lives on a **dedicated disk partition** (raw, no file system) for fastest access.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Magnetic Disk

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef hw fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef plat fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000

    spindle[Spindle Motor]:::hw
    platter1[Platter 1]:::plat
    platter2[Platter 2]:::plat
    platter3[Platter 3]:::plat

    arm[Disk Arm Assembly]:::hw
    head1[Read Write Head Top 1]:::hw
    head2[Read Write Head Bottom 1]:::hw
    head3[Read Write Head Top 2]:::hw
    head4[Read Write Head Bottom 2]:::hw

    actuator[Voice Coil Actuator]:::hw
    controller[Disk Controller Firmware]:::ctrl
    interface[SATA NVMe Interface]:::ctrl

    spindle --> platter1
    spindle --> platter2
    spindle --> platter3

    arm --> head1
    arm --> head2
    arm --> head3
    arm --> head4

    actuator --> arm
    arm --> controller
    controller --> interface
```

### 4.2 Disk Geometry — Track, Sector, Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef geo fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000

    cylinder[Concept Cylinder]:::geo
    track[Single Track on Platter]:::geo
    sector[Single Sector 512 or 4096 bytes]:::geo
    headR[Read Write Head]:::geo

    cylinder --> track
    track --> sector
    headR --> track
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading a cylinder is optimal**: When the OS wants to read consecutive data spanning multiple platters, it can keep the heads stationary and read vertically through all platters — no extra seek time. File systems exploit this by placing related blocks on the same cylinder.

### 4.3 I/O Request Flow Through the OS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef app fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef fs fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef sch fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000

    app[User Application]:::app
    syscall[Read Write System Call]:::app
    fs[File System VFS Layer]:::fs
    buf[Buffer Cache]:::fs
    sched[Disk Scheduler FCFS SSTF SCAN etc]:::sch
    drv[Device Driver]:::drv
    ctrl[Disk Controller]:::drv
    hdd[Physical Magnetic Disk]:::hw

    app --> syscall --> fs --> buf --> sched --> drv --> ctrl --> hdd
    hdd -. feedback .-> sched
```

### 4.4 RAID Level Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef r0 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    classDef r1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef r5 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef r6 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000

    r0[RAID 0 Striping Only]:::r0
    r1[RAID 1 Mirroring]:::r1
    r5[RAID 5 Striping plus Distributed Parity]:::r5
    r6[RAID 6 Striping plus Double Parity]:::r6

    r0 -->|no redundancy| r0n[Capacity = N times D]
    r1 -->|1 copy| r1n[Capacity = N by 2 times D]
    r5 -->|1 disk fault tolerance| r5n[Capacity = N minus 1 times D]
    r6 -->|2 disk fault tolerance| r6n[Capacity = N minus 2 times D]
```

### 4.5 State Machine of the Disk Scheduler

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> RequestArrived: I O request enters queue
    RequestArrived --> SelectingNext: Pick request per algorithm
    SelectingNext --> HeadMoving: Issue command to drive
    HeadMoving --> Servicing: Head over target track
    Servicing --> Idle: Transfer complete
    Servicing --> RequestArrived: New request during transfer
    HeadMoving --> RequestArrived: Preemption in some schedulers
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define seek time and rotational latency. Why are they important for disk performance?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Seek time** is the time required for the disk arm to move the read/write head from its current position to the track containing the desired data. It is a function of the number of cylinders the head has to cross. Typical average seek time is **3 to 10 ms** in modern drives.
- **Rotational latency** is the time spent waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head once the head is positioned over the correct track. Its average value is $\frac{30}{RPM}$ ms (half a rotation).
- Both are mechanical delays, **not** data transfer delays. They dominate disk I/O cost — together they form **> 99%** of the typical access time. Optimizing them is the entire goal of disk scheduling algorithms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse rotational latency with transfer time. **Latency = waiting** for the sector to come around. **Transfer = actually reading** the bytes. These are two distinct phases.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between FCFS, SSTF, and SCAN disk scheduling algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FCFS | SSTF | SCAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Order | Arrival order | Closest first | Sweep in one direction |
| Starvation | No | **Yes** | No (bounded) |
| Throughput | Poor | Good | Very good |
| Variance | High | Moderate | Low (uniform) |
| Implementation | Easiest | Moderate | Moderate |

**Key insight:** SCAN is the practical default in operating systems because it offers the best balance between throughput and fairness.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a) Explain the different levels of RAID with neat diagrams. Compare their performance, capacity, and fault tolerance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) is a technology that combines multiple physical disk drives into a single logical unit to improve performance, reliability, or both. There are seven standard RAID levels (0 through 6), plus nested levels (10, 01, 50, 60).

**RAID 0 — Striping (No Redundancy)**
- Data is split into blocks and distributed ("striped") across all disks.
- **Capacity**: $N \times D$ (e.g., 4 × 1 TB = 4 TB).
- **Fault tolerance**: **None**. If any single disk fails, the entire array fails.
- **Performance**: Excellent for both reads and writes (parallelism).
- **Use case**: Video editing, scratch space.

**RAID 1 — Mirroring**
- Every disk has an identical "mirror" copy.
- **Capacity**: $(N/2) \times D$ (e.g., 4 × 1 TB = 2 TB).
- **Fault tolerance**: Excellent — can survive $N/2$ disk failures (if they are the right ones).
- **Performance**: Reads can be parallelized; writes are slower (must write to both).
- **Use case**: OS drives, critical databases.

**RAID 2 — Bit-level striping with Hamming code**
- Obsolete. Uses Hamming error-correcting codes across disks. Modern disks have ECC built-in, so this level is rarely used.

**RAID 3 — Byte-level striping with dedicated parity disk**
- Data is striped at the byte level; a single disk stores parity.
- **Capacity**: $(N-1) \times D$.
- **Fault tolerance**: 1 disk failure.
- **Drawback**: Parity disk is a bottleneck for small writes.

**RAID 4 — Block-level striping with dedicated parity disk**
- Like RAID 3, but stripes at the block level (better for large I/O).
- Same capacity and fault tolerance as RAID 3.
- Still suffers the parity disk bottleneck.

**RAID 5 — Block-level striping with distributed parity**
- Parity is distributed across all disks (no single parity bottleneck).
- **Capacity**: $(N-1) \times D$.
- **Fault tolerance**: 1 disk failure.
- **Read**: Very fast (parallel). **Write**: Moderate (4 I/O operations per logical write — read old data, read old parity, write new data, write new parity).
- **Use case**: General-purpose servers, file servers.

**RAID 6 — Block-level striping with double distributed parity**
- Uses two independent parity schemes (e.g., P + Q).
- **Capacity**: $(N-2) \times D$.
- **Fault tolerance**: **2 simultaneous** disk failures.
- **Use case**: Mission-critical systems, large-capacity arrays where rebuild time is long.

**Nested Levels:**
- **RAID 10 (1+0)**: Mirror pairs first, then stripe. Best performance + best reliability. Capacity = $N/2 \times D$. Used in high-end databases.
- **RAID 50**: Stripe of RAID 5 sets. Capacity = $(N - k) \times D$ where $k$ = number of RAID 5 sets.

**Comparison Table:**

| Level | Min Disks | Capacity | Fault Tolerance | Read Perf | Write Perf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2 | $N \times D$ | 0 | Excellent | Excellent |
| 1 | 2 | $(N/2) \times D$ | $N/2$ | Good | Fair |
| 5 | 3 | $(N-1) \times D$ | 1 | Excellent | Fair |
| 6 | 4 | $(N-2) \times D$ | 2 | Excellent | Poor |
| 10 | 4 | $(N/2) \times D$ | Up to $N/2$ | Excellent | Good |

---

**(b) A disk rotates at 7200 RPM, has an average seek time of 9 ms, and 600 sectors per track of 512 bytes each. Find the total time to read 20 sectors that are randomly distributed on the disk. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_{RPM} = 7200$
- $T_{seek} = 9$ ms
- Sectors per track = 600
- Sector size = 512 bytes
- Total sectors to read = 20

**Step 1: Rotational Latency (full half-rotation)**
$$T_{rot} = \frac{30}{7200} = 4.17 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Transfer Time per sector**
Full track rotation time:
$$T_{track} = \frac{60}{7200} = 8.33 \text{ ms}$$

Time per sector:
$$T_{per\,sector} = \frac{8.33}{600} = 0.0139 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: For 20 randomly distributed sectors**
Since the 20 sectors are **randomly scattered**, each sector requires a **full seek + full rotational latency** before its own transfer. There is no sequential benefit.

Time per sector:
$$T_{per\,sector} = 9 + 4.17 + 0.0139 = 13.18 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Total time for 20 random sectors**
$$T_{total} = 20 \times 13.18 = 263.6 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 263.6 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common mistake is to treat "20 sectors" as if they are consecutive on the same track. If the problem says "randomly distributed" or "scattered across the disk," assume worst-case (one seek + one rotational latency per sector). Only when sectors are **contiguous** on the same track can you amortize the seek and rotational latency.

---

#### **QUESTION B** (Alternative Choice for the same 14 marks)

**(a) Explain FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK disk scheduling algorithms with a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a disk with 200 cylinders (0 to 199). Head is at cylinder **53**. Request queue: **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**.

#### (1) FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)
Requests served in arrival order: 98 → 183 → 37 → 122 → 14 → 124 → 65 → 67.
- $53 \to 98 \to 183 \to 37 \to 122 \to 14 \to 124 \to 65 \to 67$
- Distances: $45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 = 640$ cylinders.

#### (2) SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)
Always pick the nearest pending request. As computed in Section 3.2: **236 cylinders**.
Order of service: 65, 67, 37, 14, 98, 122, 124, 183.

#### (3) SCAN (Elevator)
Head moves to one end, then reverses. Assuming initial direction is **increasing**:
- $53 \to 199$ (covers 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183) = 146
- $199 \to 0$ (covers 37, 14) = 199
- **Total = 345 cylinders**.

#### (4) C-SCAN
Head moves in one direction, then **jumps back** to 0 without servicing, then continues:
- $53 \to 199$ = 146
- $199 \to 0$ (jump) = 199
- $0 \to 37$ (service 14, 37) = 37
- **Total = 382 cylinders**.

#### (5) LOOK
Like SCAN, but reverses at the **last request** (not at the disk edge):
- $53 \to 183$ (covers 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183) = 130
- $183 \to 14$ (covers 37, 14) = 169
- **Total = 299 cylinders**.

#### (6) C-LOOK
Like C-SCAN, but reverses at the last request and jumps back to the **first request** (not to 0):
- $53 \to 183$ = 130
- $183 \to 14$ (jump back) = 169
- **Total = 299 cylinders**.

**Summary of total head movements:**

| Algorithm | Head Movement |
|---|---|
| FCFS | 640 |
| SSTF | 236 |
| SCAN | 345 |
| C-SCAN | 382 |
| LOOK | 299 |
| C-LOOK | 299 |

**Conclusion:** SSTF has the lowest head movement, but it can cause starvation. **LOOK/C-LOOK** are the practical sweet spot — they offer SCAN-like fairness with SSTF-like efficiency.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a magnetic disk. Discuss the concept of swap-space management. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Magnetic Disk Structure (as shown in Section 4.1):**
- One or more **platters** mounted on a central **spindle**.
- Each platter has two surfaces, each with a **read/write head**.
- Heads are attached to a **disk arm** moved by a **voice coil actuator**.
- The disk rotates at a constant angular velocity (**CAV**).
- Data is organized into **tracks** (concentric circles) and **sectors** (arcs of a track).
- All tracks at the same radius across platters form a **cylinder**.
- Modern disks expose a flat **Logical Block Address (LBA)** space to the OS; the firmware handles CHS translation.

**Performance Zones:**
- The outer tracks are longer than the inner tracks.
- **Zone Bit Recording (ZBR)** groups tracks into zones; outer zones have more sectors per track. This increases total capacity by ~20-30% over uniform-sector recording.

**Swap-Space Management:**

Swap space is the **disk area used as an extension of physical RAM** by the OS. It allows the system to support more memory than physically installed, by moving inactive pages to disk.

**Two Implementation Strategies:**

1. **Swap Partition (Raw partition)**
   - A dedicated disk partition with no file system.
   - The OS uses it directly as an array of pages.
   - **Fastest** because no file-system overhead, no block-allocation algorithm.
   - Sizes fixed at partition creation.

2. **Swap File (Within file system)**
   - A regular file inside an existing file system.
   - Easier to create/resize.
   - Slower due to file-system metadata operations and possible fragmentation.
   - Linux/Windows support both.

**Key Concepts in Swap Management:**
- **Page-in / Page-out**: Moving pages between RAM and swap.
- **Swapping vs. Paging**: 
  - *Swapping* = entire processes moved in/out (coarse-grained, older Unix).
  - *Paging* = individual pages moved in/out (fine-grained, modern).
- **Thrashing**: When the system spends more time swapping than executing — a sign of insufficient RAM or over-aggressive multiprogramming.
- **Placement Policy**: Some kernels reserve a small swap area for the OS itself (kernel memory cannot be swapped out).

**Swap Space Sizing Rules of Thumb:**
- Old rule: swap = 2 × RAM
- Modern: swap $\approx$ equal to RAM (for systems with < 8 GB)
- For hibernation: swap $\geq$ RAM size

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Don't confuse **paging** (moving individual pages) with **segmentation** (moving variable-sized segments). The KTU 2024 syllabus treats paging as the default memory-management scheme, so when the question says "swap-space management," it is about page-level movement.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Storage Management: Magnetic Disks"**

- [ ] **Three time components of disk access**: Seek time (arm movement) + Rotational latency (sector rotation) + Transfer time (data read/write). Always sum them.
- [ ] **Rotational latency formula**: $T_{rot} = \frac{30}{RPM}$ ms (assuming average = half-rotation).
- [ ] **Disk geometry hierarchy**: Platter → Surface → Track → Sector. Cylinder = set of tracks at the same radius across platters.
- [ ] **Modern addressing**: LBA (Logical Block Address), not CHS. Disk firmware does the translation.
- [ ] **Sector size**: 512 bytes (legacy) or 4096 bytes (Advanced Format / 4Kn).
- [ ] **FCFS**: Fair but slow. **SSTF**: Fast but starves. **SCAN / LOOK**: Practical balance. **C-SCAN / C-LOOK**: Uniform wait time.
- [ ] **LOOK vs SCAN**: LOOK reverses at last request; SCAN goes to the disk edge. LOOK is the real-world default.
- [ ] **RAID 0**: Stripe only, no fault tolerance. **RAID 1**: Mirror. **RAID 5**: Stripe + 1 distributed parity. **RAID 6**: Stripe + 2 distributed parities.
- [ ] **RAID capacity formulas**: RAID 0 = N×D, RAID 1 = N/2×D, RAID 5 = (N−1)×D, RAID 6 = (N−2)×D.
- [ ] **Swap space**: Either a raw partition (faster) or a swap file (flexible). Sized approximately equal to RAM for modern systems.
- [ ] **Hot exam keywords**: *Starvation, Throughput, Average seek time, Access time, Cylinder locality, ZBR, Page-out, Thrashing, Rebuild time*.
- [ ] **Numerical must-know**: Always state units (ms vs µs), show formula substitution, and round only at the final step.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU 2024 ESE Module 4:**
> 1. Master the **disk scheduling numerical** (head movement calculation) — it appears almost every exam.
> 2. Memorize the **RAID level table** — questions often ask to "compare RAID 0, 1, 5, and 6" or "which RAID is suitable for a banking application?"
> 3. Be ready to draw the **disk geometry** (tracks, sectors, cylinder) and label the **three time components** on a graph.
> 4. Know the **swap-space sizing** rules and the difference between a **swap partition** and a **swap file**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-State Disks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid-State Disks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solid-State Disk (SSD)** is a persistent, non-volatile data storage device that uses **NAND-flash-based semiconductor memory** (typically NAND or, less commonly, NOR flash) to store data, replacing the electromechanical spinning platters and read/write heads of a traditional Hard Disk Drive (HDD). From an Operating Systems perspective, an SSD is presented to the host via standard interfaces (SATA, SAS, NVMe over PCIe) and behaves as a **block device** — identical in abstraction to a magnetic disk — but is governed internally by a **Flash Translation Layer (FTL)** firmware that manages out-of-place updates, wear-leveling, and garbage collection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, SSDs are studied as an evolution of secondary storage. The focus is on **internal architecture (dies, planes, blocks, pages)**, **performance characteristics (read/write/erase latencies)**, **the Flash Translation Layer (FTL)**, **wear leveling**, **garbage collection**, **TRIM**, and the **OS-visible performance differences** between SSDs and HDDs.

### 1.2 Physical Building Blocks

SSDs are hierarchically organized. From the chip level up:

| Level | Component | Typical Count | Function |
|-------|-----------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | SSD Package | 1 per device | Outer physical casing |
| 2 | Die (Chip) | 4–16 per package | Contains the flash memory array |
| 3 | Plane | 2–4 per die | Independent I/O unit |
| 4 | Block (Erase Block) | 1024–4096 per plane | Smallest erasable unit (**128–512 KB**) |
| 5 | Page | 64–256 per block | Smallest readable/writable unit (**4–16 KB**) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Notebook

Think of an SSD like a **library's lending notebook** rather than a physical bookshelf:

- **HDD Analogy**: A traditional HDD is like a librarian who must physically walk to a specific shelf, pull a book, and copy a page. Every access involves *mechanical motion* (seek time + rotational latency).
- **SSD Analogy**: An SSD is like a librarian who, the moment you ask for a topic, **electronically pulls up the exact scanned image** of that page on a screen. There is no walking; no spinning; no physical arm movement. The librarian keeps a **master index** (the FTL) that remembers where everything currently lives.
- **The Catch**: Because each "page" of the notebook can only be **erased a limited number of times** (like pencil lead wearing down), the librarian must constantly **rewrite old pages elsewhere and erase in batches** to make pages last longer. This is *wear-leveling* and *garbage collection*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for OS**: Unlike RAM, flash cells **must be erased before being rewritten**. The erase unit (block) is *much larger* than the write unit (page). This asymmetry is the root cause of nearly every SSD performance peculiarity the OS must handle.

### 1.4 Performance Constants (KTU High-Yield Values)

| Metric | Typical SSD Value | Typical HDD Value |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Read Latency | **~25 µs (NVMe)** to **~100 µs (SATA)** | **~5,000–10,000 µs** |
| Write Latency | **~200–500 µs** | **~5,000–10,000 µs** |
| Erase Latency | **~1,500–3,000 µs** | N/A |
| IOPS (4 KB Random Read) | **50,000 – 1,000,000+** | **~75–200** |
| Sequential Read Bandwidth | **500 MB/s (SATA) – 7,000 MB/s (NVMe Gen4)** | **~100–200 MB/s** |
| P/E (Program/Erase) Endurance | **1,000 (TLC) – 100,000 (SLC)** cycles per cell | Unlimited |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operations on NAND Flash

NAND flash supports exactly **three** primitive operations, each with very different latencies:

1. **Read** — Operates at *page granularity*. Fastest of the three.
2. **Program (Write)** — Operates at *page granularity*. Requires the page to be in an **erased state** (all bits = 1).
3. **Erase** — Operates at *block granularity*. Resets all pages in a block to the erased state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Asymmetry Rule**: 
> - Read latency $\ll$ Program latency $\ll$ Erase latency
> - A block contains **N pages** (typically 64–256), so the erase unit is **N× larger** than the read/program unit.

### 2.2 The Out-of-Place Update Problem

Because an erased page cannot be selectively re-programmed without first erasing its parent block, SSDs **cannot perform in-place updates**. The Flow:

1. OS issues a write to logical address **L**.
2. FTL maps **L** to a free physical page **P_new**.
3. Old version of **L** at **P_old** is marked **stale (invalid)**.
4. Background **garbage collector** later consolidates live pages and erases blocks.

This induces **write amplification** — the actual NAND write traffic exceeds the host-issued write traffic.

### 2.3 Flash Translation Layer (FTL)

The **FTL** is the SSD's internal firmware — its "mini-OS" — that exposes a logical block interface to the host while managing:

- **Logical-to-Physical (L2P) mapping**
- **Wear leveling** (distributing writes evenly)
- **Garbage collection** (reclaiming invalid pages)
- **Bad-block management**
- **Error correction (ECC)**

### 2.4 Wear Leveling Strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Dynamic Wear Leveling** | Selects a free block with the *lowest P/E count* for new writes | Fast, but static data never moves |
| **Static Wear Leveling** | Periodically moves **read-only/cold** data to high-wear blocks, freeing low-wear blocks for hot data | Better longevity, higher write amplification |

### 2.5 Garbage Collection (GC)

When free blocks run low, the GC process:

1. Selects a victim block containing the **fewest live pages**.
2. Copies live pages to a new free block.
3. Erases the victim block.
4. Returns it to the free pool.

> [!TIP]
> **GC Trigger**: Modern SSDs reserve **~7–28%** of raw NAND capacity as **over-provisioning (OP)** to absorb GC overhead. This OP space is *invisible* to the host but dramatically improves steady-state performance.

### 2.6 TRIM Command

The **TRIM** command (ATA) / **DEALLOCATE** (NVMe) tells the SSD that a range of LBAs is no longer in use. This allows the FTL to:

- Mark those pages invalid *proactively* (during the OS's delete, not later).
- Reduce GC overhead.
- Restore write performance that would otherwise degrade after sustained writes ("**write cliff**").

### 2.7 Write Amplification Factor (WAF)

A critical metric for SSD performance and endurance:

$$\text{WAF} = \frac{\text{Actual NAND writes (bytes)}}{\text{Host writes (bytes)}}$$

An ideal SSD has WAF = 1; typical consumer SSDs in steady state achieve **WAF ≈ 1.5 – 3.0**.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Notes |
|------------------|------------|-------|
| Write Amplification Factor | $\text{WAF} = \dfrac{\text{NAND writes}}{\text{Host writes}}$ | Ideal = 1; Lower is better |
| SSD Endurance (TBW) | $\text{TBW} = \dfrac{\text{Capacity (TB)} \times \text{P/E cycles}}{\text{WAF}}$ | Total Bytes Written |
| Effective P/E Cycles per Cell | $\text{Effective P/E} = \dfrac{\text{Rated P/E}}{\text{WAF}}$ | Account for WAF |
| Approx. IOPS | $\text{IOPS} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Average Latency (s)}}$ | For single-queue saturation |
| Mean Time Between Failures (indicator) | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{SSD}} \approx 1.5\text{–}2.5 \text{ million hours}$ | Far higher than HDDs |
| Page-to-Block ratio | $N_{\text{pages/block}}$ | Typically 64–256 |
| Raw-to-User Capacity | $\text{User} = \text{Raw} \times (1 - \text{OP\%}) \times \text{Format\%}$ | OP = over-provisioning |
| Random Read Time (approx.) | $T_r \approx 25\text{–}100\ \mu s$ | SATA–NVMe range |
| Erase Time (approx.) | $T_e \approx 1.5\text{–}3\ \text{ms}$ | Per block |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Databases (OLTP)**: SSDs eliminate rotational latency, yielding **50–1000×** IOPS improvement for transaction processing.
- **Boot & Application Launch**: Random read dominance makes SSDs ~10× faster in real-world responsiveness.
- **AI/ML Training**: SSDs serve as fast checkpoints for large model snapshots where HDD bandwidth is insufficient.
- **Edge & Embedded Systems**: Low-power, no-moving-parts SSDs dominate IoT and automotive systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How WAF Affects SSD Lifetime

Let us derive the relationship between host write traffic and the total bytes physically written to NAND.

**Given:**
- SSD usable capacity: $C$ (in TB)
- Rated P/E endurance per cell: $E$ cycles
- Write Amplification Factor: $\text{WAF}$
- NAND write traffic per day: $W_d$ (in TB/day)

**Step 1** — Total host writes supported over the device's life:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TBW}_{\text{host}} &= \frac{C \times E}{\text{WAF}} \quad \text{[TB]}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *Terabytes Written* rating quoted by manufacturers. Dividing by WAF accounts for the fact that every host byte of write causes WAF bytes of physical NAND wear.

**Step 2** — Lifetime in years for a given workload:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lifetime (days)} &= \frac{\text{TBW}_{\text{host}}}{W_d} \\
\text{Lifetime (years)} &= \frac{C \times E}{W_d \times \text{WAF} \times 365}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Numerical example:
Suppose a 1 TB SSD with $E = 3{,}000$ P/E cycles and $\text{WAF} = 2$ is written at $W_d = 20$ GB/day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lifetime (years)} &= \frac{1 \times 3000}{0.02 \times 2 \times 365} \\
&= \frac{3000}{14.6} \\
&\approx 205.5 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$
