In practice the controller, capacitors, and firmware bugs limit life to ~5–10 years, but the NAND itself would outlast this easily.

### 3.2 Derivation: Garbage Collection Overhead

Let us compute the cost of reclaiming one block. Suppose:

- Block size: $B_{\text{block}}$ = 256 pages
- Page size: $B_{\text{page}}$ = 16 KB
- Block capacity: $B_{\text{block}} = 256 \times 16 = 4096$ KB
- Live page ratio in victim block: $u$ (0 to 1)

**Step 1** — Bytes that must be migrated (live pages):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Migration bytes} &= u \times B_{\text{block}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Total physical writes to reclaim this block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total NAND writes} &= u \times B_{\text{block}} \quad \text{(migration)} \\
&\quad + B_{\text{block}} \quad \text{(erase doesn't write)} \\
&\quad \text{(plus re-programming of migrated pages)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The actual migration itself is *one write per live page* plus the *erase operation* (no data write but consumes P/E cycles on the block).

**Step 3** — Approximate WAF contribution from GC alone:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WAF}_{\text{GC}} &\approx 1 + u
\end{aligned}
$$

If $u = 0.5$ (half the block is live), GC alone doubles the write traffic, giving $\text{WAF}_{\text{GC}} \approx 1.5$.

**Step 4** — Time to reclaim one block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{GC}} &= u \times N_{\text{pages}} \times T_{\text{program}} + T_{\text{erase}} \\
&= u \times 256 \times 250\ \mu s + 2000\ \mu s \\
&= u \times 64000\ \mu s + 2000\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

For $u = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{GC}} &= 0.5 \times 64000 + 2000 \\
&= 32000 + 2000 \\
&= 34000\ \mu s \\
&= 34\ \text{ms per block reclaimed}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — FTL Mapping Schemes

| Mapping Granularity | Mapping Table Size | Performance | Used In |
|--------------------|--------------------|-------------|---------|
| **Page-level** | Largest (e.g., 1 GB for 1 TB drive) | Best random write | High-end NVMe |
| **Block-level** | Smallest | Poor random write | Older/embedded |
| **Hybrid (Log-block)** | Medium | Balanced | Mid-range SATA SSDs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Page-level mapping** delivers best random write performance because any LBA can go to any free page. **Block-level** must perform full block erases for small updates — disastrous for random writes.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: SSD vs HDD (OS Perspective)

| Property | HDD | SSD |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Access time | ~5–10 ms (seek + rotation) | ~25–100 µs |
| Random IOPS | ~75–200 | 50,000–1,000,000+ |
| Sequential bandwidth | 100–200 MB/s | 500–7,000 MB/s |
| Mechanical failure | High (motors, heads) | Very low |
| Power consumption (active) | 6–10 W | 2–5 W (NVMe), 0.5–3 W idle |
| Noise | Audible | Silent |
| Effect of fragmentation | Significant performance loss | Minimal impact |
| OS scheduling | Elevator / anticipatory helpful | No benefit; random ≈ sequential |
| Read disturb | N/A | Possible (handled by FTL) |
| Write cliff | No | Yes (without OP/TRIM) |

### 3.5 Detailed Worked Example — IOPS Calculation

**Problem**: A SATA SSD has an average read latency of 80 µs and average write latency of 300 µs. In a 70% read / 30% write workload with queue depth 1, compute the IOPS.

**Step 1** — Weighted average latency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{T} &= 0.7 \times 80 + 0.3 \times 300 \\
&= 56 + 90 \\
&= 146\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — IOPS at queue depth 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS} &= \frac{1}{\bar{T}} = \frac{1}{146 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 6{,}849 \text{ IOPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — With queue depth $Q = 32$ (parallel commands, typical for NCQ/NVMe):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS}_{\text{parallel}} &\approx Q \times \frac{1}{\bar{T}} \\
&= 32 \times 6{,}849 \\
&\approx 219{,}168 \text{ IOPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

Real-world benchmarks with NVMe exceed this substantially due to internal parallelism across dies and planes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SSD Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HostIF["Host Interface SATA SAS NVMe PCIe"] --> FW["SSD Controller Firmware FTL Engine"]
    FW --> DRAM["DRAM Cache Mapping Table Buffer"]
    FW --> ECC["ECC Engine LDPC or BCH"]
    FW --> WL["Wear Leveling Module"]
    FW --> GC["Garbage Collector"]
    FW --> BBU["Bad Block Manager"]
    FW --> NANDCtl["NAND Controller"]
    NANDCtl --> D1["Die 0"]
    NANDCtl --> D2["Die 1"]
    NANDCtl --> D3["Die 2"]
    NANDCtl --> D4["Die 3"]
    D1 --> P1["Plane 0A Plane 0B"]
    D2 --> P2["Plane 1A Plane 1B"]
    D3 --> P3["Plane 2A Plane 2B"]
    D4 --> P4["Plane 3A Plane 3B"]
    P1 --> B1["Blocks 1024 to 4096 each"]
    P2 --> B2["Blocks 1024 to 4096 each"]
    P3 --> B3["Blocks 1024 to 4096 each"]
    P4 --> B4["Blocks 1024 to 4096 each"]
```

### 4.2 Read / Program / Erase State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Erased: Factory Fresh
    Erased --> Programmed: Program Page Write 1 to 0
    Programmed --> Erased: Erase Block Reset to 1
    Programmed --> Programmed: Out of Place Write New Page
    note right of Programmed: Old page becomes INVALID
    note right of Erased: Only whole blocks can erase
```

### 4.3 OS-to-NAND Data Flow (FTL Operation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant HBA as Host Bus Adapter
    participant FTL as FTL Engine
    participant NAND as NAND Flash Array
    OS->>HBA: WRITE LBA 0x1000, 16 KB
    HBA->>FTL: Forward write command
    FTL->>FTL: Lookup L2P map for 0x1000
    FTL->>FTL: Allocate new free page P_new
    FTL->>NAND: Program page P_new with data
    NAND-->>FTL: Acknowledge program complete
    FTL->>FTL: Mark old page INVALID
    FTL->>FTL: Update L2P table in DRAM
    FTL-->>HBA: Write complete ACK
    HBA-->>OS: Interrupt I O done
    Note over FTL,NAND: Later GC migrates live pages and erases blocks
```

### 4.4 Wear Leveling — Dynamic vs Static

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["New Write Request from OS"] --> B{"Block has Low P E Count"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Select as Free Block"]
    B -- No --> D["Dynamic Wear Leveling Choose different free block"]
    C --> E["Program Page"]
    D --> E
    E --> F{"Cold Static Data Detected"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Static Wear Leveling Move to High P E Block"]
    G --> H["Free Up Low P E Block for Hot Data"]
    F -- No --> I["Continue Operation"]
    H --> I
```

### 4.5 Garbage Collection Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Free Pool Below Threshold"] --> Sel["Select Victim Block Fewest Live Pages"]
    Sel --> Copy["Copy Live Pages to Free Block"]
    Copy --> Upd["Update L2P Mapping"]
    Upd --> Era["Erase Victim Block"]
    Era --> Pool["Return Block to Free Pool"]
    Pool --> Done["GC Cycle Complete"]
```

### 4.6 SSD Hierarchy (Zoom-in)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SSD["SSD Package"] --> D1["Die 1"]
    SSD --> D2["Die 2"]
    D1 --> Pl1["Plane 1A"]
    D1 --> Pl2["Plane 1B"]
    Pl1 --> Blk1["Block 0 Erase Unit 4 MB"]
    Blk1 --> Pg1["Page 0 16 KB Read Write"]
    Blk1 --> Pg2["Page 1 16 KB Read Write"]
    Blk1 --> Pg3["Page 2 16 KB Read Write"]
    Blk1 --> Pgn["Page N 16 KB"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between the page-level and block-level mapping strategies used in the Flash Translation Layer. Which provides better random write performance and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Page-level mapping**: Each logical page can be mapped to *any* physical page in any block. The L2P table is large (GBs for TB drives) but allows fine-grained out-of-place updates.
- **Block-level mapping**: Logical blocks map to physical blocks; pages within a block have a fixed offset. The mapping table is small, but any small update requires erasing the entire block.
- **Better random write**: **Page-level mapping** is superior because it avoids the need to read-modify-write entire blocks for small updates, drastically reducing write amplification.

*(Award: 1 mark definition page-level, 1 mark definition block-level, 1 mark justification — KTU pattern.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is wear leveling? Why is it necessary in SSDs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition**: Wear leveling is the FTL technique of distributing write/erase cycles *uniformly* across all NAND blocks to prevent premature failure of frequently-written cells.
- **Necessity**: NAND cells have a finite P/E endurance (1,000–100,000 cycles). Without wear leveling, hot data (e.g., log files, file system journals) would repeatedly write to the same blocks, wearing them out long before the rest of the drive.
- **Types**: Dynamic (rotates free blocks) and Static (moves cold data to high-wear blocks). Static is more thorough but adds WAF.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark necessity/justification, 1 mark types — KTU standard split.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[Internal Choice Rule — KTU 2024 Scheme]**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternatives (A or B) are offered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain the internal architecture of a Solid-State Disk with a neat block diagram. Discuss the roles of the controller, FTL, and NAND packages.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. SSD Architecture (3 marks):**
The SSD consists of:
- **Host Interface**: SATA/SAS/NVMe/PCIe connector presenting a logical block device.
- **SSD Controller**: A microprocessor (often ARM-based) running the FTL firmware. Contains DRAM for mapping tables, ECC engine (LDPC/BCH), and NAND controllers.
- **NAND Flash Packages**: Multiple packages, each containing several dies. Each die has 2–4 planes, each plane has thousands of blocks, each block has 64–256 pages of 4–16 KB.

**2. Controller Role (2 marks):**
The controller executes the FTL, handles command queuing (NCQ for SATA, multi-queue for NVMe), applies ECC, performs encryption (AES-256), and manages power-loss protection.

**3. FTL Role (2 marks):**
The FTL provides:
- **Logical-to-physical mapping** (L2P)
- **Wear leveling** (dynamic + static)
- **Garbage collection** with victim block selection
- **Bad block remapping**

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 above — draw equivalent block diagram showing Host Interface → Controller → NAND Packages.)*

---

#### **(b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Discuss garbage collection and TRIM operations in SSDs. How do they interact to maintain steady-state performance?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Garbage Collection (3 marks):**
When the free block pool drops below a threshold, the GC module:
- Selects a victim block (typically with the *fewest live pages*).
- Copies the live pages to a new free block.
- Updates the L2P mapping table.
- Erases the victim block and returns it to the free pool.
GC is essential because SSD cannot reclaim individual pages — only full blocks.

**2. TRIM Command (2 marks):**
TRIM is an ATA command (DEALLOCATE in NVMe) issued by the OS when files are deleted. It tells the SSD: *"Logical blocks X to Y are no longer in use."* Without TRIM, the SSD would not know that those pages are dead until it later does a read and discovers the OS no longer references them — wasting GC effort.

**3. Interaction (2 marks):**
- TRIM *proactively* invalidates pages → GC has more free pages to consolidate.
- Reduces WAF.
- Prevents the **write cliff** (steady-state performance drop after sustained writes).
- With TRIM, GC cost is bounded by *user-issued* writes rather than by *all-time* writes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **TRIM** with a *secure erase*. TRIM does **NOT** zero out data; it merely marks LBAs as free. Always state this distinction.
> - Do **NOT** claim TRIM alone eliminates the write cliff. It *mitigates* it by reducing GC pressure; OP space is also required.
> - When drawing the GC flow, students frequently forget the **L2P mapping update** step — that is where KTU evaluators deduct the 2-mark design step.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### **(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of Write Amplification Factor (WAF). Derive the relationship between SSD endurance (TBW), capacity, P/E cycles, and WAF.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. WAF Definition (2 marks):**
$$\text{WAF} = \frac{\text{Actual NAND writes}}{\text{Host writes}}$$

An ideal SSD has WAF = 1. Real SSDs experience WAF > 1 due to garbage collection, wear leveling, and metadata updates.

**2. Derivation (3 marks):**

The total physical NAND writes permitted before wear-out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TBW}_{\text{NAND}} &= C \times E
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C$ = capacity (TB), $E$ = rated P/E cycles.

Host-visible writes correspond to NAND writes divided by WAF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TBW}_{\text{host}} &= \frac{\text{TBW}_{\text{NAND}}}{\text{WAF}} \\
&= \frac{C \times E}{\text{WAF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Lifetime derivation (2 marks):**

For daily host write workload $W_d$ (TB/day):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lifetime (years)} &= \frac{C \times E}{W_d \times \text{WAF} \times 365}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Refer to Section 3.1 above for full numerical illustration.)*

---

#### **(b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Compare SSDs and HDDs from an OS perspective. Discuss how OS scheduling policies should differ between the two devices.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Performance Comparison Table (3 marks):**
*(Reproduce comparison matrix from Section 3.4.)*

**2. OS Scheduling Implications (3 marks):**

| Scheduler | HDD Suitability | SSD Suitability |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| FCFS | Inefficient due to seek storms | Acceptable |
| SSTF / SCAN / C-SCAN | Excellent (reduces seek) | **Unnecessary** — no seek penalty |
| NOOP | Underperforms | **Optimal** for SSDs |
| Deadline / Anticipatory | Good | Overhead may hurt latency |
| Multi-queue (BFQ, Kyber) | Reasonable | **Best for NVMe** |

**Key OS Differences:**
- **Fragmentation**: HDDs suffer severe random-access penalty for fragmented files; SSDs do not.
- **Read-ahead**: Useful on HDDs (hides rotational latency); useless on SSDs.
- **I/O merging**: HDDs benefit; SSDs have internal parallelism that makes per-process ordering less critical.
- **Filesystem choice**: HDDs benefit from block-group layouts (ext4, XFS with extent alignment); SSDs are indifferent.

**3. Conclusion (1 mark):**
Modern OS schedulers (Linux `mq-deadline`, `kyber`, Windows Storport stack) detect device type and adapt queue depths and elevator policies accordingly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing page-level and block-level FTL mapping** — students often swap definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting that TRIM does NOT erase data** — it only hints at free LBAs.
> 3. **Stating "SSD has no latency"** — false. SSDs have ~25–100 µs read latency; they are *faster*, not *zero-latency*.
> 4. **Ignoring WAF in lifetime calculations** — leads to overestimated SSD life.
> 5. **Omitting the L2P update step** in GC sequence — loses 2 marks in design questions.
> 6. **Confusing OP (over-provisioning) with usable capacity** — OP is *invisible* to the host but consumes raw NAND.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ SSD = NAND-flash block device with no moving parts; exposed via SATA/SAS/NVMe.
- ✅ **Three primitive ops**: Read (page, ~25 µs), Program (page, ~250 µs), Erase (block, ~2 ms).
- ✅ **Erase-before-write** forces out-of-place updates → requires FTL.
- ✅ **FTL** = mapping table + wear leveling + GC + bad-block management + ECC.
- ✅ **Page-level mapping** gives best random write performance; **block-level** is too coarse.
- ✅ **Wear leveling** = dynamic (free blocks) + static (moves cold data). Necessary because NAND has finite **P/E endurance** (1k–100k cycles).
- ✅ **Garbage collection** consolidates live pages and erases full blocks; victim selection targets fewest-live-pages block.
- ✅ **TRIM** = OS tells SSD which LBAs are free → reduces WAF, restores steady-state performance, mitigates write cliff.
- ✅ **WAF** = NAND writes / Host writes. TBW = (Capacity × P/E cycles) / WAF.
- ✅ **Over-provisioning (7–28%)** reserves raw NAND for GC; invisible to host.
- ✅ SSDs eliminate seek/rotation → OS scheduler should switch to **NOOP / Kyber / mq-deadline** instead of elevator algorithms.
- ✅ Fragmentation matters for HDDs, **not for SSDs**.
- ✅ SSD bottlenecks: **write cliff** (without OP/TRIM), **read disturb** (mitigated by FTL refresh), **endurance limit** (mitigated by wear leveling).
- ✅ NVMe over PCIe >> SATA SSDs in latency and IOPS due to lower protocol overhead and massive queue depth (64k queues × 64k commands).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Disk Structure** refers to the logical and physical organization of data on secondary storage devices (magnetic hard disks, SSDs, and optical media). In the context of a Modern Operating System, disk structure defines the geometric and electromagnetic arrangement of storage media into hierarchical units — **Platters → Surfaces → Tracks → Sectors → Cylinders → Blocks/Clusters** — that allow the OS to address, read, write, and manage data efficiently through standardized addressing schemes such as **CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector)** and **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Disk structure is the foundation for understanding **Disk Scheduling, RAID, File Systems (FAT, NTFS, ext4), and Storage Management**. Every disk-related OS module (scheduling algorithms, swapping, virtual memory backing store) builds upon this topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of a hard disk as a **massive multi-story library building**:

- The **building** = the entire hard disk drive.
- Each **floor** = a *platter* (a circular disk coated with magnetic material).
- Each **floor has two sides** (top and bottom) = two *surfaces* per platter.
- On every surface, there are **concentric circular rings** where books are placed = *tracks*.
- Each ring is divided into **pie-shaped slices (wedges)** = *sectors*, the smallest addressable unit.
- The **vertical alignment of the same ring across all floors** = a *cylinder* (this is the unit that minimizes head movement).
- A **book** = a logical *block* (typically 512 B or 4 KB), which groups multiple sectors for OS use.

So when the OS wants data, it tells the disk: *"Go to floor 3, top side, ring 250, slice 7"* — that is **CHS addressing**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value (Magnetic HDD) |
|---|---|
| Sector size | **512 bytes (legacy) / 4096 bytes (Advanced Format)** |
| Track density | 15,000 – 100,000+ tracks per inch (TPI) |
| Areal density | ~1 Tbit/in² (modern drives) |
| Platter diameter | **3.5" (desktop), 2.5" (laptop)** |
| Spindle speed | **5,400 / 7,200 / 10,000 / 15,000 RPM** |
| Heads | One read/write head per surface (actuator arm shared) |
| Average seek time | **3 – 12 ms** |
| Rotational latency | ½ × rotation time ≈ 2 – 4 ms (at 15K RPM) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **sector** is the *physical* minimum addressable unit (512 B / 4 KB). A **block** (or **cluster**) is the *logical* minimum unit the OS uses for file allocation, typically comprising 1–128 sectors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Anatomy of a Magnetic Disk

A modern magnetic hard disk drive (HDD) is a sealed, precision-engineered electromechanical device. The disk structure is organized as a strict hierarchy:

1. **Platter** – A rigid substrate (aluminum or glass) coated with a thin ferromagnetic layer. Modern drives contain 1–5 platters stacked on a common spindle.
2. **Surface** – Each platter has 2 surfaces (top and bottom). Surfaces that are not used for storage are called *guard surfaces*.
3. **Track** – A single concentric circle on a surface. A 3.5" disk may have **50,000 – 150,000 tracks per surface**.
4. **Sector** – A small arc (typically **512 bytes** in legacy, **4096 bytes / 4 KiB** in Advanced Format drives). Sectors are separated by *inter-sector gaps* containing preamble, address marks, and error-correcting codes (ECC).
5. **Cylinder** – The set of all tracks that lie at the same radial distance from the center, across all surfaces. It is the *unit of head switching*, not the unit of rotation.
6. **Block / Cluster** – The OS-level grouping of consecutive sectors. Defined when the file system is formatted.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Layer

- **Why platters?** To maximize storage density per drive volume (volumetric density).
- **Why tracks?** To allow linear (sequential) reading within a ring, which is much faster than jumping randomly across the surface.
- **Why sectors?** To provide the smallest independently addressable unit, simplifying the controller's job and allowing error recovery to happen at sector granularity.
- **Why cylinders?** Because the **actuator arm moves all heads together**, switching heads is *electronic* (microseconds), while moving to a new cylinder is *mechanical* (milliseconds). Cylinders exploit this to minimize seek time.
- **Why blocks?** To reduce OS metadata overhead (one inode/FAT entry per block instead of per sector) and to align with the **page size (4 KB)** of virtual memory, easing the paging I/O.

## 2.3 Disk Addressing Schemes

### (a) CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector) — 3D Addressing
- Used in early PCs and the original IDE interface.
- Limited to **1,024 cylinders × 16 heads × 63 sectors = 1,032,192 sectors ≈ 528 MB** (under the original 16-bit BIOS INT 13h interface).
- Modern drives *emulate* CHS even when they use LBA internally.

### (b) LBA (Logical Block Addressing) — 1D Flat Addressing
- Introduced with ATA-2 (1996) and the SCSI standard.
- Treats the disk as a **linear array of sectors** numbered `0, 1, 2, …, N-1`.
- A 2 TB drive needs only **32-bit LBA**; drives > 2 TiB require **LBA-48** (48-bit addressing = 128 PiB).
- All modern OSes (Windows, Linux, macOS) and file systems (NTFS, ext4, APFS) use LBA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion between LBA and CHS** is governed by the BIOS geometry translation (L-CHS, LBA-assisted, or Large). On modern systems, the controller firmware performs this translation transparently.

## 2.4 Modern Storage: Solid State Drives (SSDs)

While traditional disk structure is magnetic, the OS treats SSDs through the same interface (block-level LBA). However, the internal structure differs:

- **Pages** (4 KB – 16 KB) – read/write unit.
- **Blocks** (256 KB – 4 MB) – erase unit (a block contains many pages).
- **Planes / Dies / Channels** – parallel internal architecture.
- **FTL (Flash Translation Layer)** – maps LBA → physical NAND address, handles wear-leveling and garbage collection.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exam purposes, "Disk Structure" defaults to magnetic disk. SSDs are covered in the *Storage Devices* module. Focus your answers on **magnetic HDD structure, CHS/LBA, and disk geometry**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value bars in the table below use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Disk Capacity** | $C = S \times T \times N_{sec}$ | Bytes | $S$ = bytes/sector, $T$ = tracks/surface, $N_{sec}$ = sectors/track |
| 2 | **Total Sectors** | $N = C_{yl} \times H \times S_{pt}$ | sectors | $C_{yl}$ = cylinders, $H$ = heads, $S_{pt}$ = sectors/track |
| 3 | **Rotation Time (full)** | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ | seconds | One full revolution |
| 4 | **Avg. Rotational Latency** | $T_{lat} = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ | seconds | Average = half rotation |
| 5 | **Avg. Access Time** | $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{lat} + T_{trans}$ | seconds | Time to start reading data |
| 6 | **Transfer Time** | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{\text{Bytes requested}}{D_{rot} \times S_{pt}}$ | seconds | $D_{rot}$ = sectors passing per second |
| 7 | **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{\text{Data transferred}}{T_{trans}}$ | B/s | Effective bandwidth |
| 8 | **LBA to CHS mapping** | $L = (C \cdot H + H_d) \cdot S_{pt} + S - 1$ | — | $L$ = LBA, $H_d$ = head (0-indexed), $S$ = sector (1-indexed) |
| 9 | **Block Address** | $b = \lfloor L \ / \ K \rfloor$ | — | $K$ = sectors per block (cluster) |
| 10 | **Sectors per Track (ZBR)** | $N_{sec}(r) \approx N_{outer} \cdot \dfrac{r}{r_{outer}}$ | sectors | *Zone Bit Recording* (more sectors on outer tracks) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Engineering:** DBAs align table extents to **cylinder boundaries** to minimize head switches during sequential scans.
- **File System Design:** Block size is chosen to match the **rotational latency / sector size** trade-off (4 KB is the modern sweet spot for page-cache alignment).
- **Storage Arrays (RAID):** Striping across disks relies on LBA-based block addressing; CHS is irrelevant.
- **Embedded / IoT:** Some micro-controllers (e.g., SD cards in raw mode) still expose a CHS-like geometry for FAT16/32 boot sectors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Disk Capacity

We start from the hierarchical definition:

A disk has:
- $P$ platters → $H = 2P$ surfaces (heads).
- Each surface has $T$ tracks.
- Each track has $S$ sectors.
- Each sector stores $B$ bytes.

Therefore, the **total number of sectors** is the product of all geometric counts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= T \times H \times S \\
&= T \times (2P) \times S
\end{aligned}
$$

The total **disk capacity in bytes** is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= N_{total} \times B \\
  &= T \cdot H \cdot S \cdot B \\
  &= 2 \cdot P \cdot T \cdot S \cdot B
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion to higher units:**
> $C_{MB} = \dfrac{C}{2^{20}}$, $C_{GB} = \dfrac{C}{2^{30}}$, $C_{TB} = \dfrac{C}{2^{40}}$.

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**
A disk has 4 platters, 8192 tracks/surface, 256 sectors/track, 512 bytes/sector.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ heads} \\
N_{total} &= 8192 \times 8 \times 256 = 16\,777\,216 \text{ sectors} \\
C &= 16\,777\,216 \times 512 \\
  &= 8\,589\,934\,592 \text{ bytes} \\
  &= 8 \times 2^{30} \text{ bytes} \\
  &= 8\ \text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Access Time

The **average time to access a block of data** on a magnetic disk is the sum of three independent components:

$$
T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{lat} + T_{trans}
$$

### Step A — Average Seek Time ($T_{seek}$)
This is the time for the actuator arm to move the heads from the current cylinder to the target cylinder. A common simplified model is **1/3 of the full-stroke seek time** (a heuristic widely used in textbooks and OS literature, e.g., Silberschatz, Tanenbaum):

$$
T_{seek} = \dfrac{T_{full}}{3}
$$

In practice, manufacturers quote an *average* seek time directly (e.g., 8 ms for a 7200 RPM desktop drive).

### Step B — Average Rotational Latency ($T_{lat}$)
The disk must rotate so that the desired sector arrives under the head. The worst case is a full rotation, the best case is 0. Assuming a uniform random distribution of sector positions, the expected wait is **half a rotation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rot} &= \dfrac{60}{RPM} \quad \text{(time per full revolution in seconds)} \\
T_{lat} &= \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Transfer Time ($T_{trans}$)
Once the head is positioned and the right sector is under it, reading the requested bytes takes time proportional to the amount of data and the angular speed:

$$
T_{trans} = \dfrac{\text{Bytes requested}}{\text{Bytes per revolution}} \times T_{rot}
$$

Equivalently, if $S_{pt}$ = sectors/track and $B$ = bytes/sector:

$$
T_{trans} = \dfrac{b_{req}}{S_{pt} \cdot B} \times \dfrac{60}{RPM}
$$

### Final Composite Formula

$$
\boxed{T_{acc} = T_{seek} + \dfrac{30}{RPM} + \dfrac{b_{req}}{S_{pt} \cdot B} \cdot \dfrac{60}{RPM}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A 7200 RPM disk has 8 ms average seek, 256 sectors/track, 512 B/sector. Find the access time to read 8 KB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{seek} &= 8\ \text{ms} \\
T_{lat} &= \dfrac{30}{7200} = 4.167\ \text{ms} \\
T_{trans} &= \dfrac{8192}{256 \times 512} \times \dfrac{60}{7200} \\
          &= \dfrac{8192}{131\,072} \times 0.00833 \\
          &= 0.0625 \times 8.333\ \text{ms} \\
          &= 0.521\ \text{ms} \\
T_{acc} &= 8 + 4.167 + 0.521 = 12.688\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LBA to CHS Translation

Given an LBA value $L$ (0-indexed) and a disk geometry with $C_{yl}$ cylinders, $H$ heads, $S_{pt}$ sectors/track, we compute the cylinder, head, and sector as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Temp} &= L \\
S &= (Temp \bmod S_{pt}) + 1 \\
Temp &= \lfloor Temp \ / \ S_{pt} \rfloor \\
H_d &= Temp \bmod H \\
C   &= \lfloor Temp \ / \ H \rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** Geometry = 16 heads, 63 sectors/track. Find CHS for LBA = 7890.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (7890 \bmod 63) + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16 \\
Temp &= \lfloor 7890 / 63 \rfloor = 125 \\
H_d &= 125 \bmod 16 = 13 \\
C   &= \lfloor 125 / 16 \rfloor = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

So LBA 7890 → **Cylinder 7, Head 13, Sector 16**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Components

| Component | Magnitude | Affects | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seek time | 1 – 15 ms | Cylinder-to-cylinder movement | Disk scheduling (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK) |
| Rotational latency | 0 – 8 ms | Rotational position | *Cannot be scheduled*; reduced by higher RPM |
| Controller overhead | < 0.1 ms | Command processing | NCQ (Native Command Queuing) |
| Transfer time | µs – ms / MB | Density of data on track | ZBR, increased areal density |
| Queue delay | ms – s | Concurrent I/O load | OS-level elevator / anticipation algorithms |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Physical Disk Structure (Top-Down)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DISK DRIVE UNIT"] --> B["SPINDLE ASSEMBLY"]
    A --> C["ACTUATOR ARM ASSEMBLY"]
    A --> D["CONTROLLER BOARD"]
    A --> E["PLATTER STACK"]
    B --> B1["Holds 1 to 5 platters rotating at fixed RPM"]
    C --> C1["One Read Write head per surface"]
    C --> C2["Heads move radially in unison"]
    D --> D1["Microcontroller and Cache RAM"]
    D --> D2["Interfaces SATA SAS NVMe"]
    E --> P1["PLATTER 1"]
    E --> P2["PLATTER 2"]
    E --> P3["PLATTER N"]
    P1 --> S1["SURFACE TOP"]
    P1 --> S2["SURFACE BOTTOM"]
    S1 --> T1["TRACK 0 Outer Ring"]
    S1 --> T2["TRACK 1"]
    S1 --> T3["TRACK 2"]
    S1 --> TN["TRACK N-1 Inner Ring"]
    T2 --> SEC1["SECTOR 0 512B or 4KB"]
    T2 --> SEC2["SECTOR 1"]
    T2 --> SEC3["SECTOR 2"]
    T2 --> SECN["SECTOR N-1"]
    SEC1 --> Z["CYLINDER Vertical stack of all tracks at the same radius across all surfaces"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP["APPLICATION PROCESS"] --> SYS["SYSTEM CALL read write open"]
    SYS --> VFS["VFS LAYER"]
    VFS --> FS["FILE SYSTEM ext4 NTFS FAT32"]
    FS --> BUF["BUFFER CACHE Page Cache"]
    BUF --> DSK["DISK DRIVER generic block layer"]
    DSK --> Q["REQUEST QUEUE NCQ or TCQ"]
    Q --> CTRL["DISK CONTROLLER Firmware"]
    CTRL --> GEOM["GEOMETRY TRANSLATION CHS to LBA or LBA to physical"]
    GEOM --> ARM["ACTUATOR ARM Seek Phase"]
    ARM --> SPIN["SPINDLE MOTOR Rotational Latency"]
    SPIN --> HEAD["READ WRITE HEAD Transfer Phase"]
    HEAD --> RET["DATA RETURNED TO USER BUFFER"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Addressing Scheme Evolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    LBA["LBA Logical Block Addressing 1D Linear Array"] -->|"Most modern OS"| FS["FILE SYSTEM NTFS ext4 APFS"]
    CHS["CHS Cylinder Head Sector 3D Geometry"] -->|"Legacy BIOS INT 13h"| BIOS["BIOS BOOT SECTOR"]
    LBA -->|"Translation Layer"| CHS
    CHS -->|"Translation Layer"| LBA
    LBA48["LBA-48 48-bit addressing for drives over 2 TiB"] -->|"Used by SATA and modern NVMe"| LBA
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — ZBR Zone Bit Recording

```mermaid
graph TD
    Z["ZONE BIT RECORDING ZBR"] --> Z1["OUTER ZONE More sectors per track higher data rate"]
    Z --> Z2["MIDDLE ZONE Medium sector count"]
    Z --> Z3["INNER ZONE Fewer sectors per track lower data rate"]
    Z1 --> N1["e.g. 600 sectors per track"]
    Z2 --> N2["e.g. 500 sectors per track"]
    Z3 --> N3["e.g. 400 sectors per track"]
    Z1 --> NOTE["Constant linear bit density achieved across all radii"]
    Z2 --> NOTE
    Z3 --> NOTE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a *sector*, a *track*, and a *cylinder* in a magnetic disk. Why is the cylinder concept useful for OS-level disk scheduling? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Size Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Track** | A single concentric circle on a disk surface. | Thousands per surface |
| **Sector** | The smallest physical addressable arc of a track. | 512 B or 4 KB |
| **Cylinder** | The set of all tracks at the same radius across *all* surfaces. | Equals # of tracks per surface |

**Usefulness of Cylinder Concept:**
Since the actuator arm moves all read/write heads in *unison*, switching between heads (different surfaces, same cylinder) requires only an **electronic selection (~0.1 ms)**, not a mechanical seek. Therefore, a disk scheduler that keeps I/O requests within the *same cylinder* can avoid expensive seeks and dramatically reduce access time. Cylinder-based optimization forms the basis of algorithms like **C-LOOK** and influences the design of file-system allocation strategies (e.g., placing a file's blocks within a few contiguous cylinders).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. What is LBA? Why has LBA replaced CHS in modern systems? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**LBA (Logical Block Addressing)** is a linear, 1-dimensional addressing scheme that numbers each sector on a disk sequentially from `0` to `N-1`, treating the disk as a flat array of fixed-size blocks.

**Reasons for replacing CHS:**

1. **Capacity Limit:** Original CHS under BIOS INT 13h was capped at **528 MB** (1024 cyl × 16 heads × 63 sec × 512 B), which became obsolete by the mid-1990s.
2. **Geometry Independence:** LBA eliminates the need to know the physical Cylinder/Head/Sector counts, allowing the drive to use **ZBR** (Zone Bit Recording) where outer tracks have more sectors than inner tracks — impossible to express in fixed CHS.
3. **Simplicity:** A single 32-bit (or 48-bit for LBA-48) number is far easier for OS and controller firmware to manipulate than three coupled variables.
4. **Plug-and-Play:** LBA is the universal addressing used by **SATA, SAS, NVMe, USB, and SD cards**, enabling cross-platform storage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### ✦ Option A

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **physical structure of a magnetic hard disk drive**. List the major components and describe the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A disk has **4 platters, 8192 tracks/surface, 256 sectors/track, and 512 bytes/sector**. Calculate:
  (i) Total disk capacity in GB.
  (ii) Number of cylinders.
  (iii) Number of read/write heads.
  (iv) Total number of sectors. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Components of a Magnetic HDD:**

1. **Platter(s):** Circular disks coated with a ferromagnetic film, mounted on a common spindle. Each platter has 2 surfaces.
2. **Spindle Motor:** Rotates the platters at a constant angular velocity (e.g., 5400, 7200, 10 000, or 15 000 RPM).
3. **Read/Write Heads:** One per surface, mounted on the actuator arm. They float on a thin air cushion (nanometers above the surface).
4. **Actuator Arm Assembly:** Moves all heads radially in unison, driven by a voice-coil motor.
5. **Controller Board:** Contains the disk controller, cache (typically 16–256 MB DRAM), and host interface (SATA, SAS, NVMe).
6. **Sector Format:** Each sector contains a *preamble*, *address mark*, *synchronization field*, *data field*, and *ECC field*, separated by *gaps*.

**Geometric Hierarchy:** Platter → Surface → Track → Sector; the vertical stack of same-radius tracks across all surfaces is called a **Cylinder**.

*(A labelled diagram should accompany the answer; refer to the Mermaid block in Section 4.1.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $P = 4$, $T = 8192$ tracks/surface, $S_{pt} = 256$ sectors/track, $B = 512$ B/sector.

**(i) Number of Heads (H):**
Each platter has 2 surfaces, each with one head.
$$
H = 2 \times P = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ heads}
$$

**(ii) Number of Cylinders:**
Number of cylinders = number of tracks per surface.
$$
C_{yl} = 8192
$$

**(iii) Total Number of Sectors:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= T \times H \times S_{pt} \\
          &= 8192 \times 8 \times 256 \\
          &= 16\,777\,216 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Total Disk Capacity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= N_{total} \times B = 16\,777\,216 \times 512 = 8\,589\,934\,592 \text{ bytes} \\
C_{GB} &= \dfrac{8\,589\,934\,592}{2^{30}} = \dfrac{8\,589\,934\,592}{1\,073\,741\,824} = 8\ \text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ✦ Option B

**Q. (a)** Explain the difference between **CHS and LBA** addressing schemes. Show the LBA-to-CHS conversion procedure with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 7200 RPM disk has **8 ms average seek time**, **256 sectors/track**, and **512 bytes/sector**. Calculate the **average time to read 16 KB of data** assuming a random location. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**CHS vs LBA:**

| Feature | CHS | LBA |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensions | 3-D (C, H, S) | 1-D (linear) |
| Max Capacity (BIOS) | 528 MB | 2 TiB (LBA-28) / 128 PiB (LBA-48) |
| Use case | Legacy BIOS, MBR | Modern OS, GPT, SSD, NVMe |
| ZBR compatible | No | Yes |
| Complexity | High (3 variables) | Low (1 variable) |

**LBA → CHS Conversion Procedure:**

Given LBA $L$ and geometry $(C_{max}, H_{max}, S_{pt})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Sector number (1-based)} &\quad S = (L \bmod S_{pt}) + 1 \\
\text{Step 2: Reduce} &\quad L' = \lfloor L \ / \ S_{pt} \rfloor \\
\text{Step 3: Head number (0-based)} &\quad H_d = L' \bmod H_{max} \\
\text{Step 4: Cylinder number (0-based)} &\quad C = \lfloor L' \ / \ H_{max} \rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**
Geometry: $H_{max} = 16$, $S_{pt} = 63$, LBA = 4500.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (4500 \bmod 63) + 1 = 33 + 1 = 34 \\
L' &= \lfloor 4500 / 63 \rfloor = 71 \\
H_d &= 71 \bmod 16 = 7 \\
C   &= \lfloor 71 / 16 \rfloor = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** LBA 4500 → **Cylinder 4, Head 7, Sector 34**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $RPM = 7200$, $T_{seek} = 8$ ms, $S_{pt} = 256$, $B = 512$ B, request = 16 KB.

**Step 1 — Rotational latency:**
$$
T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{7200} = 0.00833\ \text{s} = 8.333\ \text{ms}
$$
$$
T_{lat} = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = 4.167\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Transfer time:**
Number of sectors requested:
$$
n = \dfrac{16\ \text{KB}}{512\ \text{B}} = 32\ \text{sectors}
$$
Time to read 32 sectors (each revolution reads 256 sectors):
$$
T_{trans} = \dfrac{32}{256} \times T_{rot} = 0.125 \times 8.333 = 1.042\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Total average access time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{acc} &= T_{seek} + T_{lat} + T_{trans} \\
        &= 8 + 4.167 + 1.042 \\
        &= 13.209\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $T_{acc} \approx \mathbf{13.21\ ms}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up *Cylinder* and *Track*:** A *cylinder* is a 3-D concept (set of tracks at the same radius across *all* surfaces), whereas a *track* is 2-D (one circle on one surface). Students often write "the disk has 8192 cylinders and 8 heads" — the *cylinder* count is 8192, but only when there are 8 heads, meaning **8 tracks share a cylinder** (one per surface).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert RPM → Seconds:** A 7200 RPM disk has rotation time $60/7200$ seconds, *not* $1/7200$ seconds. Many students write $\frac{1}{7200}$ s, which is off by a factor of 60. **Always include the factor of 60.**
>
> 3. **CHS Limits:** Do *not* claim CHS can address 2 TB drives — the legacy 528 MB limit is a favorite KTU board question.
>
> 4. **Unit Confusion:** When computing capacity, present your intermediate answer in *bytes* and convert to GB only at the end using $2^{30}$ (not $10^9$); the KTU paper expects **binary GB (GiB)**.
>
> 5. **ZBR Pitfall:** If a question mentions *more sectors on outer tracks*, you must state that **CHS cannot represent this geometry** — that is one of the principal reasons LBA was introduced.
>
> 6. **Block vs Sector:** Do not equate *block* (OS-level, 4 KB typical) with *sector* (hardware-level, 512 B / 4 KB). In file-system contexts, "block" usually means a *cluster*.
>
> 7. **Seek Time Formula:** The $T_{full}/3$ heuristic is acceptable only when *full-stroke* seek is given. If the problem states "average seek time", use that value directly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Disk Structure**

- ☐ A **magnetic disk** stores data on concentric **tracks**, divided into **sectors** of typically **512 B or 4 KB**.
- ☐ Each **platter** has **2 surfaces**; each surface has **1 read/write head**.
- ☐ A **cylinder** = all tracks at the *same radius* across all surfaces.
- ☐ Number of **heads** = $2 \times$ number of platters.
- ☐ Number of **cylinders** = number of tracks per surface.
- ☐ **Disk capacity** = $T \times H \times S_{pt} \times B$ (bytes).
- ☐ **Rotation time** $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ seconds; **avg. latency** = $T_{rot}/2$.
- ☐ **Access time** = $T_{seek} + T_{lat} + T_{trans}$.
- ☐ **Transfer time** = $\dfrac{\text{bytes}}{S_{pt} \cdot B} \times T_{rot}$.
- ☐ **CHS** = 3-D addressing; capped at ~528 MB in legacy BIOS; supports ZBR poorly.
- ☐ **LBA** = 1-D linear addressing; LBA-28 supports up to 128 GiB; LBA-48 supports up to 128 PiB.
- ☐ **LBA → CHS**: $S = (L \bmod S_{pt})+1$, then $H_d = \lfloor L/S_{pt} \rfloor \bmod H$, then $C = \lfloor L/(S_{pt} \cdot H) \rfloor$.
- ☐ **ZBR (Zone Bit Recording)**: outer tracks have more sectors; constant linear bit density.
- ☐ **Block (cluster)** is the OS-level unit, distinct from the physical sector.
- ☐ SSDs use **pages** (R/W unit) and **blocks** (erase unit), managed by the **FTL**; OS still sees LBA.
- ☐ Common **KTU keywords**: *platter, surface, track, sector, cylinder, seek time, rotational latency, transfer time, LBA, CHS, ZBR, geometry translation*.
- ☐ Remember: **switching heads is electronic (µs), but moving the arm is mechanical (ms)** — this is the *entire reason* cylinders exist and why disk schedulers prefer *same-cylinder* requests.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disk Scheduling** is a fundamental **Input/Output (I/O) optimization technique** employed by the operating system's **I/O scheduler** to determine the **order in which pending disk I/O requests are serviced** by the disk arm (actuator). Since mechanical disk access time is dominated by *seek time* (mechanical repositioning of the read/write head) and *rotational latency*, minimizing the total head movement is critical to system throughput.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, disk scheduling belongs to the broader domain of **secondary storage management**, specifically under **Device Management** functions of the OS kernel. The disk controller maintains a **request queue (FIFO queue)** of cylinders to be accessed, and the scheduler reorders this queue based on a chosen algorithm to minimize the **total head movement (THM)**.

## 1.2 Key Physical Parameters (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Seek Time** | $T_s$ | milliseconds (ms) | **3 – 15 ms** |
| **Rotational Latency** | $T_r$ | ms | **4.15 ms (7200 RPM)** |
| **Transfer Time** | $T_{tr}$ | ms | **0.05 – 0.5 ms** |
| **Total Access Time** | $T_a$ | ms | Sum of above three |
| **Platters / Tracks / Sectors** | — | — | Logical geometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Seek Time + Rotational Latency" duo is the dominant cost** in any disk I/O. Modern OS designers target **seek time reduction** as the primary optimization goal because mechanical head motion is **5–10× slower** than rotation and **1000× slower** than electronic transfer.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza Delivery Boy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Pizza Delivery Optimization**

Imagine a **pizza delivery boy** standing at position **50** on a long road numbered **0 to 199**. The shop receives **8 orders** in a queue, each pointing to a house number: `98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67`.

**Naive Approach (FCFS):** Deliver in the order received — bounce back and forth chaotically across the entire road. **Total travel = 643 houses**. Tired boy, slow service.

**Smart Approach (SSTF/SCAN):** Always pick the *nearest unvisited house* in the current direction. **Total travel ≈ 205–334 houses**. Faster deliveries, fewer customer complaints, lower fuel cost.

This is *exactly* the problem disk scheduling solves — except the "delivery boy" is the **disk actuator arm**, the "road" is the **cylinder address space**, and "fuel cost" is the **seek time** (every crossing of a cylinder costs ~0.1 ms).

## 1.4 Why Disk Scheduling is Essential (KTU Expectation)

- **Starvation Prevention** of requests sitting at extreme cylinders.
- **Minimization of average response time** for I/O-bound processes.
- **Maximization of disk bandwidth** (I/Os per second).
- **Fairness** among competing processes in multi-programmed systems.
- **Mechanical wear reduction** on actuator arms (lifetime extension).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Disk I/O Performance Model

A disk access involves **three sequential mechanical/electronic phases**:

$$T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational} + T_{transfer}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K1-Level Definition (Must Memorize):**
> - **Seek Time ($T_s$):** Time to move the actuator arm to the target cylinder. Proportional to distance. $T_s \approx a + b \cdot d$ (where $d$ is cylinder difference, $a$ is startup time, $b$ is slope).
> - **Rotational Latency ($T_r$):** Average half-rotation time. $T_r = \dfrac{60}{2 \cdot RPM}$ seconds.
> - **Transfer Time ($T_{tr}$):** Time to read/write data once head is positioned.

## 2.2 The Scheduling Algorithms (KTU Core Syllabus — 8 Algorithms)

### A. FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)
- **Logic:** Service requests in arrival order. No reordering.
- **Pros:** Simple, fair, no starvation.
- **Cons:** Wild head movement, worst throughput.

### B. SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)
- **Logic:** Always pick the request closest to current head position.
- **Pros:** Greedy minimization of immediate movement.
- **Cons:** **Starvation** for far-away requests; not optimal globally.

### C. SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)
- **Logic:** Head moves in one direction servicing all requests, reverses at end, services in opposite direction.
- **Pros:** Bounded waiting, good throughput.
- **Cons:** Requests at the *just-passed end* wait longer.

### D. C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)
- **Logic:** Like SCAN, but on reaching the end, jumps back to **0** without servicing, then resumes.
- **Pros:** Uniform wait time (better fairness than SCAN).
- **Cons:** Extra wasted movement to return to start.

### E. LOOK
- **Logic:** Like SCAN, but reverses at the *last request* in current direction (not at cylinder 0 or 199).
- **Pros:** Avoids unnecessary travel to physical ends.

### F. C-LOOK
- **Logic:** Circular variant of LOOK — jump from highest request to lowest, then continue.

### G. FSCAN
- **Logic:** Uses **two queues** — current scan services one queue, new requests accumulate in the other; queues swap at the end of each scan. Prevents "arm-stickiness" from new arrivals.

### H. N-Step SCAN
- **Logic:** Splits the request queue into sub-queues of size **N**, services each sub-queue using SCAN. Prevents head-stickiness in FSCAN's two-queue scheme.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All distances are measured in cylinders. No pipes (`|`) inside markdown — use `\vert` in LaTeX.**

| # | Metric | Formula | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Head Movement (THM)** | $THM = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \vert c_{i+1} - c_i \vert$ | Algorithm comparison |
| 2 | **Average Seek Distance** | $\bar{d} = \dfrac{THM}{n-1}$ | Throughput proxy |
| 3 | **Rotational Latency** | $T_r = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ (in ms) | Latency analysis |
| 4 | **Total Access Time** | $T_a = T_s + T_r + T_{tr}$ | Per-request timing |
| 5 | **Maximum Wait Bound (SCAN)** | $2 \cdot (max\_cyl - min\_cyl)$ | Real-time guarantees |
| 6 | **Throughput** | $IOPs = \dfrac{1}{T_a}$ | I/O capacity |
| 7 | **C-SCAN Worst Case** | $(max - min) + (max - min) = 2(max-min)$ | Bounded comparison |
| 8 | **SSTF Non-Preemptive** | $T_{process} = T_s + T_r + T_{tr}$ | Same formula, no change |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Systems:** PostgreSQL / Oracle use **elevator + deadline-aware** schedulers (`deadline_scheduler` in Linux kernel) for SSD + HDD hybrids.
- **Linux Kernel:** Default since 2.6.16 is the **CFQ / BFQ / mq-deadline / Kyber** scheduler — descendants of classical SCAN.
- **Solid State Drives (SSDs):** Concept of *seek time* vanishes, replaced by **flash translation layer (FTL)** garbage collection — but **NCQ (Native Command Queuing)** in SATA still uses reordering.
- **RAID Controllers:** Reorder I/O across multiple disks to minimize actuator thrashing.
- **HPC Storage:** Lustre and GPFS use **look-ahead I/O scheduling** for parallel file systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Working Example (used throughout this section):**
> - Initial head position: **50**
> - Request queue: **{98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67}**
> - Cylinder range: **0 to 199**
> - Head moving direction: **Towards larger cylinder numbers (outward)**

## 3.1 Algorithm 1 — FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)

The head simply services requests in the order they were received: **50 → 98 → 183 → 37 → 122 → 14 → 124 → 65 → 67**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{FCFS} &= \vert 98 - 50 \vert + \vert 183 - 98 \vert + \vert 37 - 183 \vert + \vert 122 - 37 \vert \\
&\quad + \vert 14 - 122 \vert + \vert 124 - 14 \vert + \vert 65 - 124 \vert + \vert 67 - 65 \vert \\
&= 48 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 \\
&= \boxed{643 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average seek distance** = $643 / 8 = \mathbf{80.375}$ **cylinders/request**.

## 3.2 Algorithm 2 — SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)

At each step, choose the pending request with the **minimum absolute distance** from the current head.

| Step | Current Head | Distances to Pending | Chosen | New Head |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 50 | 98(48), 183(133), 37(13), 122(72), 14(36), 124(74), 65(15), 67(17) | **37** | 37 |
| 1 | 37 | 14(23), 65(28), 67(30), 98(61), 122(85), 124(87), 183(146) | **14** | 14 |
| 2 | 14 | 65(51), 67(53), 98(84), 122(108), 124(110), 183(169) | **65** | 65 |
| 3 | 65 | 67(2), 98(33), 122(57), 124(59), 183(118) | **67** | 67 |
| 4 | 67 | 98(31), 122(55), 124(57), 183(116) | **98** | 98 |
| 5 | 98 | 122(24), 124(26), 183(85) | **122** | 122 |
| 6 | 122 | 124(2), 183(61) | **124** | 124 |
| 7 | 124 | 183(59) | **183** | 183 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{SSTF} &= \vert 37 - 50 \vert + \vert 14 - 37 \vert + \vert 65 - 14 \vert + \vert 67 - 65 \vert \\
&\quad + \vert 98 - 67 \vert + \vert 122 - 98 \vert + \vert 124 - 122 \vert + \vert 183 - 124 \vert \\
&= 13 + 23 + 51 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\
&= \boxed{205 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average seek distance** = $205 / 8 = \mathbf{25.625}$ **cylinders/request**.

## 3.3 Algorithm 3 — SCAN (Elevator)

The head moves towards **199** (outward), servicing all requests on the way. On hitting **199**, it reverses and services in the inward direction.

**Service order:** 50 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → 37 → 14

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{SCAN} &= (65 - 50) + (67 - 65) + (98 - 67) + (122 - 98) + (124 - 122) \\
&\quad + (183 - 124) + (199 - 183) + (199 - 37) + (37 - 14) \\
&= 15 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 \\
&= \boxed{334 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The travel from `199 → 37` = **162** is the "penalty" for servicing the inward queue after reaching the end. In **LOOK**, this penalty is removed.

## 3.4 Algorithm 4 — C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)

The head services outward up to **199**, then **jumps to 0** (without servicing), and resumes servicing inward from **0** upward.

**Service order:** 50 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → [jump] → 0 → 14 → 37

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{C\text{-}SCAN} &= (65 - 50) + (67 - 65) + (98 - 67) + (122 - 98) + (124 - 122) \\
&\quad + (183 - 124) + (199 - 183) + (199 - 0) + (14 - 0) + (37 - 14) \\
&= 15 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 199 + 14 + 23 \\
&= \boxed{385 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** C-SCAN's *worst-case wait* is uniform: every request waits for at most **one full pass of the head** through the entire disk. SCAN's worst case is **2 passes** for requests at the far end.

## 3.5 Algorithm 5 — LOOK (SCAN Optimized)

Same direction logic as SCAN, but reverses at the *last pending request* in current direction, **not at the physical end**.

**Service order:** 50 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 37 → 14

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{LOOK} &= (65 - 50) + (67 - 65) + (98 - 67) + (122 - 98) + (124 - 122) \\
&\quad + (183 - 124) + (183 - 37) + (37 - 14) \\
&= 15 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 146 + 23 \\
&= \boxed{302 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Algorithm 6 — C-LOOK

C-LOOK services the outward queue, then **jumps from the highest serviced cylinder to the lowest pending cylinder**, then services upward.

**Service order:** 50 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → [jump] → 14 → 37

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{C\text{-}LOOK} &= (65 - 50) + (67 - 65) + (98 - 67) + (122 - 98) + (124 - 122) \\
&\quad + (183 - 124) + (183 - 14) + (37 - 14) \\
&= 15 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 169 + 23 \\
&= \boxed{325 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.7 Consolidated Comparison Matrix (KTU Expected Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-asked table in KTU ESE for this topic. Memorize the trend.**

| Algorithm | Service Sequence | THM | Starvation? | Fairness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | 50→98→183→37→122→14→124→65→67 | **643** | No | High (FIFO) |
| **SSTF** | 50→37→14→65→67→98→122→124→183 | **205** | **Yes** | Low |
| **SCAN** | 50→65→67→98→122→124→183→199→37→14 | **334** | No | Medium |
| **C-SCAN** | 50→65→67→98→122→124→183→199→0→14→37 | **385** | No | **Highest** |
| **LOOK** | 50→65→67→98→122→124→183→37→14 | **302** | No | Medium |
| **C-LOOK** | 50→65→67→98→122→124→183→14→37 | **325** | No | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** For this specific dataset, **SSTF gives the lowest THM**, but it suffers starvation. **C-LOOK offers the best fairness-to-overhead trade-off** in real-time systems. FCFS is the worst performer in every metric except fairness and simplicity.

## 3.8 Rotational Latency & Transfer Time Calculation (Auxiliary)

Given: Disk speed = **7200 RPM**, Sector size = **512 bytes**, Request size = **64 KB**, Seek time = **8 ms average**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rotational} &= \frac{30}{7200} = 4.167 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
T_{transfer} &= \frac{64 \times 1024 \text{ bytes}}{512 \text{ bytes/sector} \times \frac{7200}{60} \text{ rev/s}} = \frac{65536}{61440} \text{ rev} \approx 1.067 \text{ rev} \\[4pt]
T_{transfer} &\approx 1.067 \times \frac{60}{7200} \text{ s} = 8.889 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
T_{access} &= T_s + T_r + T_{tr} = 8 + 4.167 + 8.889 = \boxed{21.056 \text{ ms per request}}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Decision Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Disk Request Arrives] --> B{Scheduling Policy}
    B -->|FCFS| C[Service in Arrival Order]
    B -->|SSTF| D[Find Nearest Pending Cylinder]
    B -->|SCAN| E[Move Outward to End Then Reverse]
    B -->|CSCAN| F[Move Outward to End Then Jump to Zero]
    B -->|LOOK| G[Move Outward to Last Request Then Reverse]
    B -->|CLOOK| H[Move Outward to Last Request Then Jump to Lowest]
    B -->|FSCAN| I[Two Queues Swap on Scan End]
    B -->|NSCAN| J[Subqueues of Size N Served by SCAN]
    C --> K[Service Next Request]
    D --> K
    E --> K
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Queue Empty?}
    L -->|No| A
    L -->|Yes| M[Idle Disk Arm]
```

## 4.2 SCAN Algorithm Execution Trace (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Start at 50] --> A2[Move to 65] --> A3[Move to 67] --> A4[Move to 98] --> A5[Move to 122] --> A6[Move to 124] --> A7[Move to 183] --> A8[Move to 199]
    A8 --> A9[Reverse Direction] --> A10[Move to 37] --> A11[Move to 14] --> A12[End of Scan]
```

## 4.3 C-SCAN Circular Jump Diagram (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Head at 50 Outward] --> P2[Service 65 to 67] --> P3[Service 98] --> P4[Service 122 to 124] --> P5[Service 183] --> P6[Reach End 199]
    P6 --> P7[Circular Jump Back to 0]
    P7 --> P8[Service 14] --> P9[Service 37] --> P10[Idle or Continue Outward]
```

## 4.4 Disk I/O Subsystem Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Process] --> B[System Call Read Write]
    B --> C[Kernel I/O Scheduler]
    C --> D[Request Queue FIFO]
    D --> E{Disk Scheduling Algorithm}
    E --> F[FCFS Engine]
    E --> G[SSTF Engine]
    E --> H[SCAN LOOK Engine]
    E --> I[CSCAN CLOOK Engine]
    F --> J[Disk Controller]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Actuator Arm and Platter]
    K --> L[Sector Data Transfer]
    L --> M[Completion Interrupt to Kernel]
    M --> A
```

## 4.5 Starvation vs Fairness Trade-off Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowFairness
        A1[SSTF]
    end
    subgraph MediumFairness
        B1[SCAN]
        B2[LOOK]
    end
    subgraph HighFairness
        C1[FCFS]
        C2[CSCAN]
        C3[CLOOK]
    end
    subgraph HighThroughput
        D1[SSTF]
        D2[LOOK]
        D3[CLOOK]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this diagram:** SSTF lives in the *intersection* of Low-Fairness and High-Throughput. C-SCAN / C-LOOK live in *High-Fairness* and *High-Throughput* — making them the production-grade choice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Read Before Attempting)**
> - Students **lose 2 marks per question** for *not showing the seek sequence in arrow notation* (e.g., `50 → 65 → 67 → ...`). Always draw the path.
> - Students confuse **SCAN** with **C-SCAN**. SCAN reverses at the end; C-SCAN *jumps to 0*. Mixing these up means **wrong THM** — full 7-mark loss.
> - Students forget to convert **RPM to ms** in rotational latency. $T_r = 30 / RPM$ (not $60 / RPM$).
> - The **direction of initial motion** must be stated explicitly. If not stated, examiners assume *outward* by default.
> - In LOOK, students add unnecessary travel to cylinder **0 or 199**. The trick: reverse at *last serviced request*, not the *physical end*.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define disk scheduling. Why is it necessary in an operating system?**

**Model Answer:**
Disk scheduling is the process by which the operating system reorders pending I/O requests in the disk queue to minimize the total head movement (seek time) of the disk actuator. It is necessary because mechanical head positioning is the dominant cost in disk access; without scheduling, the arm oscillates chaotically under FCFS, increasing average response time, reducing I/O throughput, and causing unfair starvation of requests at distant cylinders. Modern OSes use algorithms like SCAN, C-SCAN, or their LOOK variants to balance throughput and fairness.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Distinguish between SCAN and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SCAN | C-SCAN |
|---|---|---|
| End behavior | Reverses at last cylinder | Jumps to cylinder 0, resumes outward |
| Service on return | Services requests in reverse direction | Does not service on return — starts fresh from 0 |
| Wait-time uniformity | Uneven (newly passed end waits long) | Uniform (every request waits for one full sweep) |
| Total movement | Lower for small queues | Slightly higher due to the 0→max reset |
| Starvation | Possible at extremes (revisit delay) | Effectively eliminated |

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — 14 Marks

Consider a disk with **1000 cylinders (numbered 0 to 999)**. The read/write head is initially at cylinder **215** and is moving towards the larger cylinder numbers. The following requests are pending in the queue: **{112, 290, 180, 372, 68, 530, 760, 920, 410, 235}**. Determine the total head movement using:
- **(a)** FCFS algorithm (7 marks)
- **(b)** SCAN algorithm (7 marks)

#### (a) FCFS Solution

Service order: **215 → 112 → 290 → 180 → 372 → 68 → 530 → 760 → 920 → 410 → 235**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{FCFS} &= \vert 112 - 215 \vert + \vert 290 - 112 \vert + \vert 180 - 290 \vert + \vert 372 - 180 \vert \\
&\quad + \vert 68 - 372 \vert + \vert 530 - 68 \vert + \vert 760 - 530 \vert + \vert 920 - 760 \vert \\
&\quad + \vert 410 - 920 \vert + \vert 235 - 410 \vert \\
&= 103 + 178 + 110 + 192 + 304 + 462 + 230 + 160 + 510 + 175 \\
&= \boxed{2424 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) SCAN Solution

Head moves outward (towards 999), servicing pending requests in ascending order, then reverses to service inward.

**Service order:** 215 → 235 → 290 → 372 → 410 → 530 → 760 → 920 → 999 → 180 → 112 → 68

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{SCAN} &= (235 - 215) + (290 - 235) + (372 - 290) + (410 - 372) + (530 - 410) \\
&\quad + (760 - 530) + (920 - 760) + (999 - 920) + (999 - 180) + (180 - 112) + (112 - 68) \\
&= 20 + 55 + 82 + 38 + 120 + 230 + 160 + 79 + 819 + 68 + 44 \\
&= \boxed{1715 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inference:** SCAN reduces head movement by **2424 − 1715 = 709 cylinders (≈ 29.2% improvement)** over FCFS in this instance.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Internal Choice to A)

For the same disk geometry (1000 cylinders, head starting at 215, moving outward), with request queue **{112, 290, 180, 372, 68, 530, 760, 920, 410, 235}**, compute the total head movement for:
- **(a)** SSTF algorithm (7 marks)
- **(b)** C-LOOK algorithm (7 marks)

#### (a) SSTF Solution

Greedy nearest-neighbor at each step.

| Step | Current | Pending (Distance) | Chosen | New |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 215 | 112(103), 290(75), 180(35), 372(157), 68(147), 530(315), 760(545), 920(705), 410(195), 235(20) | **235** | 235 |
| 1 | 235 | 112(123), 290(55), 180(55), 372(137), 68(167), 530(295), 760(525), 920(685), 410(175) | **180** (tied with 290) | 180 |
| 2 | 180 | 112(68), 290(110), 372(192), 68(112), 530(350), 760(580), 920(740), 410(230) | **112** | 112 |
| 3 | 112 | 68(44), 290(178), 372(260), 530(418), 760(648), 920(808), 410(298) | **68** | 68 |
| 4 | 68 | 290(222), 372(304), 530(462), 760(692), 920(852), 410(342) | **290** | 290 |
| 5 | 290 | 372(82), 530(240), 760(470), 920(630), 410(120) | **372** | 372 |
| 6 | 372 | 530(158), 760(388), 920(548), 410(38) | **410** | 410 |
| 7 | 410 | 530(120), 760(350), 920(510) | **530** | 530 |
| 8 | 530 | 760(230), 920(390) | **760** | 760 |
| 9 | 760 | 920(160) | **920** | 920 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{SSTF} &= 20 + 55 + 68 + 44 + 222 + 82 + 38 + 120 + 230 + 160 \\
&= \boxed{1039 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) C-LOOK Solution

Service all requests in current direction (outward), then jump from the *highest serviced* to the *lowest pending*, then continue outward again.

**Service order:** 215 → 235 → 290 → 372 → 410 → 530 → 760 → 920 → 68 → 112 → 180

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{C\text{-}LOOK} &= (235 - 215) + (290 - 235) + (372 - 290) + (410 - 372) + (530 - 410) \\
&\quad + (760 - 530) + (920 - 760) + (920 - 68) + (112 - 68) + (180 - 112) \\
&= 20 + 55 + 82 + 38 + 120 + 230 + 160 + 852 + 44 + 68 \\
&= \boxed{1669 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Summary:**

| Algorithm | THM | Improvement vs FCFS |
|---|---|---|
| FCFS | 2424 | — |
| SCAN | 1715 | 29.2% |
| SSTF | 1039 | 57.1% (but starvation possible) |
| **C-LOOK** | **1669** | **31.2% (with no starvation)** |

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets**

- **Total Head Movement (THM)** is the *single most-asked metric* — always compute as sum of `|c_{i+1} − c_i|` along the actual service order.
- **FCFS** = no reordering, max THM, max starvation-prevention.
- **SSTF** = greedy minimum-distance, can cause starvation of distant cylinders.
- **SCAN** = elevator, reverses at cylinder boundary (0 or max).
- **C-SCAN** = circular, jumps to 0, no reverse-servicing, uniform wait.
- **LOOK** = SCAN without traveling to physical ends; reverses at *last request*.
- **C-LOOK** = circular LOOK; jump from highest serviced to lowest pending.
- **FSCAN** = two-queue freeze-and-swap scheme to avoid arm-stickiness.
- **N-Step SCAN** = generalization of FSCAN with **N** sub-queues.
- **Rotational Latency** formula: $T_r = 30 / RPM$ in milliseconds (not 60).
- **Total Access Time** = $T_s + T_r + T_{tr}$ — *always* in the order seek → rotate → transfer.
- **Direction matters**: If the head is at 50 and "moving outward," the next service in SCAN is the *smallest request greater than 50*, not the closest overall.
- **Initial direction must be assumed** if not stated — KTU default is usually *outward* (toward higher cylinder number).
- **SSD does not need seek-based scheduling** — uses NCQ, FTL, GC instead.
- **Linux default for HDD**: `mq-deadline` / `BFQ` — both are SCAN derivatives with deadline awareness.
- **Starvation rule of thumb**: SSTF > SCAN > C-SCAN in decreasing starvation risk.
- **Fairness rule of thumb**: C-SCAN ≈ C-LOOK > SCAN > SSTF in increasing fairness order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Formatting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT521 - MODERN OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Formatting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Disk Formatting** is the preparatory process of initializing a physical storage device (magnetic hard disk, SSD, or optical media) by structuring its raw storage capacity into addressable, manageable units (sectors, tracks, and cylinders) and subsequently organizing those units under a logical **file system** that the operating system can recognize, navigate, and manipulate.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Modern Operating Systems, PEEOT521, Module 4)**, disk formatting is treated as the foundational bridge between **hardware-level disk geometry** and the **software-level file system abstraction** — it is the operation that transforms a "blank slab of magnetic platters" into a usable storage volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Distinction (High-Yield):**
> The KTU syllabus strictly differentiates between two types of formatting:
>
> 1. **Physical Formatting (Low-Level Formatting / LLF)** — Performed at the factory; establishes the actual **sectors, tracks, and cylinders** on the platters by writing sector headers, sync bytes, and ECC (Error Correcting Code) fields. It is rarely done by end users.
> 2. **Logical Formatting (High-Level Formatting / HLF)** — Performed by the OS or user utility; writes the **boot sector, file system metadata (superblock, FAT, MFT, inode table, journal)**, and creates the **root directory**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Empty Library" Intuition

Imagine you have just been handed a brand new, massive **empty warehouse** with no shelves, no signs, no aisles, and no catalog system.

- **Step 1 — Physical Formatting** is like a construction crew coming in and **building identical cubbies (sectors) along rows (tracks) and stacks (cylinders)**. Every cubby has a number painted on it. This is a physical, hardware-level transformation.
- **Step 2 — Partitioning** is like erecting **walls to divide the giant warehouse into smaller, manageable rooms** (e.g., one for documents, one for media, one for the OS).
- **Step 3 — Logical Formatting** is like installing a **librarian and a card catalog (file system)**. The librarian decides: "This cubby is the *index*, this holds the *table of contents*, this is the *front desk* (root directory)."
- **Step 4 — Bad Block Mapping** is like discovering that **cubby #4,521 has a broken shelf** — you paste a sticker on it saying "Do Not Use" and quietly reroute all requests to **cubby #4,521-S (a spare)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Storage Metrics used in KTU Calculations:**
> - **Sector Size:** **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes (4 KiB)** — called "Advanced Format" in modern drives.
> - **Block / Cluster Size:** Typically **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, or 64 sectors** per cluster.
> - **Track:** Concentric ring on a platter surface.
> - **Cylinder:** Set of tracks at the same radius across all platters (the unit addressed when the heads do not move).

## 1.3 Why Disk Formatting Matters in Modern OS Design

Disk formatting is not merely a "one-time setup" task. In modern operating systems it directly impacts:

- **I/O Performance** — Sector size, interleave factor, and block alignment determine throughput.
- **Reliability** — ECC fields, bad block remapping, and S.M.A.R.T. integration depend on formatting metadata.
- **Security** — Secure formatting (e.g., ATA Secure Erase) overwrites sectors to defeat forensic recovery.
- **Capacity Efficiency** — Cluster size vs. internal fragmentation trade-off.
- **Bootstrapping** — The boot sector (created during HLF) is what allows the BIOS/UEFI to load the OS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Formatting Model

### Stage 1 — Physical (Low-Level) Formatting

Performed at the **disk controller / drive firmware** level. It establishes the **sector layout** that the drive electronics will use for every subsequent read/write operation.

**Sector Structure (written by LLF):**

| Field | Purpose | Typical Size |
|---|---|---|
| **Sync Bytes / Preamble** | Allows the drive's PLL to lock onto the bit stream | ~15 bytes |
| **Sector ID / Address Mark** | Unique sector identifier (cylinder, head, sector number) | ~7 bytes |
| **Data Field** | Actual user data payload | **512 B / 4096 B** |
| **ECC / CRC Field** | Error detection and correction bits | ~40 bytes |
| **Inter-sector Gap** | Physical buffer between sectors | varies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Modern drives use **Zone Bit Recording (ZBR)** — outer tracks contain **more sectors** than inner tracks (because outer tracks are physically longer). This means the LLF must define *zoned* geometry, not uniform geometry.

### Stage 2 — Logical (High-Level) Formatting

Performed by an **OS utility** (e.g., `mkfs.ext4`, `format` in Windows, `newfs_hfs` in macOS). It writes the file-system-specific structures onto the already-physically-formatted medium.

**Logical structures created:**

1. **Volume Boot Record (VBR) / Boot Sector** — Contains the bootloader stub and BPB (BIOS Parameter Block) describing the volume.
2. **File System Metadata**
   - FAT32 → **FAT tables** + **root directory**
   - NTFS → **Master File Table (MFT)**
   - ext4 → **Superblock, Group Descriptors, Inode Table, Journal**
   - HFS+ → **Volume Header, Catalog File, Allocation File**
3. **Free Space Bitmap / Block Bitmap** — Tracks which clusters are in use.
4. **Root Directory Entry** — The starting point of the file hierarchy.

## 2.2 The Partitioning Step (Often Confused With Formatting)

Partitioning divides a single physical disk into one or more **logical volumes**. Each partition is then independently formatted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Partition Table Schemes (KTU expects awareness):**
> - **MBR (Master Boot Record)** — 4 primary partitions max, 2 TiB limit, legacy BIOS.
> - **GPT (GUID Partition Table)** — 128 partitions, 8 ZiB limit, required for UEFI boot.

## 2.3 Bad Block Management — A Direct Consequence of Formatting

During LLF, the manufacturer scans the platters and identifies defective regions. These are handled by:

| Strategy | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Sector Sparing** | Defective sector is remapped to a spare sector from a reserved pool. Translation done in drive firmware. |
| **Sector Slipping** | Defective sector is logically "skipped" — the next logical sector is physically located one sector later. |
| **Bad Block Table (OS-level)** | OS marks the cluster as bad in its free-space bitmap (e.g., `mke2fs -c` runs a read-scan). |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in KTU numerical questions.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity (raw) | $C = \text{bytes/sector} \times \text{sectors/track} \times \text{tracks/surface} \times \text{surfaces}$ | bytes |
| Drive capacity | $C_{drive} = S \times T \times H$ (S=sectors/track, T=tracks/surface, H=surfaces) | bytes |
| Transfer Time (1 sector) | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{\text{sector size}}{\text{transfer rate}}$ | seconds |
| Cylinder addressing | Cylinder = same track number across all platters (all heads stationary) | — |
| Cluster size | $\text{Cluster} = 2^n \times \text{sector size},\ n \in \{0,1,\ldots,6\}$ | bytes |
| Effective capacity | $C_{eff} = \text{Cluster size} \times \text{Number of usable clusters}$ | bytes |
| Internal fragmentation per file | $W_{waste} = \text{Cluster size} - (\text{file size} \bmod \text{cluster size})$ | bytes |
| Max partitions (MBR) | 4 primary, or 3 primary + 1 extended (with many logical inside) | — |
| MBR max addressable LBA | $2^{32} \times 512\,\text{B} = 2\,\text{TiB}$ | — |
| GPT max addressable LBA | $2^{64} \times 512\,\text{B} = 8\,\text{ZiB}$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse** disk *transfer rate* (MB/s) with disk *capacity* (MB). They use the same acronym but are entirely different quantities.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers** — Re-formatting enterprise SSDs with a larger block size improves sequential throughput for databases.
- **Forensics** — Understanding LLF vs. HLF is critical for *undeleting* files and recovering data from "formatted" drives.
- **Embedded Systems** — Raw formatting (no file system, e.g., `mkfs.ext4 -O ^has_journal`) reduces flash wear on IoT devices.
- **OS Boot Engineering** — The boot sector written by HLF is what the BIOS/UEFI executes; corruption here prevents the OS from loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Raw Disk Capacity

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A magnetic hard disk has **4 platters (8 surfaces)**, **2,048 tracks per surface**, and an average of **256 sectors per track**. Each sector is **512 bytes**. Compute the raw formatted capacity of the drive.

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**

Let $S$ = sectors per track, $T$ = tracks per surface, $H$ = number of recording surfaces.

$$
S = 256, \quad T = 2048, \quad H = 8, \quad B = 512\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the capacity formula.**

The total capacity is the product of the number of recording surfaces, the number of tracks per surface, the number of sectors per track, and the bytes per sector:

$$
C = S \times T \times H \times B
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
C = 256 \times 2048 \times 8 \times 512
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate step-by-step (do not skip).**

$$
256 \times 2048 = 524{,}288\,\text{sectors per surface}
$$

$$
524{,}288 \times 8 = 4{,}194{,}304\,\text{total sectors}
$$

$$
4{,}194{,}304 \times 512 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to engineering units.**

$$
C = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648\,\text{B} = 2\,\text{GB} = 2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{B (decimal, marketed)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Disk manufacturers use decimal (1 GB = $10^9$ B), while OS utilities report binary (1 GiB = $2^{30}$ B). The formatted capacity *shown by the OS* will therefore be ~**1.86 GiB**, not 2 GB.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Capacity After Cluster Overhead

> **Problem Statement:** A 40 GB disk is formatted with FAT32 using a **cluster size of 16 KB**. The file system reserves **65,536 clusters** for metadata. Compute the **effective user data capacity**.

**Step 1 — Convert disk size to bytes.**

$$
C_{raw} = 40 \times 10^9\,\text{B}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert cluster size to bytes.**

$$
\text{Cluster size} = 16\,\text{KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16{,}384\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total clusters available on the disk.**

$$
N_{total} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{C_{raw}}{\text{Cluster size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{40 \times 10^9}{16{,}384} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
N_{total} = \left\lfloor 2{,}441{,}406.25 \right\rfloor = 2{,}441{,}406\,\text{clusters}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract reserved metadata clusters.**

$$
N_{user} = N_{total} - N_{reserved} = 2{,}441{,}406 - 65{,}536 = 2{,}375{,}870\,\text{user clusters}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute effective user capacity.**

$$
C_{eff} = N_{user} \times \text{Cluster size}
$$

$$
C_{eff} = 2{,}375{,}870 \times 16{,}384\,\text{B} = 38{,}925{,}230{,}080\,\text{B} \approx 38.93\,\text{GB}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Formatting Strategies

| Parameter | Physical (Low-Level) Format | Logical (High-Level) Format | Partitioning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Performed by** | Drive manufacturer firmware | OS utility / user command | `fdisk`, `parted`, Disk Management GUI |
| **What it writes** | Sector headers, ECC, sync bytes | Boot sector, FS metadata, root dir | MBR / GPT partition table entries |
| **Erases data?** | No (writes structural fields only) | **Yes — destroys all file system data** | **Yes — destroys partition table** |
