| **Reversible?** | Practically **irreversible** | Yes, by re-formatting | Yes, by re-partitioning |
| **Frequency** | Once at factory | Anytime by user | Anytime by user |
| **Failure impact** | Drive unusable | OS cannot mount volume | Cannot boot / partitions invisible |
| **Bad-block action** | Marked in factory P-list | Detected via `fsck` / `chkdsk` | None |
| **KTU question weight** | 40% | 50% | 10% |

## 3.4 The Boot Process — Why Formatting Matters for OS Boot

When the PC powers on, the following sequence occurs, all depending on what the formatting steps wrote:

$$
\text{BIOS/UEFI POST} \rightarrow \text{Read MBR/GPT} \rightarrow \text{Locate Active Partition} \rightarrow \text{Load VBR (written by HLF)} \rightarrow \text{Execute Bootloader} \rightarrow \text{Load OS}
$$

Every step except POST depends on a correctly written formatted structure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Disk Preparation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Factory Blank Platters] --> B[Physical Low-Level Formatting]
    B --> B1[Establish Sectors per Track using ZBR]
    B --> B2[Write Sync Bytes and ECC Fields]
    B --> B3[Build Primary Defect List P-List]
    B1 --> C[Raw Addressable Disk]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Partitioning Step]
    D --> D1{Choose MBR or GPT}
    D1 --> D2[Create Partition Table Entries]
    D2 --> E[One or More Partitions]
    E --> F[Logical High-Level Formatting per Partition]
    F --> F1[Write Volume Boot Record VBR]
    F --> F2[Write File System Metadata]
    F --> F3[Create Root Directory and Bitmap]
    F1 --> G[Ready to Mount Volume]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[OS Reads Directory and Serves User I/O]
```

## 4.2 Disk Geometry — Cylinder / Track / Sector Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Hard Disk Drive Geometry] --> B0[Platter 1]
    A0 --> C0[Platter 2]
    A0 --> D0[Platter N]
    B0 --> B1[Surface Top]
    B0 --> B2[Surface Bottom]
    C0 --> C1[Surface Top]
    C0 --> C2[Surface Bottom]
    B1 --> B1A[Track 0 outermost]
    B1 --> B1B[Track T middle]
    B1 --> B1C[Track max innermost]
    B1A --> B1A1[Sector 0]
    B1A --> B1A2[Sector 1]
    B1A --> B1A3[Sector 2]
    B1A --> B1A4[Sector N minus 1]
    C1A1[Cylinder Concept: All tracks at same radius across all platters read simultaneously]
```

## 4.3 Sector Internal Layout (What LLF Writes)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 6
    block:sec1
        A["Preamble 15B"]
        B["Sync 1B"]
        C["Addr Mark 1B"]
        D["Sector ID 4B"]
        E["Data 512B or 4096B"]
        F["ECC 40B"]
    end
```

## 4.4 File System Metadata Layout (What HLF Writes — Generic View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boot Block] --> B[Superblock]
    B --> C[Free Space Bitmap]
    C --> D[Inode or FAT Table]
    D --> E[Root Directory]
    E --> F[Data Blocks / Clusters]
    F --> G[Journal or Log optional]
```

## 4.5 Bad Block Remapping Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant FS as File System Driver
    participant CTL as Disk Controller Firmware
    participant P as Physical Platter

    OS->>FS: Write to LBA 4521
    FS->>CTL: Translate LBA to CHS or PBA
    CTL->>P: Attempt write to PBA 4521
    P-->>CTL: WRITE FAULT sector defective
    CTL->>CTL: Map PBA 4521 to spare PBA 9999
    CTL-->>FS: Write completed at PBA 9999
    FS-->>OS: Write ACK to LBA 4521 transparent
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **Differentiate between Low-Level Formatting and High-Level Formatting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — structured presentation expected):**

| Aspect | Low-Level Formatting | High-Level Formatting |
|---|---|---|
| **Who performs it** | Disk manufacturer (factory) | Operating system / user |
| **What is created** | Physical sectors, tracks, cylinders with ECC | File system structures (FAT, MFT, superblock) |
| **Affects** | The actual platter surface (sector headers written magnetically) | Logical organization on an already-LLF-formatted disk |
| **Data destruction** | Does not affect user data areas (just writes structure) | Destroys all existing user data on the partition |
| **Frequency** | Once at manufacturing | Anytime by user |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the comparison table = 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
> **What is a sector spare? How does it relate to bad block management during disk formatting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sector spare** is a physically good sector on the platter that is **reserved in a hidden spare area** and is **not exposed** to the operating system during logical formatting. When the disk controller's firmware detects that a primary sector has become defective (during manufacturing via the P-List, or during the drive's lifetime via the G-List), it **transparently remaps** all future reads/writes intended for the defective sector to one of these spares.

**Relation to formatting:**
1. During **physical formatting**, the spare sectors are *reserved but not addressable* through the public LBA space.
2. The **logical formatting** layer (FAT, MFT, etc.) is unaware of this remapping — it just issues LBAs.
3. The mapping is maintained inside the drive's firmware translation table.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark relation to formatting = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q3. (a)** Explain in detail the **process of disk formatting**, covering both **physical (low-level)** and **logical (high-level)** formatting, with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Disk formatting is the process of preparing a storage device for use by an operating system. It is performed in two major stages: **Physical (Low-Level) Formatting** and **Logical (High-Level) Formatting**.

**Physical Formatting (3 marks):**
- Performed at the **factory** by the drive manufacturer.
- Divides each platter surface into **concentric tracks**, and each track into **sectors** of fixed size (typically **512 bytes** legacy or **4096 bytes** Advanced Format).
- Each sector is stamped with:
  - **Preamble / Sync bytes** — for clock synchronization.
  - **Sector ID / Address Mark** — uniquely identifies (cylinder, head, sector).
  - **Data field** — the user data region.
  - **ECC / CRC field** — for error detection and correction.
  - **Inter-sector gap** — a physical buffer.
- Modern drives use **Zone Bit Recording (ZBR)**, where outer tracks contain more sectors than inner tracks (since the circumference is longer).
- A **Primary Defect List (P-List)** is built and defective sectors are mapped to **spares** via sector slipping or sector sparing.

**Logical Formatting (2 marks):**
- Performed by the OS or a user utility (e.g., `mkfs.ext4`, Windows `format` command).
- Writes file-system-specific structures onto the partition:
  1. **Volume Boot Record (VBR)** — contains bootloader stub and BPB.
  2. **File system metadata** — Superblock (ext4), FAT (FAT32), MFT (NTFS), Catalog File (HFS+).
  3. **Free space bitmap** — tracks allocated vs. free clusters.
  4. **Root directory** — top of the directory tree.
- A logical format **does not touch** the physical sector layout; it only writes data into the sectors already created by LLF.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A pipeline showing **LLF → Partitioning → HLF → Mount**.

**Conclusion:** The two stages work in tandem — LLF gives the disk physical structure, HLF gives it a file-system personality.

---

> **Q3. (b)** A hard disk has **6 platters (12 surfaces)**, **1,024 tracks per surface**, with outer zones containing **256 sectors/track** and inner zones containing **128 sectors/track**. The drive uses **2 zones** with the outer zone covering **60%** of the tracks and the inner zone **40%**. Sector size is **512 bytes**. Compute the **total formatted capacity**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
- Surfaces: $H = 12$
- Tracks per surface: $T = 1024$
- Outer zone: 60% of tracks → $0.6 \times 1024 = 614.4 \approx 614$ tracks, with $S_{outer} = 256$ sectors/track
- Inner zone: 40% of tracks → $0.4 \times 1024 = 409.6 \approx 410$ tracks, with $S_{inner} = 128$ sectors/track
- Sector size: $B = 512$ bytes

**Step 2 — Sectors in outer zone per surface (1 mark):**

$$
N_{outer} = 614 \times 256 = 157{,}184\,\text{sectors}
$$

**Step 3 — Sectors in inner zone per surface (1 mark):**

$$
N_{inner} = 410 \times 128 = 52{,}480\,\text{sectors}
$$

**Step 4 — Sectors per surface (1 mark):**

$$
N_{surface} = 157{,}184 + 52{,}480 = 209{,}664\,\text{sectors}
$$

**Step 5 — Total sectors on drive (1 mark):**

$$
N_{total} = N_{surface} \times H = 209{,}664 \times 12 = 2{,}515{,}968\,\text{sectors}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute total capacity (1 mark):**

$$
C = N_{total} \times B = 2{,}515{,}968 \times 512 = 1{,}288{,}175{,}616\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to GB (1 mark):**

$$
C \approx 1.288 \times 10^9\,\text{B} \approx 1.29\,\text{GB}
$$

### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q4. (a)** Discuss the role of **bad block management strategies** — sector sparing and sector slipping — during physical formatting. How does the operating system remain *unaware* of these remappings? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Bad block management is the process of detecting, recording, and **transparently remapping** defective sectors on a disk so that the OS perceives the disk as fully functional.

**Sector Sparing (2 marks):**
- The manufacturer reserves a pool of **spare sectors** at the end of each track, cylinder, or zone (typically 1–2% of total capacity).
- When a sector is found defective during the LLF scan, it is **replaced** by a spare sector.
- The mapping (defective LBA → spare PBA) is stored in a table inside the disk controller's firmware (often in a non-volatile region of the platter).
- All future I/O to the defective LBA is **automatically redirected** by the controller to the spare PBA.

**Sector Slipping (2 marks):**
- An *alternative* to sector sparing.
- The defective sector is logically "**skipped**" — the next logical sector number is physically located one sector later on the same track.
- Advantage: no extra physical sectors needed; no latency penalty for seek-to-spare.
- Disadvantage: complex to implement for the controller; harder to grow the spare pool dynamically.

**OS Transparency (2 marks):**
- The OS issues I/O using **Logical Block Addresses (LBA)** — a linear, abstract address space starting at 0.
- The disk controller has its own internal **Physical Block Address (PBA)** space, which includes the hidden spares.
- A **firmware translation table** maps LBA → PBA, applying any sector-spare or sector-slip remappings on every access.
- Because this translation happens *below* the disk interface (ATA/SATA/NVMe), the OS and file system are **completely unaware** of defects or remapping — they simply see a working LBA space.

---

> **Q4. (b)** Compare **MBR and GPT** partitioning schemes. Why is GPT mandatory for drives larger than 2 TiB? Show the relevant calculation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**MBR (Master Boot Record) (2 marks):**
- Located at **LBA 0** of the disk.
- Contains: 446 B bootloader code + 64 B partition table (4 entries × 16 B) + 2 B signature (`0x55AA`).
- Supports a maximum of **4 primary partitions** (or 3 primary + 1 extended containing many logical).
- Partition entries use **32-bit LBA addresses**.

**GPT (GUID Partition Table) (2 marks):**
- Defined as part of the **UEFI** standard.
- Protective MBR at LBA 0 (for backward compatibility) + Primary GPT header at LBA 1 + partition entries from LBA 2.
- Supports up to **128 partitions** by default (each entry 128 B).
- Partition entries use **64-bit LBA addresses**.
- Stores a CRC32 checksum for integrity.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | MBR | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Max partitions | 4 primary | 128 |
| Max addressable LBA | $2^{32}-1$ | $2^{64}-1$ |
| Max capacity (512 B sector) | $2^{32} \times 512 = 2\,\text{TiB}$ | $2^{64} \times 512 = 8\,\text{ZiB}$ |
| Boot mode | Legacy BIOS | UEFI |
| Checksum | None | CRC32 |
| Backup | None | Secondary GPT at end of disk |

**2 TiB Limit Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
C_{MBR,max} = 2^{32}\,\text{LBA} \times 512\,\text{B} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \times 512 = 2{,}199{,}023{,}255{,}552\,\text{B} = 2\,\text{TiB}
$$

Any sector with LBA $\geq 2^{32}$ is **unaddressable** in MBR — so GPT is mandatory for larger drives.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LLF with HLF** — Many students describe "formatting" as just one operation. *Always* state both stages explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting OS transparency** — When asked about bad block handling, students often say "the OS handles it" — the correct answer is that **the disk controller** handles it, transparently to the OS.
> 3. **Decimal vs. binary units** — Always show unit conversions explicitly; examiners deduct 1 mark if `1 GB = 1024 MB` is assumed without stating it.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark answers** — A well-labeled pipeline diagram earns at least 1 mark even if the prose is weak.
> 5. **Forgetting the bootloader** — A disk is "formatted" but not "bootable" until the HLF writes a valid VBR.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> 📌 **Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**

- ⭐ **Two stages of formatting:** **Physical (LLF)** at the factory creates sectors/tracks/cylinders; **Logical (HLF)** by the OS creates the file system.
- ⭐ **Sector size:** **512 B** legacy, **4096 B** Advanced Format.
- ⭐ **ZBR (Zone Bit Recording):** Outer tracks hold **more sectors** than inner tracks.
- ⭐ **Bad block handling is done in the disk controller firmware**, not the OS — OS sees a clean LBA space.
- ⭐ **Sector sparing** uses a reserved pool; **sector slipping** logically skips the bad sector.
- ⭐ **Cluster size** = $2^n \times$ sector size; larger clusters = less metadata overhead, more internal fragmentation.
- ⭐ **MBR limit:** 4 primary partitions, **2 TiB max** (because LBA is 32-bit × 512 B).
- ⭐ **GPT limit:** 128 partitions, **8 ZiB max** (64-bit LBA).
- ⭐ **Bootstrapping depends on HLF:** BIOS/UEFI → MBR/GPT → VBR (written by HLF) → Bootloader → OS.
- ⭐ **Formatting pipeline order:** Factory LLF → Partitioning (MBR/GPT) → HLF per partition → Mount.
- ⭐ **Modern drives hide spare sectors** from the OS, reducing *advertised* capacity slightly.
- ⭐ **Forensic recovery** of a "quick-formatted" drive is often possible because HLF only rewrites metadata, not user data sectors.
- ⭐ **Capacity formulas:** $C = S \times T \times H \times B$; effective capacity excludes reserved clusters.
- ⭐ **Secure Erase / ATA Secure Erase** is a drive-level command that overwrites *all* sectors (including remapped ones) — it is the only true "low-level" data destruction.
